RNA information: 5'CNG/3'CNG refers to repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide), commonly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₂ RNA is a repetitive hairpin-forming sequence that adopts a stable secondary structure characterized by regularly spaced U–U mismatches within a C–G base-paired stem. These noncanonical interactions create specific binding pockets that sequester the splicing regulator MBNL1, thereby disrupting its normal function and driving disease phenotypes such as mis-splicing in myotonic dystrophy type 1. The structured repeats also provide discrete molecular recognition elements that can be selectively targeted by small molecules, enabling competitive displacement of MBNL1 and partial restoration of RNA processing functions.
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[(2S)-2-[methyl-[(2S)-2-[methyl-[(2S)-2-[methyl-[(2S)-2-[methyl-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]butyl]-N-methyl-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-K4NMeS, is a massive N-methylated oligopeptide-heterocycle conjugate, which is a rationally designed, modular small molecule that functions as a potent and selective inhibitor of the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA; its structure features two benzimidazole RNA-binding modules (H motifs) displayed on an N-methyl peptide backbone, with the structural core being a five-residue N-methylated (2S)-alanine chain where N-methylation is incorporated to improve metabolic stability against peptidases and enhance cell membrane penetration, and this oligopeptide backbone is connected via amide bonds to a complex bis-benzimidazole unit, a structural motif well-known as a high-affinity DNA minor groove binder, while the design of 2H-K4NMeS enables the molecule to bind multivalently to two adjacent 1x1 UU internal loops within the r(CUG)exp hairpin; functionally, it acts through non-covalent binding to displace the sequestered MBNL1 protein from the pathogenic RNA, and in DM1 patient-derived cells, it effectively ameliorates disease-associated phenotypes including correcting MBNL1-dependent pre-mRNA splicing defects, reducing the number of r(CUG)exp nuclear foci and improving the impaired nucleocytoplasmic transport of transcripts containing the expanded repeat, and the molecule exhibits improved metabolic stability compared to previous analogues and serves as the foundational core for developing advanced compounds like covalent binders, cleavers, and components for on-site probe synthesis, and the compound is designed to utilize the oligopeptide as a high-penetration vector to deliver the high-affinity DNA-binding unit into cells, holding significant potential in the development of anti-tumor therapeutics, gene silencing agents, or diagnostic probes that interfere with gene expression or replication.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-one, also referred to as SMSM10, is a pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-one derivative substituted at position 2 with the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine moiety and at position 7 with the (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium cation, which contains a specific chiral center, and it is a non-planar coumarin derivative with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, bearing a 1 charge that binds the single-G bulge of RNA1 via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, validated by FP and SAR comparison, representing a weak but selective RNA ligand; the presence of the chiral methyl group is vital for highly selective binding to stereospecific biological targets such as chiral enzymes or receptors, potentially leading to superior activity and selectivity compared to non-chiral counterparts, the quaternary ammonium center ensures a positive charge, aiding in water solubility and electrostatic target binding, and the combination of the pyrido-pyrimidine core and the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group suggests potential pharmacological activity as a phosphodiesterase or tyrosine kinase inhibitor, promising applications in oncology or inflammation regulation.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-diethyl-N-(5-methyl-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)ethane-1,2-diamine iodide is an indoloquinoline quaternary ammonium salt derivative. The structure features the indoloquinoline scaffold, with the nitrogen at the 5-position quaternized by a methyl group, and a diethyl-ethylenediamine side chain at the 11-position, with iodide as the counterion (molecular formula C22H27N4I). This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and a positive charge, resulting in enhanced DNA intercalation capacity and inherent cytotoxicity, making it a compelling anti-tumor drug candidate.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as an indolo[3,2-b]quinoline with the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 3-position and a 2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, is a neutral indolo[3,2-b]quinoline with an N-methylpiperazine side chain; this design aims to balance good membrane permeability (due to the neutral amine) with improved solubility and target affinity (due to the piperazine), and the compound demonstrated binding affinity for the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex while showing good anti-proliferative activity, and it is investigated as a potential antiproliferative agent.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: N-cyclopentyl-4-[5-[4-(N-cyclopentylcarbamimidoyl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as DB244 (2,5-bis-[4-(N-cyclopentylamidino)phenyl]furan), is a bis-cyclopentyl amidine-substituted biphenyl furan structure, and DB244 is a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative that belongs to the same chemotype as DB60 and DB75 but is endowed with bulkier cyclopentylamidine end-groups, furan-phenyl scaffolds are often used to construct fluorescent probes or molecules with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, the bis-amidine substitution suggests potential DNA binding or serine protease inhibitory activity, with the cyclopentyl substitution likely intended to enhance lipophilicity and cell membrane permeability, DB244 was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii (PCP) therapy, exhibits excellent DNA minor-groove binding and ranks among the most potent agents against PCP in rat models (DB244 DB75 DB226 DB60), when redirected to the HIV-1 TAR RNA target, the added bulk reduces RNA affinity, in vitro gel-shift assays show notable IC50 for displacing Tat peptide from the 59-nt TAR hairpin, while the FIGS cellular assay shows essentially no suppression of Tat-driven trans-activation up to a high tested concentration, melting-temperature experiments record a modest Tm with DB244TAR complexes, and RNase A footprints reveal protection of the GCUCU site opposite the bulge only at high concentrations, confirming weaker, lower-occupancy binding, like DB60, DB244 recognizes the three-dimensional cavity created by the pyrimidine bulge rather than specific nucleotides: mutation of U23 or the GC26-39 pair has little influence on affinity, whereas deletion of the bulge abolishes the interaction, the compound is essentially non-toxic to reporter cells below a certain concentration and retains full anti-PCP potency, illustrating how increasing the steric demand of the terminal basic chains tips the selectivity balance toward DNA and away from RNA recognition, thus, although DB244 itself is not an effective anti-HIV lead, its profile delineates structureselectivity relationships that guide the design of next-generation TAR-targeted diphenylfurans. Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: 7,8-dimethyl-10-(2,3,4,5-tetrahydroxypentyl)benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also known as vitamin B2 and also referred to as Riboflavin, is an important water-soluble vitamin whose structure consists of an isoalloxazine aromatic ring and a ribitol side chain, adopting a flat yet slightly flexible molecular configuration; it serves as a precursor for flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD), two molecules that act as coenzymes in various redox reactions in organisms, playing a crucial role in cellular energy metabolism and biosynthetic processes, and as a weak competitive ligand, riboflavin can enter the aptamer pocket of bacterial FMN riboswitches, and although its affinity is lower than that of FMN, it can still induce conformational changes in RNA. In the article, the binding ability of riboflavin to the FMN riboswitch was studied, revealing its role in regulating gene expression: riboflavin can bind to the FMN riboswitch, thereby regulating the expression of genes related to riboflavin biosynthesis and transport, and partially inhibiting the expression of downstream riboflavin synthesis genes, thereby participating in the negative feedback regulation of its own biosynthesis; when riboflavin or its derivative FMN binds to the riboswitch, it induces the formation of a transcription termination structure, thereby inhibiting the expression of downstream genes, a mechanism that is vital for maintaining the homeostasis of riboflavin levels in cells.

Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-[[4-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]methoxy]phenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5c (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a 4-(trifluoromethyl)benzyloxy group, and Compound 5c is synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings and was characterized by methods like NMR and SPR, which functionally binds to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB with certain binding affinity, has binding specificity ratios relative to control hairpins GGh and TARh comparable to Rev, shows higher IC in inhibiting Bh-Rev interaction and no antiviral activity in cell assays, while the introduction of trifluoromethyl is an effective means in medicinal chemistry to increase a molecules lipophilicity, metabolic stability, and trans-membrane permeability, and this compound remains a tetracationic polyamine, functionally, the strong electron-withdrawing effect and high lipophilicity of the trifluoromethyl group may enable it to possess superior pharmacokinetic properties while retaining the core polyamines nucleic acid affinity, and such compounds are often explored for developing a new generation of gene delivery or anticancer drug lead compounds with high bioavailability.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: N-(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine chloride This molecule couples the complex cationic scaffold with an N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine side chain. The propane-1,3-diamine backbone is a common structural motif found in natural polyamines (e.g., spermine, spermidine precursors), lending the molecule dicationic tendencies. Structurally similar compounds are often designed as ion channel blockers or GPCR ligands, particularly targeting histamine or serotonin receptors. The combination of the tertiary amine terminus and the quaternary ammonium core suggests potential for modulating intracellular pH or interfering with transmembrane signaling. Its activity might be focused on the treatment of CNS disorders or as an antipsychotic drug lead.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-5, is an ultra-long flexible bis-head benzimidazole molecule with seven repeating polyamide units, 2H-5 employs five propylamine spacers which produce a distance between Ht-N3 units closely mimicking the natural periodicity of 5CGG/3GGC motifs in r(CGG)exp, this structural optimization endows it with the highest potency and selectivity within the series with minimal loss of activity in the presence of tRNA, it also has strong binding affinity to r(CGG)12 and is the most effective compound at restoring splicing defects in FXTAS cellular models without interfering with downstream ORF translation, making it the optimal candidate, and this compound represents a further increase in length from the six-unit analogue (N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), in drug development, this minor length increase is vital for determining the optimal physiological length of the linker arm, the design with this maximum length is intended to simulate or achieve the farthest effective binding distance between proteins or nucleic acids, and this molecules potential real-world activity is focused on highly specific and low-toxicity targeted anti-cancer drugs, acting by long-range cross-linking or chelation of biomacromolecules to disrupt cellular function.

RNA information: RNA 2 (Drosha 515) is an RNA fragment that mimics the Drosha processing site in pri-miR-515, with its core structural feature being the presence of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515. Functionally, it serves as an in vitro research tool for detecting the binding properties between small molecules and RNA: the monomeric Compound 1 can bind to it, while the dimeric Compound 2 has no binding ability to it. Through such interaction analyses, it provides key data support for screening selective small molecules that target pri-miR-515.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, featuring multiple repeating amide units and a benzimidazole core with an extremely complex molecular formula, and it is a dimeric small molecule designed via fragment assembly that features a Drosha processing site and adjacent motif of pri-miR-515 to inhibit its processing into mature miRNA, which in turn upregulates SK1 and HER2 expression, induces cancer cell apoptosis, sensitizes HER2 cancer cells to Herceptin, has no effect on normal cells and provides tools and insights for RNA-targeted anticancer drug development, this compound presents as a large molecule with high lipophilicity and multiple coordination sites, functionally, this type of compound is likely used as a macromolecular ligand, potentially for targeting specific intracellular signaling pathways or cell regulation.

Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-fluorophenyl)butyl]-7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as 5FDQD (5-(3-(4-fluorophenyl)butyl)-7,8-dimethylpyrido[3,4-b]quinoline-1,3(2H,5H)-dione), is a flavin analog whose core scaffold is benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, a tricyclic heterocyclic system also known as isoalloxazine, which is central to Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) and its coenzymes (FAD and FMN); the molecule is substituted at the 10-position with a long 4-fluorophenylbutyl chain, a structure indicative of a flavin analog or antagonist, and it shares a similar chemical structure with FMN but features enhanced chemical selectivity and stability due to the replacement of the ribose phosphate group with an arylalkyl moiety; flavins are crucial cofactors for Oxidoreductases in biological systems, therefore this compound likely exerts its effect by interfering with cellular redox reactions or enzyme activity, such as acting as a Monoamine Oxidase (MAO) inhibitor or a regulator of other flavin-dependent enzymes, and in vitro it can bind to the FMN riboswitch, trigger its functional activity, and exhibit comparable potency to FMN, showing potent antibacterial activity against Clostridium difficile and, in mouse models, effectively preventing lethal Clostridium difficile infection (CDI) while exerting minimal impact on the gut microbiota; flavin analogs are investigated for antitumor and antiviral applications, with the lipophilic 4-fluorophenylbutyl group aiding cellular uptake, and these properties suggest that 5FDQD or its analogs hold promise as novel antibacterial agents for the treatment of Clostridium difficile infections.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-4-thiophen-2-ylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 4, is a heterocyclic compound based on the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold. The key structural distinction lies in the substituent at position 4: a thiophen-2-yl group replaces the typical phenyl group. Thiophene is a five-membered sulfur-containing aromatic heterocycle, and its incorporation is a strategy of bioisosteric replacement, used to modify the substituents electronic effects and spatial conformation while retaining aromaticity. This structural adjustment is aimed at optimizing the molecular fit with the binding pocket of a target protein (e.g., a kinase). The large planar polycyclic structure of this compound still suggests its potential as a DNA intercalator or enzyme inhibitor, and featuring a thiophene substitution, it displayed moderate RNA-binding activity, which was less potent than the lead compounds but still showed specificity for CAG repeat RNA over control sequences. In practical applications, thiophene-containing drugs are often studied for anti-inflammatory, anti-tumor, and Central Nervous System diseases.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-methyl-11-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropylamino)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]-N-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropyl)acetamide iodide is a disubstituted, dicationic [1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with two 3-piperidin-1-ylpropylamine side chains. The dicationic charge and highly conjugated planar core classify it as a potent polycationic intercalator. These compounds are used in the development of DNA minor groove binders and bifunctional topoisomerase inhibitors, exhibiting high anticancer and broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, particularly against drug-resistant strains.

RNA information: Cy3-(CUG)10 is a chemically synthesized, fluorescently labeled RNA oligonucleotide comprising 10 CUG repeats and conjugated to a Cy3 fluorophore. It is widely used as a model RNA probe to study the binding interactions between expanded CUG repeat RNA and Muscleblind-like (MBNL) proteins, which are implicated in the pathogenesis of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). By enabling fluorescence-based assays, Cy3-(CUG)10 facilitates the investigation of small molecules or ligands that can disrupt the MBNL1-CUG RNA interaction, thereby serving as a valuable tool in high-throughput screening and mechanistic studies aimed at developing potential therapeutics for DM1.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as the saturated analog of DAPI (whose full name is 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide), is a compound featuring a dihydroindole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, with the formula C16H16N4, while DAPI is a fluorescent stain binding AT-rich DNA regions, widely used in microscopy for nuclear labeling and is cell-impermeant unless membranes are compromised, making it useful for viability assays and chromosome visualization; this compound is the saturated analog of 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, the saturation of the indole ring removes aromaticity and significantly increases the flexibility and changes the 3D shape of the molecule, this structural change dramatically influences its binding mode and affinity for the DNA minor groove, and this compound is investigated to explore the impact of molecular rigidity/flexibility on the activity of DNA-binding drugs, showing potential for anticancer and anti-infective activity.

RNA information: H10 is an RNA aptamer with a predicted stem - loop structure (via Mulfold) that specifically binds Hoechst 33258. It enables dose - dependent translation inhibition in vitro and gene expression regulation in cells. Though not directly linked to diseases, its application in fluorescent imaging and targeted molecular interactions makes it a potential tool in cancer research, facilitating studies on DNA - related pathological processes.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenol, also referred to as Hoechst 33258 (H33258), is a fluorescent dye with morpholine rings, benzimidazole moieties, and methylated benzene rings, binding to DNAs minor groove. It enables fluorescent imaging and acts as a ligand for RNA aptamers to regulate gene expression. While not a drug, it supports research in cancer and other DNA-associated pathological processes, aiding investigations into disease mechanisms. 4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenol This compound is a bis-benzimidazole derivative featuring a rigid, extended conjugated system formed by two linked benzimidazole units. The molecule is substituted with a basic N-methylpiperazinyl group on one end and a phenolic hydroxyl group on the other. Due to its structural characteristics, this compound is often studied as a DNA minor groove binder. By binding to the minor groove of DNA, it interferes with DNA replication and transcription, thus exhibiting potential anticancer activity.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as the indolo[3,2-b]quinoline with the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 3-position and a 2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, is a neutral indolo[3,2-b]quinoline derivative that exhibited affinity for the NRQ and demonstrated potent anti-proliferative activity in A375 cells, and the non-quaternary amine allows for better cell membrane permeability, which is essential for reaching intracellular targets, while its large planar structure and basic side chain suggest potential utility as an anticancer agent or a fluorescent dye for biological imaging.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-[8-[3-(dimethylamino)propoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(dimethylamino)-1H-benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as 8-(Dimethylamino)-benzo-pteridine-2,4(1H,3H)-dione, is a benzo[g]pteridine derivative structurally related to Riboflavin (Vitamin B2), it is a planar azaaromatic compound with a fused benzo-pteridine core (containing multiple nitrogen atoms), a dimethylamino group at the 8-position, and dione groups at the 2 and 4 positions, and is a redox-active molecule that binds to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) via non-specific interactions but with lower affinity than potent ligands like chelerythrine, fails to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis (which aids in studying riboswitch ligand structure-activity relationships for azaaromatic sensing) and is used as a photosensitizer or fluorescence probe in cellular redox studies.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: [4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-phenylmethanone, also referred to as Compound 16 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative with the piperazines secondary amine modified where Rphenyl and synthesized via one-step coupling that binds the influenza A RNA promoter and has improved anti-influenza activity), has an acyl side chain that is the benzoyl group (phenylmethanone); the introduction of the aromatic benzoyl group significantly increases lipophilicity and provides a large hydrophobic surface area for interaction with biological targets, while the core activity remains 1-adrenergic receptor antagonism, and the benzoyl substitution is often found in compounds with anti-anxiety or antipsychotic potential, thus it might be studied for treating hypertension complicated by anxiety or depression due to its potential affinity for certain CNS receptors.

RNA information: 5′CGG/3′GGC is a 1×1 GG internal-loop RNA motif of the 5'CNG/3'GNC family, featuring CGG/GCC pairing, no U·U mismatches, high stability, and low "breathing effects". Linked to FXTAS (mutant FMR1 mRNA’s r(CGG) repeats), it acts as a disease-specific RNA switch, recruiting ligands like D2, D6, and 2H-5 . It also serves as a minimal binding unit for affinity characterization and an experimental control for RNA-small molecule binding specificity.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide diiodide, also referred to as PymDH2, is a dicationic compound with a pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide core (containing a central pyrimidine core with 1-methylquinolinium lateral groups) linking two 1-methylquinolin-1-ium cationic groups via methylideneamino (imine) bonds, this structure is a complex, multicharged system with a rigid structure and four potential cationic centers, counterbalanced by two iodide ions, this polycationic nature gives it extremely high affinity for negatively charged biomolecules (such as DNA, ATP, etc.), it showed moderate G4 affinity but no effect on EBNA1 expression, in practical applications, it is studied as a nucleic acid probe and a therapeutic agent, binding to DNA through strong electrostatic interactions and stacking, particularly exhibiting high selectivity for Guanine-rich G-quadruplex structures, making it a potential anticancer G4 ligand.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[2-[[2-[(5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetic acid is the benzofuroquinoline core conjugated via a triglycine (Gly-Gly-Gly) linker. The triglycine chain is a flexible, hydrophilic spacer primarily used to increase the molecules water solubility and improve pharmacokinetic properties for administration and distribution. This compound is a highly soluble quaternary ammonium benzofuroquinoline derivative.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.
Small_molecule_information: N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanamide, also referred to as Braco-19, is an acridine derivative with multiple amine and pyrrolidine side chains; Braco-19 is a classic, high-affinity G-quadruplex ligand and telomerase inhibitor which, like other quadruplex binders in this study, effectively bound the r(G4C2)exp quadruplex in vitro but failed to significantly inhibit RAN translation in cells, underscoring that quadruplex binding alone is insufficient to block the toxic pathway, while acridines are a known class of anti-tumor drugs that work by DNA intercalation and are studied as high-affinity nucleic acid binders to overcome multidrug resistance (MDR).

Tyrosine is an aromatic amino acid with a phenolic hydroxyl group that can undergo phosphorylation by protein kinases, a critical regulatory mechanism. The compound is potentially designed as a tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) or a signaling pathway modulator, retaining the cores antineoplastic potential. It may also serve as a dual-fluorescence probe due to the intrinsic fluorescence of the quinoline core and the Tyrosine residue.

Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: N-pentan-3-yl-4-[5-[4-(N-pentan-3-ylcarbamimidoyl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as DB226 (2,5-bis-[4-(N-isopentylamidino)phenyl]furan), is a bis-amidine compound containing biphenyl and furan structures, which is a bis-cationic diphenylfuran congener in which the terminal amidines carry branched isopentyl side-chains; its structure is based on the benzene and furan parent skeletons: two benzene rings are linked by a furan ring, and the para-position (position 4) of each benzene ring is substituted with an N-pentan-3-ylcarboximidamide group; in terms of biological function, amidine compounds are often used as antiparasitic agents (such as for Trypanosoma and Plasmodium), antibacterial agents, or as DNA binding agents, and this compound was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii (PCP) therapy, with its lipophilic modification strengthening minor-groove binding to AT-rich DNA and preserving high anti-PCP potency in rat models, while when tested against the HIV-1 TAR RNA target, the extra bulk reduces RNA affinity, showing no measurable suppression of Tat-driven trans-activation in the relevant cellular assay, and melting-temperature experiments record a modest Tsubm/sub for the DB226TAR complex, with RNase A footprints requiring a certain concentration for detectable protection of the GCUCU site opposite the bulge, confirming low-occupancy binding; like its analogues, DB226 recognizes the three-dimensional cavity created by the pyrimidine bulge rather than specific nucleotidesmutation of certain sites has little influence, whereas deletion of the bulge abolishes interaction; the compound is non-toxic to reporter cells below a specific concentration and retains full anti-PCP efficacy, illustrating that enlarging the alkyl-amidine substituent tips selectivity toward DNA while diminishing RNA recognition, thus, although DB226 itself is not an effective anti-HIV lead, its profile further defines structureselectivity relationships guiding the design of next-generation TAR-targeted diphenylfurans.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide;dibromide, also referred to as PhenDH4, which contains a central phenanthroline core with 1-ethylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and demonstrated good G4 binding but no biological effect, incorporates the large, aromatic 1,10-phenanthroline core, a structure well-known in coordination chemistry as a powerful transition metal ion chelator, this core is symmetrically linked to two permanently charged quinolinium fragments, the significance of 1,10-phenanthroline lies in its ability to form a perfect bidentate pocket for metal ions like Fe2 or Cu2, consequently, 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide;dibromide likely exhibits a dual mechanism of action: it acts as a bis-cationic DNA intercalator and as a metal chelator, potentially disrupting cellular metal homeostasis or generating reactive oxygen species via metal complexes, such molecules are often investigated as highly potent multi-target anticancer agents.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-chloro-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 12, is a highly substituted chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one derivative that combines chlorine and methoxy substituents, characterized by a chloro substitution at the 9-position and a methoxy substitution on the 2-benzoyl group; it demonstrated good binding to CAG RNA and was part of the group that informed the design of more potent compounds, and the introduction of a chlorine atom is a common strategy in medicinal chemistry to increase the molecules hydrophobicity, lipophilicity, and metabolic stability, potentially enhancing binding affinity through stronger halogen bonding, this complex, multi-aromatic substituted scaffold suggests it could be a highly selective enzyme inhibitor, and in practical applications, these chromenone derivatives are explored for their distinct biological activities, commonly in the development of novel antitumor agents, potentially through disruption of the cell cycle or inhibition of specific signaling kinases.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-1-ium-6-yl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM13, is a dicationic compound based on the coumarin core as well as a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge in addition to two permanent positive charges: one at the C3 substituent, which is the quaternized 2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-1-ium-6-yl group (N1 is methylated), and a second one at the C7 substituent, the quaternized (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium group, this double positive charge provides the molecule with extremely high hydrophilicity and charge density, it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR profiling, and shows 4-fold selectivity over GA-rich RNA, and dicationic molecules often exhibit unique biological properties, such as potent DNA intercalation or strong electrostatic interactions with negatively charged bacterial or fungal cell walls, thus, it is likely explored as a novel broad-spectrum antibiotic or as a fluorescent dye for nucleic acid staining.

Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-methoxy-2-(4-methylbenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 8, is a rigid and structurally complex derivative of chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one (a fused chromenone-pyridine ring system) that features a methoxy group on the chromene ring, multiple substituents including 3-amino, 4-phenyl, 9-methoxy, and 2-(4-methylbenzoyl), and exhibited moderate RNA-binding behavior which helped illustrate the importance of substituent positioning on biological activity; Chromenone-based compounds are common in natural products and exhibit broad bioactivities, particularly anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticancer effects, and this highly substituted, complex heterocyclic system is often designed as a kinase inhibitor or receptor antagonist, with the benzoyl and phenyl substituents providing the necessary hydrophobic interactions and spatial orientation for protein target binding, while in drug research, such molecules are pursued for their unique structure and potential antitumor activity, possibly by interfering with the cell cycle or inhibiting specific signaling enzymes.

Small_molecule_information: trimethyl-[2-[[5-methyl-11-(2-piperidin-1-ylethylamino)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]ethyl]azanium diiodide. This is a dicationic compound with a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium core and diiodide ions. The side chain at position 7 is a trimethylazanium group, and the substituent at position 11 is 2-piperidin-1-ylethylamino. The introduction of the piperidine ring affects the molecules lipophilicity and metabolic stability. The practical application of this compound lies in its potential as a broad-spectrum anti-tumor agent. This type of polycyclic cationic structure can interfere with the proliferation of cancer cells through the dual mechanisms of DNA intercalation and inhibition of key enzymes (like topoisomerases), and may help overcome certain forms of cancer cell resistance.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 1-methyl-2-[2-(3,4,5-trimethoxyphenyl)ethenyl]quinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 11, is a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative and a Cyanine Dye analog (monocationic), which has a 3,4,5-trimethoxystyryl group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring, no substitution at the 4-position, and its phenyl ring substituent is the well-known 3,4,5-trimethoxyphenyl group; the trimethoxyphenyl scaffold (also known as the combretastatin scaffold) is present in many natural products with anti-microtubule activity (such as colchicine), and this compound combines the fluorescent and intercalating properties of the cyanine dye with the anti-tumor potential of the trimethoxyphenyl group, additionally it has certain affinity for A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence) as shown by SPR assay, was not tested in the dual-luciferase reporter assay so its effect on ADAM10 translation is unknown, and exhibits cytotoxicity; in practical applications, this dual-functional structure is often explored as a novel anticancer lead compound that can both track cells via fluorescence and exert cytotoxic effects by interfering with microtubules or acting as an intercalating agent.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(1,2,4-triazol-1-ylmethyl)-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl]pyridine is a poly-nitrogen heterocyclic compound featuring two 1,2,4-triazole rings and one pyridine ring. One 1,2,4-triazole ring (connected to the pyridine ring) serves as the core, while the other 1,2,4-triazole ring is linked to the 5-position of the core triazole ring via a methylene bridge. The triazole ring is an important pharmacophore, widely present in antifungal drugs and antivirals. This molecule is rich in nitrogen atoms, granting it strong hydrogen bonding and coordination capabilities, suggesting potential biological activities such as antifungal, antiviral, or anticancer effects, or applications as a metal coordinating agent.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₄ sequence is an intermediate-length repeat consisting of four triplets, forming a more stable stem–loop than the dimer but still shorter than pathogenic expansions. Structurally, it offers a limited number of binding sites for ligands, sometimes resulting in inconsistent stoichiometry. Its reduced size diminishes the cooperativity observed in longer repeats, and it can behave differently depending on ligand structure. Functionally, r(CUG)₄ serves as a bridge model between minimal repeats and pathological expansions, highlighting how the length of the repeat influences recognition dynamics. However, it cannot fully mimic the complex misfolded conformations that drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,11S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,11-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]dodec-6-enediamide is a complex, symmetrical dimeric peptidic analog. Its structure is based on a C12 diene-diamide (dodec-6-enediamide, E-configured double bond) core, with the 2 and 11 positions linked to two N-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)proline units, and the termini connected to (2S)-2-(3-aminopropylamino)-6-aminohexanoyl (a lysine derivative) groups. It incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline aromatic heterocycle and contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds. This compound has an amphiphilic structure. The benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the two free amino groups (on the side chain and terminus of the lysine derivative) likely make it cationic at physiological pH. In terms of biological function, peptidic analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands or enzyme inhibitors; its cationic and hydrophobic nature may confer membrane permeability or the ability to bind negatively charged biomolecules (such as DNA/RNA), and benzo[g]quinoline compounds, like their quinoline isomers, typically possess diverse biological activities.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-diethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine diiodide is a benzofuroquinoline bis-quaternary ammonium salt derivative. Its structure is based on the benzofuroquinoline scaffold, with quaternization at the 5-position nitrogen and the morpholine ring on the 7-position side chain, and a diethylpropanediamine side chain at the 11-position (molecular formula C32H43N4O222I). This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and two positive charges. The oxygen atom within the morpholine ring may affect its hydrophilicity or metabolic stability, while maintaining its strong capacity as a nucleic acid binder and anti-tumor candidate.

Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.
