Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions, featuring a 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium quaternary ammonium side chain and a 2-piperidin-1-ylethyl substituted chain. The molecule structurally integrates two different saturated heterocycles (morpholine and piperidine). The potent cytotoxicity is a key feature of this class of compounds. In practical applications, it is regarded as a potential broad-spectrum germicide and antitumor agent. Its mechanism involves complex interactions with biological membranes, DNA, and RNA, with the quaternary ammonium structures being particularly important for its antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.
Small_molecule_information: 3,6-bis[(E)-2-(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-9H-carbazole diiodide, also referred to as BMVC/BMVC2/BMVC4, is a dicationic compound built on a carbazole scaffold, where two 1-methylpyridinium groups are attached at positions 3 and 6 via trans-ethenyl linkers, with the formula C28H27I2N3; as a series of carbazole derivatives and well-known fluorescent probes with high affinity for DNA/RNA G-quadruplex structures, it served as a representative G-quadruplex ligand in a related study, and despite binding strongly to the G-quadruplex form of r(G4C2)exp in vitro, it was a poor inhibitor of RAN translation in cellular models, while its carbazole core provides optoelectronic properties, the long conjugated chain and dicationic nature contribute to its strong fluorescence and nucleobase affinity, it is typically employed as a high-performance fluorescent dye in biological systems, and biologically, its ability to electrostatically bind to and potentially intercalate with biomacromolecules suggests potential anticancer and antimicrobial activities by disrupting cell membrane function or DNA replication.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-chlorophenyl)sulfanyl-3,5,6-trifluoro-2-pyridinyl]piperazin-4-ium, also referred to as Compound 4k (a 2-aminomethylene-dihydropyrrolo[3,2]-pyridine derivative), is a complex quaternary ammonium salt featuring a trifluoropyridine, piperazine, and a chlorophenyl sulfide moiety, with the formula C15H13ClF3N3S (cationic part), while Compound 4k is synthesized via ruthenium-catalyzed [222] cycloaddition, amine substitution, and Boc deprotection, binds pre-miR-21 with moderate affinity and shows good selectivity over duplex DNA/tRNA, lacks Dicer cleavage inhibition activity likely due to its methoxy-substituted aniline moiety failing to block the pre-miR-21 cleavage site (G28-A29), and its scaffold retains RNA-binding potential but requires aromatic/fluorinated modifications for enhanced bioactivity against oncogenic miR-21 biogenesis; this complex structure (of 1-[4-(4-chlorophenyl)sulfanyl-3,5,6-trifluoro-2-pyridinyl]piperazin-4-ium) is often designed to leverage the pharmacophore properties of piperazine and the metabolic stability of the trifluoropyridine scaffold, the quaternary ammonium group imparts a cationic character, potentially making it an ion channel modulator or a cell-permeable probe, and pyridine derivatives (including its related Compound 4k) have potential applications in cardiovascular, metabolic, and CNS drug development.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: quinazoline-2,4,5,6-tetramine, also referred to as 2,4,5,6-Tetraaminoquinoxaline, is a simple polyamino derivative based on the fundamental quinazoline heterocyclic core, substituted with four amino groups at positions 2, 4, 5, and 6. Quinazolines are a prominent scaffold in medicinal chemistry known for diverse biological activities. Due to the multiple electron-donating amino groups, the compound is highly electron-rich and basic, and it is also a low-molecular-weight heteroaromatic base that mimics arginine-rich peptides by lodging into the HIV-1 TAR RNA three-nucleotide bulge, with its planar bicyclic quinoxaline core stacking between conserved purines while the four exocyclic amino groups donate a precisely positioned network of hydrogen bonds that closely match the electrostatic signature required for Tat recognition; consequently, the compound competitively blocks Tat binding, abolishes Tat-activated transcription in HeLa reporter cells, and suppresses viral p24 production in latently infected OM-10.1 cells without accelerating the off-rate of pre-bound Tat, and its small size, high chemical stability, and preferential affinity for the bulge over double-stranded DNA or tRNA make it an attractive scaffold for further optimization toward orally available, transcription-targeted anti-HIV therapeutics. Research on polyamino quinazolines often focuses on their potential as enzyme inhibitors, particularly kinase inhibitors, as the quinazoline core is found in many successful anti-cancer kinase drugs (e.g., gefitinib). Furthermore, its structure may also be explored for antimalarial or immunomodulatory activities.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[2-methoxy-4-[2-[1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium-2-yl]ethenyl]phenyl]morpholine, also referred to as Compound 21, is a green-yellow complex quinoline quaternary ammonium salt derivative, with a 3-methoxy-4-morpholinostyryl group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl group at the 4-position of the quinoline ring; its core is the permanently positively charged 1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium cation, linked by a vinylene bridge to a phenyl group substituted with a morpholine and a methoxy group, forming a large, extended conjugated system from the piperazine nitrogen to the morpholine oxygen, and the molecule exhibits distinct cationic and highly fluorescent properties typical of a biological fluorescent dye; SPR assay data for its affinity to A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence) is unreported, it was not tested in the dual-luciferase reporter assay so its effect on ADAM10 translation remains unknown, no explicit cytotoxicity data (IC values) for it is provided in the tested compound tables; in biological applications, such compounds are often used as cell membrane potential indicators or mitochondrial probes due to their positive charge enabling selective uptake into negatively charged intracellular compartments, and its pharmacological activity may relate to antimicrobial or anticancer potential through membrane disruption or nucleic acid binding.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-aminophenyl)-N-[4-[2-(diethylamino)ethoxy]phenyl]quinazolin-4-amine, also referred to as a11 and a12, is a quinazoline derivative that shares structural similarity with the pharmacophore of many Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKIs), such as Gefitinib; while a11 and a12 are only confirmed to bind G-quadruplex sequences in vitro, with their screening target hVEGF-A closely associated with tumor angiogenesis yet merely serving as members of the screening library for potential anti-tumor candidates without further functional or disease-related validation, this compound is often developed as a potential anticancer agent targeting the EGFR or other kinases in cellular signaling pathways.

RNA information: Prokaryotic 16S rRNA has 5', central, and 3' domains; its helix 34  is critical for spectinomycin binding. It assembles into the 30S ribosomal subunit, facilitates mRNA-tRNA interaction in translation, and is a major antibiotic target. Mutations cause bacterial resistance to spectinomycin, affecting antibiotic efficacy against pathogens.
Small_molecule_information: (1R,3S,5R,8R,10R,11S,12S,13R,14S)-8,12,14-trihydroxy-5-methyl-11,13-bis(methylamino)-2,4,9-trioxatricyclo[8.4.0.03,8]tetradecan-7-one, also referred to as Spectinomycin, mainly acts on Gram-negative bacteria like Neisseria gonorrhoeae, clinically treats gonorrhea, and bacterial resistance to it occurs via 16S rRNA mutations (e.g., G1064/C1193) which weakens its therapeutic efficacy; this compound has an extremely complex polycyclic structure with multiple hydroxyl, methylamino, and ether functional groups, its structure strongly suggests it is a natural product or a semi-synthetic analog, often associated with the macrolide antibiotic or related complex polyketide families, and given its complexity and functional groups, this compound is expected to possess potent antibacterial activity, likely by inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis or cell wall formation, positioning it as a potential anti-infective drug.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-chloro-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 12, is a highly substituted chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one derivative that combines chlorine and methoxy substituents, characterized by a chloro substitution at the 9-position and a methoxy substitution on the 2-benzoyl group; it demonstrated good binding to CAG RNA and was part of the group that informed the design of more potent compounds, and the introduction of a chlorine atom is a common strategy in medicinal chemistry to increase the molecules hydrophobicity, lipophilicity, and metabolic stability, potentially enhancing binding affinity through stronger halogen bonding, this complex, multi-aromatic substituted scaffold suggests it could be a highly selective enzyme inhibitor, and in practical applications, these chromenone derivatives are explored for their distinct biological activities, commonly in the development of novel antitumor agents, potentially through disruption of the cell cycle or inhibition of specific signaling kinases.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-7-fluoro-2-methyl-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-10-oxo-4-oxa-1-azatricyclo[7.3.1.05,13]trideca-5(13),6,8,11-tetraene-11-carboxylic acid is a derivative of the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics. Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 13, is a dimeric ligand with an oligoether linker that is an acridine-triazine dimer structurally similar to 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, but with a longer polyethylene glycol (PEG) like linker chain connecting the two acridine units, containing six ethoxy units (PEG6-like), molecular formula C69H78N20O9; it showed only a small bivalent effect in the SPR-based inhibition assay, indicating poor performance, and in terms of real-world activity, its fundamental function is the same as 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, acting as a potent DNA intercalator/cross-linker and a potential antitumor drug, the significantly extended linker chain is a key optimization in the design, as it affects the molecules pharmacokinetic properties (e.g., solubility, half-life) and pharmacodynamic properties, particularly influencing the span and spatial geometry of the two acridine units across the DNA helix, which may lead to different topoisomerase inhibition selectivity and cytotoxic activity, this fine-tuning is aimed at optimizing drug efficacy and reducing side effects.

Small_molecule_information: trimethyl-[3-[[5-methyl-11-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropylamino)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]propyl]azanium diiodide. This is a dicationic compound based on the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium scaffold, featuring two quaternary ammonium centers and two iodide ions. It specifically features a simple trimethylazanium (trimethylammonium) cationic side chain. This structure is engineered to maximize interaction with nucleic acids. In bioactivity studies, compounds with long side chains and dicationic nature often display potent DNA binding and Topoisomerase inhibition. Its real-world application potential is in anticancer drug development, aiming to kill cancer cells by inhibiting Topoisomerase, thereby preventing DNA unwinding and replication.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-1H-pyrimidine-2,4-dione is an aromatic heterocyclic compound containing a pyrimidine structure. Its structure is based on the pyrimidine parent skeleton: a diazabenzene ring, substituted with keto groups at positions 2 and 4, and a methyl group at position 5 (Thymine). In terms of biological function, pyrimidine compounds are essential components of nucleic acid bases and typically exhibit biological activities such as antiviral, antitumor, or act as metabolic inhibitors.

RNA information: r(GCGC): This model RNA is a GC-rich hairpin with a stem composed of repeating 5'-GC/3'-CG base pairs. Its key functional role in the study was to act as a stringent control for determining the selectivity of the investigated small molecules. Due to its high thermodynamic stability, the stem was shortened to 8 base pairs to make its melting temperature comparable to the longer AU-rich hairpins. Compounds with true AU selectivity, such as molecule 2 and AU-azide, demonstrated very weak binding (Kd in the micromolar range) to r(GCGC), thereby highlighting their specificity and minimizing the potential for off-target interactions with highly abundant GC-rich regions in the transcriptome.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol, also referred to as Amodiaquine Di HCl, is a quinoline derivative structured around a quinoline core, with a chloro substitution at the 7-position and an amino link at the 4-position to a benzene ring containing a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group (molecular formula C20H22ClN3O), and it is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory effects that shares mechanisms with chloroquine but has higher toxicity risks, limiting its use despite efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains; this compound possesses a planar conjugated system and multiple functional groups (chloro, amine, phenol, quinoline), functionally, the quinoline core is famous as the scaffold for well-known antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine), and derivatives are commonly investigated for broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antimalarial, and anti-cancer activities, the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups suggests potential for metal complexation or binding to enzyme active sites.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(1,2,4-triazol-1-ylmethyl)-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl]pyridine is a poly-nitrogen heterocyclic compound featuring two 1,2,4-triazole rings and one pyridine ring. One 1,2,4-triazole ring (connected to the pyridine ring) serves as the core, while the other 1,2,4-triazole ring is linked to the 5-position of the core triazole ring via a methylene bridge. The triazole ring is an important pharmacophore, widely present in antifungal drugs and antivirals. This molecule is rich in nitrogen atoms, granting it strong hydrogen bonding and coordination capabilities, suggesting potential biological activities such as antifungal, antiviral, or anticancer effects, or applications as a metal coordinating agent.

RNA information: RPPH1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) that can form a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by only two G-tetrads, which distinguishes it from canonical rG4s that typically consist of three or more tetrads. This structural configuration contributes to its role in transcriptional regulation. Functionally, RPPH1 has been identified as a regulatory RNA molecule, and its rG4 structure can be stabilized or induced by specific small molecule ligands like **PhenDC3 and PDS. In vitro analyses, including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, demonstrate that the RPPH1 rG4 structure causes approximately 13% stalling efficiency in potassium-containing buffers, confirming its ability to form a stable secondary structure that can impede enzymatic activity. The integrity of this rG4 structure is crucial for its biological function, particularly in the context of gene expression regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 4,16-difluoro-8,12,20-trimethyl-8-aza-20-azoniapentacyclo[11.7.1.0{2,7}.0{9,21}.0{14,19}]henicosa-1(20),2(7),3,5,9(21),10,12,14(19),15,17-decaene, also referred to as PhenDC3, a bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative), is a highly complex and rigid pentacyclic fused nitrogen-containing heterocyclic quaternary ammonium salt that binds rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, whose structure enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations), functionally acts as a molecular chaperone inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation to aid G4 biology studies, the chemical name clearly outlines a twenty-one-carbon skeleton composed of multiple bridged and fused rings, the two fluorine atoms, strategically positioned at the 4 and 16 positions, are common bioisosteres used to enhance metabolic stability, lipophilicity, and binding affinity to biological targets, the presence of a quaternary ammonium group imparts a positive charge at physiological pH, enabling strong electrostatic interactions with negatively charged biomolecules such as DNA or acidic residues on proteins, and this rigid polycyclic scaffold and its specific three-dimensional structure suggest its potential application as a novel anti-tumor or antiviral agent, possibly functioning as a topoisomerase inhibitor or DNA intercalator in real-world drug discovery.

RNA information: (CAG)₁₂ has five 5’CAG/3’GAC repeats . It binds MBNL1, forming complexes causing splicing defects in Huntington models. Used to test compound selectivity: 2 binds it with lower affinity  vs r(CUG)₁₂, showing specificity for r(CUG)exp in DM1 studies.
Small_molecule_information: 1,8-diamino-3,6-dipyrrolidin-1-yl-2,7-naphthyridine-4-carbonitrile, also referred to as Compound 1 (2) (N-(3-(1H-imidazol-1-yl)propyl)-2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)quinolin-4-amine), is a compound featuring a highly substituted 2,7-naphthyridine scaffold with multiple amino and pyrrolidine substituents, and a cyano group at position 4, with the formula C19H23N7, and Compound 1 (2), identified via Inforna, selectively binds miR-544 precursors Dicer site and r(CUG)exp U loops, naphthyridine cores are often investigated as fluorescent dyes and DNA binders, the high polarity and multiple hydrogen bond sites suggest good water solubility and potential biological activity, it is likely designed as a DNA minor groove binder, aiming to interfere with replication and transcription processes through non-covalent binding, thus having prospects in antimicrobial and anticancer research, it inhibits miR-544 biogenesis, derepresses mTOR, disrupts ATMmTOR axis (induces TNBC apoptosis to a notable extent, with significant efficacy vs antagomirs), displaces MBNL1, improves DM1 splicing/translation, and disperses foci, a single i.p. injection of a low dose abolishes MDA-MB-231 tumor growth (low toxicity), acting as an RNA-directed probe for TNBC and DM1. Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride The side chain of this molecule terminates with a piperidine ring attached via a three-carbon chain to the cationic core. Piperidine, a six-membered saturated amine, is one of the most common pharmacophores in medicinal chemistry, used to increase lipophilicity and tune conformation. In clinical practice, piperidine scaffolds are found in antipsychotics, antihistamines, and cardiovascular drugs. The combination of the bulky quaternary core and the piperidine terminus suggests that it may act as a voltage-gated ion channel modulator (e.g., a potassium channel blocker) or a potent Multidrug Resistance (MDR) reversal agent by interfering with cell membrane transporter function to enhance chemotherapy efficacy.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(5Z)-5-[(3,5-dimethyl-1H-pyrrol-2-yl)methylidene]-4-methoxypyrrol-3-yl]-1H-indole, also referred to as Obatoclax (chemical full name: 2-(2-((3,5-dimethyl-1H-pyrrol-2-yl)methylene)-3-methoxy-2H-pyrrol-5-yl)-1H-indole), is a conjugated molecule containing indole and pyrrole heterocycles that is also a clinically evaluated prodiginine derivative sharing the characteristic tripyrrolic structure, its core structure is formed by two pyrrole rings linked by a methylidene bridge, creating an extended, Z-configured conjugated system, where one pyrrole ring is substituted at the 3-position with an indole group, indole and pyrrole are scaffolds of many natural product alkaloids with broad bioactivities, this extensive conjugated system gives the molecule excellent photophysical properties, making it highly likely to be a fluorescent dye or a photosensitizer, and it also demonstrated high affinity binding to pre-miR-21 with a significant stabilizing effect on the RNA, exhibited potent anti-proliferative effect in HCT-116 cancer cells with selectivity for cancer cells, in practice, such compounds are often studied for use as photosensitizers in Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) for cancer treatment, or as fluorescent probes for biological imaging, with potential drug activities including antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, while its established molecular mechanism is that of a pan-Bcl-2 inhibitor (BH3 mimetic), relevant studies have revealed its previously unreported capability to bind regulatory RNAs, suggesting a potential additional contribution to its complex anticancer profile.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: Trisodium;5-oxo-1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-[(4-sulfonatophenyl)diazenyl]-4H-pyrazole-3-carboxylate, also referred to as Tartrazine (and known as the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)), is the chemical name for the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110), a synthetic azo dye with the formula C16H10N2Na3O7[(2R,3S,4S,5R,6R)-2-[(2R,3S,4S,5S,6S)-2-[(1R,2S)-3-[[(2R,3S,4S)-5-[[(2S,3R)-1-[2-[4-[4-[3-[4-[[2-[[2-[[2-[acetyl(propyl)amino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-prop-2-ynylamino]acetyl]amino]butylamino]propylcarbamoyl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]ethylamino]-3-hydroxy-1-oxobutan-2-yl]amino]-3-hydroxy-4-methyl-5-oxopentan-2-yl]amino]-2-[[6-amino-2-[(1S)-3-amino-1-[[(2S)-2,3-diamino-3-oxopropyl]amino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-methylpyrimidine-4-carbonyl]amino]-1-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-3-oxopropoxy]-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]oxy-3,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-4-yl]carbamate, and Tartrazine is a synthetic yellow azo dye used as a food coloring agent while primarily an industrial colorant, its structure features a pyrazolone core linked by an azo bond (NN) to two benzene rings, both substituted with sodium sulfonate groups, and exists as a trisodium salt, the azo structure imparts a vibrant color, in real-world use, Sunset Yellow is widely employed as an orange-yellow coloring agent in foods, beverages, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics, such as orange juice, sodas, candies, and ice cream, to enhance visual appeal, as a common food colorant, its use is strictly regulated globally to ensure safety within prescribed limits, despite some controversy regarding its potential to affect behavior in sensitive individuals, particularly children.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[2-[[2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]acetyl]amino]ethyl]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-1G, is a symmetrical bicephalic compound featuring two 4-methylpiperazine-substituted benzimidazole aromatic units as the active cores, while 2H-1G itself is an -peptide derivative with a single glycine unit between modules; the two cores of the main compound are connected by a relatively short, flexible linker composed of an ethylenediamine unit and two glycinamide (-NHCH2CO-) units, and 2H-1G exhibited activity against the RNAprotein complex with its potency being less impressive than longer oligoglycine constructs, which helped illustrate how spacing length correlates with binding efficiency, though beyond these structureactivity observations, little is reported about 2H-1Gs biochemical stability or in-cell performance and it was not prioritized as a promising scaffold; benzimidazole structures are valued in biochemistry for their excellent fluorescent properties and affinity for nucleic acids, the glycinamide linker of the main compound is shorter and moderately flexible, designed to precisely control the spatial separation of the two active parts, and given its structural properties, this molecule could be studied as a potent DNA or RNA binding agent, possessing potential as an antimicrobial or antiviral drug, utilizing a dual-binding mode for enhanced activity.

RNA information: (5'CUG/3'GUC)x6: This RNA motif contains CUG repeats, which are associated with myotonic dystrophy type 1. It serves as a negative control to test the specificity of compounds designed for CAG repeats. The study shows that the lead compounds have low affinity for this RNA, confirming selectivity for CAG-rich structures.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 3, shares the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core with 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one but lacks the bromine atom, and Compound 3 emerged as a lead compound with high affinity and selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, its large aromatic and amino-substituted structure suggests high lipophilicity and the potential to interact strongly with biological targets, it is likely investigated as an antiproliferative agent targeting intracellular enzymes or signaling pathways, Compound 3 significantly reduced polyQ aggregation in cellular models, showed low cytotoxicity, and was cell-permeable, localizing primarily in the cytoplasm. RNA information: TERRA (Telomeric Repeat-containing RNA) is a long non-coding RNA essential for telomere maintenance. It forms a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by three G-tetrads, which is more stable than non-canonical two-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies using reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays demonstrate that TERRA's rG4 causes significant stalling (~40%) under potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, confirming its stable formation. This structural integrity is crucial for its role in telomere length regulation and heterochromatin formation. Notably, ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS act as effective molecular chaperones, significantly inducing and stabilizing the TERRA rG4 structure even under unfavorable ionic conditions (Li⁺), highlighting its dynamic nature and functional importance in genome stability.; TERRA (telomeric repeat-containing RNA) is a well-characterized RNA that forms stable G-quadruplex structures, with its sequence featuring repeated G-tracts . It binds selectively to RGB-1, showing a 1:1.2 binding stoichiometry and inducing a Tₘ increase of the RNA G-quadruplex upon binding. As a 5'UTR insert in reporter mRNAs, it mediates translation repression by RGB-1 in vitro and HEK293 cells, serving as a model for studying RNA G-quadruplex-related translational regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,8-N-bis(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-3-yl)-1,9-phenanthroline-2,8-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PhenDC3, is a highly conjugated dicationic compound and a bisquinolinium ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative), its structure enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations), and functionally it acts as a molecular chaperone that induces folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation, aiding G4 biology studies, while it binds rG4s via stacking on G-quartets; its core scaffold is the 1,9-phenanthroline-2,8-dicarboxamide, where phenanthroline is a well-known metal chelating ligand, at the amide nitrogen atoms of C2 and C8, it is substituted with a positively charged 1-methylquinolin-1-ium-3-yl group, the molecule possesses an extended, planar aromatic structure and carries two fixed positive charges, this design is characteristic of a nucleic acid intercalator, where planarity allows insertion between base pairs of DNA or RNA, given the ability of the phenanthroline core to coordinate transition metal ions (like Copper or Iron), this compound may be developed as a potential chemical nuclease: upon metal coordination and reduction, it can generate free radicals to cause oxidative damage to DNA, holding promise in anti-tumor drug development.

RNA information: HCV IRES subdomain IIa is an evolutionarily conserved RNA structural element that can specifically bind to benzimidazole-class small molecules. It controls the homeostasis of viral protein synthesis by modulating the conformation of the internal ribosome entry site, thereby regulating cap-independent translation initiation.
Small_molecule_information: 8-[(dimethylamino)methyl]-1-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]-8,9-dihydro-7H-pyrano[3,2-e]benzimidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Benzimidazole 12, this compound features a benzimidazole scaffold and two dimethylamino side chains, a structure similar to many drugs with anticancer, antifungal, and antiviral activities, and it is a conformationally locked analog of the 2-aminobenzimidazole class that was purposely designed to bind and modulate the hepatitis C virus (HCV) internal ribosome entry site (IRES) subdomain IIa, a phylogenetically conserved RNA element that governs cap-independent viral translation; in this derivative the flexible C6 side-chain of the parent scaffold is cyclized into a six-membered tetrahydropyran ring, which brings about significant affinity gain over its open-chain predecessor while retaining negligible cytotoxicity to human hepatoma cells; crystallographic and FRET analyses show that the ligand slips into the IIa internal bulge where its two protonatable dimethylamino groups replace two divalent-metal hot spots and its planar heteroaromatic face -stacks with G5, forcing the native inter-helical bend to widen, which further mis-orients the downstream IIb hairpin, blocks eIF2-GDP release and stalls 80-S ribosome assembly; in relevant antiviral activity assays it suppresses viral RNA and infectious particle production with notable potency that tracks quantitatively with its RNA-binding constant and is retained against common clinical IIa mutants, and its favorable in-vitro window, synthetic accessibility and structure-verified mode of action make it a validated lead for the structure-guided design of next-generation RNA-targeted antivirals against hepatitis C, in addition, benzimidazole derivatives are widely studied as potential new anti-tumor lead compounds due to their favorable interaction with biomacromolecules such as DNA and topoisomerases.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide;dibromide, also referred to as PyDH4, is a bis-cationic Schiff base dye that contains a central pyridine core with lateral 1-ethylquinolinium-6-yl groups, differing from an analogue by having a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core instead of a pyrimidine; this substitution of the central scaffold slightly alters the molecules electronic distribution and spatial geometry, affecting the overall conjugation and properties, and the compound displayed moderate G4-RNA binding but had no significant effect on EBNA1 expression, while in real-world chemical biology, this subtle change in the core is a strategic approach to tune the dyes excitation/emission wavelength and its selectivity towards different nucleic acid structures, and it would still be classified as a nucleic acid fluorescent probe, but with properties optimized for specific imaging or detection applications.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-(7-fluoro-5-methyl-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine iodide, this compound is an indolo[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

Small_molecule_information: 2,3-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Nitidine, is another methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid, similar to 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium but with different methoxy substitution (2,3-positions), and as a benzophenanthridine alkaloid it also possesses antitumor and antimalarial activity, can inhibit topoisomerases and telomerase to disrupt DNA replication, and its fluorescent properties aid in cellular imaging studies; due to its planar, rigid, and positively charged structural features, its primary activity in practice is as a DNA intercalator and cytotoxic agent, exhibiting strong anti-tumor properties, minor changes in the substitution pattern (from 1,2- to 2,3-dimethoxy) are critical in drug design to modulate the molecules charge distribution and steric bulk, which can alter its interaction strength and selectivity for specific DNA sequences or Topoisomerase isozymes, and this makes it valuable in the structure-activity relationship studies of novel anticancer agents.

RNA information: RNA5 contains the GG internal loop for compound 2 binding, but the UU loop for compound 1 has been mutated to a UA base pair. This mutation severely diminishes binding by dimeric compound 3, demonstrating that the UU loop is essential for its high-affinity, bivalent interaction.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(1,2,4-triazol-1-ylmethyl)-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl]pyridine is a poly-nitrogen heterocyclic compound featuring two 1,2,4-triazole rings and one pyridine ring. One 1,2,4-triazole ring (connected to the pyridine ring) serves as the core, while the other 1,2,4-triazole ring is linked to the 5-position of the core triazole ring via a methylene bridge. The triazole ring is an important pharmacophore, widely present in antifungal drugs and antivirals. This molecule is rich in nitrogen atoms, granting it strong hydrogen bonding and coordination capabilities, suggesting potential biological activities such as antifungal, antiviral, or anticancer effects, or applications as a metal coordinating agent.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(3R)-1-[[2-(methylamino)pyrimidin-5-yl]methyl]piperidin-3-yl]-4-thiophen-2-yl-1H-pyrimidin-6-one, also referred to as Ribocil-A, is a single chiral isomer, with the defined stereochemistry at the piperidine rings 3-position as (3R), and Ribocil-A is the R-configured enantiomer of ribocil, in pharmacology, the specific stereoisomer often dictates the binding affinity and efficacy towards biological targets, therefore, the activity of this compound (e.g., kinase inhibition strength) is likely to be different from its enantiomer or the racemic mixture, representing the single, potentially more potent or selective, active pharmaceutical ingredient, compared with the S-configuration, the orientation of its aromatic ring system and the spatial arrangement of the methylamino-piperidinyl group are unfavorable for intercalation into the aptamer pocket of the FMN riboswitch, resulting in a significant decrease in binding affinity, functionally, it almost loses the ability to inhibit riboswitch-mediated ribB gene expression and cannot effectively deplete intracellular riboflavin, thus essentially lacking antibacterial activity.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]pentanedioic acidThis is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline-peptide conjugate similar to 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]butanedioic acid, maintaining the potentially cytotoxic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. The peptide chain consists of Arginine and Glutamic acid. As an acidic amino acid, Glutamic acid enhances hydrophilicity. The molecule is likely designed as a cell-penetrating or tumor-targeted agent. The Glutamic acid residue might be leveraged for transport via amino acid transporters or cleaved by tumor-specific enzymes to release the active drug, indicating potential applications in targeted cancer therapy.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[methyl(quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 10, is a complex peptide analog of N-[(2S)-1-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[methyl(quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, with a simplified amino group, and Compound 10 also lacks the charged amines found in more potent FSS-binding molecules, but it retained moderate solubility and measurable affinity, its affinity was substantially weaker than compound 6 yet significantly stronger than compound 8, this compound is often designed as a peptide mimetic and researched for potential activity as caspase inhibitors or protease inhibitors, no antiviral potency comparable to sub-micromolar binders was observed for Compound 10, indicating that the affinity gap directly correlates with functional activity in cells, Compound 10 reinforces the conclusion that positive charge is essential for strong HIV-1 FSS targeting and that removing these groups compromises activity, nonetheless, it provided valuable insight into structureactivity relationships within this compound series.

RNA information: RNA3 is a key model RNA that incorporates both the Drosha site (1x1 UU loop, target of 1) and the adjacent site (1x1 GG loop, target of 2). It binds the dimeric compound 3 with high avidity (Kd = 85 nM), demonstrating simultaneous engagement of both modules.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1, is a mono-benzimidazole Click Chemistry probe with tert-butyl and azide groups, which is a benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module previously identified by the Informa platform to avidly bind a 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop within the Drosha processing site of the pri-miR-96 hairpin precursor, inhibits miR-96 biogenesis and induces apoptosis in breast cancer cells with activity that limits its in vivo utility, shows no detectable binding to DNA, and its real-world activity is dedicated to biological orthogonal labeling experiments for target validation and mechanism elucidation.

RNA information: GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Small_molecule_information: 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-ethylphenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 5f (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single ethyl group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a large, polyfunctionalized phenol derivative, which is synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings; its structure is based on a phenol core substituted at the 4-position with a complex phenyl group, which itself contains a 4-methoxyphenyl, an ethyl, and two 2-aminoethyl groups, the 2 and 6 positions of the phenol core are also substituted with two 2-aminoethyl groups, the central scaffold is composed of two substituted benzene rings connected by a C-C bond, this compound adopts a non-planar structure, containing one phenolic hydroxyl group (which can act as a hydrogen bond donor) and four free amino groups (which can be protonated and positively charged at physiological pH); in terms of biological function, phenolic compounds are generally known for their antioxidant activity, the multiple amino groups confer cationic properties and potential for DNA/RNA binding, it can bind to HIV-1 RRE Bh (ITC-measured), has a certain specificity ratio, inhibits Bh-Rev interaction and shows anti-HIV activity in cell assays, and its structural characteristics may render it a novel drug or biological probe.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1H-imidazole-5-carboxamide, also referred to as 5-Amino-4-imidazolecarboxamide (AICA), is a non-azaaromatic imidazole derivative with an imidazole ring bearing amino and carboxamide groups that lacks planar hydrophobic azaaromatic features, which binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; it is the base moiety of the AICAR nucleoside, known as 5-aminoimidazole-4-carboxamide (AICA-amide), and is not only a precursor for AICAR synthesis in the de novo purine synthesis pathway (serving as a key metabolic intermediate in de novo purine biosynthesis, unrelated to azaaromatic sensing) but also an important synthetic building block, and the molecule itself or its derivatives have been researched in fields such as anticancer, antiviral, and immunomodulation.

RNA information: Located at positions 36–55 in the KRAS 5′-UTR. It forms a parallel RG4 with Tm = 64°C and ΔG = –3.7 kcal/mol. It binds strongly to BG4 antibody and is highly stabilized by ligands 2a and 2b, showing significant translational repression in luciferase assays.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione, also referred to as a thiophene-fused analogue of 2a with similar side chains, is an organic compound featuring a complex polycyclic core of naphthoquinone and benzothiole, forming the naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione skeleton. This framework is a sulfur-containing heteroaromatic system with a highly conjugated electron structure and redox-active quinone carbonyl groups at positions 5 and 10. The substituents at positions 4 and 11 are two 2-aminoethylamino groups, which introduce primary amines capable of protonation, granting the molecule polycationic character and increasing its water solubility under physiological conditions. This specific structural combination, characterized by its planarity, extended conjugation, redox activity, and cationic side chains, makes it a molecule of interest in biomedicine, typically investigated as a potential DNA intercalator or topoisomerase inhibitor, suggesting potential cytotoxic or anticancer activities; it has moderate cellular uptake, stabilizes RG4s, and also suppresses translation and promotes apoptosis, though less potently than 2a.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(5-methyl-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]ethanol iodide. This is an indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with an iodide counterion. It features a shorter ethanolamine side chain at position 11. This structure makes it an effective DNA and RNA binder, capable of intercalating between nucleic acid base pairs. In real-world applications, this class of compounds is extensively studied for anticancer and anti-drug resistance infection applications. Its cationic nature imparts specific toxicity towards rapidly proliferating cells and microorganisms, making it an important chemical scaffold for developing new antifungal and antibiotic agents.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR(WT) RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: N-cyclopentyl-4-[5-[4-(N-cyclopentylcarbamimidoyl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as DB244 (2,5-bis-[4-(N-cyclopentylamidino)phenyl]furan), is a bis-cyclopentyl amidine-substituted biphenyl furan structure, and DB244 is a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative that belongs to the same chemotype as DB60 and DB75 but is endowed with bulkier cyclopentylamidine end-groups, furan-phenyl scaffolds are often used to construct fluorescent probes or molecules with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, the bis-amidine substitution suggests potential DNA binding or serine protease inhibitory activity, with the cyclopentyl substitution likely intended to enhance lipophilicity and cell membrane permeability, DB244 was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii (PCP) therapy, exhibits excellent DNA minor-groove binding and ranks among the most potent agents against PCP in rat models (DB244 DB75 DB226 DB60), when redirected to the HIV-1 TAR RNA target, the added bulk reduces RNA affinity, in vitro gel-shift assays show notable IC50 for displacing Tat peptide from the 59-nt TAR hairpin, while the FIGS cellular assay shows essentially no suppression of Tat-driven trans-activation up to a high tested concentration, melting-temperature experiments record a modest Tm with DB244TAR complexes, and RNase A footprints reveal protection of the GCUCU site opposite the bulge only at high concentrations, confirming weaker, lower-occupancy binding, like DB60, DB244 recognizes the three-dimensional cavity created by the pyrimidine bulge rather than specific nucleotides: mutation of U23 or the GC26-39 pair has little influence on affinity, whereas deletion of the bulge abolishes the interaction, the compound is essentially non-toxic to reporter cells below a certain concentration and retains full anti-PCP potency, illustrating how increasing the steric demand of the terminal basic chains tips the selectivity balance toward DNA and away from RNA recognition, thus, although DB244 itself is not an effective anti-HIV lead, its profile delineates structureselectivity relationships that guide the design of next-generation TAR-targeted diphenylfurans.
