Small_molecule_information: (4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-6-(2-aminopurin-9-yl)-2-hydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-7-ol, also referred to as 2-aminopurine riboside-3,5-cyclic monophosphate, is an Adenine (specifically 2-aminopurine) nucleoside derivative bearing the cyclic phosphonate structure, similar to the one in 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-2,7-dihydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-2-amino-8-bromo-1H-purin-6-one, and 2-aminopurine riboside-3,5-cyclic monophosphate is a modified cyclic nucleotide, featuring a 2-aminopurine base (a purine analog with an amino group at the 2-position) linked to a ribose sugar with a 3,5-cyclic phosphate bridge, this structure is analogous to a modified form of cyclic Adenosine Monophosphate (cAMP), structurally, it retains the cyclic nucleotide scaffold but has an altered purine core that enhances hydrogen bonding and fluorescence, cAMP is a crucial second messenger in cells, mediating the effects of numerous hormones and neurotransmitters, in the real world, the compound is likely designed as a modulator of adenylate cyclase or phosphodiesterase to affect the cAMP signaling pathway, offering potential for treating cardiovascular diseases, inflammation, asthma, and neurological disorders, functionally, it acts as a cGMP signaling probe, binds cyclic nucleotide receptors/riboswitches, monitors nucleotide-dependent pathways, and serves as a fluorescent tool for studying RNA-ligand interactions and cyclic nucleotide-mediated gene regulation in bacteria and eukaryotes. 2-

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride This compound is characterized by a pyrrolidine ring at the side chain terminus, linked by a propyl spacer to the rigid cationic core. Pyrrolidine, a five-membered saturated heterocycle with a basic nitrogen, is commonly used to build drugs with central nervous system (CNS) activity. In real-world drug design, pyrrolidine derivatives are frequently part of Dopamine, Serotonin, or Norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, indicating its potential for research as an antidepressant or anti-anxiety agent. The molecules high amphiphilicity and spatial complexity may also confer high selectivity for specific receptor subtypes, such as a potential lead for a -opioid receptor antagonist.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine chloride This molecule couples the complex cationic scaffold with an N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine side chain. The propane-1,3-diamine backbone is a common structural motif found in natural polyamines (e.g., spermine, spermidine precursors), lending the molecule dicationic tendencies. Structurally similar compounds are often designed as ion channel blockers or GPCR ligands, particularly targeting histamine or serotonin receptors. The combination of the tertiary amine terminus and the quaternary ammonium core suggests potential for modulating intracellular pH or interfering with transmembrane signaling. Its activity might be focused on the treatment of CNS disorders or as an antipsychotic drug lead.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-N-[3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]phenyl]-3-phenylpropanamide, also referred to as N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-phenylalaninamide (4b), is a small molecule peptidomimetic centered on an amide structure derived from Phenylalanine, and N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-phenylalaninamide (4b) is a synthetic nucleobaseamino acid conjugate engineered to occupy the Tat-binding pocket of HIV-1 TAR RNA, the 2-amino-3-phenylpropanoyl fragment, a structural unit of the natural amino acid Phenylalanine, is connected via an amide bond to a complex aromatic amine substituent: 3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]aniline, the molecule combines the features of an amino acid (chiral center, amino acid side chain) with thiazole, a common pharmacophore, the phenylalanine side-chain replaces the cationic guanidinium-rich segment of Tat with a single aromatic ring that is intended to -stack with the exposed purines of the TAR bulge, the thiazole ring and aniline moiety provide aromaticity and additional hydrogen bonding sites, due to its peptidomimetic nature, this compound is frequently investigated in medicinal chemistry as a protease inhibitor, targeting enzymes such as caspases or HIV proteases, thiazole derivatives are also common scaffolds for developing signal transduction kinase inhibitors, with wide applications in anti-inflammatory and anti-tumor drug screening, in fluorescence-binding assays 4b displays moderate affinity and a 1:1 RNA-ligand stoichiometry, yet it lacks the basic residues required for electrostatic anchoring, resulting in a three-fold weaker interaction than its lysyl or arginyl homologues and no measurable anti-HIV activity, CD titrations show that 4b does not distort the A-form helical structure of TAR, while competition experiments reveal a 1.5-fold loss of signal in the presence of 100 tRNA, indicating modest selectivity, although 4b itself is not antiviral, its well-defined structureactivity relationship illustrates that simple hydrophobic contacts are insufficient for biological potency, guiding further optimization of the aromatic spacer length and electronics for future generations of TAR-directed artificial riboswitch ligands. RNA

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 7-methyl-1H-benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione is an aromatic heterocyclic compound containing a pteridine structure. Its structure is based on the pteridine parent skeleton: two benzene rings are fused with two pyrimidine rings to form the pteridine skeleton, which is substituted with keto groups at positions 2 and 4, and a methyl group at position 7. In terms of biological function, pteridine compounds are widespread in living organisms and typically possess activities such as enzyme cofactors, anticancer, antimalarial, or antioxidant properties.

RNA information: This RNA forms a well-ordered binding pocket through interlocking structural motifs . In the presence of Mg²+, it can bind theophylline with high affinity  and exhibits molecular discrimination specificity towards caffeine.
Small_molecule_information: 1,3-dimethyl-7H-purine-2,6-dione, also referred to as Theophylline, a 1,3-dimethylxanthine structurally related to caffeine (differing from caffeine only by lacking a methyl group at the N7 position), is a naturally occurring alkaloid in green and black tea that acts as a bronchodilator, vasodilator, muscle relaxant, adenosine-receptor antagonist and phosphodiesterase inhibitor; as a ligand, it binds theophylline-binding RNA in a Mg-dependent way, via hydrogen bonds with the RNAs C22 and U24, supported by base triples, and RNA discriminates it from caffeine via such structural interactions. Despite its narrow therapeutic index, the SELEX-derived 38-nt RNA aptamer TCT8-4 binds theophylline with high affinity and high specificity over caffeine, exploiting a hydrogen bond to N-7, favorable contacts with the N-3 methyl and C-2/C-6 carbonyls, and a steric barrier against C-8 substitution, thereby providing a high-resolution RNA sensor for this multifunctional drug and human serum metabolite.

RNA information: HCV IRES subdomain IIa is an evolutionarily conserved RNA structural element that can specifically bind to benzimidazole-class small molecules. It controls the homeostasis of viral protein synthesis by modulating the conformation of the internal ribosome entry site, thereby regulating cap-independent translation initiation.
Small_molecule_information: 7-[(dimethylamino)methyl]-1-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]-7,8-dihydrofuro[3,2-e]benzimidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Benzimidazole 11 (Isis-11), is a dihydro derivative of 7-[(dimethylamino)methyl]-1-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]furo[3,2-e]benzimidazol-2-amine, featuring a dihydrofuran ring fused to the benzimidazole, and Benzimidazole 11 (Isis-11) is a conformationally constrained small-molecule analog developed to selectively target and modulate the hepatitis C virus (HCV) internal ribosome entry site (IRES) subdomain IIa, a phylogenetically conserved RNA element essential for cap-independent viral translation, its activity is likely similar to 7-[(dimethylamino)methyl]-1-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]furo[3,2-e]benzimidazol-2-amine, still being a structurally novel benzimidazole derivative often explored in medicinal chemistry for its potential anti-tumor, antiviral, and antiparasitic effects, built on a 2-aminobenzimidazole core, Isis-11 locks the flexible C6 side-chain into a five-membered tetrahydrofuran ring, yielding a significant affinity boost over its unconstrained predecessor while remaining non-toxic to human hepatoma cells, crystallographic and FRET analyses reveal that the ligand slips into the IIa internal bulge, where its two protonatable dimethylamino groups replace two divalent-metal hot spots and its planar heteroaromatic face -stacks with G5, forcing the native 90 inter-helical bend to widen by 23, this straightening mis-orients the downstream IIb hairpin, blocks eIF2-GDP release and stalls 80-S ribosome assembly, in genotype-1b replicon and infectious JFH-1 luciferase-reporter assays Isis-11 suppresses viral RNA and infectious particle production with notable potency, a potency that tracks quantitatively with its RNA-binding constant and is retained against common clinical IIa mutants, the favorable in-vitro window, synthetic accessibility and structure-verified mode of action identify Isis-11 as a validated lead for the structure-guided design of next-generation RNA-targeted antivirals against hepatitis C.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 3-position and a 2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, is a neutral benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with an N-methylpiperazine side chain; this molecule is studied for its potential anticancer properties, benefiting from the cell-permeable nature of the neutral amine and the often-enhanced metabolic stability of the benzofuran core, and while moving the styryl group to the 3-position led to a notable reduction in binding affinity relative to its 2-position isomer 4a-16, it still maintained good anti-proliferative activity in A375 cells.

RNA information: RPPH1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) that can form a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by only two G-tetrads, which distinguishes it from canonical rG4s that typically consist of three or more tetrads. This structural configuration contributes to its role in transcriptional regulation. Functionally, RPPH1 has been identified as a regulatory RNA molecule, and its rG4 structure can be stabilized or induced by specific small molecule ligands like **PhenDC3 and PDS. In vitro analyses, including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, demonstrate that the RPPH1 rG4 structure causes approximately 13% stalling efficiency in potassium-containing buffers, confirming its ability to form a stable secondary structure that can impede enzymatic activity. The integrity of this rG4 structure is crucial for its biological function, particularly in the context of gene expression regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)naphthalen-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PhenDC3, is a large, structurally symmetrical bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative) with the molecular formula C37H37N5O5, centered on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core; this core is linked via two amide bonds to two identical naphthyl units, which are functionalized with aminoethoxy chains, and its structure further enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations). The molecule features multiple aromatic rings (pyridine and naphthyl) providing a hydrophobic, planar surface that allows it to bind rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, and four primary amine groups (-O-CH2CH2NH2) contributing to hydrophilicity and multiple positive charges; this combination of hydrophobicity and multiple positive charges is characteristic of amphiphilic molecules, often designed as DNA minor groove binders or transmembrane transport vectors, and it can interact with DNA bases via - stacking interactions while electrostatically attracting the negatively charged phosphate backbone. Functionally, it acts as a molecular chaperone, inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation, aiding G4 biology studies, and consequently, this compound has potential as an anti-tumor or antiviral drug by interfering with the replication or transcription of nucleic acids.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as the derivative with a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline scaffold that bears a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and a 3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, integrates the propylamine spacer with the 4-methylpiperazine ring, and this compound showed strong binding to the NRQ as well as potent anti-proliferative activity in several cell lines while being less active in some other cell lines; this piperazine-propylamine side chain is found in various CNS-acting agents like antipsychotics and antidepressants, suggesting an ability to cross the blood-brain barrier and modulate G-protein coupled receptors, and the compound is a structural candidate for anticancer, antifungal, or CNS-active drugs, leveraging the high basicity and lipophilicity of the side chain to enhance bioavailability and tissue distribution.

RNA information: The r(CCUG)8 RNA forms an A-form structure with consecutive CU mismatches, sequestering MBNL1 protein, leading to splicing defects and DM2 disease symptoms.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[4-(2,4,6-triaminopyrimidin-5-yl)butyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 8, is built on a highly symmetrical benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide core, where the imidamide groups are strongly basic and capable of bearing a positive charge; the para-substituted core is linked via two four-carbon butane flexible chains to the terminal 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine rings, and the use of a longer butane chain compared to a propylene chain increases the overall flexibility of the molecule and modifies the effective distance between the two terminal triaminopyrimidine rings, which is critical for achieving optimal spatial alignment for binding to biological targets like nucleic acids; with multiple strongly basic, planar aromatic heterocycles, this compound is classified as a potential DNA/RNA binder, capable of disrupting nucleic acid function through multiple interactions (electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, stacking), and it also boasts the highest potency in its series, can efficiently disrupt the MBNL1-r(CCUG)8 interaction, dissolve disease foci in DM2 model cells while maintaining very low toxicity, highlighting its potential as a therapeutic agent, while such compounds are commonly screened and developed as antiparasitic drugs, particularly against parasitic organisms with distinct nucleic acid metabolism.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x20: This is an expanded CAG repeat RNA with twenty AA mismatches, modeling the longer pathogenic repeats found in advanced stages of Huntington’s disease and SCAs. It shows strong binding to the lead compounds, with nanomolar affinity, and exhibits significant thermal stabilization and structural changes upon ligand binding, confirming its relevance as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 15, is a highly substituted chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, and Compound 15 was identified as a top lead compound with very high affinity and exceptional selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, the chromene moiety is utilized in antiviral and antitumor drug design, the presence of a bromo atom, amino group, and benzoyl group suggests it may act as a potent enzyme inhibitor or receptor antagonist, potentially blocking key processes in cancer cell proliferation, its bromine substituent enhanced RNA binding through additional halogen bonding, it significantly reduced polyQ aggregates, was cell-permeable, and showed low toxicity. RNA

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 3, shares the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core with 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one but lacks the bromine atom, and Compound 3 emerged as a lead compound with high affinity and selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, its large aromatic and amino-substituted structure suggests high lipophilicity and the potential to interact strongly with biological targets, it is likely investigated as an antiproliferative agent targeting intracellular enzymes or signaling pathways, Compound 3 significantly reduced polyQ aggregation in cellular models, showed low cytotoxicity, and was cell-permeable, localizing primarily in the cytoplasm. RNA

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

RNA information: (CUG)₅₀ is a toxic CUG trinucleotide repeat RNA linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it adopts a hairpin structure with multiple 1×1 UU internal loops (its key RNA Motif) and has the sequence 5'-r(CUG)₅₀-3'.Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1, causing RNA mis-splicing; it’s used as a model to screen ligands (e.g., pentamidine, EBAB) via affinity capillary electrophoresis (ACE) for DM1 therapy .
Small_molecule_information: (2R,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(1R,2R,3S,4R,6S)-4,6-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,5R)-4-[(2R,3R,4R,5S,6S)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-4,5-dihydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, also referred to as Neomycin, is an aminoglycoside antibiotic whose molecular structure is rich in nitrogen (containing amino groups and other nitrogen-based functional groups), and as a natural aminoglycoside it is characterized by multiple cationic amino groups arranged around a polycyclic sugar framework, structurally lacking the specific aromatic stacking or spatial precision of synthetic benzo[g]quinoline derivatives which results in broad activity but little discrimination among different repeat sequences. This structural feature enables it to interact strongly with the (CUG) RNA probe associated with the pathology of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), with its binding arising primarily from electrostatic interactions with the phosphate backbone of nucleic acids that makes it a promiscuous and non-selective RNA binder. In affinity capillary electrophoresis (ACE) experiments, its binding constant to (CUG) RNA is significantly higher than that of pentamidine (PTMD) and 1,2-ethane bis-1-amino-4-benzamidine (EBAB), and functionally it can engage RNA targets but does not achieve the selectivity or therapeutic relevance of designed compounds. Moreover, during screening at a high concentration of 1 mM, it can completely eliminate the electrophoretic peak of the (CUG) RNA probe, demonstrating high affinity. However, it should be noted that Neomycin cannot rescue the mis-splicing of affected genes in DM1, so despite its strong RNA-binding activity, it is difficult to serve as a core therapeutic candidate for DM1, and in relevant studies it serves as a control benchmark to highlight advantages of tailored synthetic scaffolds.

RNA information: Streptomyces davawensis carries a 149-nucleotide FMN riboswitch (149 ribB) in the 5′-UTR of its ribB operon. This sequence is highly consistent with the consensus structure of classical FMN aptamers and can fold into a compact three-dimensional pocket composed of six stem-loops (P1-P6). Functionally, 149 ribB binds to FMN, riboflavin, and roseoflavin with high affinity simultaneously. By recognizing these ligands, it inhibits the expression of downstream riboflavin synthesis and transport genes, achieving negative feedback regulation. However, it does not show significant exclusion of roseoflavin, suggesting that the drug resistance of S. davawensis does not rely on a specialized riboswitch, but rather requires other mechanisms to avoid self-inhibition.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(dimethylamino)-7-methyl-10-(2,3,4,5-tetrahydroxypentyl)benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as roseoflavinRoF, is a natural derivative of riboflavin in which the methyl group at the C8 position is replaced by a dimethylamino group. It retains the basic skeleton consisting of an isoalloxazine aromatic ring and a ribitol chain, with only subtle structural modifications introduced by the positive charge and steric hindrance from the additional nitrogen atom. Functionally, as an antimetabolite, its phosphorylated form RoFMN (roseoflavin mononucleotide) can directly intercalate into the aptamer pocket of bacterial FMN (flavin mononucleotide) riboswitch with high affinity, mimicking the conformational changes induced by FMN, and this binding regulates the expression of genes related to flavin metabolism in bacteria, thereby blocking the expression of downstream riboflavin synthesis and transport genes, leading to impaired bacterial growth due to cofactor depletion. Although RoFMN exhibits high binding affinity and antibacterial activity, its dependence on intracellular phosphorylation processes limits its application in certain bacteria, and furthermore, the antibacterial activity of RoFMN may be affected by bacterial mutations in ribityl transporter proteins, leading to resistance, and notably, this inhibitory effect can be attenuated by drug-resistant mutations. 8-(dimethylamino)-7-methyl-10-(2,3,4,5-tetrahydroxypentyl)benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione is a nitrogen-containing tetracyclic aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure is based on the benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione (flavin core) skeleton, with 8-(dimethylamino) and 7-methyl substitution on the benzene ring, and a tetrahydroxypentyl side chain (similar to the ribityl chain) attached at the N10-position. The molecular formula is CHNO. This compound is a substituted riboflavin analog. It has an extensive conjugated structure. In terms of biological function, as a riboflavin analog, its derivatives may interfere with flavin coenzyme-dependent enzyme activities, suggesting potential biological activity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (4S,4aS,5aS,6S,12aR)-4-(dimethylamino)-1,6,10,11,12a-pentahydroxy-6-methyl-3,12-dioxo-4,4a,5,5a-tetrahydrotetracene-2-carboxamide, also referred to as nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, is the core structure or a derivative of a tetracycline antibiotic, which is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that binds to the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis by interacting with bacterial rRNA but not directly with mRNA or other RNA types; tetracycline has a rigid hydrophobic skeleton with four rings connected together, the side chains of C-10 phenol dione and C-1 to C-3 triketone formamide provide negative charge and hydrogen bond donors, this planar aromatic region can be stacked at the single-stranded/double-stranded junction of RNA, meanwhile, magnesium ions mediate the electrostatic bridging between its dimethylamino group and the phosphate skeleton, inducing conformational distortion at the active site and demonstrating a non-competitive inhibitory function for Class I intron splicing; tetracyclines are clinically important broad-spectrum antibiotics used in reality to treat various bacterial infections like pneumonia, acne, and Lyme disease, by inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 7-[(1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium-4-yl)methoxy]-5-methyl-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium compound, featuring the 1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium group at position 7 and a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino chain at position 11. This structure integrates a polycyclic aromatic core with multiple cationic centers on hydrophilic side chains. Its practical value lies in its potent cytotoxicity and high affinity for specific biological targets. As a dicationic dye or drug, it can penetrate cell membranes, bind to the minor groove of DNA, or intercalate between base pairs in the nucleus. Compounds of this type generally exhibit significant anti-tumor activity by disrupting the replication and transcription processes of cancer cells genetic material.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-(4-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as C60, is a non-planar coumarin derivative and analog with the 4-methylpiperazin-4-ium cation at position 7 and the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group at position 3, which constitutes a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system and carries a 1 charge, recognizing the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand; notably, this compound lacks any chiral centers, the positive charge of the 4-methylpiperazin-4-ium quaternary ammonium salt improves water solubility and electrostatic binding, the coumarin core combined with the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group suggests its potential as a fluorescent probe or a kinase inhibitor, compared to its chiral counterparts, its synthesis might be simpler, but it may lack stereoselective advantages in biological activity, and this class of compounds is often investigated as potential anti-tumor agents, targeting key kinases in cell signaling pathways.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-naphthalen-2-yl-7-piperazin-4-ium-1-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C39, is a cationic compound based on the coumarin (chromen-2-one) scaffold, with the molecular formula C23H21N2O2, and it is a non-planar coumarin analog with a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system that carries a 1 charge (where the nitrogen atom at position 4 of the piperazine is quaternized to carry a fixed positive charge). Its structural features include a naphthyl group at position 3 and a piperazinyl group at position 7, the introduction of the naphthyl group enhances the molecules hydrophobicity and - stacking capability, and the positively charged piperazin-4-ium group significantly improves the molecules water solubility and enhances its electrostatic interaction with negatively charged biological targets (such as cell membranes or nucleic acids); additionally, this compound recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1, driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand. Coumarin derivatives are widely used as fluorescent probes due to their excellent fluorescent properties, and the compounds potential activities include acting as an enzyme inhibitor, anticoagulant, or anti-cancer agent, and it is also a valuable fluorescent dye for cell or tissue imaging.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.
Small_molecule_information: 3,6-bis[(E)-2-(1-methylpyrazin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-9H-carbazole diiodide, also referred to as BMVC/BMVC2/BMVC4 (a series of carbazole derivatives and well-known fluorescent probes), is a dicationic cyanine dye based on the highly conjugated carbazole core, substituted with two 1-methylpyrazin-1-ium (quaternary ammonium) groups; these compounds have high affinity for DNA/RNA G-quadruplex structures and served as representative G-quadruplex ligands in a related study, and despite binding strongly to the G-quadruplex form of r(G4C2)exp in vitro, they were poor inhibitors of RAN translation in cellular models, while the dicationic charge and large planar system of 3,6-bis[(E)-2-(1-methylpyrazin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-9H-carbazole diiodide make it a potent DNA intercalator, and these polycationic dyes are used in the development of anticancer and broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents, and as high-performance fluorescent dyes for nuclear staining and photodynamic therapy (PDT) research.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: bis(2-acetamidoacetic acid);4-[2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)iminohydrazinyl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Diminazine, is a complex salt molecule consisting of a large cationic formamidine hydrazonebased diamidine species and two 2-acetamidoacetic acid anions, and the ,-diamidine core connected through a biphenyl and hydrazone bridge corresponds to the class of aromatic diamidine compounds used as antiprotozoal agents that exert activity by binding to the minor groove of DNA, with such cationic diamidines commonly displaying antiprotozoal, antifungal, antiviral, anticancer, or broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: The miR-182 precursor is a structured RNA hairpin that can be specifically bound by compound 3, a small molecule identified through the Inforna approach. This interaction inhibits the precursor’s processing by Drosha, reducing mature miR-182 levels and thereby modulating the expression of its downstream targets, such as FOXO1 and other tumor-suppressive genes. By fine-tuning miR-182 biogenesis, this mechanism helps restore cellular homeostasis in cancer contexts where miR-182 is aberrantly overexpressed.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 1 (Velagapudi et al., 2014), is a simplified mono-benzimidazole scaffold containing the tert-butyl and azide groups, which is a small-molecule mimic of the FMN riboswitch ligand rationally designed to bind a Drosha-site hairpin in the human pre-miR-96 precursor instead of a bacterial RNA; by replacing the ribityl-phosphate tail of FMN with a 4-(3-aminopropyl)-benzimidazole core, the compound gains chemical stability and enhanced selectivity for the miR-96 stem-loop over other miRNA precursors, can bind the precursor with high affinity in vitro, block Drosha cleavage, reduce mature miR-96 levels, thereby restoring FOXO1 tumor-suppressor expression and inducing apoptosis in breast-cancer cell lines, and in mouse xenograft models, daily oral dosing can decrease tumor growth without measurable gut-microbiota perturbation, indicating it or its analogs may serve as precision antibacterial-sparing agents for miRNA-driven cancers, while its real-world activity is designed as a Click Chemistry probe for bioconjugation and target identification, and such probes are critical for studying the pharmacodynamics and metabolism of new drug candidates by allowing tracking of the molecule and identification of its binding partners in biological systems.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(1,2,3,4,4a,5,7,7a-octahydropyrrolo[3,4-b]pyridin-1-ium-6-yl)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM51, is a non-planar coumarin derivative substituted at position 7 with an octahydropyrrolo[3,4-b]pyridin-1-ium quaternary ammonium salt, which has a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system and carries a 1 charge, this is a fused bicyclic system, comprising a five-membered pyrrole ring and a six-membered pyridine ring, saturated to an octahydro form, which provides a degree of conformational flexibility, the positive charge of the quaternary ammonium salt enhances water solubility and binding affinity to negatively charged biological targets, it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, nitrogen-containing fused heterocycles of this type are often used in the design of drugs with central nervous system activity, as they can mimic the structure of endogenous neurotransmitters, this suggests a potential role as an antagonist or agonist for dopamine or serotonin receptors.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-amino-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridin-3-yl)-3-(trifluoromethyl)benzamide, also referred to as Compound 19, an isosteric derivative of the hit compound designed to systematically explore structural alternatives to the 3-amino/2-amide pharmacophore, was synthesized to evaluate the effect of bioisosteric replacement on binding affinity and selectivity, potentially improving metabolic stability or other drug-like properties, and this analog provides critical insights for optimizing the core scaffold in medicinal chemistry; the core structure is an N-(aminothienopyridinyl)benzamide, where the benzamide group is attached to the 3-amino group of the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine ring, with a free amino group at the 2-position, the molecule is substituted with a 3-(trifluoromethyl)benzoyl group, a highly lipophilic group, and this specific positional isomer of the amide bond is important in SAR studies, as it changes the molecules conformation and electronic properties compared to the 2-amide analogs, and this structural type is commonly investigated as a candidate for kinase inhibitors or antimicrobial agents, with its activity dictated by the precise interaction geometry with the target.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 1-ethyl-2-methyl-3-phenylquinazolin-1-ium-4-one, also referred to as CP8 which is based on a quinazolinone core with N-ethyl, N-methyl, and phenyl substitutions, is a quinazolinone quaternary ammonium salt; the substituted quinazolinone scaffold of this compound showed measurable binding but was ultimately superseded by compounds with superior affinity and selectivity, and quaternary ammonium salts often possess broad-spectrum antibacterial activity by non-specifically disrupting bacterial membranes, making this a potential antimicrobial agent.

RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: 6-phenylmethoxyimino-1,9-dihydropurin-2-amine, also referred to as G9, is a uniquely structured purine derivative with the chemical formula C12H12N6O. It is based on a purine scaffold, with an amino group at position 2 and a phenylmethoxyimino (-NOCH2Ph, also described as Benzyloxyamino group -N-O-CH2-C6H5) group at position 6 (i.e., modified at the guanine C6 site in its purine analogue structure). The phenylmethoxy (benzyloxy) substituent is connected via a flexible methylene group, which grants the substituent greater rotational freedom and steric bulk compared to a directly linked phenoxy group, potentially influencing its conformational fit with target proteins. The electron-rich nature of the purine ring and its multiple hydrogen bonding sites allow for diverse interactions with proteins and nucleic acids, and this compound can bind to guanine ribose aptamers in vitro, though its binding affinity is relatively weak. Purine analogs like this are typically considered potential kinase inhibitors, used to block signal transduction pathways related to cancer or inflammation, such as by inhibiting cAMP or cGMP-dependent protein kinases; the activity of this compound may manifest in cell cycle regulation or as an immunomodulatory agent, while it has also demonstrated a certain riboswitch binding ability in vitro. However, in the antibacterial activity test, its growth inhibitory effect on Bacillus subtilis at the specified concentration was less than 50, and it did not show significant antibacterial activity, which indicates that it may fail to effectively exert its role in regulating gene expression through riboswitches due to reasons such as low cellular uptake efficiency, excretion by transport proteins, or metabolic transformation within cells.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

RNA information: 5'CCG/3'GCC is a 1×1 CG internal-loop RNA motif of the 5'CNG/3'GNC family, with a short stem loop (5'CCG/3'GCC), no U·U mismatches, G-C/C-G pairing, high stability, and low "breathing effects". Pathogenically linked to FXTAS (via mutant FMR1 mRNA’s r(CCG) repeats), it acts as a disease-specific RNA switch, recruiting proteins/ligands (e.g., diguanidinobenzoates) to modulate splicing/RAN translation. It also serves as an experimental control for RNA-small molecule binding specificity.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[2-[2-(9-ethylcarbazol-3-yl)ethenyl]-1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl]piperazin-1-yl]-N,N-dimethylethanamine, also referred to as Compound 26 (a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 2-(2-(9-ethyl-9H-carbazol-3-yl)vinyl) group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring and a 4-(2-methoxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl group at the 4-position), belongs to the carbazole-quinolinium cationic series, differentiated by its side chain length: a N,N-dimethylethanamine group linked to the piperazine ring, making it one carbon shorter than a similar analogue; for this compound, SPR assay indicates its affinity for A10-RNA-Wt is lower than that of Compound 24, it was not subjected to the dual-luciferase reporter assay so its impact on ADAM10 translation remains unreported, and no clear cytotoxicity data is provided for it in the tested compound tables, while in medicinal chemistry, this subtle variation in linker length is a critical strategy to fine-tune a molecules lipophilicity, basicity, and spatial orientation, which collectively dictate its cell permeability and target binding mode, consequently, it would be systematically compared with its homologues in applications such as nucleic acid probing or antineoplastic activity to identify the optimal chain length that maximizes the therapeutic index or probe performance.

RNA information: Ca.LSU (Candida albicans Large Subunit) is a self-splicing intron of Class I located in the ribosome large subunit of the Candida albicans 26S rRNA gene. It plays a key role in the maturation process of rRNA, removing itself from the rRNA precursor through the self-splicing mechanism, thereby allowing for further processing and maturation of rRNA. The structure and function of Ca.LSU are of vital importance in the biosynthesis of ribosomes. Its correct folding and catalytic activity are crucial for the maturation of rRNA and the assembly of ribosomes. Studies have shown that certain compounds, such as pentamidine, can inhibit the self-splicing activity of Ca.LSU by binding to specific regions of introns and altering their folding structure. This inhibitory effect can be partially or completely reversed by adding positively charged molecules such as magnesium ions, calcium ions or arginine, which indicates that these positively charged molecules compete with pentamidine for binding sites, protecting the normal folding and function of introns.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)pentoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 24 (EBAB, Pentamidine), is an amidine linked by an alkoxy chain, where two amidine groups are connected by a relatively long pentyloxy ether chain, this molecule is an aromatic diamidine with benzamidine pharmacophore and features a 1,2-ethane/1,5-bis(4-amidinophenoxy)pentane scaffold and bisamidinium groups, its bis-amidine ether structure is highly significant in medicinal chemistry, exemplified by the well-known antiprotozoal drug Pentamidine which acts by binding to parasite DNA, it can also bind r(CUG)exps UU loops/5CUG/3GUC hairpins, displace MBNL1, correct DM1 splicing defects, act as an antibiotic adjuvant against multidrug-resistant bacteria, inhibit Class I intron self-splicing, and has low toxicity, the structural features of the molecule make it a candidate for a potential antiprotozoal or antifungal drug particularly against diseases like Pneumocystis pneumonia, and its activity is closely related to the length and flexibility of the linker chain, while also showing promise for DM1 and drug-resistant infections.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]ethyl]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-3G (an -peptide construct with a three-glycine spacer that proved to be one of the most potent in its series and whose performance validated the role of flexible peptide linkers in positioning modules for effective engagement with r(CUG), though it had limited proteolytic stability in vitro which motivated the search for alternative scaffolds such as PTAs), is a large, symmetrical molecule featuring two benzimidazole-piperazine active units linked by a flexible spacer consisting of four repeating glycinamide units; the glycinamide repeats offer a controllable length and moderate polarity, adaptable to various biological environments, the benzimidazole core is notable for its unique fluorescent properties and potential affinity for nucleic acids, and the dual-head structure, with its precisely engineered span, suggests it may be designed to bind biological targets with specific spatial separation, such as the minor groove of DNA or receptor dimers, while in practice it could be employed as a novel biological probe for live cell imaging or as a potential anticancer agent, and 2H-3G itself was comparable in potency to the most active oligoglycine-based derivatives in relevant evaluations.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,7-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,8-naphthyridine-2,7-dicarboxamide;dibromide, also referred to as NaphDH4, features the 1,8-naphthyridine core (NaphDH4 contains a central naphthyridine core with 1-ethylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and showed good G4 stabilization and moderate FID affinity but no effect on EBNA1), a fused pyridine system that is structurally similar to phenanthroline and also possesses metal ion coordination potential, this core is symmetrically attached to two cationic quinolinium segments, the naphthyridine scaffold provides a different electronic and steric environment compared to the phenanthroline analogue, allowing for a distinct set of physical-chemical properties, in practice, 2-N,7-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,8-naphthyridine-2,7-dicarboxamide;dibromide is developed as a bis-cationic nucleic acid dye, potentially optimized for sequence-selective DNA recognition or as a pH-sensitive fluorescent probe, given the basicity of the naphthyridine nitrogen atoms.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]ethanamine is composed of the cationic pentacyclic scaffold linked via an ethoxy group to a primary amine (ethanamine). At physiological pH, the primary amine is typically protonated, forming another cation, thus allowing the molecule to exhibit dicationic or zwitterionic character. The primary amine is a common functional group in medicinal chemistry, an important hydrogen bond donor and acceptor, and can also serve as a conjugation site. Functionally, large lipoidal derivatives containing primary amines are often used as novel liposomal carriers, capable of efficiently forming stable nanoparticles with negatively charged nucleic acids for targeted gene drug delivery.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-2, is an ultra-long chain bis-head benzimidazole derivative, with the active unit being the classic DNA minor groove binderthe bis-benzimidazole-piperazine structure, and 2H-2 expands the linker to two propylamine spacers, increasing the distance between Ht-N3 modules to better approximate the periodicity of r(CGG)exp motifs, the molecule connects the two active units via a linker arm composed of a triazole ring and three repeating polyamide units, this long-chain design aims to allow the two benzimidazole heads to interact with DNA or oligomeric protein receptors at a precise, adjustable distance, and this extension improves its ability to compete with DGCR8 binding, the linker length of three repeating units in this compound is crucial in multivalent drug design for finding the optimal binding geometry, thus, it is designed as a highly efficient DNA cross-linker or multivalent ligand, with potential application in antitumor drug development, and compared with Ht-N3, 2H-2 shows enhanced cellular performance, restoring splicing defects more effectively, however, like other shorter-spacer constructs, its scaffold is still not fully optimized for the repeat spacing, limiting the degree of preorganization and explaining why mid-length analogs such as 2H-5 achieve superior potency and selectivity.

Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.
