Small_molecule_information: Phenazine, a tricyclic nitrogen-containing aromatic compound formed by the fusion of two benzene rings with a pyrazine ring and also referred to as an azaaromatic ligand that interacts non-specifically with the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) with low activating ability in Bacillus subtilis, has the molecular formula CHN, is a planar conjugated yellow crystalline compound forming the core structure of many natural dyes and antibiotics such as pyocyanin, and exhibits redox activity that underlies its use as a dye, a redox indicator, and a lead scaffold for drugs with antimicrobial, antiparasitic, and potential anticancer activities.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[3-[2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]ethylamino]-3-oxopropyl]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-1A, is a massive, symmetrical dimeric DNA binder constructed from two nearly identical bis-benzimidazole binding units linked by an ethylenediamine/propionamide spacer, and 2H-1A is a -peptide-based compound with one -alanine unit in the linker, each fragment is highly competent at binding to DNA, the dimeric structure is strategically employed to achieve significantly higher DNA binding affinity, enhanced sequence specificity, and a longer duration of action by bridging two binding sites on the DNA double helix, it demonstrated modest potency in disrupting RNAprotein complexes with notable activity, while it showed that -peptides can be used as alternative scaffolds, the overall activity was weaker than that of the most potent -peptides or polyamine-based compounds, in practice, 4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[3-[2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]ethylamino]-3-oxopropyl]butanamide is typically developed as an extremely potent antineoplastic agent due to its effective interference with nucleic acid structure and function, no extensive discussion of stability or cell-based performance was provided for 2H-1A, and the compound remained part of the scaffold survey.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(E)-[10-[(E)-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-ylhydrazinylidene)methyl]anthracen-9-yl]methylideneamino]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Bisantrene, is a complex anthracene derivative with known antitumor relevance, where the anthracene ring (three linearly fused benzene rings) is substituted at the 9- and 10-positions with hydrazone/imine-linked 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) groups; anthracene is a well-known fluorescent chromophore, making this compound highly likely to exhibit strong fluorescence, and the molecule contains two imidazolinium groups and a conjugated hydrazone-imine structure, elements that facilitate binding to biomolecules like DNA, consistent with its use as a DNA-intercalating topoisomerase IIrelated agent, and such compounds are typically designed as DNA-binding fluorescent probes or potential anticancer agents

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-2-(2-phenylethenyl)quinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 17, is the simplest mono-cationic quinolinium dye of this series, with the phenyl group lacking any further substitution; it is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a styryl group at the 2-position and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl moiety at the 4-position of the quinoline ring, synthesized via general procedure B, which binds ADAM10 mRNAs QGRS with moderate affinity but shows weak translational up-regulation, and its simple styryl group limits optimal RNA G-quadruplex interactions, making it less potent than analogs with substituted aromatic groups at C2, while its core 1-methylquinolin-1-ium and 4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl) groups define its cationic nature and lipophilicity, and lacking strong electron-donating or -withdrawing substituents, its fluorescence properties are relatively straightforward; in real applications, this class of simplified cationic dyes is primarily used as non-sequence-specific DNA stains or mitochondrial probes, and they are concentrated by the negative membrane potential of mitochondria, making this compound useful for cell viability assays and indicators of mitochondrial membrane potential changes during apoptosis.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: amino-[4-[amino-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butylazaniumylidene]methyl]phenyl]methylidene]-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]azanium, also referred to as a bisamidinium-derived molecule with added amine groups, is a massively complex, highly positively charged compound, likely a dicationic or polycationic dye or polyamine derivative; this compound binds both DNA and RNA CTG/CUG repeats, potently inhibits transcription, cleaves (CUG) selectively (not CCUG), and is highly effective in reversing DM phenotypes with low toxicity, while its core features two Triazine units each linked to a polyamine tail, the central azaniumylidene and methylidene groups point to a highly stabilized guanidinium or amidinium derivative, conferring high basicity and charge, and due to its multiple positive charges and hydrogen bond donors, the molecule is researched as a highly efficient gene transfection reagent (for delivering DNA/RNA into cells) or as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting by disrupting the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purine-6-carbonitrile, also referred to as 6-cyanopurine, is a simple purine derivative where the C6 position is substituted with a cyano group, with the formula C6H3N5, and 6-cyanopurine is a purine analog featuring a purine bicyclic core (fused pyrimidine and imidazole rings) with a cyano (-CN) group at the 6-position, replacing the typical carbonyl or amino moiety of natural purines, the cyano group (CN) is often used in medicinal chemistry as a prodrug or a potential covalent enzyme inhibitor, structurally, it retains the planar aromatic heterocyclic scaffold of purines while introducing a polar cyano functional group that enhances hydrogen bonding potential, this compound is an important intermediate for synthesizing other biologically active purine nucleoside analogs, functionally, it acts as a potential disrupter of purine metabolism, thereby influencing normal cellular processes and exhibiting possible antiviral or antiproliferative activity, it also acts as a nucleotide metabolism modulator, competes with endogenous purines for binding to purine-associated enzymes or riboswitches, and serves as a tool for studying purine-dependent pathways, with potential applications in antiviral or anticancer drug development due to its ability to disrupt nucleic acid synthesis and gene regulation. 6-

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(7-chloroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-1-ium-2-yl)-7-piperazin-4-ium-1-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C32, is a dicationic coumarin derivative based on the coumarin scaffold (note: C32 is characterized as a non-planar coumarin analog, while this full-named compound has a planar aromatic system), with the molecular formula C20H18ClN4O2. The molecule carries two fixed positive charges (C32 carries a 1 charge): one on the piperazin-4-ium group at position 7 of the coumarin, and another on the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-1-ium ring connected at position 3, and the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine ring is also substituted with a chloro atom, with C32 possessing a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system. This combination of high charge density and a planar aromatic system makes it an ideal DNA or RNA fluorescent probe, which binds strongly to the nucleic acid backbone via electrostatic attraction while the planar structure allows for intercalation with nucleobase pairs; additionally, C32 functions as a selective RNA ligand that recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1, with this binding driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics and confirmed by FP and SAR comparison. Its high water solubility and charge give it significant cell membrane permeability and the potential to target the cell nucleus or mitochondria, thus suggesting potent broad-spectrum antibacterial or anti-cancer activity.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: Trisodium;5-oxo-1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-[(4-sulfonatophenyl)diazenyl]-4H-pyrazole-3-carboxylate, also referred to as Tartrazine (and known as the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)), is the chemical name for the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110), a synthetic azo dye with the formula C16H10N2Na3O7[(2R,3S,4S,5R,6R)-2-[(2R,3S,4S,5S,6S)-2-[(1R,2S)-3-[[(2R,3S,4S)-5-[[(2S,3R)-1-[2-[4-[4-[3-[4-[[2-[[2-[[2-[acetyl(propyl)amino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-prop-2-ynylamino]acetyl]amino]butylamino]propylcarbamoyl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]ethylamino]-3-hydroxy-1-oxobutan-2-yl]amino]-3-hydroxy-4-methyl-5-oxopentan-2-yl]amino]-2-[[6-amino-2-[(1S)-3-amino-1-[[(2S)-2,3-diamino-3-oxopropyl]amino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-methylpyrimidine-4-carbonyl]amino]-1-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-3-oxopropoxy]-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]oxy-3,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-4-yl]carbamate, and Tartrazine is a synthetic yellow azo dye used as a food coloring agent while primarily an industrial colorant, its structure features a pyrazolone core linked by an azo bond (NN) to two benzene rings, both substituted with sodium sulfonate groups, and exists as a trisodium salt, the azo structure imparts a vibrant color, in real-world use, Sunset Yellow is widely employed as an orange-yellow coloring agent in foods, beverages, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics, such as orange juice, sodas, candies, and ice cream, to enhance visual appeal, as a common food colorant, its use is strictly regulated globally to ensure safety within prescribed limits, despite some controversy regarding its potential to affect behavior in sensitive individuals, particularly children.

RNA information: r(CUG)₄ is a short ribonucleic acid fragment consisting of four consecutive CUG repeat units. Structurally, it tends to form a more stable hairpin secondary structure than r(CUG)₂, with multiple characteristic U·U mismatches in the stem region (the number of mismatch sites increases with the number of repeat units). In some cases, its conformation can be further stabilized by GC base pairs at both ends (similar to the GC clamp design of (CUG)₁₂ mentioned in the document), making it a key short-chain model for simulating the structural characteristics of long-chain CUG repeat sequences. Functionally, it does not possess the toxicity of long expanded r(CUG)ⁿ (n≥50, pathogenic type) — which forms nuclear foci and sequesters MBNL1 (Muscleblind-like family proteins) — but it can specifically bind to MBNL1 (with lower affinity than long pathogenic sequences). Therefore, it is mainly used as an in vitro research tool to analyze the binding mode between MBNL1 and CUG repeat sequences, verify the inhibitory effect of small-molecule compounds on their interaction, and provide structural and functional references that are closer to the characteristics of long-chain sequences for exploring the pathogenic mechanism of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and the preliminary screening of related therapeutic drugs.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 6-methyl-2-[5-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]-1,3,4-oxadiazol-2-yl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridin-3-amine, also referred to as a derivative of the core structure 3-aminothieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, is a compound containing thienopyridine and oxadiazole heterocyclic rings, its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton which features a fused thienopyridine heterocyclic system, substituted with an essential amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a 5-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]-1,3,4-oxadiazol-2-yl group at C2, and this thienopyridine parent skeleton is highly versatile as demonstrated by various substituents at the 6-position (e.g., phenyl, cyclopropyl, chloro, methyl) and on the carboxamide nitrogen (different aryl or benzyl groups), these structural modifications are designed to fine-tune the compounds properties, making this core a privileged structure in medicinal chemistry for developing potential bioactive molecules, particularly as kinase inhibitors or anticancer agents, while in terms of biological function, 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives often exhibit various antimicrobial activity, including antibacterial, antitubercular, antifungal, and antiprotozoal effects.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethylamino)phenol, also referred to as 4o, is a small-molecule heterocyclic amine compound with the molecular formula C14H15N3O, and 4o is a 2-aminomethylene-dihydropyrrolo-[3,2]-pyridine derivative with a fluorine-substituted aniline moiety, synthesized via ruthenium-catalyzed [222] cycloaddition and nucleophilic substitution (sustainable, modular route), the molecule contains an o-aminophenol structure, where the adjacent hydroxyl and amine groups can serve as chelating sites for metal ions and impart potential antioxidant properties to the molecule, it is connected via a methylene secondary amine to a 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine heterocyclic scaffold, a fused heterocyclic structure found in many potentially biologically active molecules, this secondary amine linker provides some molecular flexibility, due to its aminophenol moiety, the compound may act as an inhibitor of metalloenzymes, exerting its pharmacological effect by chelating metal ions like zinc or iron in the active site, its potential activity ranges across anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, or kinase inhibition, 4o exhibits weak affinity for pre-miR-21, far lower than analogs with unsubstituted or CF3-aniline groups, it lacks Dicer cleavage inhibition activity, showing poor selectivity and potency, likely due to its aniline substituent failing to form critical H-bonds or -stacking with pre-miR-21. pre-miR-21

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x20: This is a control RNA with twenty repeats of perfectly paired CAG-GUC duplex. It is used to contrast with the mismatched x20 RNA and shows negligible binding and structural changes, underscoring the importance of the AA mismatch in selective compound recognition.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 15, is a highly substituted chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, and Compound 15 was identified as a top lead compound with very high affinity and exceptional selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, the chromene moiety is utilized in antiviral and antitumor drug design, the presence of a bromo atom, amino group, and benzoyl group suggests it may act as a potent enzyme inhibitor or receptor antagonist, potentially blocking key processes in cancer cell proliferation, its bromine substituent enhanced RNA binding through additional halogen bonding, it significantly reduced polyQ aggregates, was cell-permeable, and showed low toxicity. RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

Small_molecule_information: 3-(4-chlorophenyl)-7-(4-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as C20 (a non-planar coumarin analog with a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system carrying a 1 charge), is another cationic coumarin derivative. It retains the fluorescent coumarin property, the cationic 4-methylpiperazin-4-ium group, and the para-chlorophenyl group at C3, but lacks the fluorine atom at C5, and can recognize the single-G bulge of RNA1, with this binding driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics (confirmed by FP and SAR comparison), serving as a selective RNA ligand; the compounds cationic nature allows it to target negatively charged cellular components, while the coumarin scaffold provides potential as a fluorescent probe for bioimaging, and its biological activity is likely centered around enzyme inhibition or receptor modulation, such as anticoagulant effects or kinase inhibition.

RNA information: GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. Small_molecule_information: 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 5b (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a structurally simpler tetracationic polyamine (compared to 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]phenol and 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]-2-phenylmethoxyphenyl]ethanamine), containing four primary amine groups and a phenol, and is synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings, characterized by NMR, SPR, and ITC, its rigid scaffold features only methoxy substituents, resulting in less steric hindrance, functionally, due to its high symmetry and lower steric hindrance, it may possess a higher nucleic acid binding affinity or more effective gene transfection ability than more complex derivatives, while it also binds to HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIBs hairpin RNA (Bh) with high-affinity binding detected by ITC, induces chemical shift changes in Bhs loop, has certain specificity ratios for related nucleic acid structures, shows limited DNA selectivity, has high IC in inhibiting Bh-Rev interaction, and no antiviral activity in cell assays, in practical applications, such compounds are often used as probes to study the biological effects of polyamine analogs on cell proliferation and apoptosis, as polyamine metabolism in cellulo is closely linked to cancer cell growth.

RNA information: SA2 RNA is an FMN riboswitch located upstream of the ribU gene in methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Its aptamer structure can specifically bind to flavin mononucleotide (FMN). When the concentration of FMN increases, it undergoes a conformational switch and forms a hairpin that sequesters the ribosome-binding site, inhibiting the translation of downstream mRNA encoding riboflavin transporters and thereby shutting down the uptake of exogenous riboflavin. The synthetic compound ribocil-C and the natural product roseoflavin can also bind to this structure, mimicking the role of FMN to block bacteria from acquiring riboflavin from the environment and synergistically inhibiting their growth.
Small_molecule_information: [5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), is a derivative of riboflavin generated through phosphorylation, which is a coenzyme produced by the phosphorylation of riboflavin; its isoalloxazine ring has a planar aromatic structure, while the phosphate group is flexibly linked to the ring system, and its isoalloxazine tricyclic and ribitol-phosphate skeleton together form a reversible electron-transferring redox center; as the natural ligand of bacterial riboswitches, FMN inserts into the aptamer pocket and induces conformational changes in RNA, causing the expression platform to form a terminator/sequestering loop, and it can also induce RNA structure folding into terminator clips or blocking ribosome binding sites by binding to the SA1 riboswitch upstream of riboflavin synthesis genes and the SA2 riboswitch upstream of transport genes, using its own concentration changes as a signal, which consequently shuts down the transcription and translation of the downstream riboflavin synthesis gene ribB as well as the de novo synthesis and uptake pathways of riboflavin, thereby achieving negative feedback regulation of riboflavin biosynthesis; because it is crucial for bacterial survival and does not exist in mammals, FMN and its riboswitch have become targets for antibiotic design, which can be competitionally occupied by structural analogues such as rosettin and ribocil-C, blocking the supply of riboflavin and forcing bacteria to starve to death.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]ethyl]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-5G, is a very high molecular weight bicephalic compound, where the two benzimidazole active cores are separated by an extremely long linker composed of six repeating glycinamide units (while 2H-5G itself has a five-glycine linker) and maintained micromolar activity, though somewhat reduced relative to 2H-3G and 2H-4G, this ultra-long, flexible spacer provides the molecule with the ability to span large distances within a biological system and achieve multipoint binding, and the compound still demonstrated significant disruption of the RNAprotein complex but was not the most optimized variant, its performance suggests that excessive linker length can slightly compromise binding affinity, the benzimidazole and piperazine structures collaboratively provide cationic characteristics and aromatic stacking capabilities, making it an ideal scaffold for nucleic acid binding agents, additional biochemical or cellular data were not reported, and the molecule served primarily as part of the systematic length-dependence study, in practical applications, such giant molecules could be developed as highly specific multivalent ligands, for example, to study receptor clustering or as a component of gene transfection reagents by forming stable complexes with nucleic acids to facilitate cellular uptake.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]-3-(1H-indol-3-yl)propanoic acidThis conjugate combines the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core with Arginine and Tryptophan. Featuring two strong fluorophores, this compound is highly likely to be designed as a high-sensitivity dual-fluorescence probe for live-cell imaging and molecular interaction studies. Its potential activity involves functioning as a cytotoxic drug precursor while allowing for real-time tracking of its distribution and target binding.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-methyl-N-pyridin-4-ylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as the final product of a reaction that undergoes nucleophilic substitution and subsequent cyclization under basic conditions (sodium ethoxide) to construct a thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, is an amide compound containing thienopyridine and pyridinyl structures. Its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a pyridin-4-ylcarboxamide group at C2, and this complex heterocyclic system also bears amino, methyl, carboxamide, and trifluoromethylphenyl groups indicative of potential biological activity. In terms of biological function, thienopyridine derivatives are known for their broad biological activity, including herbicide safening effects against certain herbicides and strong fluorescence properties.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (Ht-N3, a bis-benzimidazole derivative with Hoechst 33258 core, 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethoxy side chain, azide group, and 4-fluorophenylethyl tail replacing FMNs ribose-phosphate), is an azide-containing bis-benzimidazole probe without the tert-butyl groups (compared to N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), its core activity remains the same: strong DNA binding, and it additionally binds 5CGG/3GGC and pre-miR-210, disrupts r(CGG)-DGCR8, restores SMN2/Bcl-x splicing, inhibits tumor growth, has low cytotoxicity while tRNA reduces its selectivity, serving as a probe/lead for FXTAS, CGG-repeat disorders, and miR-210-driven cancers; its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for nucleic acid binding studies and labeling, which enables researchers to map the interaction site of the bis-benzimidazole unit in a biological context using the high specificity of the azide group.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (2R,3S,4S,5R,6R)-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(1R,2R,3S,4R,6S)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-3,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxyoxane-3,4,5-triol, also referred to as Kanamycin, is a non-azaaromatic aminoglycoside antibiotic, with its chemical name clearly indicating its oligosaccharide structure, which is derived from aminocyclitol and aminosugar units linked by glycosidic bonds, specifically composed of three linked amino sugar ringskanosamine, 6-amino-6-deoxy-D-glucose, and 2-deoxy-D-streptamineconnected via such bonds, and it lacks planar azaaromatic features and binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; this molecular backbone contains multiple primary and secondary amine groups, providing a high positive charge density in aqueous solution, which enables it to efficiently target the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane and ribosomal RNA, and its core biological activity stems from inhibiting protein synthesis by binding to the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking protein synthesis and ultimately causing bacterial cell death, serving as an important treatment for sensitive bacterial infections; similar to other aminoglycosides, it represents an important class of anti-infective agents, but due to the potential for bacterial aminoglycoside modifying enzymes to recognize and inactivate the amino groups, research is continually directed towards the study of resistance mechanisms and the development of novel derivatives of this compound.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of 4c-6, is a large, planar quaternary ammonium salt based on the indolo[3,2-b]quinoline core, which demonstrated binding affinity for the NRQ, exhibited variable anti-proliferative activity with efficacy in A375 cells but no effect in HeLa cells at relevant concentrations; quaternary salts are often potent DNA intercalators and broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents due to their positive charge and ability to disrupt cell membranes, and this structure is typically investigated for its strong anticancer activity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: Trisodium;5-oxo-1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-[(4-sulfonatophenyl)diazenyl]-4H-pyrazole-3-carboxylate, also referred to as Tartrazine (and known as the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)), is the chemical name for the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110), a synthetic azo dye with the formula C16H10N2Na3O7[(2R,3S,4S,5R,6R)-2-[(2R,3S,4S,5S,6S)-2-[(1R,2S)-3-[[(2R,3S,4S)-5-[[(2S,3R)-1-[2-[4-[4-[3-[4-[[2-[[2-[[2-[acetyl(propyl)amino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-prop-2-ynylamino]acetyl]amino]butylamino]propylcarbamoyl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]ethylamino]-3-hydroxy-1-oxobutan-2-yl]amino]-3-hydroxy-4-methyl-5-oxopentan-2-yl]amino]-2-[[6-amino-2-[(1S)-3-amino-1-[[(2S)-2,3-diamino-3-oxopropyl]amino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-methylpyrimidine-4-carbonyl]amino]-1-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-3-oxopropoxy]-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]oxy-3,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-4-yl]carbamate, and Tartrazine is a synthetic yellow azo dye used as a food coloring agent while primarily an industrial colorant, its structure features a pyrazolone core linked by an azo bond (NN) to two benzene rings, both substituted with sodium sulfonate groups, and exists as a trisodium salt, the azo structure imparts a vibrant color, in real-world use, Sunset Yellow is widely employed as an orange-yellow coloring agent in foods, beverages, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics, such as orange juice, sodas, candies, and ice cream, to enhance visual appeal, as a common food colorant, its use is strictly regulated globally to ensure safety within prescribed limits, despite some controversy regarding its potential to affect behavior in sensitive individuals, particularly children.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-[(E,2S,7S)-8-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-azaniumylpropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2,7-bis[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-8-oxooct-4-enoyl]pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]-3-phenylpropanoyl]amino]propylazanium, also referred to as Compound 5E, is a highly complex, poly-cationic peptidomimetic that shares the benzo[g]quinoline-expanded scaffold of 4Z and yields similarly high affinity for HIV-1 FSS RNA, while its selectivity profile is slightly weaker than 4Zshowing less discrimination between RNA and DNAit retains good specificity over unrelated viral FSS elements, can penetrate cells efficiently, shows no cytotoxicity at relevant concentrations, stimulates 1 frameshifting to a notable extent in reporter assays, reduces HIV infectivity significantly in viral assays and has corresponding effective concentration performance, confirming it as an effective FSS-targeted antiviral lead, and it is based on the (E)-oct-4-enediamide scaffold, featuring phenylalanine residues and the bulky 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group, with its termini linked to the 3-azaniumylpropyl groups (quaternized/protonated) that impart strong poly-cationic properties, and this structure which combines poly-cationic centers, high aromaticity, and the hydrophobicity of phenylalanine is ideally suited as a high-selectivity DNA minor groove binder or as an analog of cell-penetrating peptides, holding potential for development as a potent anti-cancer or antiviral agent.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

Small_molecule_information: 5,7-dimethoxy-1-methyl-2-[4-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropoxy)phenyl]quinolin-4-one, also referred to as Phenylquinolone 5b, is an analog of 2-[3-amino-4-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one (the monosubstituted derivative 2b), but the 3-amino group on the phenyl ring is removed, being the desamino analog of derivative 2b that lacks the amino group on the phenyl ring and retains only the single O-propylpiperidine side chain; the lack of the amino group eliminates a hydrogen bond donor, making the molecule a simpler, more lipophilic, monobasic quinolinone, and this compound exhibited the highest in vitro potency in its series for Tat/TAR inhibition, demonstrating that the amino group is not essential for activity, however it displayed residual cytotoxicity in Jurkat cells and very low cellular uptake, which ultimately prevented the translation of its strong target affinity into effective antiviral activity in cell-based assays; functionally, the absence of the amino group may alter its mechanism of action, preventing it from targeting sites that require the amino group for binding, and in practical applications, this structure is often explored as a calcium channel blocker, histamine antagonist, or monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitor, commonly used in the development of cardiovascular or nervous system drugs.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[4-[[2,6-bis[[4-(2-aminoethoxy)quinolin-2-yl]carbamoyl]-4-pyridinyl]oxymethyl]triazol-1-yl]propanoic acid, also referred to as PDS cPDS, is a complex heterocyclic molecule often constructed via click chemistry, which is structurally similar to 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)quinolin-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide but modified at the pyridine 4-position, and it is a potent and selective G-quadruplex-stabilizing small molecule that demonstrated excellent binding to the r(G4C2) G-quadruplex, though it was ineffective at inhibiting RAN translation, providing key evidence that the G-quadruplex is not the therapeutically relevant structure for this disease mechanism; the molecule maintains the core features of the multicationic backbone with a 2,6-bis-amide substituted pyridine and two 2-aminoethoxy groups on the quinoline side chains, however, the modification at the pyridine 4-position introduces a side chain containing a stable and polar 1,2,3-triazole ring, terminated by a carboxylic acid group (propanoic acid), the carboxylic acid group introduces an ionizable negative charge, creating an amphoteric or zwitterionic-like molecule that combines polycationic properties (from the primary amines) with a terminal negative charge, and this complex charge distribution may enable unique functionalities, such as pH-sensitive molecular probes, ligands for specific cell receptors, or prodrugs designed to release or change activity in specific environments, like the acidic tumor microenvironment.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoic acidA simpler benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline conjugate with a short peptide chain consisting of three Glycine units and one Arginine residue. The potential activity is anticancer driven by the cores mechanism (DNA intercalation/Topoisomerase inhibition) and the Arginines affinity for nucleic acids. This structure is often utilized in drug optimization to determine the minimal peptide length required for retaining biological activity and desired pharmacokinetic properties.

Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(4-fluorophenyl)-2,5-dimethyl-7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidin-1-ium-4-imine, also referred to as CP1 which features an imidazopyrimidine fused heterocyclic core with multiple nitrogen atoms, substituted by two methyl groups and a 4-fluorophenyl ring, is based on the 7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidine core, which is an important purine nucleobase analog (7-deazaguanine); the molecule has an unusual electronic structure, being quaternized at N1 and featuring an imine (CN) at C4, forming a 1-ium-4-imine system, and it is substituted with a 4-fluorophenyl group at C3 and methyl groups at C2 and C5, while its binding data showed higher affinity for the control RNA, indicating a lack of specificity for the pathogenic AA mismatch motif, which limited its further development as a lead compound, and this unique ionic and imine-containing nucleobase analog makes 3-(4-fluorophenyl)-2,5-dimethyl-7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidin-1-ium-4-imine an excellent candidate for antimetabolites or enzyme inhibitors that interfere with nucleic acid biosynthesis or metabolism, and it may be designed as a novel antiviral or anti-cancer agent by mimicking natural substrates.

Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-(trifluoromethyl)-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 6, is a thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide derivative containing two trifluoromethyl substituents (with a trifluoromethyl substitution at the pyridine rings C-6, modified from initial active analogs) that enhance its lipophilicity; the thienopyridine scaffold is the structural basis for many kinase inhibitors and anti-inflammatory drugs, and this molecule can bind HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNAkey for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection via interacting with these regulatory RNAs with moderate affinity though it lacks further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity, while it may also be developed as an antineoplastic agent by inhibiting specific tyrosine kinases or signaling pathways to block cancer cell proliferation.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-phenyl-3,4-dihydroisochromen-1-one, also referred to as CP11, is an isocoumarin derivative, and CP11 is a chromanone (dihydrobenzopyranone) derivative with a phenyl ring whose saturated chromone core exhibited a single, modest-affinity binding mode, lacking the desired specificity for the target RNA structure, the isocoumarin scaffold, fused from a benzene ring and a six-membered lactone ring (dihydro-pyranone), is prevalent in natural products, especially fungal and plant metabolites, these naturally occurring isocoumarin derivatives often exhibit diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, 3-phenyl-3,4-dihydroisochromen-1-ones structure includes a phenyl group at the 3-position of the lactone ring, which increases the molecules lipophilicity and steric complexity, in reality, compounds with this structure serve as basic building blocks for the synthesis of various natural product analogues and pharmaceutical precursors, where researchers explore and optimize their potential pharmacological activity through further chemical modifications (e.g., introducing other functional groups on the phenyl or isocoumarin rings). Small_molecule_information: (2S)-7-fluoro-2-methyl-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-10-oxo-4-oxa-1-azatricyclo[7.3.1.05,13]trideca-5(13),6,8,11-tetraene-11-carboxylic acid is a derivative of the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics. Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: Ca.LSU (Candida albicans Large Subunit) is a self-splicing intron of Class I located in the ribosome large subunit of the Candida albicans 26S rRNA gene. It plays a key role in the maturation process of rRNA, removing itself from the rRNA precursor through the self-splicing mechanism, thereby allowing for further processing and maturation of rRNA. The structure and function of Ca.LSU are of vital importance in the biosynthesis of ribosomes. Its correct folding and catalytic activity are crucial for the maturation of rRNA and the assembly of ribosomes. Studies have shown that certain compounds, such as pentamidine, can inhibit the self-splicing activity of Ca.LSU by binding to specific regions of introns and altering their folding structure. This inhibitory effect can be partially or completely reversed by adding positively charged molecules such as magnesium ions, calcium ions or arginine, which indicates that these positively charged molecules compete with pentamidine for binding sites, protecting the normal folding and function of introns.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(6-carbamimidoyl-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)ethenyl]-3H-benzimidazole-5-carboximidamide, also referred to as BBE, is a compound containing a bis-benzimidazole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups connected by an ethenyl linker, with the formula C18H16N8; its skeleton is rigidly connected by two benzimidazole-amidine groups through a trans-ethylene bridge, forming a conjugated plane that can be inserted between the twisted double helix and the convex ring of RNA, while its double-terminal amidine group electrostably chelates with phosphate oxygen at multiple points, locking the active site into an inactive conformation, thereby blocking the entry of guanosine and achieving non-competitive inhibition of self-splicing. These are known as benzimidazole diamidines, recognized as some of the most potent DNA minor groove binders; they work by deeply inserting into the minor groove and forming multiple hydrogen bonds and van der Waals interactions, thus strongly inhibiting DNA replication and transcription, and in reality, they hold immense potential as anticancer, antiviral, and antiparasitic agents, and are widely used as high-sensitivity fluorescent probes for DNA.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-(cyclohexylmethoxy)phenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5d (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a cyclohexylmethoxy group, the compound was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings and characterized by methods like NMR and SPR, the cyclohexyl group is a saturated alicyclic ring that can significantly increase the molecules lipophilicity but removes the possibility of - stacking, as a tetracationic polyamine, this increase in lipophilicity may enhance its interaction with lipid-rich environments (such as the interior of cell membranes), functionally, Compound 5d binds to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB with low binding affinity and has corresponding binding specificity relative to control hairpins GGh and TARh, shows higher IC in inhibiting the Bh-Rev interaction and no antiviral activity in cell assays, while such derivatives may be explored for targeting membrane-associated proteins or interfering with lipid metabolism pathways, for instance, as novel antiviral or antifungal agents.

RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: N-(6-oxo-1,7-dihydropurin-2-yl)acetamide, also referred to as G4, is a purine derivative whose C2 position bears an N-keto acetamide substituent that enables it to adopt a guanine-like RNA-binding conformation while exhibiting high binding affinity without notable antibacterial activity; 6-oxo-1,7-dihydropurine is essentially hypoxanthine, and this acetylation at the 2-position may influence its cellular metabolism, transport, or interactions with enzymes such as hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase, suggesting potential as an antiviral or anticancer agent.

These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.

The side chain at position 7 is a trimethylazanium group, and the substituent at position 11 is 2-piperidin-1-ylethylamino. The introduction of the piperidine ring affects the molecules lipophilicity and metabolic stability. The practical application of this compound lies in its potential as a broad-spectrum anti-tumor agent. This type of polycyclic cationic structure can interfere with the proliferation of cancer cells through the dual mechanisms of DNA intercalation and inhibition of key enzymes (like topoisomerases), and may help overcome certain forms of cancer cell resistance.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[4-[(2-amino-5-methyl-4-oxo-3,6,7,8-tetrahydropteridin-6-yl)methylamino]benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid, also referred to as 5-methyl THF (5-methyltetrahydrofolate), is a non-azaaromatic folate derivative with a pteridine core (reduced, lacking planarity), a p-aminobenzoyl group, a glutamate chain, and a methyl group at the 5-position, which binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis and whose key role is as a methyl donor in biological methylation reactions unrelated to azaaromatic sensing, this compound is an analog of Folic Acid, structurally related to the crucial anticancer drug Methotrexate (a DHFR inhibitor), and is likely a metabolite or a potential antimetabolite anti-tumor agent.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Small_molecule_information: N-[7-[(1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium-4-yl)methoxy]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]-N,N-diethylpropane-1,3-diamine diiodide is a benzofuroquinoline bis-quaternary ammonium salt derivative. Its structure features the benzofuroquinoline scaffold, with the nitrogen at the 5-position quaternized and the piperidine ring on the 7-position side chain also quaternized, and a diethylpropanediamine side chain at the 11-position, with diiodide as the counterion (molecular formula C34H47N4O22I). This compound possesses a planar conjugated system and two positive charges, giving it a significantly enhanced ability to intercalate DNA or bind within the minor groove. It is considered a precursor for highly potent anti-cancer drugs designed to overcome multidrug resistance.

It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(2,3,3a,4,6,6a-hexahydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyrrol-2-ium-5-yl)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM54, is a non-planar coumarin derivative where the substituent at position 7 is a hexahydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyrrol-2-ium quaternary ammonium salt, carrying a 1 charge, this is a complex fused ring structure formed by the fusion of two five-membered pyrrolidine rings with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, this fused bicyclic structure is relatively rigid, which helps provide precise spatial positioning during binding to a biological target, the quaternary ammonium positive charge is a crucial factor for improving water solubility and electrostatic binding affinity, the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group is connected at position 3 of the coumarin core, the molecules overall conjugated system may impart unique fluorescent or spectral properties, it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, and in medicinal chemistry, this type of structure is often designed to target enzymes or receptors with complex binding pockets, such as proteins involved in cell membrane transport or signal transduction.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.
