Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

Small_molecule_information: 7-(3-aza-8-azoniabicyclo[3.2.1]octan-3-yl)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM23, is a non-planar coumarin derivative, with the substituent at position 7 being the 3-aza-8-azoniabicyclo[3.2.1]octan-3-yl group, which has a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, bearing a 1 charge and binding the single-G bulge of RNA via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, validated by FP and SAR comparison, and showing 10-fold selectivity over GA-rich RNA, ranking it among the most potent and selective ligands; this is a bridged-ring structure formed by connecting a six-membered ring and a seven-membered ring across two common carbon atoms, possessing extremely high rigidity and a highly defined three-dimensional conformation, which is essential for the precise fitting of the drug molecule into the active pocket of an enzyme or receptor, and the quaternary ammonium positive charge provides enhanced water solubility and electrostatic interaction capabilities, the entire molecular structure is indicative of a molecule designed for high selectivity and affinity binding, and based on the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group and the coumarin core, its potential activities include inhibiting tumor cell proliferation or acting as a ligand for specific G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs).

Small_molecule_information: 1-[(2R,3R,4S,5R)-3,4-dihydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]pyrimidine-2,4-dione, also referred to as uracil riboside and known as Uridine, is a naturally occurring non-azaaromatic pyrimidine nucleoside, with the formula C9H12N2O6, structured with a uracil base (pyrimidine-2,4-dione) linked via an N-glycosidic bond at the N1 position to a D-ribose sugar molecule (the oxolan ring) and no phosphate groups, which lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch), and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis. Uridine is one of the four fundamental building blocks of Ribonucleic Acid (RNA), and its core roles include being a building block for RNA synthesis, a precursor for UDP-glucose (for glycogen synthesis), and a regulator of pyrimidine metabolism in eukaryotic cells; functionally, it also plays a key role in nucleic acid synthesis, glycogen synthesis, and cell signaling, is active in the liver and acts as a modulator of certain neurotransmitters in the brain, suggesting potential benefits for cognitive function and nerve repair. In nutritional supplements and drug research, Uridine is often used as a neurotrophic agent or to promote RNA synthesis; for example, in combination with choline and DHA, it is believed to support the formation of synaptic membranes, improving memory and learning.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-bromo-2-phenylquinoline, also referred to as CP12, is a brominated quinoline derivative with a phenyl substitution and is a bromine-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure is based on the quinoline core: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, with a phenyl group substituted at the 2-position and a bromine atom at the 3-position. The molecular formula is C15H10BrN. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system, where the free rotation between the phenyl group and the quinoline ring is restricted. The bromine atom reduces the electron density on the ring and enables the molecule to participate in halogen bonding. In terms of biological function, quinoline compounds are generally known for possessing a variety of biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, antispasmodic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methylbenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 2, which is structurally identical to the aforementioned compound but lacks the chlorine atom at the 9-position, exhibited selective binding to r(CAG)exp RNA with improved affinity compared to some analogs and was synthesized with a methyl substitution on the benzoyl ring that contributed to its RNA-binding properties without significant cytotoxicity; its core is the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, substituted with a 3-amino group, a 4-phenyl group, and a 2-4-methylbenzoyl group, and this planar, multi-ring system is investigated for its potential as a kinase inhibitor or PDE inhibitor, often in anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory research, serving as a fundamental analog to determine the necessity and role of substituents (such as the 9-chloro group in the related compound) on the biological activity profile.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 6-nitro-8-(2H-tetrazol-5-yl)-4,7,8,9-tetrahydro-1H-cyclopenta[f]quinoxaline-2,3-dione, also referred to as tetrazole, nitro, and dione functional groups, and 2,3-Dioxo-8-[2-(5-tetrazolyl)]-2,3,4,7,8,9-hexahydro-1H-6-nitro-cyclopenta[f]quinoxaline is a rationally designed small-molecule ligand that targets the bulge region of the HIV-1 TAR RNA stem-loop, the quinoxaline scaffold itself, formed by fusing a benzene ring and a pyrazine ring, has inherent biological potential and is frequently used in dye and pharmaceutical synthesis, the complexity of this molecule suggests it may be highly specific, the tetrazole ring (2H-tetrazol-5-yl) is a relatively acidic group, often employed as a bioisostere for the carboxylic acid group, capable of forming strong hydrogen or ionic bonds with positively charged protein sites, the dione (-2,3-dione) and nitro (-nitro) groups on the quinoxaline impart potential redox activity and stronger electron-accepting properties, overall, this molecule is likely designed as a highly specific receptor ligand or enzyme inhibitor, in real-world applications, such polycyclic derivatives are often explored for the development of drugs targeting the nervous system, for example, as modulators of GABA receptors or other central nervous system targets, where complex scaffolds are beneficial for achieving high selectivity, by inserting its fused cyclopentaquinoxaline core into the widened major groove adjacent to the three-nucleotide bulge, the compound makes specific contacts (protection of C24, G26, G28) that overlap the recognition surface used by the viral Tat protein, this competitive, high-affinity binding in gel-shift assays displaces Tat from TAR, blocks Tat-activated transcription in HeLa cell reporter systems, and suppresses p24 production in latently infected OM-10.1 cells without accelerating Tat dissociation, the appended tetrazolyl moiety provides aqueous solubility and an additional anionic anchor that favors RNA over DNA or tRNA, giving the compound a clear selectivity profile, thus, this quinoxaline derivative validates the Its structure provides potential recognition elements but no detailed functional data in the context of HCV was reported.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[3-[[2,5-bis[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]carbamoylamino]phenyl]carbamoylamino]-1-N,4-N-bis[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7, is an extremely large, highly branched polycationic compound, and it can selectively bind internal loop motifs such as 5UUA/3A_U and 5UGU/3G_A with submicromolar affinity, with structural studies revealing that its binding is driven by recognition of asymmetric bulges within RNA stems, it can directly interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II by targeting motifs present in viral RNA, functional assays have demonstrated that it inhibits HCV replication in cell culture without affecting host ribosomal RNAs, and RNase H/ASO mapping has confirmed its intracellular engagement of the predicted binding sites; its intricate scaffold contains multiple phenyl rings, amide bonds, and urea groups, terminated by several 4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, structural analysis indicates that this molecule bears at least six imidazolinyl groups, making it a potent hexacationic system, this structural design is an extreme example used to construct supramolecular nucleic acid binders, where the molecular length, rigidity, and multiple charges enable high-affinity interactions with multiple sites on the DNA helix, including the minor groove, major groove, or phosphate backbone, and such compounds are often investigated as non-viral gene delivery vectors (by charge-neutralizing DNA), highly effective anticancer agents, or for use as DNA structural probes, aiming for extremely high binding stability and cytotoxicity.

Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 1-methyl-2-(2-phenylethenyl)quinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 9, is the structurally simple 1-methyl-2-styrylquinolin-1-ium dye, and Compound 9 is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a simple styryl group at the 2-position (no 4-position amine substitution), synthesized via general procedure B, this cationic conjugated system forms the basis of many cyanine dyes, due to its planar structure and positive charge, it is used as a basic DNA intercalator or a non-specific nuclear stain in biological research, as well as an active material in organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs), it binds ADAM10 mRNAs QGRS very weakly and shows negligible translational up-regulation (dual-luciferase assay: 101.1 activity vs control), lacking the 4-methylpiperazine motif and having an unsubstituted styryl group, it fails to form optimal interactions with the RNA G-quadruplex, exhibiting no robust activity in enhancing ADAM10 expression. RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propoxy]phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one, also referred to as Phenylquinolone 2, is a 2-phenylquinolone derivative with N1-methyl (RMe), 5-methoxy (ROMe), 7-methoxy (ROMe) on the quinolone nucleus, and an O-propyl (n3, XO) side chain linked to an N-methylpiperazinyl head, it is synthesized via sequential reactions including nucleophilic displacement, can inhibit HIV-1 TatTAR interaction but shows low cellular uptake, and has a side chain terminal amine group that is 4-methylpiperazine; piperazine is a diamine (with two basic nitrogen atoms), a very successful universal drug scaffold in medicinal chemistry, the introduction of piperazine enhances the molecules water solubility and ability to bind to multiple targets, functionally, piperazine often imparts affinity for Central Nervous System (CNS) targets, and in practical applications, these types of compounds are often explored for the development of antidepressants, antipsychotics, or antimalarial drugs.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-chloro-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 8, is a heterocyclic amide based on the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine scaffold (a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with a chloro substitution at the C-6 position of its pyridine ring) that shares the overall structure and function with similar compounds. It contains the 3-amino, 2-carboxamide, and 3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl substituents; the key structural feature is the substitution of a chloro atom at position 6 of the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, which is a moderately sized halogen substituent that exerts an electron-withdrawing effect and some hydrophobicity, often used in drug design to replace bulkier groups (like phenyl) to optimize the spatial fit within the target binding pocket and adjust the bioavailability. The structure suggests this compound is a highly lipophilic small molecule kinase inhibitor or receptor antagonist, primarily investigated for applications in oncology and inflammatory diseases, and it shows no detectable binding to HIV-1 TAR RNA but binds HIV-1 RRE RNA (a key element for viral replication); targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with RRE RNA, it has moderate affinity and lacks further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity.

RNA information: The structure of MAPT pre-mRNA contains a hairpin structure located at the junction of exon 10 and intron, which is crucial for splicing regulation. Under normal circumstances, the hairpin structure is stable and helps maintain the correct splicing ratio of exon 10. However, certain mutations (such as C+14U) can disrupt the stability of hairpins, leading to excessive inclusion of exon 10 and subsequently the production of 4R-tau protein, which is associated with the neurodegenerative disease FTDP-17. The article points out that small molecule compounds can promote the excretion of exon 10 by binding to and stabilizing the mutated hairpin structure, thereby restoring the normal proportion of tau protein and having potential therapeutic effects.
Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-N-[2-[2-(diethylamino)ethylcarbamoyl]-1H-indol-6-yl]-1H-indole-2-carboxamide, also referred to as the compound with structural design similar to that of other compounds, is a complex bis-indole carboxamide structure, consisting of two indole cores linked by an amide bond, with one indole bearing a long side chain that terminates in a diethylaminoethyl group; however, no measurable binding to DDPAC RNA was detected in in vitro experiments, and correspondingly, in cell experiments, it had no significant effect on luciferase expression, indicating that it could not effectively bind to the target RNA and failed to regulate the splicing of exon 10, thus having basically no functional activity, while indole scaffolds are ubiquitous in natural products and drugs, often associated with neuroactivity, anti-inflammatory, and antineoplastic properties, this bis-indole structure suggests a strong potential for binding to biological macromolecules, such as DNA via stacking, or specific protein kinases, the terminal tertiary amine (diethylamino) is readily protonated at physiological pH, aiding in cellular uptake or lysosomal trapping, and in real-world applications, molecules like this compound are frequently investigated as kinase inhibitors or tubulin polymerization disruptors for cancer therapy.

The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)quinolin-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PDS cPDS, is a large, linear polyamine compound centered on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core, which links two substituted quinoline rings via amide bonds, with an additional substituted aminoethoxy group at the 4-position of the pyridine ring. The quinoline and pyridine moieties are nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics that contribute planarity and conjugation. The most significant feature is the presence of three 2-aminoethoxy substituents, introducing multiple primary amine groups that confer strong polycationic properties upon the molecule, making it highly protonated and positively charged at physiological pH. This multicationic structure and extended planar scaffold are common design elements for novel DNA/RNA binding agents, which interact with nucleic acids via electrostatic and intercalation mechanisms. Such compounds and their analogues are often explored for antiviral, antibacterial properties, and as novel non-viral gene transfection reagents or nucleic acid function inhibitors; additionally, PDS cPDS are potent and selective G-quadruplex-stabilizing small molecules that demonstrated excellent binding to the r(G4C2) G-quadruplex, however, they were ineffective at inhibiting RAN translation, providing key evidence that the G-quadruplex is not the therapeutically relevant structure for this disease mechanism.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: (3-amino-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridin-2-yl)-morpholin-4-ylmethanone, also referred to as Compound 14, is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with a morphinyl side chain modified from initial active analogs, containing thienopyridine and morpholine heterocyclic rings. Its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a morpholinylcarbonyl group at C2. In terms of biological function, thienopyridine compounds are known for their diverse pharmacological activities, including anticancer, antiviral, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial effects, and this compound binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, showing moderate affinity and no further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-(3-aminopropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one, also referred to as Phenylquinolone 2a (a quinolone derivative lacking the C-3 carboxylic group, with a phenyl ring attached at the C-2 position and a mono-O-propyl amine side chain (-NH-(CH)-NH) at the para position of the phenyl ring), has a side chain that is a 3-aminopropoxy group, terminating in a primary amine, and Phenylquinolone 2as amine side chain lacks a nitrogen-containing heterocycle which likely prevents optimal protonation state or spatial orientation, resulting in reduced binding efficiency to TAR RNA and limited activity, while the overall molecule has two primary amine groups (on the side chain and the phenyl ring of 2-[3-amino-4-(3-aminopropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one, and a mono-O-propyl amine side chain with amine groups on Phenylquinolone 2a), giving it dibasic properties, the introduction of primary amines allows it to act as a hydrogen bond donor, enhancing interaction with targets, and features minimal steric hindrance, functionally, this structure is often explored as a novel receptor ligand, particularly for binding certain G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), with potential application in the regulation of anti-inflammatory or neurotransmitter systems, and Phenylquinolone 2a can inhibit the formation of the Tat/TAR complex in vitro with moderate activity that is weaker than other derivatives in the series.

RNA information: (CUG)₁₆ is a synthetic RNA oligonucleotide mimicking pathogenic expanded (CUG)ₙ repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it folds into a stable A-form hairpin with repeating 5'CUG/3'GUC units, featuring U-U mismatches flanked by G-C base pairs. Functionally, it high-affinity binds Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1), sequestering it to disrupt alternative splicing (DM1’s core gain-of-function mechanism) and serving in vitro for cleavage assays to screen catalytic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: amino-[4-[amino-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butylazaniumylidene]methyl]phenyl]methylidene]-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]azanium, also referred to as a bisamidinium-derived molecule with added amine groups, is a massively complex, highly positively charged compound, likely a dicationic or polycationic dye or polyamine derivative; this compound binds both DNA and RNA CTG/CUG repeats, potently inhibits transcription, cleaves (CUG) selectively (not CCUG), and is highly effective in reversing DM phenotypes with low toxicity, while its core features two Triazine units each linked to a polyamine tail, the central azaniumylidene and methylidene groups point to a highly stabilized guanidinium or amidinium derivative, conferring high basicity and charge, and due to its multiple positive charges and hydrogen bond donors, the molecule is researched as a highly efficient gene transfection reagent (for delivering DNA/RNA into cells) or as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting by disrupting the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane.

RNA information: Lactobacillus plantarum 100yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, and its structural characteristics and functional mechanisms show significant diversity and specificity in regulating gene expression. It is composed of 100 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures: P1, P2, P3 and P4
Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol, also referred to as Amodiaquine Di HCl, is a quinoline derivative structured around a quinoline core, with a chloro substitution at the 7-position and an amino link at the 4-position to a benzene ring containing a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group (molecular formula C20H22ClN3O), and it is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory effects that shares mechanisms with chloroquine but has higher toxicity risks, limiting its use despite efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains; this compound possesses a planar conjugated system and multiple functional groups (chloro, amine, phenol, quinoline), functionally, the quinoline core is famous as the scaffold for well-known antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine), and derivatives are commonly investigated for broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antimalarial, and anti-cancer activities, the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups suggests potential for metal complexation or binding to enzyme active sites.

RNA information: XIST is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) crucial for X-chromosome inactivation in female mammalian cells. It functions by coating the future inactive X chromosome and recruiting repressive complexes to silence gene expression. Structurally, XIST contains a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) motif composed of only two G-tetrads, making it less stable than canonical three-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies, such as reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, confirm its ability to form an rG4 structure, showing approximately 14% stalling in potassium buffer. Notably, specific G4 ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, significantly enhancing the formation and stability of the XIST rG4 structure under unfavorable ionic conditions, highlighting its dynamic nature and regulatory potential.
Small_molecule_information: N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-piperidin-1-ylpropanamide, also referred to as Braco-19, is an acridine analog of N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanamide, replacing pyrrolidine with a piperidine side chain, it is an acridine-derived G4 ligand with a tricyclic acridine core modified by cationic side chains, enabling selective binding to G-quadruplexes (G4s) via -stacking, can stabilize pre-formed RNA G4s (rG4s) with moderate affinity but lacks robust chaperone activity for rG4 folding especially for non-canonical 2-quartet rG4s (e.g., XIST, RPPH1), weakly induces tetramolecular rG4 assembly and does not significantly enhance reverse transcriptase stalling in Li buffers, making it suitable for stabilizing existing rG4s rather than promoting de novo folding, and is explored as a potential acridine-based anticancer drug with possible altered pharmacokinetics and selective cytotoxicity.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-diethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine diiodide is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline analog where the piperidinium is replaced by a morpholinium quaternary ammonium salt. This fused quinoline core is a common scaffold in medicinal chemistry, primarily screened for cytotoxic activity against various cancer cell lines.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 6,7-dimethyl-1H-pteridine-2,4-dione is an aromatic heterocyclic compound containing a pteridine structure. Its structure is based on the pteridine parent skeleton: two benzene rings are fused with two pyrimidine rings to form the pteridine skeleton, which is substituted with keto groups at positions 2 and 4, and methyl groups at positions 6 and 7. In terms of biological function, pteridine compounds are well-known and attract attention in medicinal chemistry for their potential anticancer and diverse pharmacological features.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: The r(CUG)₆ oligonucleotide is a key in vitro model used to study the pathogenic CUG repeat expansion. Its repetitive sequence forms a stable stem-loop structure characterized by canonical GC base pairs and non-canonical, dynamic UU mismatches. This unique structural motif is the primary binding site for both the Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein and the designed small molecule ligands. The RNA was synthetically labeled with a TAMRA fluorophore at its 5' end for binding assays. In these assays, ligand binding to the UU mismatches induces a structural change that quenches the TAMRA fluorescence, allowing the direct measurement of binding affinity (K_D), thus establishing r(CUG)₆ as a critical tool for quantifying ligand-RNA interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 12, is an extremely large, bifunctional molecule whose core structure consists of two acridine rings and is a dimeric ligand with an oligoether linker, its binding affinity was close to that of the monomer, it showed a small bivalent effect and no melting temperature data could be determined for this compound; Acridine is a tricyclic planar aromatic system, the parent structure of famous DNA intercalators, the two acridine units are linked by a flexible tetraethylene glycol (PEG-like) spacer, which enhances the molecules water solubility and biological flexibility, each acridine ring also carries a triazine-butane amine side chain, introducing additional basicity and nucleic acid binding capacity, the structure of this compound represents a classic dimerized drug design, aimed at enhancing its affinity for DNA and cytotoxicity through double intercalation or multivalency effects, in practice, this compound is researched as a potent anti-cancer agent, especially against multi-drug resistant (MDR) cell lines, or as a highly specific fluorescent probe.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6: This is a six-repeat control RNA with perfect Watson-Crick base pairing. It is used to evaluate the specificity of compound binding and shows minimal structural perturbation upon ligand interaction, confirming that small molecules do not significantly bind to regular duplex RNA.; The (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6 sequence serves as the extended control duplex. Its repetitive yet perfectly paired structure lacks the distinctive features of disease-repeat RNA, allowing researchers to conclusively prove that compound efficacy is due to specific recognition of the pathological mismatch motif and not general RNA binding.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[(4-methylanilino)methyl]naphthalen-2-ol, also referred to as CP3, is a naphthol-containing compound whose structure is based on the naphthol parent skeleton: a benzene ring is fused with another benzene ring to form the naphthalene skeleton, which is substituted with a hydroxyl group at position 2 and a (4-methylanilino)methyl group at position 1, and this amphiphilic structure built around a naphthalene core with a hydroxyl group connected via a methylene bridge to an amino group and a 4-methylphenyl ring allowed moderate RNA binding, but it was not among the most potent compounds in biological assays; in terms of biological function, naphthalenes and their derivatives generally exhibit diverse biological activities such as antifungal, anti-inflammatory, or are used as fluorescent probes.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 9-ethyl-3-[2-(1-methyl-4-piperidin-1-ylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 28 (a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 2-(2-(9-ethyl-9H-carbazol-3-yl)vinyl) group at the quinoline 2-position and a 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperidin-1-yl group at the 4-position), is a structurally critical compound in the carbazole-quinoline series because the quinoline nitrogen atom is N-methylated, resulting in a permanently positively charged quinolin-1-ium cation; SPR assay reveals that Compound 28s affinity for A10-RNA-Wt is lower than that of Compound 24, it was not examined in the dual-luciferase reporter assay so its influence on ADAM10 translation is unreported, and no clear cytotoxicity data (IC values) is provided for it in the tested compound tables, while this permanent charge of the compound is a dominant feature, significantly enhancing the molecules electrostatic interaction with negatively charged biological structures, notably the mitochondrial membrane potential and the DNA phosphate backbone; in reality, this class of cationic dyes is well-known as selective mitochondrial probes, and 9-ethyl-3-[2-(1-methyl-4-piperidin-1-ylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]carbazole is therefore likely employed as a mitochondrial function tracer or a mitochondria-targeting cytotoxic agent to selectively induce apoptosis in cancer cells by disrupting their energy supply.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride This molecule incorporates a morpholine group linked via a propyl chain to the quaternary ammonium core. Morpholine, a heterocycle containing both an ether and a tertiary amine, is typically introduced to increase hydrophilicity and metabolic stability. In drug discovery, morpholine derivatives are often used as kinase inhibitors and signal transduction pathway modulators, particularly in anticancer drug development. Its overall amphiphilic structure might also enable it to function as an antifungal or antiparasitic agent by interacting with the pathogens cell membranes or intracellular lipid structures. The oxygen atom on the morpholine ring provides a hydrogen bond acceptor site, enhancing binding to specific protein targets.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-diethyl-N-[2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine, also referred to as the indolo[3,2-b]quinoline with the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and an N1,N1-diethylpropane-1,3-diamine side chain at the 11-position, is an indoloquinoline derivative based on the indolo[3,2-b]quinoline scaffold, with the molecular formula C31H34N4S, this compound exhibits a planar conjugated system, and its rigid polycyclic structure combined with a long diamine side chain makes it a classic DNA intercalator, it can bind to the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex and has moderate anti-proliferative activity in A375 melanoma cells, in biological function, indoloquinoline alkaloids (like Cryptolepine) are renowned anti-cancer, antimalarial, and antibacterial agents, whose mechanism involves DNA intercalation and Topoisomerase II inhibition, and this compound is a potential anti-cancer or antiparasitic drug candidate.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₀ sequence represents a pathogenic-length model for expanded CUG repeats and folds into a stable hairpin with regularly spaced U–U mismatches. Structurally, it provides multiple equivalent binding sites, enabling one ligand to associate with each trinucleotide repeat and producing cooperative binding. This repeat length captures essential features of toxic RNA found in myotonic dystrophy, including the ability to sequester MBNL1 and form nuclear foci. Functionally, r(CUG)₁₀ is the most relevant in vitro construct for evaluating selective small-molecule recognition, since its length allows the manifestation of cooperative interactions that mimic disease-relevant RNA–protein complexes.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-2-(propylamino)propanoyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 6 (2012), is the (Z)-geometric isomer (cis) of the oligo-peptide analogue (a Z-isomer lacking the benzo[g]quinoline moiety that was designed as a control to evaluate the importance of this heteroaromatic group), meaning it possesses the same connectivity but the alkene bond within the core chain has a different spatial arrangement; this minor change in geometry dictates a profound difference in the molecules overall three-dimensional shape and flexibility, and additionally, without the benzo[g]quinoline moiety, the compound maintains only weak interactions with RNA and loses significant selectivity, which confirms that the quinoline system is essential for high-affinity and discriminating recognition of repeat motifs, structurally it emphasizes the critical contribution of aromatic stacking in RNA binding, and functionally it has no significant biological activity, underscoring its role as a negative control and supporting the rationale for incorporating benzo[g]quinoline in active derivatives; in real-world drug discovery, geometric isomerism is a critical factor, as biological targets are highly sensitive to ligand shape; therefore, (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-2-(propylamino)propanoyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide (also referred to as DCC 6 (2012)) is expected to exhibit drastically different biological activity, potency, and target selectivity compared to its (E)-isomer, potentially acting on entirely distinct biological pathways, and is essential for structure-activity relationship studies.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₀ sequence represents a pathogenic-length model for expanded CUG repeats and folds into a stable hairpin with regularly spaced U–U mismatches. Structurally, it provides multiple equivalent binding sites, enabling one ligand to associate with each trinucleotide repeat and producing cooperative binding. This repeat length captures essential features of toxic RNA found in myotonic dystrophy, including the ability to sequester MBNL1 and form nuclear foci. Functionally, r(CUG)₁₀ is the most relevant in vitro construct for evaluating selective small-molecule recognition, since its length allows the manifestation of cooperative interactions that mimic disease-relevant RNA–protein complexes.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-2,7-bis[[(2S)-6-amino-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]hexanoyl]amino]-N-[(2S)-1-(2-aminoethylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N-[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N,N-dipropyloct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 8 (2012), is an asymmetric peptidomimetic with a cis (Z) double bond in its core eight-carbon chain, which forces the molecule into a rigid bent conformation; while DCC 8 (2012) contains the benzo[g]quinoline unit, it features a scrambled peptide sequence that alters the relative spatial positioning of functional groups, a design that tests whether the orientation of the heteroaromatic group and backbone is essential for recognition, and though the compound can interact with RNA, its selectivity is poor and its affinity reduced compared to ordered analogs, structurally demonstrating that sequence context is crucial for cooperative interactions and functionally failing to show strong biological rescue, thus serving as a control that validates the need for precise arrangement of components to achieve productive recognition and therapeutic activity, and this specific cis geometry of the main peptidomimetic is crucial for this molecule with asymmetric polyamine side chains (two- and three-carbon), as it determines the relative spatial position and distance of the two different side chains, thereby influencing target interaction, with both sides of the molecule also linked to benzo[g]quinoline-lysine groups, and it is a key molecule for studying the effect of conformational locking on the activity of asymmetric multifunctional molecules, whose target activity remains DNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, but whose activity is expected to exhibit unique selectivity due to its characteristic Z-conformation.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).

In vitro, 2HE-5NMe binds r(CGG)12 with high affinity (Kd 5065 nM), increases RNA thermal stability, and selectively disrupts r(CGG)protein interactions. These findings establish 2HE-5NMe as a promising chemical probe and potential therapeutic lead for FXTAS and related repeat-expansion disorders.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.

This class of compounds is often obtained through chemical synthesis or structural modification of natural flavonoids. In pharmaceutical chemistry research, oxime derivatives of flavonoids are often explored for metal ion chelation or as enzyme inhibitors. This specific molecule and its analogues are studied for their potential antioxidant and antitumor activities, for instance, by inhibiting certain tyrosine kinases or acting as neuroprotective agents. Such oxime-containing molecules are also sometimes used in drug chemistry to improve lipophilicity or alter metabolic stability, though the presence of the oxime in this compound likely contributed to a very high Kd2 value, indicating poor or non-specific binding and disqualifying it as a candidate.

It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.

RNA information: The structure of MAPT pre-mRNA contains a hairpin structure located at the junction of exon 10 and intron, which is crucial for splicing regulation. Under normal circumstances, the hairpin structure is stable and helps maintain the correct splicing ratio of exon 10. However, certain mutations (such as C+14U) can disrupt the stability of hairpins, leading to excessive inclusion of exon 10 and subsequently the production of 4R-tau protein, which is associated with the neurodegenerative disease FTDP-17. The article points out that small molecule compounds can promote the excretion of exon 10 by binding to and stabilizing the mutated hairpin structure, thereby restoring the normal proportion of tau protein and having potential therapeutic effects.

Indole compounds are widely found in natural products and possess various biological activities. The amidine groups impart a potential for nucleic acid binding, and the molecule is often developed as an antiprotozoal and antiviral agent. Bis-amidine-indole compounds with similar structures have been studied for the treatment of tropical diseases like African trypanosomiasis or as DNA minor groove binding probes.

The proteins encoded by these genes are responsible for the synthesis and salvage pathways of thiamine precursors HMP-PP and Thz-P. Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 7-methoxy-1-methyl-9H-pyrido[3,4-b]indole, also referred to as Harmine, is a planar azaaromatic -carboline alkaloid with a fused tricyclic core (indole and pyridine rings, containing multiple nitrogen atoms) and a methoxy group at the 7-position that belongs to the -carboline alkaloid family, it binds to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) via non-specific interactions but with lower affinity than potent ligands like chelerythrine and fails to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, while -carbolines are often studied for significant Central Nervous System (CNS) activities, including antidepressant, anxiolytic, and neuroprotective effects, and it is a potential Monoamine Oxidase (MAO) inhibitor which also has the biological activity of inhibiting monoamine oxidase, aiding in studying riboswitch ligand structure-activity relationships and being relevant for treating neurodegenerative disorders like Parkinsons disease.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]propan-1-amine This molecule features a 3-aminopropoxy chain connected via an ether linkage. This structure, containing a primary amine, also functions as a dicationic species (quaternary core plus protonated primary amine) under physiological conditions. Compounds with the propylamine backbone are often studied as Histamine H2 receptor antagonists or antidepressants. Its structure gives it the potential for strong electrostatic interactions with negatively charged cellular components (like DNA) or the phospholipid membrane, which may confer antimicrobial activity or utility as a gene transfection aid.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) commonly in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing, underpinning disease pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-8-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-[1,2,4]triazolo[4,3-a]quinoline, also referred to as SMSM60, is a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge, and is characterized by a unique tetracyclic aromatic system: the [1,2,4]triazolo[4,3-a]quinoline core, this fused heterocycle is rich in nitrogen atoms, giving it distinct electronic properties, the compound has an 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl group at C4 and a positively charged quaternized (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium moiety at C8, it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1, driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, and triazoloquinoline derivatives are prominent in medicinal chemistry, particularly in the central nervous system (CNS) drug space, including agents for anxiety and insomnia, therefore, 4-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-8-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-[1,2,4]triazolo[4,3-a]quinoline is likely investigated as a potential neurotransmitter receptor modulator, such as a GABA receptor ligand, or for applications in cardiovascular diseases, with its cationic nature influencing its brain distribution or interaction with specific CNS targets.

RNA information: (CUG)₁₆ is a synthetic RNA oligonucleotide mimicking pathogenic expanded (CUG)ₙ repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it folds into a stable A-form hairpin with repeating 5'CUG/3'GUC units, featuring U-U mismatches flanked by G-C base pairs. Functionally, it high-affinity binds Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1), sequestering it to disrupt alternative splicing (DM1’s core gain-of-function mechanism) and serving in vitro for cleavage assays to screen catalytic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 4-N-[4-[[4-amino-6-[3-[4-[2-[[4-amino-6-[4-[[4-[N-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butyl]carbamimidoyl]benzenecarboximidoyl]amino]butylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]ethyl]triazol-1-yl]propylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]-1-N-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 2b, is a dimeric ligand structurally analogous to 2a but featuring a slightly longer linker, it exhibits a very similar high potency to 2a for inhibiting the MBNL1-rCUGexp complex, despite its comparable in vitro efficacy, it was not selected for further cellular and animal studies due to its higher synthetic cost compared to 2a, and this is an extremely large, structurally complex oligomer or conjugate incorporating multiple functional units: a benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide core, several triazine rings, multiple butane amine linkers, and a triazole ring, often introduced via click chemistry, the triazine and imidamide groups are strongly basic, making this molecule a highly charged cationic species with powerful electrostatic interaction capability, this multi-fragment complexity strongly suggests a design as a multivalent ligand, intended to bind simultaneously to multiple sites on a biological target or act as a drug delivery system, in chemical biology, structures containing triazine and imidamide are often studied as sequence-specific nucleic acid binders or potent metabolic enzyme inhibitors, with the triazole ring typically serving as a stable, bio-inert connecting unit.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine.
