It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: Quinoline, also referred to as an azaaromatic compound with a planar hydrophobic benzo[b]pyridine scaffold that interacts non-specifically with the yjdF motif RNA and shows minimal activation of riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, is one of the most important nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocycles formed by the fusion of a benzene ring with a pyridine ring with the molecular formula, and it is a colorless hygroscopic liquid with a strong odor whose skeleton forms the core of many natural products such as cinchona alkaloids and synthetic drugs such as chloroquine and quinolone antibiotics, with quinoline derivatives widely used in the synthesis of dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals and exhibiting diverse biological activities including antibacterial, antimalarial, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer effects.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-[7-[2-(dimethylamino)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as the saturated analog of DAPI (whose full name is 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide), is a compound featuring a dihydroindole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, with the formula C16H16N4, while DAPI is a fluorescent stain binding AT-rich DNA regions, widely used in microscopy for nuclear labeling and is cell-impermeant unless membranes are compromised, making it useful for viability assays and chromosome visualization; this compound is the saturated analog of 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, the saturation of the indole ring removes aromaticity and significantly increases the flexibility and changes the 3D shape of the molecule, this structural change dramatically influences its binding mode and affinity for the DNA minor groove, and this compound is investigated to explore the impact of molecular rigidity/flexibility on the activity of DNA-binding drugs, showing potential for anticancer and anti-infective activity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[(2R,5S)-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-oxathiolan-5-yl]pyrimidin-2-one, also referred to as Lamivudine (3TC) or its enantiomer, is a synthetic cytidine nucleoside analog where the 3 oxygen atom of the ribose ring is replaced by a sulfur atom, forming a 1,3-oxathiolane sugar ring, molecular formula C8H11N3O3S; it is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) that incorporates into viral DNA, terminating its synthesis, and in terms of real-world activity, Lamivudine is an extremely important antiviral drug, classified as a Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitor (NRTI), which is widely used clinically for the treatment of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) infection and Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) infection, with its mechanism of action involving being phosphorylated intracellularly to its active triphosphate form, which is then incorporated into the viral DNA chain, causing chain termination and effectively inhibiting the activity of the HIV reverse transcriptase and HBV polymerase.

RNA information: r(GGGGCC)₈ is an RNA repeat sequence that forms a structured RNA motif capable of binding small molecules. It adopts both hairpin and G-quadruplex conformations and plays a pathogenic role in c9FTD/ALS by undergoing repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation and forming nuclear RNA foci. Small molecules targeting r(GGGGCC)₈ can inhibit these toxic events, suggesting its potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 5-amino-N-[2-[2-(diethylamino)ethylcarbamoyl]-1H-indol-5-yl]-1H-indole-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 3, is a bis-indole carboxamide derivative, containing two indole rings linked by an amide bond, and featuring a diethylaminoethyl side chain (molecular formula C24H28N6O2), while Compound 3 itself is a synthetic, RNA-directed small molecule optimized to engage the r(GGGGCC) hairpin that drives C9ORF72-linked ALS/FTD, retaining the minimal 4-aminoquinoline/arylcarboxamide core required for GG-loop recognition but replacing the flexible alkyl linker with a rigid 1,3-phenylene-bis-acetamide bridge that pre-organizes the ligand for bidentate binding to two adjacent 11 GG internal loops, removes basic nitrogens to lower off-target protein binding, and adds a terminal primary amine for facile conjugation to RNase-recruiting modules; this compound possesses a conjugated system and multiple hydrogen bond donors/acceptors, and in biological function, many Di-indole derivatives, particularly those linked by amides, are analogous to natural Polyamides and are often studied as DNA minor groove binding agents, showing potential anti-tumor, antiviral, or antifungal activities, meanwhile Compound 3 can bind to r(GGGGCC), stabilize its hairpin structure, exhibit selectivity over DNA G-quadruplexes, produce a dose-dependent drop in poly(GP) RAN-translation products and reduce nuclear RNA foci in c9ALS patient lymphoblastoid cells without altering total C9orf72 mRNA (confirming a purely post-transcriptional mode of action), and has negligible cytotoxicity as well as validated compatibility with RIBOTAC derivatization, making it a versatile chemical scaffold for both probe and therapeutic development against c9ALS/FTD.

Small_molecule_information: 7,8-dimethyl-10-(2,3,4,5-tetrahydroxypentyl)benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also known as vitamin B2 and also referred to as Riboflavin, is an important water-soluble vitamin whose structure consists of an isoalloxazine aromatic ring and a ribitol side chain, adopting a flat yet slightly flexible molecular configuration; it serves as a precursor for flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD), two molecules that act as coenzymes in various redox reactions in organisms, playing a crucial role in cellular energy metabolism and biosynthetic processes, and as a weak competitive ligand, riboflavin can enter the aptamer pocket of bacterial FMN riboswitches, and although its affinity is lower than that of FMN, it can still induce conformational changes in RNA. In the article, the binding ability of riboflavin to the FMN riboswitch was studied, revealing its role in regulating gene expression: riboflavin can bind to the FMN riboswitch, thereby regulating the expression of genes related to riboflavin biosynthesis and transport, and partially inhibiting the expression of downstream riboflavin synthesis genes, thereby participating in the negative feedback regulation of its own biosynthesis; when riboflavin or its derivative FMN binds to the riboswitch, it induces the formation of a transcription termination structure, thereby inhibiting the expression of downstream genes, a mechanism that is vital for maintaining the homeostasis of riboflavin levels in cells.

RNA information: 5'CCG/3'GCC is a 1×1 CG internal-loop RNA motif of the 5'CNG/3'GNC family, with a short stem loop (5'CCG/3'GCC), no U·U mismatches, G-C/C-G pairing, high stability, and low "breathing effects". Pathogenically linked to FXTAS (via mutant FMR1 mRNA’s r(CCG) repeats), it acts as a disease-specific RNA switch, recruiting proteins/ligands (e.g., diguanidinobenzoates) to modulate splicing/RAN translation. It also serves as an experimental control for RNA-small molecule binding specificity.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 20 (DAPI, 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), is a compound containing an indole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, one imidamide directly attached to the indole ring and the other attached via a phenyl ring at the C2 position of indole, with the formula C16H14N4, and it is also a commercially available guanidino/amidino-containing compound, a routine nuclear stain and chemical progenitor of derivative D6; this indole diamidine combines the lipophilicity of indole with the cationic nature of the diamidine groups, and it can bind influenza A RNA promoter (causes NMR perturbations), AT-rich dsDNA minor groove, and r(CAG)exps 5CAG/3GAC loop, disrupt r(CAG)expMBN1, correct splicing defects in Huntingtons/ataxia type 3 models though it has cytotoxicity that limits direct optimization, this combination also results in an effective DNA minor groove binder with potent antimicrobial and antitumor activity, and it is researched for developing new anticancer agents with high target specificity and enhanced pharmacokinetic profiles.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[2-(4-fluoro-3-methoxyphenyl)ethenyl]-1-methylquinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 10, is a quinolinium quaternary salt, linked at the 2-position via a vinyl bridge to a fluorinated and methoxylated phenyl ring, and Compound 10 is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 4-fluoro-3-methoxystyryl group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring, synthesized via general procedure B, this forms a large, conjugated cationic structure, in real activity, such conjugated quaternary salts are studied as potential fluorescent dyes for bioimaging, the cationic nature also lends itself to cytotoxicity and antimicrobial activity, likely through electrostatic interactions and intercalation with nucleic acids, it binds the G-quadruplex-forming sequence (QGRS) in ADAM10 mRNAs 5-UTR with moderate affinity and low interaction with mutant QGRS, however, it shows weak ability to up-regulate ADAM10 translation, lacking robust activity in dual-luciferase assays, likely due to suboptimal structural for RNA G-quadruplex modulation.

RNA information: 5′UAG/3′A_C (SL I): The UAG/A_C motif is located within HCV 3′ UTR stem–loop I. Identified in the Inforna analysis as a ligandable element, it was enriched in binding assays, indicating its potential as a structural determinant for small molecule recognition and functional viral RNA inhibition.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[3-[[2,5-bis[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]carbamoylamino]phenyl]carbamoylamino]-1-N,4-N-bis[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7, is an extremely large, highly branched polycationic compound, and it can selectively bind internal loop motifs such as 5UUA/3A_U and 5UGU/3G_A with submicromolar affinity, with structural studies revealing that its binding is driven by recognition of asymmetric bulges within RNA stems, it can directly interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II by targeting motifs present in viral RNA, functional assays have demonstrated that it inhibits HCV replication in cell culture without affecting host ribosomal RNAs, and RNase H/ASO mapping has confirmed its intracellular engagement of the predicted binding sites; its intricate scaffold contains multiple phenyl rings, amide bonds, and urea groups, terminated by several 4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, structural analysis indicates that this molecule bears at least six imidazolinyl groups, making it a potent hexacationic system, this structural design is an extreme example used to construct supramolecular nucleic acid binders, where the molecular length, rigidity, and multiple charges enable high-affinity interactions with multiple sites on the DNA helix, including the minor groove, major groove, or phosphate backbone, and such compounds are often investigated as non-viral gene delivery vectors (by charge-neutralizing DNA), highly effective anticancer agents, or for use as DNA structural probes, aiming for extremely high binding stability and cytotoxicity.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: 5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 1f, is a pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole derivative (the 1a-derived bis-benzimidazole series member) with the tetracyclic aromatic heterocycle pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole as its parent structure: this compound lacks the hydroxyl group at position 9 but retains the methyl groups at positions 5 and 11 (while keeping the 9-hydroxy-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazolium core but being stripped of the cationic piperidinium side chain and carrying only a neutral 2-hydroxyethyl tail), making it a neutral tetracyclic aromatic molecule with the molecular formula C19H16N2, and this single structural change abolishes its high-affinity binding to the pathogenic 5CGG/3GGC internal loops of the r(CGG)exp RNA hairpin, eliminates detectable disruption of the toxic r(CGG)12DGCR8 complex, results in no rescue of SMN2 or Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing and no reduction in r(CGG)exp nuclear foci in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells at relevant concentrations, while its lack of measurable activity, low cytotoxicity and easy synthesis make it a useful negative-control probe for validating the absolute requirement of a permanent positive charge in next-generation ligands aimed at CGG-repeat disorders, and as a fundamental structure of ellipticine derivatives, it may retain the potential for DNA intercalation and topoisomerase inhibition activity or serve as a precursor for more active derivatives.

RNA information: OI-guaA refers to a guanine riboswitch from Oceanobacillus iheyensis. The structural features of this ribose switch include a specific RNA secondary structure, which is usually composed of three stem-ring regions (P1, P2 and P3), and these regions are organized around a three-way junction point, forming the guanine binding site. The function of OI-guaA is to specifically recognize and bind guanine through its aptamer region, thereby regulating gene expression. When guanine binds to the aptamer region, it triggers a rearrangement of RNA structure. This structural change may lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of downstream genes. This regulatory mechanism of the OI-guaA ribose switch enables it to respond rapidly when the intracellular guanine concentration changes, thereby playing a key role in the metabolic regulation and gene expression of bacteria.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: (2R)-6-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]hexanamide, also referred to as 2H-K4NPr, is an ultra-large oligomer that shares the identical chemical name and structural description as (2R)-6-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]hexanamide, it represents the same benzimidazole-poly-alanine-lysine complex derivative, with the active unit linked by four repeating alanine-amide units to the lysine backbone, this emphasizes its intended role as a long-chain, high-affinity, multivalent DNA minor groove binder, it is the most effective lysine-based NPr derivative, and although its biochemical potency is only moderate, it showed the strongest cellular performance in splicing correction assays together with good stability and low toxicity, its combination of such properties made it one of the top candidates for therapeutic development, and its predicted real-world activity is that of a highly potent anti-cancer or anti-fungal agent, exerting cytotoxicity by disrupting nucleic acid function, particularly DNA replication and repair processes.

RNA information: TCT8-4 has two conserved regions (5'-AUACCA-3' and 5'-CCUUGG(C/A)AG-3') . It binds theophylline  in a Mg-dependent way, showing 10,900-fold higher affinity for theophylline than caffeine. It aids in diagnosing diseases like asthma/bronchitis, where theophylline is used as a bronchodilator, by distinguishing theophylline from similar compounds.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(3-methyl-2,6-dioxo-7H-purin-1-yl)propanoic acid, also referred to as CP-theophylline (8-(3-carboxypropyl)-theophylline), is a xanthine derivative that lacks widely documented specific real-world activity, often used in drug design as an intermediate for synthesizing new drug analogs, and it is a theophylline derivative in which a 3-carboxypropyl tether is attached to N-8 of the xanthine ring, which was synthesized in a 1994 Science paper to couple theophylline to Sepharose for SELEX affinity columns, with the carboxylic arm providing a convenient linker while the rest of the molecule preserves the 1,3-dimethyl-xanthine core, and TCT8-4 RNA recognizes it almost as well as theophylline itself, indicating the N-8 substituent is solvent-exposed and does not perturb the critical contacts (N-7 H-bond, N-3 methyl hydrophobic pocket, C-2/C-6 carbonyls) that underlie high-affinity, high-specificity binding.

RNA information: ATP-54-1 is an active RNA molecule with a length of 54 nucleotides, obtained by analyzing the 5' and 3' deletions of the ATP-binding RNA clone C8-ATP-3. Structurally, its core feature is an 11-nucleotide consensus sequence flanked by base-paired stems (the deletion of the left-hand stem directly abolishes its ATP-binding activity), and all clones containing this structure have an invariant unpaired G opposite the 11-nucleotide consensus sequence, which can fold into a specific secondary structure as a whole. Functionally, it binds ATP with high affinity, with a dissociation constant (Kd) of less than 50 μM, and a conformational change of RNA occurs when binding to ATP (confirmed by kethoxal modification experiments). It exhibits specificity for ATP binding—modification or removal of groups such as the 2' hydroxyl group on ATP and the 6-amino group of adenine significantly reduces binding affinity, and it also serves as an important basis for the subsequent design of the shorter 40-nucleotide ATP-binding RNA (ATP-40-1)
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3R,4S,5S)-3,4-dihydroxy-5-purin-9-yloxolan-2-yl]dihydrogenphosphate, also referred to as Adenosine Monophosphate (AMP), with the chemical structure C10H13N5O7P, is a typical nucleotide molecule. The compound is composed of a purine nucleobase (specifically 1 molecule of adenine), a ribose (oxolan ring), and 1 molecule of phosphate group, linked via the characteristic N-glycosidic bond and phosphate ester bond (additionally connected by a phosphodiester bond), bearing high stereochemical similarity to natural nucleotides like Guanosine Monophosphate (GMP); compared with ATP, it lacks 2 phosphate groups and has no high-energy phosphate bonds, making it one of the basic units of RNA. Nucleotides are the fundamental building blocks of all living organisms, participating in essential life processes such as energy storage (ATP), nucleic acid synthesis (DNA/RNA), and cell signaling (cAMP/cGMP), and for this compound specifically, functionally it mainly serves as a precursor for nucleotide synthesis: it can be phosphorylated to form ADP (adenosine diphosphate), which is further converted into ATP, participating in the substrate cycle of cellular energy metabolism, meanwhile it is an important component of RNA, involved in the transmission and expression of genetic information, and like ATP and adenosine, it can effectively elute specifically bound RNAs from ATP-agarose columns, demonstrating that its adenosine moiety (the core structure shared with ATP) is key for RNA binding, while the number of phosphate groups has little impact on this binding process; thus, if this compound is the natural nucleotide itself, it performs its endogenous physiological functions within the cell; if it is a synthetic analog, it would be designed as an inhibitor or substrate for purine metabolism enzymes or reverse transcriptase, primarily used for antiviral or anti-cancer therapy by being incorporated into nucleic acid chains to cause chain termination.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: FN-purQ refers to a ribose switch from the genus Fusobacterium nucleatum, which is capable of recognizing and binding guanine. Although the article does not provide detailed structural information about FN-purQ, based on the general characteristics of ribose switches, we can infer that FN-purQ may have a secondary structure composed of stem-ring regions, which are organized around a three-way junction point to form guanine binding sites. Functionally, FN-purQ regulates gene expression by binding to guanine in its aptamer region. This combination may cause rearrangement of RNA structure, thereby affecting the termination or continuation of transcription and thus controlling the expression of downstream genes. However, according to the description in the article, FN-purQ exhibited a relatively low affinity in the 2-aminopine (2AP) binding experiment, which might imply that its affinity for guanine is also relatively low, or that its binding site is different from other known guanine riboswitches.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(2-phenylethyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride This complex quaternary ammonium salt features a rigid, highly conjugated pentacyclic core and a lipophilic N-(2-phenylethyl)amine side chain. The molecules large, positively charged structure and its amphiphilic nature (hydrophilic cation head, hydrophobic phenylethyl tail) suggest strong interactions with biological membranes and negatively charged biopolymers like DNA. Real-world applications for compounds with this skeleton often focus on developing broad-spectrum antimicrobial or antifungal agents that disrupt microbial cell integrity, or as DNA intercalators/Topoisomerase inhibitors in anticancer research. The phenylethyl moiety also hints at potential neuro-modulatory activity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (7S,9S)-7-[(2R,4S,5R,6S)-4-amino-5-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-6,9,11-trihydroxy-9-(2-hydroxyacetyl)-4-methoxy-8,10-dihydro-7H-tetracene-5,12-dione, also referred to as Epirubicin, is the chemical name for, or a very close analogue of, the anthracycline antibiotic Epirubicin, which is an anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent that intercalates into DNA, inhibiting topoisomerase II and causing DNA strand breaks; Epirubicin is a widely used chemotherapy drug in clinical practice for treating various cancers such as breast cancer and ovarian cancer, and its mechanism involves DNA intercalation and Topoisomerase II inhibition.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(7-fluoroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl)-7-piperazin-4-ium-1-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C30, is a mono-cationic coumarin derivative combining the coumarin scaffold with a 7-fluoro-substituted imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine, with the molecular formula C20H18FN4O2, and it is a non-planar coumarin analog with a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system that carries a 1 charge (the positive charge is on the piperazin-4-ium group at position 7, and a fluoro atom is at position 7 of the imidazopyridine ring). Fluorine is a common substituent in drug chemistry used to enhance metabolic stability and modulate molecular lipophilicity; its small size but high electronegativity can fine-tune the electron density of the entire conjugated system, thereby affecting its fluorescence properties and biological activity. This compound integrates the fluorescence of coumarin, the pharmacological activity of the imidazopyridine scaffold, and the optimization conferred by fluorine substitution, and it can recognize the single-G bulge of RNA1, with this binding driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, while its potential activity also lies in acting as a kinase inhibitor or antiproliferative agent, and it can function as a fluorescent label with favorable pharmacokinetic properties.

RNA information: 5'CCG/3'GCC is a 1×1 CG internal-loop RNA motif of the 5'CNG/3'GNC family, with a short stem loop (5'CCG/3'GCC), no U·U mismatches, G-C/C-G pairing, high stability, and low "breathing effects". Pathogenically linked to FXTAS (via mutant FMR1 mRNA’s r(CCG) repeats), it acts as a disease-specific RNA switch, recruiting proteins/ligands (e.g., diguanidinobenzoates) to modulate splicing/RAN translation. It also serves as an experimental control for RNA-small molecule binding specificity.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-16-(3-pyridin-4-yloxypropoxy)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a monocationic compound, carrying only one positive charge on the massive pentacyclic framework. It is connected via a propoxy linker to a neutral pyridin-4-yloxy (non-quaternized) substituent. The introduction of the pyridine ring enhances the molecules aromaticity and hydrogen bond acceptor capacity. Due to its large size and lipophilicity, this structure may be designed as a macromolecular ligand. Functionally, large molecules containing the pyridine motif are often studied as antagonists for specific receptors, such such as G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs). Potential applications may include therapeutic research for nervous system disorders or inflammatory diseases, leveraging its large scaffold for selective protein target binding.

RNA information: No loop (loopless RNA), also known as 5'CAG/3'CUG and 5'CAG/3'GUC, is an RNA with a hairpin structure. Structurally, its stem has fully complementary base pairs (no mismatches/deletions) and no internal loops, showing high conformational stability without inter-strand "breathing effect" as a simplified dsRNA model. Functionally, it serves as a key experimental control to distinguish small molecules’ specific/non-specific binding to RNA, with no direct link to specific diseases.
Small_molecule_information: 6-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)-2-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-1H-indol-3-amine, also referred to as D2, is a bis-imidazolinyl substituted indolamine, characterized by high basicity and hydrophilicity, which is a close congener of the DNA fluorophore DAPI identified via shape-screening the NCI library for compounds capable of recognizing the pathogenic 5CAG/3GAC 1 1 nucleotide internal loop of expanded r(CAG) repeat RNAs, structurally conserving DAPIs two amidine groups while being decorated with an extra phenyl-amidine substituent that markedly increases its -surface and positive charge, able to bind the 5CAG/3GAC hairpin with tighter affinity than DAPI and showing essentially no detectable affinity for fully-paired RNA or DNA, capable of disrupting the r(CAG)MBNL1 complex, restoring correct splicing of NCOR2 and ATP2A1 pre-mRNAs in Huntington-disease patient fibroblasts and neuroblastoma lines expressing expanded CAG repeats without altering cell morphology or nuclei staining and without affecting the splicing pattern of control cells, thus serving as a synthetically accessible, highly selective chemical probe that validates the druggability of r(CAG)exp RNAs and constitutes a lead for developing disease-modifying therapy in poly-glutamine expansion disorders; the imidazolinyl group of this molecule is a common pharmacophore for adrenergic receptor agonists or antagonists (e.g., clonidine), and concurrently, its indolamine core is related to the tryptamine structure, suggesting potential Central Nervous System (CNS) activity, while as a dicationic structure, it may also interact with nucleic acids or polysaccharides, holding potential research value in anticancer or antimicrobial fields.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-cyclopropyl-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 10, is an amide compound containing thienopyridine and cyclopropyl structures, modified from initial active analogs; its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a cyclopropylcarboxamide group at C2; in terms of biological function, Thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamides are a new class of antiplasmodial agents (anti-malarial) with strong antiparasitic activity, and this compound binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, exhibiting moderate affinity and lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(E)-[10-[(E)-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-ylhydrazinylidene)methyl]anthracen-9-yl]methylideneamino]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Bisantrene, is a complex anthracene derivative with known antitumor relevance, where the anthracene ring (three linearly fused benzene rings) is substituted at the 9- and 10-positions with hydrazone/imine-linked 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) groups; anthracene is a well-known fluorescent chromophore, making this compound highly likely to exhibit strong fluorescence, and the molecule contains two imidazolinium groups and a conjugated hydrazone-imine structure, elements that facilitate binding to biomolecules like DNA, consistent with its use as a DNA-intercalating topoisomerase IIrelated agent, and such compounds are typically designed as DNA-binding fluorescent probes or potential anticancer agents

RNA information: No loop (loopless RNA), also known as 5'CAG/3'CUG and 5'CAG/3'GUC, is an RNA with a hairpin structure. Structurally, its stem has fully complementary base pairs (no mismatches/deletions) and no internal loops, showing high conformational stability without inter-strand "breathing effect" as a simplified dsRNA model. Functionally, it serves as a key experimental control to distinguish small molecules’ specific/non-specific binding to RNA, with no direct link to specific diseases.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[5-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]phenyl]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole, also referred to as DB60 (furimidazoline, 2,5-bis-[4-(2-imidazolin-2-yl)phenyl]furan), is a symmetric or near-symmetric structure centered around a biphenyl-linked furan ring, with a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) group attached to each end. The entire molecule forms a conjugated system consisting of two imidazolinium groups, two phenyl groups, and a furan ring, where the imidazolinium groups are connected to the 2- and 5-positions of the furan ring via the phenyl linkers. Imidazolinium groups are typically weakly basic and can bind to biomacromolecules like DNA or RNA, and such polyaromatic heterocyclic systems often possess fluorescent properties. This compound was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii activity but has been repurposed as a selective HIV-1 TAR RNA antagonist, binding the pyrimidine bulge of the TAR hairpin, displacing the Tat protein, blocking Tat-mediated trans-activation, stabilizing TAR structure, and suppressing HIV-1 LTR-driven gene expression without cytotoxicity, illustrating a clear RNA-versus-DNA selectivity switch compared with amidine congeners, and identifying it as a lead structure for developing small-molecule inhibitors targeting the TARTat axis of HIV-1 replication.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) commonly in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing, underpinning disease pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: The miR-96 precursor( pri-miR-96) is a structured RNA hairpin that is specifically recognized by a benzimidazole small molecule (compound 1) identified via the Inforna pipeline. Binding occurs at the single-stranded Drosha-processing site within the precursor, thereby blocking its cleavage and reducing mature miR-96 production. Its key functional element is a UU internal loop and a nearby GG internal loop within this hairpin, which serve as the binding site for the small molecule Targaprimir-96. Binding of the molecule to this specific structural pocket inhibits Drosha cleavage, thereby blocking the biogenesis of mature oncogenic miR-96. This inhibition leads to the upregulation of the FOXO1 protein and induces apoptosis in cancer cells, highlighting the direct link between its specific secondary structure and its oncogenic function.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Targaprimir-96, is the parent, non-covalent small molecule designed to bind selectively to the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96, specifically targeting a UU internal loop within its secondary structure, and by binding to this site, it inhibits the biogenesis of oncogenic mature miR-96, which leads to the upregulation of FOXO1 protein expression and subsequently induces apoptosis in triple-negative breast cancer cells, while showing no significant effect in healthy breast epithelial cells, demonstrating its selective, phenotype-modulating efficacy; this compound is a complex benzimidazole, triazole, and amide derivative, whose structure contains multiple benzimidazole rings, piperazine groups, tert-butyl groups, and a lengthy peptidic mimicry chain, resulting in an extremely high molecular weight and high polar surface area, it presents as a large molecule with extreme complexity and multiple potential hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, and its structure is indicative of a potential Anti-cancer Agent or Antiparasitic Agent (Macromolecular Ligand), often designed to interact with DNA or essential biomacromolecular targets (such as protein kinases or enzymes), leveraging its benzimidazole core and polyamine linkers.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-[(N-methylcarbamimidoyl)amino]phenyl]-3-[4-[[4-[(N-methylcarbamimidoyl)amino]phenyl]carbamoylamino]phenyl]urea, also referred to as Compound P4, has a complex urea core, substituted by multiple N-methylcarbamimidoyl-containing phenyl groups, with an additional carbamoyl bridge on one side, while Compound P4 retains the twin benzamidine cations of pentamidine but is elongated by an extra phenyl-ether tail that improves its shape match to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat; the high content of guanidine groups makes it a highly positively charged species, and similar to other polyguanidino derivatives as well as pentamidine-like small molecules discovered through 3-D shape-based similarity screening, this compound is investigated as an antiparasitic and anticancer agent due to its strong electrostatic interaction with nucleic acids, interfering with DNA function in pathogens or cancer cells, and is also a next-generation ligand for the pathogenic r(CUG)exp RNA that underlies myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), which can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG)12, partially shift a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal in DM1 cell models though cytotoxicity becomes evident at higher doses, exemplifying how computational shape similarity can rapidly evolve an antimicrobial template into a chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics, though it is less selective, binding equally to RNAs containing 5CUG/3GCC or 5CGG/3GGC motifs and to the human A-site.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-2-[[4-[(2-amino-5-formyl-4-oxo-3,6,7,8-tetrahydropteridin-6-yl)methylamino]benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid is structurally identified as 5-Formyltetrahydrofolate (5-formyl-THF), also referred to as Leucovorin or folinic acid, a non-azaaromatic folate derivative structured with a tetrahydrofolate (THF) core and a formyl group (-CHO) attached to the 5-position of its pteridine ring that lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; in real-world medicine, the compound is an essential adjuvant in cancer therapy, with its primary activity being acting as a reduced folate form that bypasses the action of dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) to directly supply formyl groups necessary for the synthesis of purines and thymidylate (vital for rapidly dividing cells) and serves as a core active folate for delivering one-carbon units for nucleotide synthesis and homocysteine metabolism, clinically used for two main purposes: as a rescue agent for high-dose Methotrexate (MTX) therapy to protect normal cells, and in combination with 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) to enhance 5-FUs efficacy against cancers like colorectal cancer by stabilizing the complex between 5-FU metabolite and thymidylate synthase (TS), while also often being employed to counteract folate antagonist side effects.

RNA information: NA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dimethoxyphenyl)-1H-benzimidazole, also referred to as Compound H8, is a simple benzimidazole scaffold substituted at the 2-position with a 3,4-dimethoxyphenyl group, and it is a bis-benzimidazole derivative of Hoechst 33258 rationally designed to enhance RNA-binding affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic r(CUG)exp RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1); it retains the DNA-binding benzimidazole core of Hoechst 33258 but is elongated with a hydrophobic p-methoxyphenyl tail that improves its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 with potency that is approximately two-fold higher than Hoechst 33258 yet is less selective than H1, binding with similar affinity to RNAs containing 5CUG/3GCC or 5CGG/3GGC motifs and to the human A-site, and in DM1 cell models can partially shift a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal while showing cytotoxicity at higher doses, exemplifying how computational shape similarity can evolve a DNA-binding dye into a low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics; the benzimidazole core of this compound is a high-profile structural motif in drug chemistry, found in a wide array of vital pharmaceuticals including antiparasitic agents and proton pump inhibitors, the introduction of the 3,4-dimethoxyphenyl group, a common lignan pharmacophore, may confer tubulin inhibitory activity, similar to some antineoplastic drugs, thereby interfering with cell division, additionally, this class of compounds is often investigated for anti-inflammatory and antiviral effects, and it represents a lead structure with good drug-like properties and potential for high bioavailability.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[methyl(quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 6, is a complex peptide-like molecule containing quinoline-3-carboxamide and a triazole ring, this molecule is the most promising low molecular weight analog that retains strong HIV-1 FSS binding, only about four-fold weaker than compound 3 despite reduced size and lipophilicity, demonstrates strong selectivity over competing RNAs and preferentially binds to HIV-1 FSS in competition dialysis experiments with some off-target interaction to structurally similar hairpins, decreases viral production significantly in HIV-1 replication assays although with less potency than compound 3, displays minimal cytotoxicity, and highlights that high affinity and antiviral activity can be preserved in structurally streamlined scaffolds with improved ligand efficiency, these large molecules are often developed as peptide receptor ligands, protein-protein interaction inhibitors, or potential antiviral drugs (e.g., HIV protease inhibitors).

Small_molecule_information: tetrasodium;[(2R,3R,4R,5R)-2-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-5-[[[[(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(3-carbamoyl-4H-pyridin-1-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methoxy-oxidophosphoryl]oxy-oxidophosphoryl]oxymethyl]-4-hydroxyoxolan-3-yl] phosphate, also referred to as -Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate, reduced (NADPH), is a non-azaaromatic coenzyme that contains an adenine nucleoside (adenosine phosphate) and a nicotinamide nucleoside linked by a pyrophosphate bond, with its structure being a variant of Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide (NAD) or its phosphorylated form NADPH/NADH; it has a structure of two nucleotides (nicotinamide mononucleotide and adenine mononucleotide) linked by phosphate groups in its reduced form, lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and its core function is donating electrons in anabolic reactions and antioxidant defense (unrelated to azaaromatic sensing), while NAD itself is an essential coenzyme in all living cells that participates in hundreds of redox reactions, making the molecule a critical metabolic intermediate used in the study of redox balance, energy metabolism, and enzymology.

The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6: This is a six-repeat control RNA with perfect Watson-Crick base pairing. It is used to evaluate the specificity of compound binding and shows minimal structural perturbation upon ligand interaction, confirming that small molecules do not significantly bind to regular duplex RNA.; The (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6 sequence serves as the extended control duplex. Its repetitive yet perfectly paired structure lacks the distinctive features of disease-repeat RNA, allowing researchers to conclusively prove that compound efficacy is due to specific recognition of the pathological mismatch motif and not general RNA binding.
Small_molecule_information: 3-methyl-2-pyridin-4-ylquinoline, also referred to as CP4, is a nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound consisting of a naphthyridine fused heterocycle with methyl and pyridine substitutions; its structure uses the quinoline core, with a pyridin-4-yl substituent at the 2-position and a methyl group at the 3-position, the molecular formula is CHN, this compound contains two nitrogen heterocyclic rings and has an extensive conjugated system, in terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, and the introduction of the pyridine ring is often used to enhance coordination capability and modify biological activity.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-phenyl-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as compound 4, is a fused heterocyclic amide based on the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine scaffold, and it is also a 3-aminothieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide derivative characterized by a phenyl substituent at the 6-position, a structural modification critical for its enhanced potency as a TAR RNA binder. This tricyclic backbone features an amino group at position 3 and a phenyl group at position 6. It is linked via an amide bond to a 3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl substituent. The molecule is highly lipophilic, thanks to the phenyl group and the crucial trifluoromethyl (CF3) group. The trifluoromethyl group is a classic substituent in medicinal chemistry, used to enhance metabolic stability and transmembrane permeability, while also acting as a strong electron-withdrawing group. This planar, highly lipophilic amidic heterocycle is a prime example of a small molecule drug, often investigated as a non-competitive or competitive inhibitor of signal pathway enzymes like tyrosine kinases, with broad applicability in anti-cancer, immunomodulatory, and anti-inflammatory fields; additionally, it specifically interacts with key nucleobases in the HIV-1 TAR RNA elementG21, U23, and G43demonstrating dual binding affinity for both the bulge region (U23) and the lower stem (G43), a unique interaction profile that establishes it as a promising lead molecule for developing antiviral therapeutics that target RNA-protein interactions.

Small_molecule_information: Pyridine-2,3-dicarboxylic acid, also referred to as Quinolinic acid (QA), is a planar azaaromatic dicarboxylic acid with the formula C7H5NO4, featuring a quinoline ring (fused benzene and pyridine rings) that bears carboxylic acid groups (-COOH) at the 2- and 3-positions; it is a dicarboxylic acid derivative of pyridine and an important endogenous metabolite as well as an intermediate in the kynurenine pathway of tryptophan-nicotinic acid metabolism, generated in both the brain and peripheral tissues of mammals, and it should be noted that QA binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, while it also serves as a key intermediate in the biosynthesis of NAD (a critical coenzyme); in biomedical research, QAs activity is primarily linked to its neurotoxicity: it acts as an excitotoxic amino acid, functioning as an agonist for the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor, which can lead to overstimulation and death of neurons at high concentrations, therefore, elevated levels of QA in the blood and cerebrospinal fluid are considered a potential contributing factor and marker for neuroinflammatory conditions and several neurodegenerative diseases like Huntingtons disease and Alzheimers disease.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: bis(2-acetamidoacetic acid);4-[2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)iminohydrazinyl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Diminazine, is a complex salt molecule consisting of a large cationic formamidine hydrazonebased diamidine species and two 2-acetamidoacetic acid anions, and the ,-diamidine core connected through a biphenyl and hydrazone bridge corresponds to the class of aromatic diamidine compounds used as antiprotozoal agents that exert activity by binding to the minor groove of DNA, with such cationic diamidines commonly displaying antiprotozoal, antifungal, antiviral, anticancer, or broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties.

RNA information: (CCUG)₈ is an RNA oligonucleotide modeling the pathogenic expanded repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 2 (DM2). It forms a stable hairpin structure with internal loops and serves as a target for evaluating ligand selectivity. Some small molecules can bind and cleave (CCUG)₈, while others  show no activity, highlighting its role in distinguishing DM2-specific targeting.
Small_molecule_information: amino-[4-[amino-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butylazaniumylidene]methyl]phenyl]methylidene]-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]azanium, also referred to as a bisamidinium-derived molecule with added amine groups, is a massively complex, highly positively charged compound, likely a dicationic or polycationic dye or polyamine derivative; this compound binds both DNA and RNA CTG/CUG repeats, potently inhibits transcription, cleaves (CUG) selectively (not CCUG), and is highly effective in reversing DM phenotypes with low toxicity, while its core features two Triazine units each linked to a polyamine tail, the central azaniumylidene and methylidene groups point to a highly stabilized guanidinium or amidinium derivative, conferring high basicity and charge, and due to its multiple positive charges and hydrogen bond donors, the molecule is researched as a highly efficient gene transfection reagent (for delivering DNA/RNA into cells) or as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting by disrupting the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[(4-methylanilino)methyl]naphthalen-2-ol, also referred to as CP3, is a naphthol-containing compound whose structure is based on the naphthol parent skeleton: a benzene ring is fused with another benzene ring to form the naphthalene skeleton, which is substituted with a hydroxyl group at position 2 and a (4-methylanilino)methyl group at position 1, and this amphiphilic structure built around a naphthalene core with a hydroxyl group connected via a methylene bridge to an amino group and a 4-methylphenyl ring allowed moderate RNA binding, but it was not among the most potent compounds in biological assays; in terms of biological function, naphthalenes and their derivatives generally exhibit diverse biological activities such as antifungal, anti-inflammatory, or are used as fluorescent probes.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[2-(diethylamino)ethyl]-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazol-2-ium-9-olalso referred to as Compound 1b, which is also designated Compound 2is a pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole quaternary ammonium salt (a fused PAH) known for its strong DNA intercalating ability, while Compound 1b is a positively charged bis-benzimidazole analog and 1a-derivative featuring a truncated 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl side chain instead of 1as piperidinium appendage, retaining high in vitro binding affinity to displace 1a-TO and recognize the 5CGG/3GGC internal loops of r(CGG)exp hairpins, possessing the potency to disrupt the pathogenic r(CGG)DGCR8 complex, being able to restore near wild-type SMN2/Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing and reduce r(CGG)exp-containing foci in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells without affecting Sam68-independent transcripts or cell viability (yet failing to inhibit RAN translation in cells likely due to poor bioavailability), and having favorable aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity and efficient synthesis to serve as a validated chemical probe for r(CGG)exp-mediated toxicity and a promising lead scaffold for FXTAS and related CGG-repeat disorders; this class of pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole quaternary ammonium salt compounds are major candidates for anti-tumor drugs, acting as Topoisomerase II inhibitors.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropylsulfanyl)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one is a quinolin-4-one derivative containing a piperidine group linked via a thioether bond. Quinoline derivatives are known for broad antimicrobial, anti-malarial, and anti-inflammatory activities. The inclusion of the piperidine moiety enhances lipophilicity and is often employed in the development of targeted enzyme inhibitors.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: actinium;2-amino-5-carbamimidamido-N-[3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]phenyl]pentanamide, also referred to as N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-argininamide (2b), is a compound composed of a radioactive actinium (Ac) atom and a complex organic ligand, and N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-argininamide (2b) is a synthetic nucleobaseamino acid conjugate engineered to mimic and out-compete the natural Tat peptide for binding to the HIV-1 TAR RNA stembulgeloop, the organic ligand is a derivative based on arginine (containing a guanidine group) linked via an amide bond to an aniline substituted with a methylamino thiazole ring, it replaces the labile ribose-phosphate backbone with a rigid thiazolearyl core that presents an arginine side-chain optimized for electrostatic recognition of the TAR bulge, the core function of the molecule is its role as a precursor for Targeted Alpha Therapy (TAT): the organic ligand is designed as a high-affinity chelating agent to stably bind the alpha-particle emitter Actinium-225, the ligands targeting capability (e.g., high affinity for a tumor-specific receptor) ensures that Actinium-225 is delivered highly specifically to tumor cells, enabling the precise and efficient alpha-radiation killing of the tumor, in vitro, 2b binds TAR with high affinity, triggers no global RNA conformational change, but shows reduced selectivity in the presence of excess tRNA, in HIV-infected MAGIC-5B reporter cells, 2b fails to suppress viral replication, correlating with its low specificity, these properties indicate that 2b is a potent but non-specific TAR ligand, underscoring the need for balanced charge and shape to achieve antiviral activity.
