RNA information: RNA4 contains the UU internal loop for compound 1 binding, but the GG loop targeted by compound 2 has been mutated to a GC base pair. This mutation significantly reduces the binding affinity of dimeric compound 3, confirming the necessity of the GG loop for high-affinity bivalent binding.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

RNA information: The r(CCUG)₁₀ sequence models the pathogenic RNA associated with myotonic dystrophy type 2. Structurally, it contains four-nucleotide repeats that also produce hairpins with mismatches, although their geometry differs slightly from CUG repeats. These motifs create similar alternating stripes of electrostatic potential, making selectivity between CUG and CCUG challenging for both proteins and small molecules. Functionally, r(CCUG)₁₀ sequesters MBNL1 in a manner comparable to CUG repeats, contributing to splicing defects. Its inclusion in binding studies demonstrates the difficulty of achieving discrimination between closely related repeat RNAs and emphasizes the need for ligands that exploit subtle structural differences.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 5 (2012), is the E-isomer counterpart of compound 4, differing only in the configuration of the olefin bridge, and this geometric change alters the positioning of the benzo[g]quinoline group relative to the peptide scaffold, leading to slightly different recognition properties, while it retains overall high binding capability, though its cooperativity and selectivity are reduced compared to the Z-isomer, and structurally, it highlights how subtle stereochemical differences strongly affect RNA recognition, and biologically, it is still active in cellular models and displaces pathogenic complexes, demonstrating that both isomers contribute valuable insights into structureactivity relationships for RNA-binding molecules; it is a highly complex, multi-cationic peptidomimetic structure, its core is the (E)-oct-4-enediamide scaffold, with side chains derived from proline, 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl, and lysine, the molecule contains multiple primary amino groups, rendering it poly-cationic at physiological pH, the 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline is a bulky, highly hydrophobic aromatic group, this combination of poly-cationic character, multiple chiral centers, and large hydrophobic groups makes it suitable as an anti-tumor agent, a DNA/RNA binding agent, or a novel antibiotic, and the (E)-alkene provides a specific, rigid conformation.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[(2R,5S)-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-oxathiolan-5-yl]pyrimidin-2-one, also referred to as Lamivudine (3TC) or its enantiomer, is a synthetic cytidine nucleoside analog where the 3 oxygen atom of the ribose ring is replaced by a sulfur atom, forming a 1,3-oxathiolane sugar ring, molecular formula C8H11N3O3S; it is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) that incorporates into viral DNA, terminating its synthesis, and in terms of real-world activity, Lamivudine is an extremely important antiviral drug, classified as a Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitor (NRTI), which is widely used clinically for the treatment of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) infection and Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) infection, with its mechanism of action involving being phosphorylated intracellularly to its active triphosphate form, which is then incorporated into the viral DNA chain, causing chain termination and effectively inhibiting the activity of the HIV reverse transcriptase and HBV polymerase.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol;dihydrochloride, also referred to as Quinacrine Di HCl, is a quinoline-phenol heterocyclic compound presented as the dihydrochloride salt, which is an antimalarial and antiprotozoal drug with DNA intercalation properties and has been repurposed for cancer and prion diseases due to its ability to inhibit protein aggregation and nucleic acid interactions, its structure featuring a 4-aminoquinoline backbone with a 7-chloro substitution is chemically analogous to classic antimalarial drugs like chloroquine, the presence of both a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group on the phenyl ring provides multiple sites for interaction and protonation, quinolines are a broad class of compounds known for antimalarial, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activities, due to its salt form the molecule is highly protonated enhancing its water solubility and the functional groups suggest potential for metal ion chelation, in reality this compound is likely explored as a novel antimalarial candidate to combat drug resistance or possibly as an anticancer agent due to its potential to interfere with metal homeostasis or kinase activity.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methyl]amino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-7, is an ultra-long flexible bis-head benzimidazole molecule with the identical chemical name and structural description as N-[3-[4-[[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methyl]amino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, featuring a linker arm with eight repeating polyamide units, and 2H-7 incorporates seven propylamine spacers, creating the longest intermodule distance tested, it represents the maximum length among the flexible linker arms in this study, emphasizing its role as a highly flexible, multivalent DNA binder or biomacromolecular cross-linker, this excess length reduces preorganization and likely misaligns the RNA-binding modules with r(CGG)exp repeats, consequently, it demonstrates reduced potency compared to 2H-5 and 2H-6, its predicted real-world activity remains nucleic acid targeting for anti-proliferative or gene regulation purposes, likely leveraging its long reach and flexibility, while it still shows some functional rescue of splicing defects, its diminished efficiency highlights the importance of matching linker length to RNA motif periodicity, and the results from 2H-7 reinforce that optimal potency arises from precise spatial organization, with excessive linker extension leading to loss of affinity and selectivity.

RNA information: r(CUG)₆ is a short ribonucleic acid fragment consisting of six consecutive CUG repeat units. Structurally, it can form a more stable hairpin secondary structure than short-chain r(CUG)₂/r(CUG)₄, with multiple characteristic U·U mismatches in the stem region and six 5′CUG/3′GUC internal loops (a key feature simulating the structure of long-chain pathogenic CUG repeat sequences). In some cases, its conformational stability can be further enhanced by GC base pairs (similar to the GC clamp design of (CUG)₁₂ mentioned in the document). Functionally, it does not possess the toxicity of long expanded r(CUG)ⁿ ,which forms nuclear foci and sequesters MBNL1 (Muscleblind-like family protein) — but its specific binding affinity to MBNL1 is significantly improved. Competitive dialysis experiments showed that its binding preference for CUG repeats is increased by more than 4-fold compared with MBNL1 protein, and it exhibits a binding stoichiometry of approximately 1:2 (drug:5′CUG/3′GUC internal loop) when binding to small-molecule compounds (such as lomofungin and dilomofungin). Its core role is as an in vitro research tool to analyze the binding mechanism between MBNL1 and CUG repeat sequences, verify the inhibitory effect of small molecules on their interaction, and provide a structural and functional model closer to the characteristics of long-chain CUG repeat sequences for the study of the pathogenic mechanism of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and the preliminary screening of therapeutic drugs
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

RNA information: r(GGCC)4 is a short, double-stranded RNA motif composed of four tandem GGCC repeats that adopts a fully base-paired hairpin structure. It is commonly used in competition assays as a negative control because it lacks internal loops or single-stranded regions and therefore does not bind to RNA-targeted small molecules such as CB253, which selectively recognize the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp RNA .
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-[4-[N-(3-azidopropyl)carbamimidoyl]phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as AU-azide, is a highly functionalized probe molecule with the molecular formula C24H23N7O, and AU-azide is a derivative of Compound 2 with an azide group for conjugation; it exhibits even higher AU selectivity and is used to build multivalent compounds like dimers, its core is a planar diaryl-furan (biphenyl-furan) system, which provides hydrophobicity and - stacking ability, the molecule contains two key functional groups: carboximidamide and azido, the carboximidamide group (-C(NH)NH2) is typically positively charged at physiological pH, allowing it to interact strongly with negatively charged biomolecules like DNA; thus, amidine derivatives are often used as DNA minor groove binders with anti-tumor or antimicrobial activity, the azido group (-N3) is a prominent bio-orthogonal chemistry functional group, specifically a core component of Click Chemistry, which permits the molecule to be covalently linked to alkyne-containing partners under specific conditions, therefore, this compound is designed as a bifunctional molecular probe for target identification and bio-orthogonal chemical labeling.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[6-[(4-fluorobenzoyl)amino]-3-pyridinyl]pyridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound p7, is a linear heterocyclic compound composed of three aromatic rings (two pyridine rings and one phenyl ring) linked by two amide bonds; one pyridine ring is substituted at positions 3 and 6, connecting a pyridine-4-carboxamide group and a 4-fluorobenzoylamino group, and the incorporation of a fluorine atom (in the 4-fluorobenzoyl moiety) is a common strategy in medicinal chemistry used to fine-tune the molecules metabolic stability, lipophilicity, and affinity for its target, while the two amide bonds and three nitrogen heterocycles provide multiple hydrogen bond donor and acceptor sites, enhancing its potential for interaction with protein targets, and this multi-heterocyclic amide scaffold is frequently found in drug types such as kinase inhibitors and nuclear receptor ligands, suggesting that this compound or its analogues could be researched for anti-inflammatory or antitumor treatments, in addition, this compound is a small molecule identified through pharmacophore-based virtual screening as a selective ligand for the toxic RNA repeat expansion r(CUG)exp which is implicated in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), it was selected based on its alignment with a consensus pharmacophore model derived from known RNA-binding compounds particularly those targeting the CUG repeat RNA that sequesters MBNL1, a key splicing regulator, in vitro it effectively disrupts the r(CUG)12MBNL1 complex, and in cellular models it enhances nucleocytoplasmic transport of mRNA containing expanded CUG repeats as evidenced by increased luciferase activity, furthermore it improves DM1-associated splicing defects such as aberrant exon 5 inclusion in cardiac troponin T (cTNT) in a dose-dependent manner without affecting cell viability, notably it exhibits high selectivity for CUG-repeat RNA with minimal binding to other RNA or DNA structures, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing RNA-targeted therapeutics against DM1.

RNA information: TSL2 is a stem-loop RNA structure overlapping the SMN2 exon 7 5' splice site. Its conformation regulates splicing accessibility—destabilizing mutations or ligand binding (e.g., PK4C9) promotes exon inclusion by exposing the 5' ss.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-(4-carbamimidoylanilino)phenyl]-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide is an indole bis-amidine derivative, containing an indole core, an aniline bridge, and two amidine groups. Indole compounds are widely found in natural products and possess various biological activities. The amidine groups impart a potential for nucleic acid binding, and the molecule is often developed as an antiprotozoal and antiviral agent. Bis-amidine-indole compounds with similar structures have been studied for the treatment of tropical diseases like African trypanosomiasis or as DNA minor groove binding probes.

RNA information: MALAT1 is a well-studied long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) known to form a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure consisting of three G-tetrads. It functions as a key regulatory molecule in processes such as alternative splicing modulation. The rG4 structure within MALAT1 has been confirmed in vitro through techniques including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays and fluorescence-based methods using probes like NMM and ThT. Studies show that in potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, MALAT1's rG4 forms efficiently, causing significant reverse transcriptase stalling (~60%). Notably, ligands such as PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, inducing and stabilizing MALAT1's rG4 structure even under unfavorable lithium (Li⁺) conditions, highlighting its dynamic structural plasticity and potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-piperidin-1-ylpropanamide, also referred to as Braco-19, is an acridine analog of N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanamide, replacing pyrrolidine with a piperidine side chain, it is an acridine-derived G4 ligand with a tricyclic acridine core modified by cationic side chains, enabling selective binding to G-quadruplexes (G4s) via -stacking, can stabilize pre-formed RNA G4s (rG4s) with moderate affinity but lacks robust chaperone activity for rG4 folding especially for non-canonical 2-quartet rG4s (e.g., XIST, RPPH1), weakly induces tetramolecular rG4 assembly and does not significantly enhance reverse transcriptase stalling in Li buffers, making it suitable for stabilizing existing rG4s rather than promoting de novo folding, and is explored as a potential acridine-based anticancer drug with possible altered pharmacokinetics and selective cytotoxicity.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-7-[4-(1,3-benzothiazol-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-4-oxo-1-(2-pyridin-2-ylethyl)-1,8-naphthyridine-3-carboxylicacid This is a structurally complex quinolone analogue, centered on a 1,8-naphthyridine-3-carboxylic acid backbone, which is a common core for quinolone antibiotics. The molecule is substituted at position 7 with a complex amine: 4-(1,3-benzothiazol-2-yl)piperazine. The piperazine ring is common in drugs, increasing solubility and basicity, and benzothiazole is another important heterocycle. At the N1 position, it is substituted with a 2-(pyridin-2-yl)ethyl side chain. This intricate substitution pattern, particularly the combination of the naphthyridinone core with the piperazine, pyridine, and carboxylic acid functionalities, strongly suggests this compound is being investigated as a novel broad-spectrum antibacterial agent, primarily through the inhibition of bacterial DNA gyrase or topoisomerase IV.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 1H-benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as alloxazine, is the core scaffold of Isoalloxazine, a complex heterocyclic compound with the formula C10H6N4O2; alloxazine itself is a planar azaaromatic heterocyclic compound featuring a tricyclic core of fused pyrimidine and benzodiazine rings with multiple carbonyl and amino groups, possessing distinct planar azaaromatic properties but binding weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and failing to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, while the isoalloxazine structure is formed by the fusion of a benzene ring and a pteridine ring with two keto groups at positions 2 and 4, and this framework is of paramount importance in biology as it forms the essential core of Riboflavin (Vitamin B2), which is a necessary component of the vital coenzymes Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN) and Flavin Adenine Dinucleotide (FAD); these coenzymes, for which alloxazine also serves as the structural backbone, play critical roles in numerous biological processes including the electron transport chain, fatty acid metabolism, and drug metabolism, and therefore this scaffold is fundamental to life, and its derivatives are extensively applied in nutritional science and biochemical research.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₀ sequence represents a pathogenic-length model for expanded CUG repeats and folds into a stable hairpin with regularly spaced U–U mismatches. Structurally, it provides multiple equivalent binding sites, enabling one ligand to associate with each trinucleotide repeat and producing cooperative binding. This repeat length captures essential features of toxic RNA found in myotonic dystrophy, including the ability to sequester MBNL1 and form nuclear foci. Functionally, r(CUG)₁₀ is the most relevant in vitro construct for evaluating selective small-molecule recognition, since its length allows the manifestation of cooperative interactions that mimic disease-relevant RNA–protein complexes.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 5 (2012), is the E-isomer counterpart of compound 4, differing only in the configuration of the olefin bridge, and this geometric change alters the positioning of the benzo[g]quinoline group relative to the peptide scaffold, leading to slightly different recognition properties, while it retains overall high binding capability, though its cooperativity and selectivity are reduced compared to the Z-isomer, and structurally, it highlights how subtle stereochemical differences strongly affect RNA recognition, and biologically, it is still active in cellular models and displaces pathogenic complexes, demonstrating that both isomers contribute valuable insights into structureactivity relationships for RNA-binding molecules; it is a highly complex, multi-cationic peptidomimetic structure, its core is the (E)-oct-4-enediamide scaffold, with side chains derived from proline, 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl, and lysine, the molecule contains multiple primary amino groups, rendering it poly-cationic at physiological pH, the 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline is a bulky, highly hydrophobic aromatic group, this combination of poly-cationic character, multiple chiral centers, and large hydrophobic groups makes it suitable as an anti-tumor agent, a DNA/RNA binding agent, or a novel antibiotic, and the (E)-alkene provides a specific, rigid conformation.

RNA information: 5′GAU/3′C_A: This GAU/C_A motif forms a bulged loop detected in the RNA motif screen. While identified as a potential ligand-binding site, the study did not provide extensive validation of its direct role in HCV RNA or associated antiviral outcomes.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,3-N,5-N-tris[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 8, is a molecule with a trimeric structure, built upon a benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxamide core, which is a rigid, three-armed scaffold with C3h symmetry. The end of each arm is substituted with a phenyl group bearing a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl (imidazolinyl) group. Consequently, the entire molecule contains a total of three imidazolinyl groups, which are strong bases that can be protonated at physiological pH, making it a potent tricationic system. This rigid, symmetrical, polycationic architecture is highly suitable for acting as a high-affinity DNA binding agent and also displays strong selectivity for RNA internal loops, especially motifs containing 5UUA/3A_U, 5GCU/3C_A, and 5UCUAU/3ACCA, with its recognition pattern highlighting tolerance for mismatched and bulged bases within short hairpins; the molecule effectively binds HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II, consistent with motif enrichment analyses, and cellular studies revealed potent inhibition of HCV replication, with ASO competition confirming direct binding to viral RNA motifs. Its three-armed structure may allow it to interact simultaneously with multiple sites on the DNA helix, such as two minor groove sites and a phosphate backbone site. Compounds of this type are frequently designed in medicinal chemistry as anticancer, antitrypanosomal agents, or as gene transfection aids, and these results position this molecule as a promising lead for selective RNA-targeted antiviral development.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as the saturated analog of DAPI (whose full name is 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide), is a compound featuring a dihydroindole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, with the formula C16H16N4, while DAPI is a fluorescent stain binding AT-rich DNA regions, widely used in microscopy for nuclear labeling and is cell-impermeant unless membranes are compromised, making it useful for viability assays and chromosome visualization; this compound is the saturated analog of 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, the saturation of the indole ring removes aromaticity and significantly increases the flexibility and changes the 3D shape of the molecule, this structural change dramatically influences its binding mode and affinity for the DNA minor groove, and this compound is investigated to explore the impact of molecular rigidity/flexibility on the activity of DNA-binding drugs, showing potential for anticancer and anti-infective activity.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 1-cyclopropyl-7-(4-ethylpiperazin-1-yl)-6-fluoro-4-oxoquinoline-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Enrofloxacin, is a classic Fluoroquinolone antibiotic with N1-cyclopropyl, C6-fluoro, and C7-piperazine substituents (similar to Ciprofloxacin) that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA replication, and is used clinically as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial for infections including those of the respiratory and urinary tracts.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]ethanamine (also referred to as the target compound) was synthesized via palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions and characterized by methods such as Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR); functionally, this molecule can bind to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, though a high ligand/Bh molar ratio is required to produce detectable spectral changes and its binding affinity is relatively low, and it is a complex molecule containing multiple primary amine groups (RNH2) and a polyphenol ether structure, whose scaffold is composed of two substituted phenyl rings linked by an ether oxygen, with a total of four 2-aminoethyl side chains and one methoxy group on the rings, and this polyamine structure grants the molecule high cationic character and hydrophilicity at physiological pH, enabling strong interaction with anionic biomolecules, while in practice, polyamine-like molecules are often used as non-viral gene delivery vectors, forming stable complexes with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) to facilitate transfection, or as modulators of polyamine metabolic pathways, and its high cationic nature also suggests potential antimicrobial or antifungal activity.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-1-(2-hydroxyethyl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]quinolin-2-one, also referred to as SMSM47, is a heterocyclic quaternary salt based on the quinolin-2-one core as well as a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a fixed positive charge and a 1 charge, a key structural feature is the 2-hydroxyethyl group at the N1 position, the presence of the hydroxyl group significantly increases the molecules polarity and aqueous solubility, which is often desirable for formulations requiring high solubility, such as intravenous administration, or for drugs targeting extracellular proteins, the molecule incorporates the 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl group at C3 and the quaternized (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium group at C7, this highly polar and cationic character makes it a likely candidate for targeting G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) or ion channels, or for use as a fluorescent dye or antiviral agent in highly aqueous biological environments, and it can recognize the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[4-[[4-amino-6-(3-azidopropylamino)-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 1c, is an exceptionally large and structurally complex polycationic compound with the molecular formula C40H52N22O2, and Bisamidinium 1c is a functional analog of the monomeric ligand 1a, retaining the core structure of a bisamidinium scaffold linked to two triaminotriazine recognition unitswhere the bisamidinium moiety acts as a known groove binder for expanded CUG repeat RNA (r(CUG)), and the triaminotriazine units target adjacent binding sites of r(CUG), preserving the target specificity for the pathogenic RNA in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), it is based on a terephthalamidine skeleton, linked via long chains to two complex units containing both a triazine ring (1,3,5-triazine) and an azido group, the molecule incorporates multiple amidine and amine groups, leading to a high positive charge at physiological pH, positioning it as a potent polyvalent cation with high nucleic acid affinity, the triazine ring provides multiple hydrogen bonding sites, the compounds azido functional group allows for participation in Click Chemistry for covalent labeling, a key structural modification of 1c is the N-substitution on the amino groups of its triaminotriazine units, which introduces clickable functional groups (e.g., alkyne or azide) without disrupting its RNA-binding capacity; it exists as a hydrochloride salt (presumably tetrahydrochloride, similar to 1a and 1b) due to hydrochloric acid used in purification, ensuring certain water solubility, this structural design suggests it is a powerful nucleic acid binder or non-viral gene vector, also serving as a trackable biological probe, with potential applications as an anti-cancer drug or a drug delivery system, functionally, 1c exhibits strong specific binding to r(CUG), with binding affinity comparable to 1a and 1b, enabling it to target the DM1-causing RNA, it also serves as a precursor in copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (click chemistry) to synthesize dimeric ligands (e.g., 2a, 2b) that overcome the weak inhibitory activity of monomer 1a, additionally, like other bisamidinium-based ligands, 1c is inferred to have low cytotoxicity (in contrast to acridine-based dimeric ligands), providing a safe foundation for subsequent cellular experiments and potential therapeutic development. DM1RNA

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x20: This is a control RNA with twenty repeats of perfectly paired CAG-GUC duplex. It is used to contrast with the mismatched x20 RNA and shows negligible binding and structural changes, underscoring the importance of the AA mismatch in selective compound recognition.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 3, shares the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core with 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one but lacks the bromine atom, and Compound 3 emerged as a lead compound with high affinity and selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, its large aromatic and amino-substituted structure suggests high lipophilicity and the potential to interact strongly with biological targets, it is likely investigated as an antiproliferative agent targeting intracellular enzymes or signaling pathways, Compound 3 significantly reduced polyQ aggregation in cellular models, showed low cytotoxicity, and was cell-permeable, localizing primarily in the cytoplasm. RNA

RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: 6-phenoxyimino-1,9-dihydropurin-2-amine, also referred to as G10, is an aromatic heterocyclic derivative containing a purine core (a guanine analogue with a N-Phenoxyamino group (-N-O-Ph) group added at the C6 position), with the molecular structure C11H10N6O. The core of this compound is the purine ring, a bicyclic system fused by pyrimidine and imidazole rings, one of the basic units of DNA and RNA. The structure features an amino group at position 2 and a phenoxyimino (-NOPh) group at position 6, a substitution that significantly differentiates the molecule from natural nucleobases (like guanine) in terms of electronic properties and spatial conformation; this specific structural modification at the C6 position was originally designed to explore the tolerance of ribose switches to different chemical groups. The introduction of the phenoxy group increases the molecules lipophilicity, likely promoting its passage across cell membranes. As a purine derivative and guanine analogue, G10 can bind to guanine ribose switches, though its binding affinity is lower than that of natural guanine, and functionally, it has a weak inhibitory effect on the growth of Bacillus subtilis and fails to significantly inhibit the expression of reporter genes controlled by guanine ribose switches, which indicates that its antibacterial mechanism may not rely on the activation of ribose switches but rather function through other pathways; such purine-derived molecules are frequently designed in medicinal chemistry as inhibitors of nucleoside metabolism enzymes to interfere with cell signaling or proliferation, and therefore, based on its structure, this compound may possess anti-tumor or antiviral activity by mimicking or antagonizing endogenous purine molecules to regulate key kinases or phosphatases during the cell life cycle.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (7S,9S)-7-[(2R,4S,5R,6S)-4-amino-5-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-6,9,11-trihydroxy-9-(2-hydroxyacetyl)-4-methoxy-8,10-dihydro-7H-tetracene-5,12-dione, also referred to as Epirubicin, is the chemical name for, or a very close analogue of, the anthracycline antibiotic Epirubicin, which is an anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent that intercalates into DNA, inhibiting topoisomerase II and causing DNA strand breaks; Epirubicin is a widely used chemotherapy drug in clinical practice for treating various cancers such as breast cancer and ovarian cancer, and its mechanism involves DNA intercalation and Topoisomerase II inhibition.

RNA information: r(GGGGCC)₈ is an RNA repeat sequence that forms a structured RNA motif capable of binding small molecules. It adopts both hairpin and G-quadruplex conformations and plays a pathogenic role in c9FTD/ALS by undergoing repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation and forming nuclear RNA foci. Small molecules targeting r(GGGGCC)₈ can inhibit these toxic events, suggesting its potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-(4-carbamimidoylanilino)phenyl]-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide is an indole bis-amidine derivative, containing an indole core, an aniline bridge, and two amidine groups. Indole compounds are widely found in natural products and possess various biological activities. The amidine groups impart a potential for nucleic acid binding, and the molecule is often developed as an antiprotozoal and antiviral agent. Bis-amidine-indole compounds with similar structures have been studied for the treatment of tropical diseases like African trypanosomiasis or as DNA minor groove binding probes.

RNA information: The duplex sequence is a designed RNA composed of complementary CUG and CAG repeats that form a stable double helix. Structurally, it lacks the mismatches present in expanded repeats, instead adopting a continuous A-form conformation that eliminates the irregular binding pockets exploited by proteins or ligands in single-stranded hairpins. Functionally, the duplex acts as a control to demonstrate whether ligands bind specifically to mismatched hairpin structures or indiscriminately to generic RNA helices. Its inclusion highlights the importance of structural context in ligand recognition and helps identify molecules with true selectivity for pathogenic RNA conformations.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-2,7-bis[[(2S)-6-amino-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]hexanoyl]amino]-N-[(2S)-1-(2-aminoethylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N-[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N,N-dipropyloct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 9 (2012), is a highly complex asymmetric peptidomimetic bearing the benzo[g]quinoline moiety; while benzo[g]quinoline is a necessary aromatic element for relevant interactions, DCC 9 (2012) has an altered peptide framework that disrupts the orientation required for strong and selective recognition, resulting in a weaker interaction profile, limited ability to discriminate among target sequences, and minimal biological effects, which confirms that benzo[g]quinoline is necessary but not sufficient and that correct positioning within the molecular scaffold is equally critical, highlighting its role as a negative control and supporting evidence for the structureactivity relationship developed across the compound series. The molecules core eight-carbon chain contains a trans (E) double bond, and its most significant feature is the unequal length of the polyamine chains attached to the two termini: one is a 3-aminopropylamine (three carbons) and the other is a 2-aminoethylamine (two carbons); this asymmetric polyamine structure is designed to potentially cause differences in binding affinity or mode with biological targets, and the molecule is symmetrically linked to benzo[g]quinoline-lysine derivatives on both sides, integrating DNA intercalation capability with polycationic electrostatic binding, with this design combining asymmetry and dual-targeting groups aiming to investigate how regulating asymmetric conformation can enhance the selectivity and multi-action efficacy against specific biological targets, holding potential application in multi-target anti-cancer therapy.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: Located at positions 16–30 in the KRAS 5′-UTR. It forms a stable parallel RNA G-quadruplex (RG4), melting temperature (Tm) of 53°C, and ΔG of –4.9 kcal/mol. It is recognized by G4-specific antibodies and stabilized by small molecules like 2a and 2b.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione, also referred to as a thiophene-fused analogue of 2a with similar side chains, is an organic compound featuring a complex polycyclic core of naphthoquinone and benzothiole, forming the naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione skeleton. This framework is a sulfur-containing heteroaromatic system with a highly conjugated electron structure and redox-active quinone carbonyl groups at positions 5 and 10. The substituents at positions 4 and 11 are two 2-aminoethylamino groups, which introduce primary amines capable of protonation, granting the molecule polycationic character and increasing its water solubility under physiological conditions. This specific structural combination, characterized by its planarity, extended conjugation, redox activity, and cationic side chains, makes it a molecule of interest in biomedicine, typically investigated as a potential DNA intercalator or topoisomerase inhibitor, suggesting potential cytotoxic or anticancer activities; it has moderate cellular uptake, stabilizes RG4s, and also suppresses translation and promotes apoptosis, though less potently than 2a.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: 5-[8-(dimethylamino)-7-methyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl]pentanoic acid, also referred to as BRX897, is a carboxylic acid-containing pteridine derivative and a synthetic analog of FMN (flavin mononucleotide) whose structural design optimizes binding ability with the FMN riboswitch by replacing the ribityl chain of FMN with a shorter pentanoic acid side chain, its structure features the 8-(dimethylamino)-7-methylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione core, with a pentyl (five-carbon) side chain containing a terminal carboxylic acid group attached at the N10-position, the molecular formula is CHNO, this structural simplification aims to enhance the molecules passive membrane permeability while reducing dependence on the phosphate group, this compound combines the conjugated skeleton of flavin with the lipophilic side chain of a fatty acid, functionally, it exhibits good binding affinity, indicating high efficiency in binding to the FMN riboswitch, additionally, it shows certain activity in transcription termination assays, demonstrating its ability to effectively regulate gene expression, through SHAPE chemical probing technology, it has been proven to induce conformational changes in the riboswitch, further confirming its binding capacity to the riboswitch, although the binding affinity and activity of this compound are not as strong as those of natural FMN, its structural optimization provides an important foundation for the subsequent development of more effective antibacterial drugs, and is particularly significant in exploring other chemical groups that can replace the phosphate group, in terms of biological function, this structure suggests a potential ability to enter biological membranes or bind to hydrophobic regions of enzymes, possibly serving as a precursor or probe for flavin-based drugs.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[4-[[2,6-bis[[4-(2-aminoethoxy)quinolin-2-yl]carbamoyl]-4-pyridinyl]oxymethyl]triazol-1-yl]propanoic acid, also referred to as PDS cPDS, is a complex heterocyclic molecule often constructed via click chemistry, which is structurally similar to 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)quinolin-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide but modified at the pyridine 4-position, and it is a potent and selective G-quadruplex-stabilizing small molecule that demonstrated excellent binding to the r(G4C2) G-quadruplex, though it was ineffective at inhibiting RAN translation, providing key evidence that the G-quadruplex is not the therapeutically relevant structure for this disease mechanism; the molecule maintains the core features of the multicationic backbone with a 2,6-bis-amide substituted pyridine and two 2-aminoethoxy groups on the quinoline side chains, however, the modification at the pyridine 4-position introduces a side chain containing a stable and polar 1,2,3-triazole ring, terminated by a carboxylic acid group (propanoic acid), the carboxylic acid group introduces an ionizable negative charge, creating an amphoteric or zwitterionic-like molecule that combines polycationic properties (from the primary amines) with a terminal negative charge, and this complex charge distribution may enable unique functionalities, such as pH-sensitive molecular probes, ligands for specific cell receptors, or prodrugs designed to release or change activity in specific environments, like the acidic tumor microenvironment.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated quaternary salt analogue of 4a-10, is a quaternary ammonium salt built upon a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core, and this compound is the N5-methylated quaternary salt analogue of 4a-10, similar to other large planar cationic molecules, it is expected to exhibit strong DNA binding/intercalation and potential topoisomerase inhibition, making it a target for anticancer drug development, the N5-methylated analogue of 4a-10 showed notable affinity for the NRQ, consistent with other methylated derivatives, it showed poor selectivity, it exhibited notable activity in A375 cells.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-phenylhydrazinyl)-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as G6, is a purine compound featuring a phenylhydrazinyl group attached at the 2-position (G6 is a derivative of guanine with a phenylhydrazine group [-N-NH-PH] introduced at the C2 position). The hydrazinyl (NHNH2) structure itself is highly reactive, and the resulting phenylhydrazinyl group allows the lone pair electrons on the nitrogen atoms to participate in the purine rings conjugation system, influencing its reactivity and tautomeric equilibrium. This type of purine derivative is often synthesized as part of chemical libraries for biological screening, as the combination of the purine ring and the hydrazinyl moiety allows the molecule to interact with various biomacromolecules like proteins and nucleic acids; G6 can induce RNA to produce structural changes similar to natural guanine and has potent antibacterial activity against Bacillus subtilis, which can completely inhibit the growth of the bacterium for 24 hours in the lowest glucose medium, though this antibacterial effect is not related to the riboswitchG6 cannot inhibit the reporter gene expression controlled by the xpt-pbuX riboswitch, and the drug resistance mutation occurs in the purine efflux pump coding region rather than the riboswitch, suggesting that its antibacterial mechanism mainly depends on non-riboswitch targets. Its potential activity may include acting as an inhibitor of kinases or phosphatases, enzymes critical in cell signaling, proliferation, and apoptosis, thus exploring its use in therapeutic areas such as anti-inflammation, oncology, or neuroprotection.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 15° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]ethanone, also referred to as Compound 17 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative modified on the piperazines secondary amine purchased from Asinex that binds the influenza A RNA promoter and has improved anti-influenza activity), has a simple acetyl group as its acyl side chain; acetylation is a common simplification strategy and serves as a baseline for evaluating the contribution of more complex side chains, and in terms of real-world activity, the core remains the 1-adrenergic receptor antagonist pharmacophore, while its simplicity suggests it might have good bioavailability, or function as a prodrug that is hydrolyzed in vivo to the more active unsubstituted piperazine derivative (6,7-dimethoxy-2-piperazin-1-ylquinazolin-4-amine), potentially offering a sustained or targeted release profile.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide dibromide, also referred to as PhenDH5, is another derivative in the 2-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide dibromide/4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide dibromide series, this compound is similar to PhenDH3 but with 1-benzylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups, showed moderate G4 affinity and no effect on EBNA1, with poor drug-likeness, and it has a significantly more extended central scaffold: 1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide, phenanthroline is a rigid tricyclic aromatic heterocycle (fused benzene and two pyridine rings) known for its excellent planarity, extended conjugation, and its nitrogen atoms at positions 1 and 10 serving as common metal ion coordination sites, this compound is a dicationic system with two quaternized quinoline side chains and bromide counterions, the phenanthroline core dramatically increases the molecules planarity and conjugation length, resulting in a very large planar aromatic molecule, this structure means that, in addition to its potential for DNA minor groove binding, it likely possesses DNA intercalation capabilities, allowing it to insert between DNA base pairs, furthermore, the phenanthroline core can coordinate with transition metals (such as Cu2 or Fe2) to form redox-active metal complexes, thus, this type of compound and its metal complexes are studied as novel anticancer agents with mechanisms potentially involving DNA binding, intercalation, or the generation of oxidative stress.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 8, is an extremely complex conjugate molecule, essentially a dimer consisting of two Acridine units and two Diaminotriazine units connected by a long, flexible Polyethylene Glycol (PEG) linker (which is a type of oligoether linker) and this dimeric ligand showed weak activity with a low m and a small bivalent effect; the planar Acridine core provides DNA intercalation capability, while the Diaminotriazine fragments are designed for specific hydrogen-bonding interactions with nucleic acids, this design suggests the molecule is a potential dual-action anticancer or antiparasitic agent investigated for its mechanism of bis-intercalation into DNA coupled with targeted nucleic acid recognition, and due to its large size and multiple charge centers, its cellular permeability is likely optimized for delivery via advanced methods like nanocarriers.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: bis(2-acetamidoacetic acid);4-[2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)iminohydrazinyl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Diminazine, is a complex salt molecule consisting of a large cationic formamidine hydrazonebased diamidine species and two 2-acetamidoacetic acid anions, and the ,-diamidine core connected through a biphenyl and hydrazone bridge corresponds to the class of aromatic diamidine compounds used as antiprotozoal agents that exert activity by binding to the minor groove of DNA, with such cationic diamidines commonly displaying antiprotozoal, antifungal, antiviral, anticancer, or broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties.
