Small_molecule_information: benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, is the simple structure of terephthalamidine (p-diamidines), with the formula C8H10N4. It serves as a fundamental structural unit for many diamidine drugs. This rigid dicationic structure makes it a classic DNA minor groove binder. In real-world applications, terephthalamidine and its derivatives are vital drugs for treating protozoal infections (e.g., Pneumocystis pneumonia) and exhibit broad-spectrum antifungal and anticancer activity.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-7-hydroxy-2-oxido-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-1H-purin-6-one, also referred to as cIMP, is a highly stereospecific compound whose chemical name indicates a purine base (1H-purin-6-one, a tautomer of hypoxanthine or guanine) linked to a complex sugar analogue; structurally, cIMP has an inosine base, a ribose ring, and a 3,5-cyclic monophosphate ester linkage, typical of cyclic nucleotides, while the attached cyclic structure of this compound is a fused furan/dioxaphosphinane system bearing a phosphonate group, containing multiple chiral centers (4aR,6R,7R,7aS). This structure mimics the form of natural nucleosides or nucleotides, which are cornerstones of energy metabolism, signaling, and genetic material replication in biological systems; functionally, as a cyclic nucleotide analog, it may potentially regulate cyclic nucleotide-mediated signaling (e.g., via interacting with related enzymes/ion channels), but no roles like riboswitch binding or bacterial gene regulation are mentioned in the document. Consequently, nucleoside analogues like 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-7-hydroxy-2-oxido-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-1H-purin-6-one (also referred to as cIMP) are typically designed to interfere with intracellular nucleic acid synthesis or function. Its potential real-world activities include use as an antiviral drug (by inhibiting viral reverse transcriptase or polymerase to block viral replication) or an anticancer agent (by being incorporated into DNA/RNA chains or inhibiting key enzymes to disrupt cell proliferation). The stereochemical structure is paramount for its recognition by biological targets and is a critical factor in drug design.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[3-[3-[3-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]propylamino]propylamino]propyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 9, is an acridine-triazine dimer that is the most potent dimeric ligand featuring an oligoamino linker, which is aqueous-soluble, cell-permeable and can effectively disperse nuclear foci in DM1 model cells, and it differs from 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide and 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide in key structural aspects: the acridine unit is substituted at position 4 (acridine-4-carboxamide), and the linker connecting the two acridine units is a polypropylamino chain containing three propylamino units, with the molecular formula C63H72N22O2, in terms of real-world activity, the position 4 substitution on the acridine and the introduction of a polyamine linker significantly alter the molecules spatial conformation and electrostatic interactions with DNA, the polyamine structure (like spermidine/spermine derivatives) is itself a strong binding ligand for the DNA backbone, with its multiple positive charges creating intense electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged phosphate groups of the DNA backbone, therefore, this compound is a potent multifunctional DNA binding agent with ultra-high affinity, explored as a topoisomerase inhibitor and DNA cross-linker targeting tumor cells.

RNA information: r(GGCC)4 is a short, double-stranded RNA motif composed of four tandem GGCC repeats that adopts a fully base-paired hairpin structure. It is commonly used in competition assays as a negative control because it lacks internal loops or single-stranded regions and therefore does not bind to RNA-targeted small molecules such as CB253, which selectively recognize the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp RNA .
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-[6-(furan-2-carbonylamino)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenyl]furan-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 16 (2015), is a complex aromatic heterocyclic compound centered around a benzimidazole core, a planar heterocycle formed by the fusion of a benzene and an imidazole ring, and Benzimidazole 16 (2015) is a 2-phenyl-benzimidazole-4-carboxamide derivative engineered to selectively bind the toxic r(CUG)109 RNA expansion that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1, the benzimidazole is substituted at position 2 with a 4-aminophenyl-furan-2-carboxamide side chain, and at position 6 with a furan-2-carbonylamino side chain, the entire molecule is composed of multiple planar aromatic rings (benzimidazole, phenyl, and two furan rings) linked by amide bonds, creating an extended, rigid, and highly conjugated system, the amide and benzimidazole moieties provide multiple hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, this scaffold is often designed as a protein kinase inhibitor or receptor ligand, benzimidazole compounds are a privileged scaffold in medicinal chemistry due to their diverse biological activities, frequently exhibiting antiparasitic, antiviral, and anticancer properties, the activity of this specific compound is likely related to its potential as a molecular probe or as a lead compound in the search for new drugs, by mimicking the recognition surface of MBNL1, it engages the periodic UU internal loops of the CUG hairpin with notable potency in TR-FRET displacement, displaces MBNL1 from ribonuclear foci, and restores correct alternative splicing of the cTNT mini-gene in DM1 cell models without altering DMPK mRNA abundance or affecting off-target RNAs such as human A-site rRNA or r(GGCC)4, its drug-like physicochemical profilemoderate molecular weight (352 Da), cLogP 3.9, topological polar surface area 111 , and negligible cytotoxicity up to 100 Mdemonstrates that uncharged, benzimidazole-based small molecules can selectively drug RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: tetrakis(4-methylbenzenesulfonic acid);5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)-21,23-dihydroporphyrin, also referred to as TMPyP4, is an organic salt containing a porphyrin macrocycle and toluenesulfonate counterions, which is a cationic porphyrin and a widely used pan-G-quadruplex binder that bound the r(G4C2) quadruplex with high affinity among the tested ligands and whose inability to inhibit RAN translation was particularly significant, proving that even high affinity for the quadruplex does not translate to functional activity against the pathogenic hairpin; its structure is based on the porphyrin parent skeleton: four pyrrole rings linked by methine bridges form a conjugated macrocycle, where the four meso-positions of the macrocycle are substituted with 1-methylpyridinium-4-yl groups, and in terms of biological function, porphyrin compounds, due to their unique structure and photosensitivity, are often used in photodynamic therapy, bioimaging probes, and as DNA-binding agents (e.g., TMPyP).

RNA information: MALAT1 is a well-studied long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) known to form a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure consisting of three G-tetrads. It functions as a key regulatory molecule in processes such as alternative splicing modulation. The rG4 structure within MALAT1 has been confirmed in vitro through techniques including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays and fluorescence-based methods using probes like NMM and ThT. Studies show that in potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, MALAT1's rG4 forms efficiently, causing significant reverse transcriptase stalling (~60%). Notably, ligands such as PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, inducing and stabilizing MALAT1's rG4 structure even under unfavorable lithium (Li⁺) conditions, highlighting its dynamic structural plasticity and potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 4,16-difluoro-8,12,20-trimethyl-8-aza-20-azoniapentacyclo[11.7.1.0{2,7}.0{9,21}.0{14,19}]henicosa-1(20),2(7),3,5,9(21),10,12,14(19),15,17-decaene, also referred to as PhenDC3, a bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative), is a highly complex and rigid pentacyclic fused nitrogen-containing heterocyclic quaternary ammonium salt that binds rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, whose structure enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations), functionally acts as a molecular chaperone inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation to aid G4 biology studies, the chemical name clearly outlines a twenty-one-carbon skeleton composed of multiple bridged and fused rings, the two fluorine atoms, strategically positioned at the 4 and 16 positions, are common bioisosteres used to enhance metabolic stability, lipophilicity, and binding affinity to biological targets, the presence of a quaternary ammonium group imparts a positive charge at physiological pH, enabling strong electrostatic interactions with negatively charged biomolecules such as DNA or acidic residues on proteins, and this rigid polycyclic scaffold and its specific three-dimensional structure suggest its potential application as a novel anti-tumor or antiviral agent, possibly functioning as a topoisomerase inhibitor or DNA intercalator in real-world drug discovery.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-9-methyl-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 5, which contains a methyl group on the chromene ring and showed reasonable binding to CAG repeat RNA as part of the initial screening and helped establish structure-activity relationships, is a complex polycyclic ketone compound derived from the fused chromeno[4,3-b]pyridine core. The molecule is substituted with three major aromatic groups: an amino group at position 3, a benzoyl group at position 2, and a phenyl group at position 4, alongside a methyl group at position 9. The benzoyl and phenyl moieties contribute to high lipophilicity and bulk, facilitating cell membrane permeation and interaction with hydrophobic binding pockets of targets. The amino and ketone groups provide sites for hydrogen bond donation and acceptance, crucial for specific non-covalent binding with biological targets. Chromene-based structures are the foundation for many natural products and synthetic drugs, often exhibiting broad biological activities such as anti-cancer, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant effects. Structurally, this compound is a candidate for development as a kinase inhibitor, aiming to exert anti-tumor or immunomodulatory effects by disrupting intracellular signaling pathways.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide dibromide, also referred to as PyDH5, is a dicationic compound built on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide central core, with two Schiff base (methylideneamino) side chains linked to the two amide nitrogen atoms. Each side chain terminates in a N-benzyl quaternized quinoline ring, the 1-benzylquinolin-1-ium structure, attached at the 6-position of the quinoline via the methylideneamino group. The quinolinium salts are permanently positively charged groups, thus the entire molecule carries two positive charges (with Br as the counterion). This structure features an extended conjugated system, highly planar quinoline rings, and two fixed positive charge centers, and is similar to PyDH3 but with 1-benzylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups. Dicationic (or polycationic) aromatic compounds of this type are classic examples of DNA minor groove binders. They are known to enter the minor groove of the DNA double helix through electrostatic attraction and shape complementarity, and may exhibit sequence specificity. Consequently, this compound and its analogues are widely researched as DNA probes, anticancer agents, or antimicrobial agents, acting by interfering with DNA replication, transcription, and repair processes, while PyDH5 itself showed moderate G4 binding but induced GAr-independent increases in EBNA1, indicating non-specific effects or toxicity.

RNA information: RNA4 contains the UU internal loop for compound 1 binding, but the GG loop targeted by compound 2 has been mutated to a GC base pair. This mutation significantly reduces the binding affinity of dimeric compound 3, confirming the necessity of the GG loop for high-affinity bivalent binding.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Targaprimir-96, is the parent, non-covalent small molecule designed to bind selectively to the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96, specifically targeting a UU internal loop within its secondary structure, and by binding to this site, it inhibits the biogenesis of oncogenic mature miR-96, which leads to the upregulation of FOXO1 protein expression and subsequently induces apoptosis in triple-negative breast cancer cells, while showing no significant effect in healthy breast epithelial cells, demonstrating its selective, phenotype-modulating efficacy; this compound is a complex benzimidazole, triazole, and amide derivative, whose structure contains multiple benzimidazole rings, piperazine groups, tert-butyl groups, and a lengthy peptidic mimicry chain, resulting in an extremely high molecular weight and high polar surface area, it presents as a large molecule with extreme complexity and multiple potential hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, and its structure is indicative of a potential Anti-cancer Agent or Antiparasitic Agent (Macromolecular Ligand), often designed to interact with DNA or essential biomacromolecular targets (such as protein kinases or enzymes), leveraging its benzimidazole core and polyamine linkers.

RNA information: (CUG)₁₆ is a synthetic RNA oligonucleotide mimicking pathogenic expanded (CUG)ₙ repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it folds into a stable A-form hairpin with repeating 5'CUG/3'GUC units, featuring U-U mismatches flanked by G-C base pairs. Functionally, it high-affinity binds Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1), sequestering it to disrupt alternative splicing (DM1’s core gain-of-function mechanism) and serving in vitro for cleavage assays to screen catalytic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[2-aminoethyl-[2-[[2-(1H-imidazol-5-yl)acetyl]amino]ethyl]amino]ethyl]-9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as the imidazole-added acridine-triazine conjugate, is a compound that integrates Acridine (DNA intercalation), Triazine (hydrogen bonding recognition), and an Imidazole functional group, and this imidazole-added acridine-triazine design binds and cleaves both (CUG) and (CCUG) repeats effectively, inhibits transcription moderately, and shows strong bioactivity in cellular and fly models, the imidazole ring, key to molecules like Histamine and Histidine, often serves as a site for proton shuttle and acid-base catalysis in biological systems, this polyfunctional design suggests the compound aims to be a molecule with multiple targeting capabilities, for example, acridine targets nucleic acids, while the imidazole ring may be designed to target specific metalloenzymes (like zinc finger proteins) or G-quadruplex structures, its activity is thus focused on novel anticancer agents or molecular tools for investigating complex enzyme reactions and nucleic acid structures. --

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-16-(3-pyridin-1-ium-1-ylpropoxy)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene dichloride is a dicationic compound composed of the core pentacyclic azonium and unsubstituted pyridinium, existing as a dichloride salt. The unsubstituted pyridinium offers minimal steric hindrance. As a dication, its main characteristic is high cationic lipophilicity. In practical applications, such structures are often explored as DNA/RNA binding agents, interfering with gene expression or replication by electrostatic interaction and intercalation with nucleic acids. The choice of the dichloride counterion is primarily to ensure the compounds solubility and stability in aqueous media for formulation and biological testing.

RNA information: The (5'CNG/3'CNG) motif refers to repetitive RNA sequences where N is any nucleotide, commonly found in transcript regions like 5' or 3' UTRs. These repeats can form stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures, influencing post-transcriptional gene regulation. They are particularly significant in microsatellite expansion disorders, where expanded CUG or CAG repeats (a subtype of CNG) are pathogenic. For example, in myotonic dystrophy type 1, expanded CUG repeats sequester RNA-binding proteins like MBNL1, disrupting alternative splicing and leading to disease. Their structural integrity is thus crucial for function and dysfunction.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-1-(oxan-4-ylmethyl)quinolin-2-one, also referred to as SMSM69, is a non-planar coumarin analog featuring a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and an imidazopyridine-thiazole DE-fused core, carrying a 1 charge, which is built upon the quinolin-2-one scaffold and has a distinctive feature of the oxan-4-ylmethyl (tetrahydropyran-4-ylmethyl) substituent at the N1 position, a six-membered saturated ether ring that introduces an oxygen atom acting as a hydrogen bond acceptor, a strategy frequently employed in drug design to balance lipophilicity and hydrophilicity aiming to optimize oral absorption and distribution, the molecule retains the 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl pharmacophore at C3 and the permanently positively charged quaternized (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium group at C7, recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatic interactions verified by FP and SAR data, acting as a selective RNA ligand, and this combination of a cationic center and a polar ether side chain suggests that it may be designed to target specific membrane-associated proteins or as a component in anti-cancer therapy, leveraging the well-known anti-cancer potential of quinoline derivatives with enhanced pharmacokinetic properties.

RNA information: The RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element from HIV-1, with its three-dimensional architecture determined by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its functional role is to specifically bind the viral Rev protein, facilitating the nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—a critical step in viral replication. The binding pocket is highly conserved, with key residues including G70 and C61 being essential for molecular recognition, as mutations significantly disrupt ligand binding. This RNA serves as a promising target for antiviral therapeutics, as demonstrated by its selective interaction with synthetic ligands like thienopyridine derivatives, which block the essential Rev-RRE interactio
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 13, is a thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-based small molecule specifically designed to target the HIV-1 RRE RNA, which is based on the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, with the carboxamide at the 2-position linked to a 1H-benzimidazol-2-yl group; it achieves binding by forming key hydrogen bonds between its amide linkage and the phosphate group of nucleotide G70, and mutagenesis studies confirm the criticality of residues G70 and C49 for this interaction, as their mutation severely disrupts binding activity, making it a pioneering RRE-binding ligand and a valuable structural template for developing novel antiviral agents targeting RNA-protein interactions, while benzimidazole is an important bicyclic heterocyclic pharmacophore found in a wide array of drugs, including antiviral, antifungal, antihypertensive agents, and proton pump inhibitors, and linking benzimidazole via an amide bond to the thienopyridine scaffold is intended to develop molecules with multiple biological activities, and the heterocyclic nature of benzimidazole makes it an excellent hydrogen bond donor/acceptor, crucial for stable complex formation with enzyme or receptor active sites, and this molecule is likely a candidate for a kinase inhibitor or receptor antagonist, with potential antiviral or anti-cancer applications.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[3-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]propyl]-N,N-dimethylpyridin-1-ium-4-amine is a dicationic structure, where one cation is the core pentacyclic scaffold, and the other is 4-(dimethylamino)pyridinium, a positively charged derivative of DMAP. The 4-(dimethylamino)pyridinium structure has relatively high electron density and hydrophilicity, adding complexity to the molecule. Functionally, this combination of a large lipophilic scaffold and a basic quaternary ammonium salt is often explored as a mitochondria-targeting agent, utilizing the high negative potential across the mitochondrial membrane for accumulation within the organelle. Potential applications include therapeutic research for diseases involving mitochondrial dysfunction, such as certain cancers or neurodegenerative disorders.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as adenosine 3-monophosphate (3-AMP), is an isomer of Adenosine Monophosphate (AMP), specifically 3Adenosine Monophosphate (3AMP), where a phosphate group is attached to the C3 position of the ribose ring, molecular formula C10H14N5O7P; it is a non-azaaromatic nucleotide structured with an adenine base, a ribose sugar, and a single phosphate group attached to the riboses 3-carbon, lacking planar azaaromatic features, which binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; in terms of real-world activity, while 5AMP is central to cellular energy metabolism, 3AMP primarily exists as an intermediate product during nucleic acid degradation and can be hydrolyzed by 3nucleotidases, also acting as a nucleotide metabolism intermediate and a potential signaling molecule unrelated to azaaromatic sensing, as a naturally occurring nucleotide, it is involved in metabolic regulation, and its concentration changes are an indicator of cellular energy status, in biochemical research, it is often used to study and assay the activity of phosphodiesterases and nucleases, and these phosphorylated nucleosides are also explored as agents to improve myocardial metabolism, suggesting potential anti-myocardial ischemia effects.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as the saturated analog of DAPI (whose full name is 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide), is a compound featuring a dihydroindole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, with the formula C16H16N4, while DAPI is a fluorescent stain binding AT-rich DNA regions, widely used in microscopy for nuclear labeling and is cell-impermeant unless membranes are compromised, making it useful for viability assays and chromosome visualization; this compound is the saturated analog of 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, the saturation of the indole ring removes aromaticity and significantly increases the flexibility and changes the 3D shape of the molecule, this structural change dramatically influences its binding mode and affinity for the DNA minor groove, and this compound is investigated to explore the impact of molecular rigidity/flexibility on the activity of DNA-binding drugs, showing potential for anticancer and anti-infective activity.

RNA information: 5'CNG/3'CNG refers to repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide), commonly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 20 (DAPI, 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), is a compound containing an indole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, one imidamide directly attached to the indole ring and the other attached via a phenyl ring at the C2 position of indole, with the formula C16H14N4, and it is also a commercially available guanidino/amidino-containing compound, a routine nuclear stain and chemical progenitor of derivative D6; this indole diamidine combines the lipophilicity of indole with the cationic nature of the diamidine groups, and it can bind influenza A RNA promoter (causes NMR perturbations), AT-rich dsDNA minor groove, and r(CAG)exps 5CAG/3GAC loop, disrupt r(CAG)expMBN1, correct splicing defects in Huntingtons/ataxia type 3 models though it has cytotoxicity that limits direct optimization, this combination also results in an effective DNA minor groove binder with potent antimicrobial and antitumor activity, and it is researched for developing new anticancer agents with high target specificity and enhanced pharmacokinetic profiles.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

Small_molecule_information: [2-(6-hydroxy-1H-indole-3-carbonyl)-1H-imidazol-5-yl]-(1H-indol-3-yl)methanone, also referred to as PK4C9, is a complex multi-heterocyclic compound whose core is an imidazole ring substituted at positions 2 and 5 by two different indole moieties, one of which contains a hydroxyl group at position 6, and PK4C9 mainly binds to the pentaloop conformation of TSL2 and induces its transformation to the triloop conformation, this conformational change can enhance the splicing efficiency of exon 7 (E7) of the SMN2 gene and promote the production of full-length SMN protein containing E7, both indole and imidazole rings are important nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics that provide a planar, conjugated system and potentially metal-coordinating capabilities, indole derivatives are common in natural products and often exhibit broad biological activity, this structural combination (multiple planar aromatic rings and hydrogen bond donors/acceptors, such as the imidazole NH and the hydroxyl group) confers the potential to act as an enzyme inhibitor or receptor ligand, specifically, the imidazole-indole scaffold is often found in certain kinase inhibitors or molecules used to modulate neurotransmitter receptors, the introduction of the 6-hydroxyindole moiety may be used to affect its metabolic stability and affinity for biological targets. -SMN2

Small_molecule_information: trimethyl-[2-[[5-methyl-11-(2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]ethyl]azanium diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with a diiodide counterion. It features a trimethylazanium side chain at position 7 and a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino substituent at position 11. It combines the polyaromatic nature of benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline with the strong positive charge of two quaternary ammonium centers. This compound is studied for the development of drugs to combat drug resistance. Particularly in anti-infective applications, its cationic nature allows for strong binding to negatively charged components on the bacterial surface, thereby disrupting cell membrane integrity, positioning it as a potential antibiotic replacement.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[3-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]propyl]pyrimidin-2-one is a complex structure featuring a giant cationic polycyclic scaffold connected via a propoxy linker to a cytosine derivative (4-aminopyrimidin-2-one). The core pentacyclic framework (henicosa-octaene) has multiple fused aromatic rings, ether linkages, and a quaternary ammonium center (13-azonium), giving it high lipophilicity and a positive charge. The pyrimidinone moiety, a nucleobase analog, is introduced to facilitate its interaction with biomacromolecules like DNA or RNA. Functionally, compounds with this large cationic polycyclic structure are often studied as novel antimicrobial agents or cytotoxic agents. Their activity is presumed to be based on strong binding to cell membranes and nucleic acids due to their cationic nature. Potential applications include screening for broad-spectrum antibacterial drugs or novel anti-tumor lead compounds.

Small_molecule_information: 4,5-dioxo-1H-pyrrolo[2,3-f]quinoline-2,7,9-tricarboxylic acid, also referred to as Pyrroloquinoline quinone (PQQ), is a complex polycyclic compound with the formula C14H6N2O8, and it is also a planar azaaromatic redox cofactor structured with a tricyclic ring system (fused pyrrole, quinoline, and quinone rings) which is based on the pyrrolo[2,3-f]quinoline framework, featuring two ketone groups at positions 4 and 5, three carboxylic acid groups at positions 2, 7, and 9these carboxylic acid groups are key for its electron transfer ability. This highly functionalized, multi-ring structure provides multiple sites for metal chelation and potential biological interaction, and it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis. Structurally, it bears resemblance to the cores of biologically active natural products, such as certain quinones and tricarboxylic acid derivatives; its core role is supporting bacterial enzyme function as it acts as a coenzyme for dehydrogenases, facilitating redox reactions in metabolism, and it is also studied for potential antioxidant effects in eukaryotes. In real-world research, compounds with such skeletons are typically explored as potential tyrosine kinase inhibitors or DNA topoisomerase inhibitors, targets that are crucial in the development of anticancer drugs. Furthermore, its numerous oxygen and nitrogen atoms suggest it could act as an effective metal ion chelator, potentially applicable in environmental or pharmaceutical contexts, though its specific application requires further dedicated study.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis(4-carbamohydrazonoylphenyl)benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound P6, is a symmetrical, linear aromatic compound built upon a terephthalamide RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 11° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. Small_molecule_information: 4-[2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)iminohydrazinyl]benzenecarboximidamide, is a compound containing bis-imidamide groups linked by an iminohydrazinyl bridge, with the formula C14H16N6. This structure is unique, offering more conformational flexibility compared to rigid diamidines. While its bis-imidamide nature suggests DNA binding potential, its complex linkage may lead to different binding modes. Such compounds are explored for their metal-chelating or redox properties and are being studied for the development of anti-infective agents with novel mechanisms of action.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-2-[[4-[(2-amino-4-oxo-5,6,7,8-tetrahydro-3H-pteridin-6-yl)methyl-formylamino]benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid, also referred to as 10-Formyl tetrahydrofolate (10-formyl THF), is a reduced folate derivative closely related to Tetrahydrofolate (THF) and the active one-carbon donor cofactors, and it is a non-azaaromatic folate derivative structured with a tetrahydrofolate (THF) core and a formyl group (-CHO) attached to its 10-position (instead of the 5-position in folinic acid) which lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; specifically, the N-5 formyl and N-10 substitution pattern makes its structure resemble N{10}-Formyltetrahydrofolate, and this molecule acts as a crucial cofactor for one-carbon unit metabolism, transferring formyl units specifically for the early steps of purine synthesis which is essential for building the purine rings in DNA and RNA while also supplying the formyl group required for the synthesis of C-2 and C-8 positions of the purine ring, and its research value in the real world lies in its use as a probe or inhibitor for folate metabolism-related enzymes, which is important for understanding cell proliferation and metabolic disorders such as megaloblastic anemia and cancer.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-chlorophenyl)-3-methylquinoline-5-carboxylic acid, also referred to as CP15, is an aromatic carboxylic acid based on the quinoline scaffold, substituted at the 2-position with a 2-chlorophenyl group, at the 3-position with a methyl group, and at the 5-position with a carboxylic acid group; CP15 is a chloroquinoline carboxylic acid with a methyl group, and the presence of both chloro and carboxylate groups led to a high-affinity site, but the compound was not pursued for detailed mechanistic studies. Quinoline carboxylic acids are commonly used as lead structures for anti-inflammatory drugs or antineoplastic agents. The carboxylic acid group is key for ionic interactions with the targets active site, being negatively charged at physiological pH. The 2-chlorophenyl group imposes steric hindrance, restricting rotation and influencing the overall conformation. This molecule is likely a candidate for a tyrosine kinase (TK) inhibitor or an anti-inflammatory agent, relying on its planar aromatic system and the ionic interaction of the carboxylate.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-chlorophenyl)propyl]-7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as BRX1675, is a synthetic flavin analogue and a synthetic analog of FMN (flavin mononucleotide) whose structural design is optimized based on BRX1555, with the 7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione scaffold as its core: the ribitol side chain of riboflavin is replaced by a propyl chain containing a 4-chlorophenyl group, with the introduction of halogen substituents (such as chlorine atoms) to enhance binding ability and selectivity with the FMN riboswitch, significantly increasing the molecules hydrophobicity, with the molecular formula CHClNO; this structural modification not only improves the molecules binding affinity but also enhances its selectivity across different bacterial strains, and BRX1675 exhibits high binding efficiency, which has been proven to effectively induce conformational changes in the riboswitch through SHAPE chemical probing technology and transcription termination assays, further confirming its ability to bind to the riboswitch, and the selectivity of BRX1675 among different bacterial strains indicates that targeted action against specific bacteria can be achieved through chemical modifications, and these research findings provide an important structural basis for the development of more effective antibacterial drugs, and are particularly significant in exploring how chemical modifications can enhance antibacterial activity and selectivity, while such structural modifications often lead to flavin analogues being used to study enzyme inhibitors or as photosensitizers, potentially possessing applications in photodynamic therapy.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[(E)-2-(3-fluorophenyl)ethenyl]cinnoline, also referred to as CP10, is a compound based on the cinnoline core, connected to a 3-fluorophenyl group via a trans-ethenyl linker (i.e., a trans double bond, with its structure featuring a naphthalene-fused heterocycle linked to the 3-fluorophenyl ring through this trans double bond), with the formula C16H11FN2; the molecule possesses a rigid conjugated system structurally related to stilbene, though its conjugated system and fluorine atom were not sufficient to confer strong, specific binding as reflected in its poor binding parameters, and cinnoline derivatives are associated with kinase inhibition, this type of compound is explored as a receptor ligand or signaling pathway modulator, potentially exhibiting anti-inflammatory, anticancer, or utility in the study of cardiovascular diseases through its interaction with biological targets via its extended conjugation.

RNA information: TSL2 is a stem-loop RNA structure overlapping the SMN2 exon 7 5' splice site. Its conformation regulates splicing accessibility—destabilizing mutations or ligand binding (e.g., PK4C9) promotes exon inclusion by exposing the 5' ss.
Small_molecule_information: [2-(1H-indole-3-carbonyl)-1H-imidazol-5-yl]-(1H-indol-3-yl)methanone, also referred to as the bisindole alkaloid with two indole rings connected by an imidazole-4,5-dicarbonyl linking bridge, is structurally highly similar to [2-(6-hydroxy-1H-indole-3-carbonyl)-1H-imidazol-5-yl]-(1H-indol-3-yl)methanone (which also features the same imidazole-4,5-dicarbonyl linking bridge connecting two indole rings), but critically lacks the hydroxyl group at the 6-position of one indole ring; it features an imidazole core substituted by two indole moieties via carbonyl linkages, and its rigid, planar structure allows it to insert into the hydrophobic pockets of biological macromolecules (such as proteins or RNA) or undergo stacking interactions with them, thereby exerting its biological functions, in addition, this structure presents multiple planar aromatic rings and amide/ketone functionalities, lending it significant hydrophobicity and the ability to bind within protein pockets, due to its core resemblance to natural product scaffolds (such as certain alkaloids), molecules of this type are often screened as potential pharmaceutical lead compounds, the indole-imidazole framework is a common core for designing novel antifungal, antiviral, or antitumor agents, and the absence of the hydroxyl group compared to [2-(6-hydroxy-1H-indole-3-carbonyl)-1H-imidazol-5-yl]-(1H-indol-3-yl)methanone results in lower polarity and increased lipophilicity, which could significantly alter its absorption, distribution, and metabolism properties in vivo, as well as its affinity for specific targets like DNA or particular protein kinases.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-piperazin-4-ium-1-yl-3-[7-(trifluoromethyl)imidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl]chromen-2-one, also referred to as C36, is a highly optimized non-planar coumarin derivative (analog) with the molecular formula C21H18F3N4O2, carrying a 1 charge and featuring a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system; it combines the fluorescent properties of coumarin with the pharmacologically active imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine scaffold, further enhanced by a trifluoromethyl (CF3) substituent on the imidazopyridine ring, where the trifluoromethyl group significantly improves the molecules lipophilicity, metabolic stability, and binding affinity for hydrophobic enzyme pockets, while the piperazin-4-ium group at position 7 provides the positive charge and water solubility, and this compound can recognize the single-G bulge of RNA1, with the binding driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics (confirmed by FP and SAR comparison) serving as a selective RNA ligand; such functionalized imidazopyridine derivatives are often used as kinase inhibitors or GABA receptor modulators, therefore, the compounds activity may include anti-tumor and anti-inflammatory effects, or it may function as a biologically active molecule with excellent pharmacokinetics and fluorescent tracing capabilities.
