Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

RNA information: TERRA (Telomeric Repeat-containing RNA) is a long non-coding RNA essential for telomere maintenance. It forms a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by three G-tetrads, which is more stable than non-canonical two-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies using reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays demonstrate that TERRA's rG4 causes significant stalling (~40%) under potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, confirming its stable formation. This structural integrity is crucial for its role in telomere length regulation and heterochromatin formation. Notably, ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS act as effective molecular chaperones, significantly inducing and stabilizing the TERRA rG4 structure even under unfavorable ionic conditions (Li⁺), highlighting its dynamic nature and functional importance in genome stability.; TERRA (telomeric repeat-containing RNA) is a well-characterized RNA that forms stable G-quadruplex structures, with its sequence featuring repeated G-tracts . It binds selectively to RGB-1, showing a 1:1.2 binding stoichiometry and inducing a Tₘ increase of the RNA G-quadruplex upon binding. As a 5'UTR insert in reporter mRNAs, it mediates translation repression by RGB-1 in vitro and HEK293 cells, serving as a model for studying RNA G-quadruplex-related translational regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 5-(furan-2-yl)-2-methyl-3H-thieno[2,3-d]pyrimidin-4-one, also referred to as Molecule 2, is a compound containing thienopyrimidine and furan heterocyclic rings which is a half-fragment analog of RGB-1, lacking RGB-1s symmetric structure; its structure is based on the thienopyrimidine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyrimidine ring, substituted with a keto group at position 4, a methyl group at position 2, and a furan-2-yl group at position 5, and unlike RGB-1, it shows no detectable binding affinity or stabilizing effect on RNA G-quadruplexes (e.g., TERRA) or other nucleic acid structures (DNA G-quadruplexes, stem-loop/dsRNA), with no impact on their melting temperatures, has no effect on RTase elongation for TERRA-containing or non-structured RNA, and fails to alter translation of G-quadruplex-containing mRNAs or NRAS protein expression in cells, serving as a negative control; in terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically exhibits biological activities such as antibacterial, antifungal, or anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)-4-(trifluoromethyl)aniline, also referred to as Compound 4e, is a neutral nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compound; it is a 2-aminomethylene-dihydropyrrolo[3,2]-pyridine derivative synthesized via ruthenium-catalyzed [222] cycloaddition, amine substitution, and Boc deprotection, binds pre-miR-21 with high affinity and good selectivity over duplex DNA/tRNA, inhibits Dicer cleavage of pre-miR-21 and targets the G28-A29 site, with its activity slightly lower than 4a likely due to minor structural differences in its CF3-substituted aniline moiety affecting H-bonding or -stacking with pre-miR-21, and its scaffold is the dihydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine bicycle, where the C6 position is linked via a methylene bridge to a para-trifluoromethyl aniline group; the trifluoromethyl group is a common highly electron-withdrawing and lipophilic substituent in drug molecules, incorporated to improve metabolic stability and membrane permeability, the dihydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine core is frequently found in neuroactive compounds, and this compound is investigated as a potential G protein-coupled receptor ligand or a monoamine transporter inhibitor, targeting systems relevant to mood, pain, and neuropsychiatric disorders.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-7-fluoro-2-methyl-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-10-oxo-4-oxa-1-azatricyclo[7.3.1.05,13]trideca-5(13),6,8,11-tetraene-11-carboxylic acid is a derivative of the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics. Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(4-acetylpiperazin-1-yl)-3-(7-fluoroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as C30-Ac, features a coumarin (chromen-2-one) scaffold substituted at the 3-position with a 7-fluoroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group and at the 7-position with a 4-acetylpiperazin-1-yl group, and while such coumarin derivatives are known for diverse biological activities, in this compound the acetylation of the piperazine prevents protonation and removes key ionic contacts necessary for high-affinity RNA recognition, thereby weakening its selective G-bulge targeting relative to its non-acetylated counterpart and positioning it as a structurally interesting but functionally attenuated ligand that may still serve as a fluorescent probe or scaffold relevant to enzyme or receptor modulation.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]ethylamino]ethylamino]ethyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 11, is an acridine-triazine dimer with acridine substituted at position 4, and the linker chain is a polyethylamino chain containing three ethylamino units (a potent dimeric ligand featuring an oligoamino linker), molecular formula C57H60N20O2; it exhibited strong activity with notable improvements over the monomer and a substantial bivalent effect, though it was not cell-permeable. In terms of real-world activity, its role as a bifunctional DNA binding agent is similar to 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[3-[3-[3-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]propylamino]propylamino]propyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, aiming for high cytotoxic activity through DNA intercalation and strong electrostatic attraction. The polyethylamino chain offers a higher charge density, enabling tighter binding to the DNA backbone. This compound is designed to study the effect of linker chain length and chemical nature on DNA binding specificity, topoisomerase inhibitory activity, and intracellular drug transport, serving as a potential anticancer drug candidate.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3S,4R,5R)-2-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-5-methanidyloxolane-3,4-diol;cobalt(3);[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-(5,6-dimethylbenzimidazol-1-yl)-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl] [(2R)-1-[3-[(1R,2R,3R,4Z,7S,9Z,12S,13S,14Z,17S,18S,19R)-2,13,18-tris(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-7,12,17-tris(3-amino-3-oxopropyl)-3,5,8,8,13,15,18,19-octamethyl-2,7,12,17-tetrahydro-1H-corrin-21-id-3-yl]propanoylamino]propan-2-yl] phosphate is an extremely complex Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin) derivative. Its core is a Corrin Ring containing a cobalt ion (Co3), with the Corrin ring linked to multiple long side chains via amide bonds, and axially coordinated by 5,6-dimethylbenzimidazolyl and adenosyl groups. The molecular formula is C71H104CoN17O16P. This compound is a water-soluble vitamin and an essential micronutrient. In terms of biological function, it serves as a cofactor for numerous enzymes, participating in critical reactions in amino acid, fatty acid metabolism, and DNA synthesis, and is vital for nervous system function and red blood cell formation.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(Z,2S,7S)-7-amino-1,8-bis[(2S)-2-[[(E)-3-(3-aminopropylamino)-3-oxo-1-phenylprop-1-en-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-1,8-dioxooct-4-en-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7Z (a one-armed benzo[g]quinoline analog in which one heteroaromatic intercalating group is removed), is a peptidomimetic molecule characterized by a cis (Z) double bond in its core eight-carbon chain; the Z-configuration enforces a rigid bent conformation on the main chain, which is crucial for the molecules ability to achieve precise spatial arrangement and relative distance between its functional groups (like benzo[g]quinoline and polyamine side chains) when binding to a biological target (such as a receptor pocket or DNA minor groove), and this molecule combines a benzo[g]quinoline group (for DNA intercalation potential) with a dehydro-phenylalanine derivative (for enzymatic stability), while Compound 7Z retains moderate affinity for the HIV-1 FSS RNA (showing about a 200-fold decrease compared to fully dimeric analogs), this loss of potency highlights the structural necessity of both intercalating arms for strong and selective RNA binding, and though it does interact with the FSS, its affinity is insufficient to significantly alter frameshifting or inhibit viral infectivity, serving instead as a structural probe that validated the importance of full dimer symmetry in the active series, and this integrated design, combining a rigid bent conformation with enzymatic stability, aims to discover a ligand with high conformational specificity for its target, potentially applicable as a novel anti-inflammatory or anti-cancer drug that acts by precisely matching the geometry of the target site.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: 2-[1-[[2-(methylamino)pyrimidin-5-yl]methyl]piperidin-3-yl]-4-thiophen-2-yl-1H-pyrimidin-6-one, also referred to as Ribocil, is a synthetic small molecule with an overall U-shaped rigid skeleton, this complex molecule has a substituted pyrimidin-6-one core, linked at the 2-position to a substituted piperidine ring, which is further connected to a 2-(methylamino)pyrimidinyl-methyl group, the 4-position is substituted with a thiophenyl group, its left 6-thienyl-pyrimidinone ring can intercalate into the stacking plane of the RNA double helix, while the right methylamino-piperidinyl group forms weak hydrogen bonds with key guanines, such multi-heterocyclic and complex structures are characteristic of many small-molecule drugs, this compound can highly specifically bind to the aptamer pocket of the bacterial FMN riboswitch, mimicking the conformation of the natural ligand FMN and blocking the binding of FMN to RNA, thereby relieving the negative feedback inhibition of the riboswitch on the riboflavin synthesis gene ribB, leading to impaired bacterial growth due to riboflavin depletion, and importantly, its activity can be completely reversed by exogenous riboflavin, meanwhile this compound is often designed to target specific protein kinases, by inhibiting key kinases, it may find applications in the treatment of cancer, inflammation, or autoimmune diseases.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]ethanamine is composed of the cationic pentacyclic scaffold linked via an ethoxy group to a primary amine (ethanamine). At physiological pH, the primary amine is typically protonated, forming another cation, thus allowing the molecule to exhibit dicationic or zwitterionic character. The primary amine is a common functional group in medicinal chemistry, an important hydrogen bond donor and acceptor, and can also serve as a conjugation site. Functionally, large lipoidal derivatives containing primary amines are often used as novel liposomal carriers, capable of efficiently forming stable nanoparticles with negatively charged nucleic acids for targeted gene drug delivery.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) commonly in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing, underpinning disease pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-5-(4-chlorophenyl)furan-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 17 (2015), is another complex aromatic heterocycle centered on a benzimidazole core, and Benzimidazole 17 (2015) is a 2-phenyl-benzimidazole-4-carboxamide derivative engineered to selectively bind the toxic r(CUG)109 RNA expansion that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1, the benzimidazole is linked at position 2 via a phenyl ring to an amide bond, which in turn is connected to a furan ring substituted with a 4-chlorophenyl group, compared to N-[4-[6-(furan-2-carbonylamino)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenyl]furan-2-carboxamide, this structure is simpler, but its side chain is more lipophilic, incorporating a chlorine atom (4-chlorophenyl) to modulate its lipophilicity, metabolic stability, and affinity for its target, the molecule exhibits high planarity and an extended conjugated system, enabling strong binding to biomacromolecules such as proteins, enzymes, or DNA, the combination of a chlorine-substituted phenyl group and a benzimidazole is a common motif in drug design, often employed to enhance the molecules biological activity, this compound is investigated for potential anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, or antitumor properties, likely acting by inhibiting specific enzymes or protein-protein interactions, by mimicking the recognition surface of MBNL1, it engages the periodic UU internal loops of the CUG hairpin with notable potency in TR-FRET displacement, displaces MBNL1 from ribonuclear foci, and restores correct alternative splicing of the cTNT mini-gene in DM1 cell models without altering DMPK mRNA abundance or affecting off-target RNAs such as human A-site rRNA or r(GGCC)4, its drug-like physicochemical profilemoderate molecular weight (352 Da), cLogP 3.9, topological polar surface area 111 , and negligible cytotoxicity up to 100 Mdemonstrates that uncharged, benzimidazole-based small molecules can selectively drug structured, repetitive RNA motifs and rescue disease-relevant splicing defects. Small_molecule_information: 1-ethyl-7-methyl-4-oxo-1,8-naphthyridine-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as nalidixic acid, is a 1,8-naphthyridone structure characteristic of Quinolone antibiotics, which is a quinolone class of antibacterial drugs characterized by a naphthyridine ring attached to a carboxyl group; this class of drugs is a widely used broad-spectrum antibiotic in clinical practice, treating bacterial infections by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase, and while it has a strong binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, it may not trigger biological function as it does not induce gene expression.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: BS-xpt(A24G) is a mutant of Bacillus subtilis BS-xpt guanine riboswitch, with adenine at position 24 (aptamer core, near ligand-binding site) mutated to guanine. Structurally, it disrupts wild-type core folding, as position 24's nucleotide requirement relies on sequence context. Functionally, it has no detectable 2AP binding, impairs guanine binding, and likely reduces in vivo transcriptional termination regulation efficiency compared to wild-type BS-xpt.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: r(CUG)109 is a pathogenic RNA motif composed of 109 CUG repeats that folds into a stable, elongated hairpin structure containing periodic 1×1 UU internal loops. It functions as a toxic RNA by specifically binding to and sequestering muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, thereby disrupting its normal splicing regulation and contributing to the molecular pathology of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Small molecules that selectively bind r(CUG)109 can displace MBNL1, restore aberrant splicing patterns, and alleviate disease-associated phenotypes in cellular models .

Small_molecule_information: acridine-3,6-diamine, also referred to as Proflavine (commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine), is commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine, a representative acridine dye, structured as a tricyclic acridine core substituted by amino groups at positions 3 and 6, molecular formula C13H11N3, and Proflavine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, the acridine scaffold is characterized by extreme planarity and conjugation, in terms of real-world activity, acridine compounds are indispensable tools and drugs in biology and medicine, its most famous application is as a fluorescent stain for nucleic acids; it intercalates into the DNA and RNA double helix, emitting different colors of fluorescence depending on the nucleic acid type and aggregation state, widely used for cell cycle analysis and nucleic acid quantification, as a pharmaceutical agent, acridine dyes, due to their potent DNA intercalation and disruption effects, are developed as broad-spectrum disinfectants/antiseptics (e.g., acriflavine) and cytotoxic anticancer drugs, Proflavine can induce significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that Proflavine is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA. RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[(2R,3R,4S,5R)-3,4-dihydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]pyrimidin-2-one, also referred to as Cytidine, is a fundamental nucleoside in nucleic acids that is a non-azaaromatic nucleoside, made up of a cytosine base (a pyrimidine ring with an amino group) covalently linked to a ribose sugar, lacking planar azaaromatic features, with its molecular formula being C9H13N3O5; it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, while in terms of real-world activity, Cytidine plays multiple critical roles in cellular metabolism, serving as a building block for RNA and a precursor for cytidine nucleotides (e.g., CTP) to support nucleic acid synthesis, being a precursor for RNA synthesis with its phosphorylated derivatives (e.g., CTP) participating in nucleic acid transcription, and additionally, CTP and its derivatives (e.g., CDP) are vital activating molecules in the metabolic synthesis of phospholipids, crucial for cell membrane construction and repair, and in the medical and nutritional fields, Cytidine and its derivatives (e.g., CDP-choline) are often utilized as neuroprotective agents and brain function supporters to improve cognitive function, with its core role being related to nucleic acid synthesis rather than azaaromatic sensing.

RNA information: BS-pbuG refers to a guanine riboswitch from Bacillus subtilis. The structure of BS-pbuG consists of a specific RNA secondary domain, which contains three stem-loop regions (P1, P2, and P3), organized around a three-way junction to form a guanine binding site. This organizational structure is crucial for the function of the ribose switch as it allows RNA molecules to undergo conformational changes when binding to guanine. Functionally, BS-pbuG specifically recognizes and binds to guanine through its aptamer region, thereby regulating the expression of downstream genes. When guanine binds to the aptamer region, it causes rearrangement of RNA structure. This structural change can lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of related genes. This regulatory mechanism of the BS-pbuG ribose switch enables it to respond rapidly when the intracellular guanine concentration changes, thereby playing an important role in the metabolic regulation and gene expression of bacteria.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-(5-piperidin-1-ylpentoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one is a quinolinone derivative. The scaffold includes a 5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one core and an amine-substituted phenyl group linked via a five-carbon chain to a piperidine ring. The quinolinone scaffold is common in anticancer and antimalarial drugs. Piperidine is a common basic tertiary amine, usually protonated at physiological pH. Functionally, quinolinones with such basic side chains are often explored as topoisomerase inhibitors or kinase inhibitors. The piperidine side chain imparts lysosomal targeting (via the ion-trapping effect), with potential application in overcoming multidrug resistance (MDR) in cancer therapy.

RNA information: NRQ mutant is a G-base mutant of NRQ that prevents the formation of the G4 structure.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of a 3-styryl substituted benzo[furo[3,2-b]quinoline with a 2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain, is the most complex quaternary ammonium salt in this series, featuring the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core and an N-methylpiperazine side chain, it binds to the NRQ, exhibits potent anti-proliferative activity particularly in Huh7 cells, was one of the compounds identified in the lower left quadrant of the binding vs. activity plot though it had poor selectivity, and is a highly optimized candidate for anticancer research, aiming for maximum DNA-intercalating potency and favorable pharmaceutical properties.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: (2R)-6-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]hexanamide, also referred to as 2H-K1NPr, is an ultra-large oligomer, a benzimidazole-lysine derivative with the shortest repeating chain (2H-K1NPr is a lysine-based PTA with one N-propyl spacer, which showed moderate potency but was not reported to have strong effects in cellular assays, and it helped illustrate that lysine-based scaffolds generally perform better than simple NMe derivatives but that longer linkers are still required for optimal performance, contributing to understanding spacing effects in the KNPr subclass), containing only one repeating alanine-amide unit linked to the lysine backbone, the shortest linker arm signifies the closest spatial distance between the two benzimidazole active units, which is crucial for achieving specific DNA cross-linking or simultaneous occupancy of two adjacent protein binding pockets, and this precisely short length design makes this compound an excellent tool for studying the minimum cross-linking distance required for DNA minor groove binders, with potential application in the development of novel antitumor drugs targeting specific closely spaced DNA sequences.

RNA information: ATP-54-1 is an active RNA molecule with a length of 54 nucleotides, obtained by analyzing the 5' and 3' deletions of the ATP-binding RNA clone C8-ATP-3. Structurally, its core feature is an 11-nucleotide consensus sequence flanked by base-paired stems (the deletion of the left-hand stem directly abolishes its ATP-binding activity), and all clones containing this structure have an invariant unpaired G opposite the 11-nucleotide consensus sequence, which can fold into a specific secondary structure as a whole. Functionally, it binds ATP with high affinity, with a dissociation constant (Kd) of less than 50 μM, and a conformational change of RNA occurs when binding to ATP (confirmed by kethoxal modification experiments). It exhibits specificity for ATP binding—modification or removal of groups such as the 2' hydroxyl group on ATP and the 6-amino group of adenine significantly reduces binding affinity, and it also serves as an important basis for the subsequent design of the shorter 40-nucleotide ATP-binding RNA (ATP-40-1)
Small_molecule_information: [[(2S,3R,4R,5R)-5-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methoxy-hydroxyphosphoryl] phosphono hydrogen phosphate, also referred to as ADP (with ATP being its closely related triphosphate counterpart), is the standard nucleoside diphosphate, Adenosine 5-Diphosphate (ADP), where the Adenosine is linked to a pyrophosphate group at the C5 position, and structurally, ATP is a nucleotide composed of 1 molecule of adenine (a nitrogenous base), 1 molecule of ribose (a pentose sugar), and 3 phosphate groups linked by phosphodiester bonds, the latter two phosphate groups of ATP are connected via high-energy phosphate bonds, which are crucial for its energy storage, ADP is a key molecule in cellular energy metabolism, converting to ATP through oxidative or substrate-level phosphorylation, functionally, ATP is the energy currency of cells, primarily responsible for storing and transferring energy required for cellular metabolism, cells synthesize ATP through processes such as cellular respiration and photosynthesis; when the high-energy phosphate bonds of ATP hydrolyze, a large amount of energy is released, which can be used for life activities like active transport, substance synthesis, and muscle contraction, additionally, ATP acts as a coenzyme in nucleic acid synthesis and signal transduction, in real-world medicine, the compound is critically important in hematology as a major signaling molecule for platelet activation and aggregation, consequently, drugs like P2Y12 receptor antagonists (e.g., clopidogrel) function by blocking ADPs action to exert anti-thrombotic effects, the compound is widely used in laboratories to study platelet function and energy metabolism, as mentioned in the document, ATP can bind specifically to certain RNAs (e.g., ATP-54-1, ATP-40-1), with its adenosine moiety (adenine ribose) being the key for binding; the triphosphate groups tend to stay away from RNA due to negative charge repulsion, moreover, both ATP and AMP can effectively elute specific RNAs bound to ATP-agarose columns. Small_molecule_information: 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-2,7-dihydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-2-amino-8-bromo-1H-purin-6-one, also referred to as 8-Bromoguanosine-3,5-cyclic monophosphate (8-Br-cGMP), is a modified nucleotide derivative based on the Guanine nucleobase, featuring a non-natural Bromo substituent at the C-8 position of the purine ring, and is a non-azaaromatic cyclic nucleotide analog structured with an 8-brominated guanine base, a ribose sugar, and a 3,5-cyclic phosphate bond that lacks planar azaaromatic features; the most distinguishing feature is the sugar moiety, which forms a unique cyclic phosphonate structure involving C2, C3, and C5 positions of the ribose, resembling a highly constrained cyclic nucleoside; this compound binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, with its core role being a research tool that mimics natural cGMP but resists degradation, used to study cGMP-dependent signaling pathways (e.g., in smooth muscle relaxation); nucleotide analogs are broadly developed in medicine as antiviral agents (e.g., against herpesviruses or CMV) or anticancer drugs, by being incorporated into DNA/RNA or inhibiting polymerase enzymes, and the 8-Bromo and unique cyclic phosphonate modification suggest the molecule is a potential prodrug designed to improve oral bioavailability or enhance inhibitory activity against specific viral or cellular polymerases.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 9-methoxy-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 4, is a pyridocarbazole derivative structurally related to the powerful natural antitumor agent Ellipticine; it is an optimized, selective lead that potently binds the GG internal loop, selectively inhibits RAN translation without affecting canonical translation, prevents hnRNP H sequestration, and visualizes RNA foci in cells (establishing the hairpin as the therapeutically relevant target), while its large planar aromatic system facilitates DNA intercalation and topoisomerase inhibition, a characteristic that makes it a highly promising candidate in the development of new generation chemotherapeutic agents for various solid tumors.

Small_molecule_information: 4-aminobenzoic acid is commonly known as p-Aminobenzoic acid (PABA), an amino acid and carboxylic acid derivative, structured as a benzoic acid molecule with an amino group at position 4 of the benzene ring, molecular formula C7H7NO2, and 4-Aminobenzoic acid is an aromatic amino acid characterized by an amino group and a carboxyl group attached to a benzene ring, in terms of real-world activity, PABA plays a crucial role as a biochemical precursor in microorganisms and plants, it is an essential intermediate required by bacteria for the synthesis of folic acid, sulfonamide drugs (e.g., sulfamethoxazole) exert their potent antibacterial effect by structurally mimicking PABA and competitively inhibiting the key enzyme (dihydropteroate synthase) in the bacterial folic acid synthesis pathway, for humans, PABA is a non-essential nutrient, but its derivatives were once widely used in sunscreens for their effective UVB absorption properties, the figure indicates that 4-aminobenzoic acid has weak binding affinity and does not induce gene expression, suggesting that it may not be an active ligand for 108yjdF RNA.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: The miR-210 precursor is a structured RNA hairpin whose transcription is switched on by the hypoxia-inducible factor HIF-1α binding to a conserved hypoxia-responsive element in its promoter. This “hypoxia riboswitch-like” mechanism maintains cellular homeostasis under low oxygen by rapidly up-regulating mature miR-210, which in turn represses iron-sulfur cluster assembly proteins and other metabolic targets, thereby tilting metabolism from oxidative phosphorylation to glycolysis and protecting cells from hypoxic damage .
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (Ht-N3, a bis-benzimidazole derivative with Hoechst 33258 core, 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethoxy side chain, azide group, and 4-fluorophenylethyl tail replacing FMNs ribose-phosphate), is an azide-containing bis-benzimidazole probe without the tert-butyl groups (compared to N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), its core activity remains the same: strong DNA binding, and it additionally binds 5CGG/3GGC and pre-miR-210, disrupts r(CGG)-DGCR8, restores SMN2/Bcl-x splicing, inhibits tumor growth, has low cytotoxicity while tRNA reduces its selectivity, serving as a probe/lead for FXTAS, CGG-repeat disorders, and miR-210-driven cancers; its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for nucleic acid binding studies and labeling, which enables researchers to map the interaction site of the bis-benzimidazole unit in a biological context using the high specificity of the azide group.

Small_molecule_information: [[(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methoxy-hydroxyphosphoryl] [(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(3-carbamoylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl phosphate is the structure of Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide (NAD), also referred to as -Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD), or its reduced form, NADH; -Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) is a non-azaaromatic coenzyme structured with two nucleotidesnicotinamide mononucleotide and adenine mononucleotidelinked by phosphate groups, lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, while NAD is one of the most important coenzymes in biology, responsible for electron transfer in metabolic pathways, and the molecule plays a central role in cellular redox balance, energy production, and signal transduction, and is a fundamental reagent in enzymology and biochemistry research, with its core function being acting as an electron carrier in cellular redox reactions, critical for energy metabolism, unrelated to azaaromatic sensing. NADNADPH

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-(4-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl)pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-one, also referred to as C5, is a heterocyclic compound built upon a pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-one core scaffold, which is a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge; it is substituted at position 2 with an 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group and at position 7 with an N-methylated piperazin-4-ium cation, recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, the pyrido-pyrimidine scaffold forms the basis for numerous drugs with reported activities in the central nervous system and oncology, the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group is a key active pharmacophore often designed to engage with kinase active sites, the N-methylated piperazinium ring is a positively charged hydrophilic group aimed at increasing water solubility and/or enhancing affinity for anionic targets, and the molecules planar geometry and extensive conjugation facilitate efficient stacking interactions with biomolecular targets, from a structural design perspective, this compound is a candidate for developing targeted therapeutics against neurological disorders or as a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor in cancer treatment.

RNA information: BS-xpt(A73G) is a mutant of Bacillus subtilis BS-xpt guanine riboswitch, with A at position 73 (aptamer core, near ligand-binding site) mutated to G. Structurally, this mutation disrupts the wild-type core folding. Functionally, it abolishes 2AP binding (unlike natural G73-containing aptamers) and impairs guanine binding, potentially reducing its in vivo transcriptional termination regulation efficiency compared to wild-type BS-xpt.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(E)-[10-[(E)-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-ylhydrazinylidene)methyl]anthracen-9-yl]methylideneamino]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Bisantrene, is a complex anthracene derivative with known antitumor relevance, where the anthracene ring (three linearly fused benzene rings) is substituted at the 9- and 10-positions with hydrazone/imine-linked 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) groups; anthracene is a well-known fluorescent chromophore, making this compound highly likely to exhibit strong fluorescence, and the molecule contains two imidazolinium groups and a conjugated hydrazone-imine structure, elements that facilitate binding to biomolecules like DNA, consistent with its use as a DNA-intercalating topoisomerase IIrelated agent, and such compounds are typically designed as DNA-binding fluorescent probes or potential anticancer agents

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(7-aza-4-azoniaspiro[2.5]octan-7-yl)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM50, is a non-planar coumarin derivative where the substituent at position 7 is the 7-aza-4-azoniaspiro[2.5]octan-7-yl group, carrying a 1 charge, this spirocyclic quaternary ammonium salt, formed by the fusion of a three-membered ring and a six-membered ring, introduces three-dimensional complexity and rigidity, the positive charge of the quaternary ammonium center is vital for enhancing water solubility and electrostatic binding, it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, the combined imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group and coumarin core suggest broad potential biological activities, such as a highly selective kinase inhibitor or a GPCR ligand, the incorporation of this spirocyclic substituent aims to optimize the drugs pharmacokinetic properties and/or enhance its binding specificity to the target.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[4-[[4-amino-6-(prop-2-ynylamino)-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 1b, is a highly symmetrical organic molecule featuring a benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide core linked via butyl chains to complex 1,3,5-triazine rings, with prop-2-ynyl (alkyne) groups enabling Click Chemistry applications. The core diamidine structure is well-known in medicinal chemistry for potent antimicrobial, antiprotozoal, and antiparasitic activities, while triazine derivatives are commonly found in anticancer drugs and functional biological molecules. Functionally, 1b retains the RNA-binding capability of its monomeric precursor 1a, effectively targeting expanded r(CUG) repeats (r(CUG)) implicated in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) , and serves as a precursor in copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition to synthesize dimeric ligands with drastically enhanced inhibitory activity against the MBNL1-r(CUG) complex. Additionally, it maintains low cytotoxicity and favorable solubility as a tetrahydrochloride salt, supporting its potential use as a multifunctional biological probe, conjugating agent for targeted delivery or bioimaging, and a candidate for anti-infective or antitumor applications.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: acridine-3,6-diamine, also referred to as Proflavine (commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine), is commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine, a representative acridine dye, structured as a tricyclic acridine core substituted by amino groups at positions 3 and 6, molecular formula C13H11N3, and Proflavine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, the acridine scaffold is characterized by extreme planarity and conjugation, in terms of real-world activity, acridine compounds are indispensable tools and drugs in biology and medicine, its most famous application is as a fluorescent stain for nucleic acids; it intercalates into the DNA and RNA double helix, emitting different colors of fluorescence depending on the nucleic acid type and aggregation state, widely used for cell cycle analysis and nucleic acid quantification, as a pharmaceutical agent, acridine dyes, due to their potent DNA intercalation and disruption effects, are developed as broad-spectrum disinfectants/antiseptics (e.g., acriflavine) and cytotoxic anticancer drugs, Proflavine can induce significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that Proflavine is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA. RNA information: RNA3 is a key model RNA that incorporates both the Drosha site (1x1 UU loop, target of 1) and the adjacent site (1x1 GG loop, target of 2). It binds the dimeric compound 3 with high avidity (Kd = 85 nM), demonstrating simultaneous engagement of both modules.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Targaprimir-96, is the parent, non-covalent small molecule designed to bind selectively to the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96, specifically targeting a UU internal loop within its secondary structure, and by binding to this site, it inhibits the biogenesis of oncogenic mature miR-96, which leads to the upregulation of FOXO1 protein expression and subsequently induces apoptosis in triple-negative breast cancer cells, while showing no significant effect in healthy breast epithelial cells, demonstrating its selective, phenotype-modulating efficacy; this compound is a complex benzimidazole, triazole, and amide derivative, whose structure contains multiple benzimidazole rings, piperazine groups, tert-butyl groups, and a lengthy peptidic mimicry chain, resulting in an extremely high molecular weight and high polar surface area, it presents as a large molecule with extreme complexity and multiple potential hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, and its structure is indicative of a potential Anti-cancer Agent or Antiparasitic Agent (Macromolecular Ligand), often designed to interact with DNA or essential biomacromolecular targets (such as protein kinases or enzymes), leveraging its benzimidazole core and polyamine linkers.

RNA information: RPPH1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) that can form a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by only two G-tetrads, which distinguishes it from canonical rG4s that typically consist of three or more tetrads. This structural configuration contributes to its role in transcriptional regulation. Functionally, RPPH1 has been identified as a regulatory RNA molecule, and its rG4 structure can be stabilized or induced by specific small molecule ligands like **PhenDC3 and PDS. In vitro analyses, including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, demonstrate that the RPPH1 rG4 structure causes approximately 13% stalling efficiency in potassium-containing buffers, confirming its ability to form a stable secondary structure that can impede enzymatic activity. The integrity of this rG4 structure is crucial for its biological function, particularly in the context of gene expression regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,6-N-bis(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-3-yl)pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as 360A, is a pyridodicarboxamide (PDC) derivative and an early prototype of G4 ligands initially identified by Sanofi-Aventis, its chemistry is well-developed, with conjugates like PDC-biotin (for SELEX), PDC-TO (fluorophore), BrdU-linked (visualization), and PDC-XL (cross-linking), and functionally, tritiated forms of this compound aid in demonstrating telomeric G4 existence and impact telomere integrity, while it shows some renaturing activity on rG4s but has limited molecular chaperone ability on unusual rG4s compared to PhenDC3 and PDS; this is a dicationic compound with a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core, linking two 1-methylquinolin-1-ium cationic groups via two amide bonds, this rigid, highly conjugated molecule is itself a well-known G-quadruplex (G4) stabilizer ligand, its two positive charges and planar structure allow it to interact efficiently with nucleic acids, the real-world application of this compound is mainly focused on anticancer drug development and biomolecular fluorescent sensing, as a G4 stabilizer, it selectively binds to and stabilizes G4 structures found in the promoter regions of oncogenes (like cMYC) or telomeric DNA, thereby inhibiting oncogene expression or interfering with telomerase activity, achieving an anti-tumor effect.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: actinium;2-amino-N-[2-[(2-amino-5-carbamimidamidopentanoyl)amino]-3-[3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]anilino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-carbamimidamidopentanamide, also referred to as [N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-2,3-diaminopropanamide] diargininamide (14b), is another radiopharmaceutical intended for Targeted Alpha Therapy, also containing the alpha-emitter Actinium (Ac) atom, and [N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-2,3-diaminopropanamide] diargininamide (14b) is a synthetic nucleobaseamino acid conjugate engineered to occupy the HIV-1 TAR RNA stembulgeloop in place of the viral Tat protein, its organic ligand is a longer, more complex peptide structure that includes two arginine residues (guanidine groups) and a scaffold linking to the methylamino thiazole aniline, it replaces the natural ribose-phosphate backbone with a metabolically stable thiazolearyl core and uses a 2,3-diaminopropanamide bridge to present two arginine side-chains for bidentate electrostatic contact with the TAR bulge, this polypeptide structure is typically designed for highly selective recognition and binding to specific receptors overexpressed on the surface of tumor cells, the complexity of the ligand enhances both its chelation stability for the radionuclide and its affinity for the biological target, making it a highly specific carrier for high-dose alpha-radiation delivery to tumor cells in clinical TAT, in fluorescence-binding assays 14b displays ultra-high affinity, adopts a 1:1 RNA-ligand stoichiometry, and retains good specificity against excess tRNA or dsDNA, in HIV-infected MAGIC-5B reporter cells 14b blocks Tat-mediated trans-activation with notable potency, suppresses viral replication comparably to a reference concentration of AZT, and shows no cytotoxicity up to a high tested concentration, these properties establish 14b as a metabolically stable, high-affinity artificial riboswitch ligand that selectively silences TAR-controlled transcription and constitutes a promising lead for next-generation RNA-targeted antivirals. RNA
