Small_molecule_information: 1-[10-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]-5aH-phenothiazin-10-ium-2-yl]ethanone, also referred to as acetopromazine (acetylpromazine; 10-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-2-acetyl-phenothiazine), is a phenothiazine quaternary ammonium salt derivative in which a 3-(dimethylamino)propyl chain is attached to the 10-position nitrogen and an acetyl group is located at the 2-position, and this tricyclic phenothiazine core can intercalate into structured RNA such as the HIV-1 TAR bulge while its acetyl substituent and protonated side chain contribute additional noncovalent contacts, enabling the compoundoriginally known as a tranquilizerto function as a small-molecule ligand that selectively perturbs TAR recognition complexes and provides a scaffold for developing non-nucleotide antivirals that target viral RNA instead of host proteins.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-7-fluoro-2-methyl-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-10-oxo-4-oxa-1-azatricyclo[7.3.1.05,13]trideca-5(13),6,8,11-tetraene-11-carboxylic acid is a derivative of the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics. Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 3,4-dimethyl-N-[2-(pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl)benzoyl]-2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene-8-carbohydrazide;bromide, also referred to as NaphDH5 which is similar to NaphDH3 but with 1-benzylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and exhibited good G4 stabilization but only moderate FID affinity and no biological effect, with poor drug-likeness, possesses a unique, highly strained 2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene core, which is a fused system of an eight-membered ring and a four-membered ring, along with a carbohydrazide functionality. The molecule contains a quaternized pyridinium ring, specifically the pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl group, linked via a benzoyl moiety, with bromide as the counterion. The combination of this complex, rigid bicyclic system, the carbohydrazide group, and the cationic pyridinium suggests that 3,4-dimethyl-N-[2-(pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl)benzoyl]-2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene-8-carbohydrazide;bromide may be explored as a non-classical -lactam analog antibiotic or as an active site inhibitor for bacterial enzymes, leveraging its rigid structure and cationic charge for target binding. NaphDH5

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[[2-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[[1-[3-[[4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidoyl]amino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methyl]amino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-2-[[1-[3-[[4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidoyl]amino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]-N-propylacetamide, also referred to as 2AU-2 (a dimeric compound with two AU-azide modules spaced by two propylamine units), is a symmetrical bis-head molecule whose core active unit is an amidine and benzamidine derivative; this compound shows enhanced affinity, positive cooperativity, displaces bound proteins, reduces nuclear foci, mitochondrial dysfunction, and caspase-3 activity in SCA10 cells, and its amidine group is an important cationic functional group, the core active system of this molecule is a polyamidine-containing large conjugated planar system, exhibiting potent DNA minor groove binding ability, similar to classic anti-protozoal drugs like Pentamidine, the molecule links two amidine active units via a long chain containing triazole and polyamide segments, forming a bis-head structure designed to enhance DNA binding affinity and potentially achieve DNA cross-linking, therefore, the predicted real-world activity for this molecule is as a highly effective anti-protozoal, anti-fungal agent or a novel broad-spectrum antibiotic.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-chloro-2-(4-methylbenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 11, a chlorinated derivative with a methylbenzoyl group that showed promising RNA-binding properties and was used in comparative studies with other halogenated analogs, shares the complex chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core, its substituents include a 3-amino group, a 4-phenyl group, and a 2-4-methylbenzoyl group, but it is specifically substituted with a chlorine atom at the 9-position, the 9-chloro substitution is a common lipophilicity modifier in medicinal chemistry, significantly increasing lipophilicity, improving membrane permeability, and potentially engaging in halogen bond interactions with the target, this polycyclic structure is typically researched as a candidate for a kinase inhibitor or signaling pathway modulator, with potential anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory activity, and it represents a key molecule in Structure-Activity Relationship (SAR) studies, where substituents are varied to optimize pharmacological properties.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: (4S)-4-butyl-2-[(Z)-[3-methoxy-4-(1H-pyrrol-2-yl)pyrrol-2-ylidene]methyl]-1,4,5,6,7,8,9,10-octahydrocyclonona[b]pyrrole, also referred to as bPGN (a prodiginine-type natural product identified as the primary hit from a high-throughput screen for pre-miR-21 binders), is a complex macrocyclic compound, its structural core is a dipyrromethene structure (a chromophore) linked to an octahydrocyclonona[b]pyrrole macrocycle, the macrocycle is substituted with a butyl group at the 4-position (S configuration), the dipyrromethene moiety has a methoxy group at the 3-position and a 1H-pyrrol-2-yl group at the 4-position, with a Z-configured double bond, this compound adopts a non-planar, flexible structure and is a highly conjugated system, suggesting it will have strong color (as dipyrromethene is a common chromophore in pigments), in terms of biological function, bPGN has the key functional feature of specific affinity binding to the stem-loop structure of pre-miR-21, which thermally stabilizes the RNA and inhibits its Dicer-mediated processing, this novel mechanism leads to the rapid downregulation of mature oncogenic miR-21, subsequent upregulation of tumor suppressor proteins PDCD4 and PTEN, and ultimately a potent anti-proliferative effect in colorectal cancer cells with high selectivity over normal cells, while the dipyrromethene scaffold of this compound is a key part of bile pigments (like bilirubin) and the chlorophyll/heme framework, suggesting that this class of compounds may have photochemical activity or potential as biomarkers/probes, and its macrocyclic structure and hydrophobic substituents may influence its biological membrane permeability or protein binding.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: 7-[8-(cyclopropylamino)-7-methyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl]heptanoic acid, also referred to as BRX1070, is a carboxylic acid-containing pteridine derivative and a synthetic analog of FMN (flavin mononucleotide) whose structural design is optimized based on BRX1027; its structure is based on the benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione core, with a cyclopropylamino group at the 8-position, a methyl group at the 7-position, and a heptyl side chain containing a terminal carboxylic acid group attached at the N10-position, the molecular formula is CHNO, and this introduction of a cyclopropyl substituent at the N8 position further enhances binding ability with the FMN riboswitch, which not only improves the molecules binding affinity but also enhances its activity in transcription termination assays, in addition, through SHAPE chemical probing technology, it has been proven to effectively induce conformational changes in the riboswitch, further confirming its ability to bind to the riboswitch; the cyclopropylamino group is a relatively uncommon substituent that may affect its metabolic stability, in terms of biological function, this compound is a modified flavin core analog, potentially acting as an enzyme substrate or inhibitor, and likely exhibits strong cell membrane permeability, and research on it provides an important structural basis for the development of more effective antibacterial drugs, and is particularly significant in exploring how chemical modifications can enhance antibacterial activity and selectivity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: It serves as an in vitro research tool to detect the binding properties of small molecules to composite RNA motifs: the monomeric Compound 1 has weak binding affinity , while the dimeric Compound 2 shows significantly enhanced affinity for it. Such studies provide key data support for clarifying the selective binding mechanism of Compound 2 to pri-miR-515.; RNA 1 (Drosha 885) is an RNA fragment designed to mimic the Drosha processing site in pri-miR-885, with its core structural feature being the presence of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-885. It is used to detect the binding properties between small molecules and RNA. Such interaction studies provide key data support for screening selective small molecules that target specific miRNA precursors.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1 (Targaprimir-515/885), is a complex molecular probe based on the bis-benzimidazole (DNA minor groove binder) scaffold and modified with an azide group (-N) and bulky tert-butyl groups, and it is also an azide-containing monomeric small molecule that serves as an RNA-binding module coupling to peptidomimetic scaffolds via Cu-catalyzed azide-alkyne click chemistry, which binds similarly to Drosha processing site motifs of pri-miR-515 and pri-miR-885 plus an adjacent motif of pri-miR-515 to inhibit both miRNAs biogenesis and acts as a lead for selective dimers like Compound 2 to aid RNA-targeting small molecule optimization; in chemical biology, the azide group is the key functional group for Click Chemistry, its real-world activity is designed as a chemically taggable DNA-binding probe, and it is used to study the biological targets of the bis-benzimidazole core by attaching a reporter molecule (e.g., fluorophore or biotin) to the binding site via a highly specific click reaction.

RNA information: The duplex sequence is a designed RNA composed of complementary CUG and CAG repeats that form a stable double helix. Structurally, it lacks the mismatches present in expanded repeats, instead adopting a continuous A-form conformation that eliminates the irregular binding pockets exploited by proteins or ligands in single-stranded hairpins. Functionally, the duplex acts as a control to demonstrate whether ligands bind specifically to mismatched hairpin structures or indiscriminately to generic RNA helices. Its inclusion highlights the importance of structural context in ligand recognition and helps identify molecules with true selectivity for pathogenic RNA conformations.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-2-(propylamino)propanoyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 6 (2012), is the (Z)-geometric isomer (cis) of the oligo-peptide analogue (a Z-isomer lacking the benzo[g]quinoline moiety that was designed as a control to evaluate the importance of this heteroaromatic group), meaning it possesses the same connectivity but the alkene bond within the core chain has a different spatial arrangement; this minor change in geometry dictates a profound difference in the molecules overall three-dimensional shape and flexibility, and additionally, without the benzo[g]quinoline moiety, the compound maintains only weak interactions with RNA and loses significant selectivity, which confirms that the quinoline system is essential for high-affinity and discriminating recognition of repeat motifs, structurally it emphasizes the critical contribution of aromatic stacking in RNA binding, and functionally it has no significant biological activity, underscoring its role as a negative control and supporting the rationale for incorporating benzo[g]quinoline in active derivatives; in real-world drug discovery, geometric isomerism is a critical factor, as biological targets are highly sensitive to ligand shape; therefore, (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-2-(propylamino)propanoyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide (also referred to as DCC 6 (2012)) is expected to exhibit drastically different biological activity, potency, and target selectivity compared to its (E)-isomer, potentially acting on entirely distinct biological pathways, and is essential for structure-activity relationship studies.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide;dibromide, also referred to as PymDH4, presented as the dibromide salt, is a symmetric bis-cationic polymethine dye structure that contains a central pyrimidine core with 1-ethylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and showed very poor G4 binding. It features a central pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide core symmetrically linked via Schiff base connections to two permanently charged 1-ethylquinolin-1-ium cations. This molecule is a prime example of a nucleic acid fluorescent intercalator. Its bis-cationic nature and extended planar conjugation allow for strong binding to DNA or RNA through a combination of electrostatic attraction and stacking, typically exhibiting fluorescence enhancement upon binding. In reality, 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide;dibromide is used as a nuclear stain, a DNA quantitation reagent, or a potential anticancer agent designed to disrupt nucleic acid function.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: Benzoic acid is the simplest aromatic carboxylic acid with the formula C7H6O2. Its structure consists of a benzene ring directly attached to a single carboxyl group (COOH), which is the key structural feature of this simple aromatic carboxylic acid. Benzoic acid occurs naturally in many plants, such as gum benzoin and berries. The most significant real-world application of Benzoic acid is its use as a food preservative, commonly added to food, beverages, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals. It primarily functions by inhibiting the growth of microorganisms, especially yeasts and molds, through the reduction of intracellular pH and interference with cellular enzyme systems. Its sodium salt, Sodium benzoate, is often preferred for its better water solubility. Additionally, Benzoic acid is an important precursor for the synthesis of other organic chemicals, including benzoyl chloride and benzoate esters. Relevant data shows that benzoic acid does not have a low Kd value and does not induce gene expression, indicating that it may not be an active ligand for 108yjdF RNA.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: [[4-[azaniumylidene-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-1-ium-2-yl)amino]butylamino]methyl]phenyl]-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-1-ium-2-yl)amino]butylamino]methylidene]azanium, also referred to as Bisamidinium 3, is a structurally complex molecule characterized by a high positive charge, indicated by the presence of multiple imidamide and triazine groups, specifically the terms triazin-1-ium and azanium, suggesting the molecule is highly protonated under physiological pH, carrying multiple positive charges, and it possesses a bisamidinium moiety and triaminotriazine core, engineered to enter cells via the polyamine transport system; this structural patternmultiple strongly basic, planar heterocycles connected by flexible chainsis typical of fluorescent dyes and DNA/RNA binders, and the compound is a CUG-targeting ligand that selectively binds toxic CUGexp RNA with no detectable affinity for dCTG, can disperse MBNL1-CUGexp nuclear foci in DM1 model cells and partially rescue splicing defects, while unlike analog 9, it lacks RNase-like cleavage activity and CTGexp transcription inhibition, failing to reduce CUGexp levels or reverse DM1 phenotypes in Drosophila models; the numerous amino and imino/imidamide structures allow for strong electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged nucleic acid backbone and extensive hydrogen bonding, and in research, this compound is highly likely to be used as a biological probe, such as a nuclear stain or nucleic acid detection agent, with its biological activity centered on antimicrobial or anti-tumor applications through interference with nucleic acid function.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-3-[4-[(4S)-4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl]phenyl]urea, also referred to as Compound H6 which is a third-generation bis-benzimidazole derivative of Hoechst 33258 rationally engineered to enhance RNA-binding affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic r(CUG)exp RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), is a molecule centered on a urea functional group, which connects two similar 4-(4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl units, and Compound H6 retains the DNA-binding benzimidazole core of Hoechst 33258 but is elongated with a hydrophobic p-methoxyphenyl tail that improves its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, in a TR-FRET displacement assay it dissociates MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 yet is less selective than H1, binding with similar affinity to RNAs containing 5CUG/3GCC or 5CGG/3GGC motifs and to the human A-site, in DM1 cell models it partially shifts a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal but cytotoxicity becomes evident at higher doses, thus exemplifying how computational shape similarity can evolve a DNA-binding dye into a chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics, while the 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazoline) rings of 1-[4-(4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-3-[4-[(4S)-4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl]phenyl]urea are strongly basic and are typically diprotic (carrying two positive charges) at physiological pH, making this compound a dicationic species, and the combination of the urea bridge and the dicationic imidazoline groups makes 1-[4-(4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-3-[4-[(4S)-4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl]phenyl]urea an analog of guanidine compounds, which are used in medicinal chemistry to target GPCRs, ion channels, or protein phosphatases, and it is likely designed for the treatment of cardiovascular or metabolic disorders, potentially acting as an -adrenergic receptor agonist or antagonist.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: [4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-(oxolan-2-yl)methanone, also referred to as Compound 13 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative modified on the piperazines secondary amine with R being tetrahydrofuran and synthesized via one-step coupling), has an acyl side chain that is the oxolan-2-carbonyl (tetrahydrofuran-2-carbonyl) group; tetrahydrofuran, a saturated ether ring, increases the molecules hydrophilicity and introduces a stereocenter, and in practice, the tetrahydrofuran ring is often used to mimic saccharide structures or to enhance polar interactions with biological targets due to its oxygen atom acting as a hydrogen bond acceptor, while as an 1-adrenergic receptor antagonist, this polar side chain may alter the drugs tissue distribution and excretion profile, additionally, the compound binds the influenza A RNA promoter similarly to compound 1 and has improved anti-influenza activity, and the presence of a stereocenter suggests potential for stereoselective pharmacological activity.

RNA information: OI-guaA refers to a guanine riboswitch from Oceanobacillus iheyensis. The structural features of this ribose switch include a specific RNA secondary structure, which is usually composed of three stem-ring regions (P1, P2 and P3), and these regions are organized around a three-way junction point, forming the guanine binding site. The function of OI-guaA is to specifically recognize and bind guanine through its aptamer region, thereby regulating gene expression. When guanine binds to the aptamer region, it triggers a rearrangement of RNA structure. This structural change may lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of downstream genes. This regulatory mechanism of the OI-guaA ribose switch enables it to respond rapidly when the intracellular guanine concentration changes, thereby playing a key role in the metabolic regulation and gene expression of bacteria.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: Contains a G bulge (a single guanine nucleotide that is not base-paired).This structure mimics the bulge present in the Dicer processing sites of both the miR-17 and miR-20a precursors.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)phenyl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as the benzimidazole-based RNA-binding substance, is a probe that is based on a mono-benzimidazole scaffold (instead of bis-benzimidazole) but still includes the tert-butyl and azide groups, and its core structure is benzimidazole which is known to have a binding preference for RNA, it also features a methylphenylpiperazine moiety that is crucial for its RNA-binding capability, and the azide group tag facilitates immobilization onto microarrays via click chemistry; mono-benzimidazoles are often precursors for antiviral, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory drugs, the presence of the azide group dictates its real-world activity as a Click Chemistry probe, it is designed to identify the biological targets of this specific mono-benzimidazole derivative through subsequent labeling and target characterization; in terms of binding properties, this compound does not bind to DNA but exclusively binds to specific RNA bulge structures, its binding to target RNAs has moderate affinity, and it specifically inhibits the processing of miR-17, -18a, and -20a by Dicer thereby suppressing the maturation of these oncogenic miRNAs; regarding its biological function, in cells it inhibits the production of miR-18a which in turn leads to the derepression of the STK4 protein and induces apoptosis in prostate cancer cells, additionally its specific binding to pre-miR-18a in cells has been validated using Chem-CLIP (Chemical Cross-Linking and Isolation by Pull-down) technology.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (7S,9S)-9-acetyl-7-[(2R,4S,5S,6S)-4-amino-5-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-6,9,11-trihydroxy-4-methoxy-8,10-dihydro-7H-tetracene-5,12-dione is the chemical name for, or a very close analogue of, the anthracycline antibiotic Daunorubicin (also known as Daunomycin, also referred to as Daunorubicin Hydrochloride), which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound and a cornerstone medication used primarily in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), acting as a potent DNA intercalator that can also intercalate into double-stranded RNA including expanded CUG repeats, directly bind to CUG RNA, stabilize its double-stranded conformation and compete with MBNL1 for binding, thereby releasing sequestered MBNL1 to restore normal splicing patterns, and it can improve cardiac dysfunction and survival in a DM1 Drosophila model, demonstrating its potential for treating DM1-related cardiac dysfunction.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[4-[3-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propylamino]butyl]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-SPD, is a complex bis-benzimidazole dimer featuring a polyamine-like linker that incorporates multiple amine groups, and 2H-SPD employs a spermidine-like scaffold to display the two RNA-binding modules, the amine groups increase flexibility, basicity, and water solubility, this design is often employed to mimic natural polyamines, enhancing interaction with both DNA and cell membranes, in TR-FRET assays, it was more active than ED, BD, or HD analogues, with notable potency, this suggests that the natural polyamine framework provides an appropriate spacing and charge distribution for interacting with r(CUG), its potential activity is as a highly potent DNA binder with enhanced cell penetration for targeted antineoplastic applications, while the potency is notable, it was still less efficient than spermine-derived constructs, no detailed in-cell activity or stability data are given for this specific derivative.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: [5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), is a derivative of riboflavin generated through phosphorylation, which is a coenzyme produced by the phosphorylation of riboflavin; its isoalloxazine ring has a planar aromatic structure, while the phosphate group is flexibly linked to the ring system, and its isoalloxazine tricyclic and ribitol-phosphate skeleton together form a reversible electron-transferring redox center; as the natural ligand of bacterial riboswitches, FMN inserts into the aptamer pocket and induces conformational changes in RNA, causing the expression platform to form a terminator/sequestering loop, and it can also induce RNA structure folding into terminator clips or blocking ribosome binding sites by binding to the SA1 riboswitch upstream of riboflavin synthesis genes and the SA2 riboswitch upstream of transport genes, using its own concentration changes as a signal, which consequently shuts down the transcription and translation of the downstream riboflavin synthesis gene ribB as well as the de novo synthesis and uptake pathways of riboflavin, thereby achieving negative feedback regulation of riboflavin biosynthesis; because it is crucial for bacterial survival and does not exist in mammals, FMN and its riboswitch have become targets for antibiotic design, which can be competitionally occupied by structural analogues such as rosettin and ribocil-C, blocking the supply of riboflavin and forcing bacteria to starve to death.

RNA information: r(CUG)₆ is a short ribonucleic acid fragment consisting of six consecutive CUG repeat units. Structurally, it can form a more stable hairpin secondary structure than short-chain r(CUG)₂/r(CUG)₄, with multiple characteristic U·U mismatches in the stem region and six 5′CUG/3′GUC internal loops (a key feature simulating the structure of long-chain pathogenic CUG repeat sequences). In some cases, its conformational stability can be further enhanced by GC base pairs (similar to the GC clamp design of (CUG)₁₂ mentioned in the document). Functionally, it does not possess the toxicity of long expanded r(CUG)ⁿ ,which forms nuclear foci and sequesters MBNL1 (Muscleblind-like family protein) — but its specific binding affinity to MBNL1 is significantly improved. Competitive dialysis experiments showed that its binding preference for CUG repeats is increased by more than 4-fold compared with MBNL1 protein, and it exhibits a binding stoichiometry of approximately 1:2 (drug:5′CUG/3′GUC internal loop) when binding to small-molecule compounds (such as lomofungin and dilomofungin). Its core role is as an in vitro research tool to analyze the binding mechanism between MBNL1 and CUG repeat sequences, verify the inhibitory effect of small molecules on their interaction, and provide a structural and functional model closer to the characteristics of long-chain CUG repeat sequences for the study of the pathogenic mechanism of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and the preliminary screening of therapeutic drugs
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: The r(CAG)₁₀ sequence serves as a comparative motif to test ligand selectivity against repeat RNAs with A–A mismatches instead of U–U mismatches. Structurally, it forms A-form helical RNA with alternating positive and negative electrostatic stripes similar to CUG repeats, leading to comparable overall architectures. Functionally, r(CAG)₁₀ is not associated with myotonic dystrophy but provides a stringent control for assessing molecular recognition. Because many proteins and synthetic ligands show similar affinity toward both CUG and CAG repeats, this sequence demonstrates the challenges of engineering true sequence selectivity in RNA-targeted small-molecule design.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 42012, is the cis (Z) isomer of (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, a benzo[g]quinoline-containing Z-isomer designed through olefin metathesis, with the double bond in the core oct-4-enediamide scaffold having the cis configuration, and its geometry places the heteroaromatic moiety in an orientation that promotes favorable stacking interactions with RNA bases, compared to earlier disulfide-linked scaffolds, the olefin bioisostere of this compound improves chemical stability and compatibility for cellular studies, structurally, it exemplifies the importance of precise spatial placement of aromatic and peptide elements to achieve cooperative binding across repeat sequences, functionally, it enters cells efficiently and demonstrates biological activity by alleviating RNA-mediated toxicity in model systems, making it a strong lead structure for RNA-targeted small-molecule drug design, this conformational difference introduces a fixed bend in the molecules three-dimensional structure, in complex peptidomimetics, such cis-trans isomerization is often the decisive factor for biological activity, as it affects the precise spatial matching with the target binding pocket, the compound maintains the poly-cationic and highly aromatic features of its counterpart, its potential applications are similar to those of peptidomimetics, but the activity and selectivity profile of (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide will be significantly altered due to the cis conformation, making it a critical tool for studying the impact of molecular conformation on biological activity.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride The side chain of this compound is 2-morpholin-4-ylethyl. The morpholine-ethylamine backbone is a significant motif in drug design, often used to create molecules with optimized membrane permeability and specific enzyme inhibition profiles. In practice, morpholine-ethylamine derivatives are frequently found in kinase inhibitors (targeting pathways like EGFR, VEGFR) and antiviral agents. This structure allows for effective interaction with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions of a target. Its quaternary ammonium nature suggests that it might exhibit antimicrobial or antineoplastic potential in addition to any kinase inhibitory properties.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2,6-diamino-N-[3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]phenyl]hexanamide, also referred to as N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-lysinamide (1b), is a compound with the molecular formula C16H23N5OS, whose structure links the skeleton of the natural amino acid lysine (2,6-diaminohexanoic acid) via an amide bond to an aniline moiety substituted with a thiazole heterocycleTatN-HIV-1 TAR RNA--1bTatTARHIVTARRNAtRNADNAHIVTatHIV

RNA information: r(GGCC)4 is a short, double-stranded RNA motif composed of four tandem GGCC repeats that adopts a fully base-paired hairpin structure. It is commonly used in competition assays as a negative control because it lacks internal loops or single-stranded regions and therefore does not bind to RNA-targeted small molecules such as CB253, which selectively recognize the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp RNA .
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6: This RNA contains six consecutive CAG repeats with AA mismatches, forming multiple internal loops. It is a key target for small molecules due to its structural similarity to pathogenic expanded repeats. It shows high affinity and selective binding to compounds such as pyridocoumarin derivatives, leading to conformational changes and thermal stabilization.; The (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6 RNA contains multiple consecutive A-A mismatch loops. This multi-loop structure enhances conformational stability and ligand binding avidity, more accurately mimicking the expanded toxic repeats found in patient-derived RNA and providing a robust platform for biophysical testing.
Small_molecule_information: 3-methyl-2-pyridin-4-ylquinoline, also referred to as CP4, is a nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound consisting of a naphthyridine fused heterocycle with methyl and pyridine substitutions; its structure uses the quinoline core, with a pyridin-4-yl substituent at the 2-position and a methyl group at the 3-position, the molecular formula is CHN, this compound contains two nitrogen heterocyclic rings and has an extensive conjugated system, in terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, and the introduction of the pyridine ring is often used to enhance coordination capability and modify biological activity.

RNA information: r(AAUU): This RNA serves as a model hairpin construct designed with a specific AU-rich sequence in its stem region, featuring a contiguous 5'-AAUU/3'-UUAA stretch. Its primary function is to act as a tool for probing the binding specificity and affinity of small molecules towards defined AU base pair orientations. The RNA was used in fluorescence-based binding assays, where it helped establish that certain benzamidine-based compounds, like molecule 2, display a significant binding preference for AU base pairs over GC-rich counterparts. Its thermodynamic stability is lower than that of GC-rich hairpins, which is a characteristic feature influencing its interaction with small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 8-amino-7-methyl-1H-benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione is a nitrogen-containing tetracyclic aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure is based on the benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione (flavin core) skeleton, with an 8-amino and 7-methyl substitution on the benzene ring. The molecular formula is C12H11N5O2. This compound is a flavin core with an amino substitution. In terms of biological function, as an analog of the flavin chromophore, this compound may serve as a fluorescent probe or participate in the binding of specific enzymes during redox reactions.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 8-cycloheptyl-1,3,7-trimethylpurine-2,6-dione, also referred to as CP5 which contains a cycloheptane ring connected to a complex imidazolinone and dimethylurea heterocyclic system, is a xanthine derivative similar to caffeine; CP5 has a bulky, multi-heterocyclic structure that showed binding but was likely hampered by poor specificity, preventing its selection for detailed study, while this compound may have bronchodilator, myocardial stimulant, or central nervous system modulatory activity, and is often explored in drug development as a new phosphodiesterase (PDE) inhibitor.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one has a side chain terminal amine group that is morpholine. Morpholine is an oxygen-containing six-membered ring tertiary amine. The introduction of the oxygen atom reduces the basicity of the nitrogen and increases the molecules hydrophilicity. Functionally, the morpholine scaffold is common in many drugs that target kinases or receptors. In practical applications, the morpholine substituent is often used to improve a drugs solubility, and is considered to have a better safety profile due to its relatively lower cytotoxicity, frequently explored as a lead compound for novel kinase inhibitors or anti-inflammatory agents.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[[4-[6-[(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-ylhydrazinylidene)methyl]imidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl]phenyl]methylideneamino]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Compound 2 (H2), is a complex bis-imine and hydrazone derivative. The core structure includes an imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine moiety, a phenyl group, and two 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole rings. Imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine is a fused bicyclic diazaheterocyclic system known for its fluorescent properties and diverse biological activities. The molecule contains two 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) groups, which are connected to the main chain via a hydrazone (hydrazinylidene) and an imine (methylideneamino) linkage, respectively. The imidazolinium structure is often used as a ligand or a biologically active unit. This complex poly-nitrogen aromatic structure recognizes GG internal loops in r(CGG)exp hairpins and binds r(CUG)exp, partially restoring normal reporter signal in DM1 cells, although it is less selective (binding CGG/CCG/A-site RNAs) and shows high-dose cytotoxicity, serving as a tool compound for FXTAS/DM1 therapeutic development, and suggesting potential applications as a fluorescent probe, a ligand for biomacromolecules, or a drug candidate with anticancer or antiviral properties.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-2-amino-3-(1H-indol-3-yl)propanoic acid, also referred to as L-Tryptophan or tryptophan, is an incredibly vital and naturally abundant compound, classified as a non-azaaromatic essential amino acid (meaning it must be obtained through the diet as the human body cannot synthesize it) that lacks planar azaaromatic features, structured with a standard -amino acid backbone (with a chiral -carbon, amino, and carboxyl groups) and an indolyl side chain which is a fused benzene and pyrrole ring (indole ring) attached to a chiral amino acid side chain (-CH-CH(NH)-COOH); the indole moiety grants L-Tryptophan specific hydrophobicity and ultraviolet absorption properties, and it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, while it plays a pivotal role in the body, not only as a fundamental building block for protein synthesis but also as the precursor to several crucial bioactive molecules including the neurotransmitter Serotonin (which regulates mood, sleep, and appetite), the hormone Melatonin (which regulates the sleep-wake cycle), and niacin (vitamin B3), and its metabolites are heavily studied in nutritional and biochemical research for their profound effects on central nervous system function and immune modulation.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-5-hydroxy-2-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxyphenyl)-7-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxy-2,3-dihydrochromen-4-one, also referred to as Hesperidin, is a flavonoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects commonly found in citrus fruits, and is an extremely complex natural product flavonoid O-diglycoside with a dihydroflavone (flavanone) core substituted at the 7-position with a diglycoside unit (likely involving glucose and rhamnose); the numerous phenolic hydroxyl groups and the bulky glycoside moiety are the primary drivers of its activity, and in practice, such compounds are key functional components in herbal medicines, demonstrating potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and free radical scavenging capabilities, while due to its glycosidic structure, it typically has high water solubility and specific oral bioavailability (via gut microbial hydrolysis), and it is widely studied for cardiovascular benefits as well as cardioprotective, antiviral, and hepatoprotective effects, making it a focal point in natural product research.

Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 9-ethyl-3-(2-quinolin-2-ylethenyl)carbazole, also referred to as Compound 29 (a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 2-(2-(9-ethyl-9H-carbazol-3-yl)vinyl) group at the quinoline 2-position and a piperidin-1-yl group at the 4-position), is a neutral molecule linking carbazole and quinoline, valued for its solid-state fluorescence properties; SPR assay shows Compound 29s affinity for A10-RNA-Wt is weaker than that of Compound 24, it was not tested in the dual-luciferase reporter assay so its effect on ADAM10 translation remains unknown, and no explicit cytotoxicity data (IC values) for it is provided in the tested compound tables, and it is used in the development of molecular light-emitting and organic semiconductor materials.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[6-amino-2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]hexanoyl]amino]-5-(diaminomethylideneamino)pentanoic acid, this compound is a complex conjugate of a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core with amino acids. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads.
