Small_molecule_information: 2-pyridin-4-yl-3H-imidazo[4,5-c]pyridine, also referred to as Compound 3 (2015), is a bicyclic heterocyclic compound composed of an imidazopyridine skeleton and a pyridine ring. Imidazo[4,5-c]pyridine is formed by the fusion of an imidazole ring and a pyridine ring at the [4,5c] positions, making it an isomer of purine. The 4-pyridyl group is attached to the 2-position of the imidazole ring. This structure contains multiple nitrogen atoms, allowing it to act as both a hydrogen bond donor and acceptor, and to participate in coordination. Purine and its analogues (such as imidazopyridines) are crucial in nucleotide metabolism and signal transduction, and this compound is optimized to selectively recognize and bind pathogenic r(CUG) RNA expansions, mimicking key -stacking and hydrogen-bond interactions of MBNL1, engaging 11 UU internal loops, displacing MBNL1 from ribonuclear foci, and restoring correct alternative splicing in DM1 cell models, demonstrating that uncharged, heterocyclic small molecules can modulate structured, repetitive RNA motifs, with potential applications as lead compounds for kinase inhibitors, antivirals, or anticancer drugs.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[3-(dimethylamino)-2-hydroxypropyl]-4,6-dimethyl-4H-benzimidazole-5,7-dione, also referred to as Xanthinol (7-[2-hydroxy-3-[2-hydroxyethyl(methyl)amino]propyl]-1,3-dimethylpurine-2,6-dione), has a core of 4,6-dimethyl-4H-benzimidazole-5,7-dione, a structure that contains both a benzimidazole (a nitrogen heterocycle) and a quinonoid diketone (a redox-active moiety), while Xanthinol is a theophylline derivative designed to serve as a neutral, membrane-permeant surrogate of the ribityl phosphate moiety of flavin mononucleotide (FMN) which by replacing the anionic ribose phosphate with a cationic 2-hydroxy-3-[2-hydroxyethyl(methyl)amino]propyl side-chain acquires enhanced lipophilicity and metabolic stability while preserving the hydrogen-bonding face of the xanthine ring, can completely disrupt the TatTARcyclin T1 complex in electrophoretic mobility-shift assays, dose-dependently inhibits Tat-driven LTR trans-activation without affecting a heterologous Rev-RRE control in HeLa reporter assays, and induces large chemical-shift perturbations exclusively in the 5 bulge even at sub-stoichiometric ratios in NMR titrations, confirming RNA-specific recognition analogous to FMN-riboswitch binding thus providing a non-phosphate, non-peptide scaffold for antiviral agents that target viral RNA instead of host proteins, and the N1 position of 1-[3-(dimethylamino)-2-hydroxypropyl]-4,6-dimethyl-4H-benzimidazole-5,7-dione is substituted with a 3-(dimethylamino)-2-hydroxypropyl side chain, which includes a tertiary amine that can be protonated at physiological pH, carrying a positive charge, and a secondary alcohol, with the quinonoid diketone part suggesting redox activity, potentially exerting biological effects by generating reactive oxygen species or participating in electron transfer mechanisms, and this class of molecules is often studied as antimalarial or cytotoxic anti-cancer drugs.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.

Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[4-[4-[2-(diethylamino)ethoxy]anilino]quinazolin-2-yl]phenyl]-3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propanamide is a quinazoline derivative incorporating a complex N-methylpiperazine substituted propanamide side chain. This structure aims to improve solubility and bioavailability and is viewed as a precursor to a highly active kinase inhibitor for treating cancer or autoimmune diseases.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(azetidin-1-ium-3-ylamino)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM32, is a coumarin derivative, with the 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl group at C3, the C7 substituent, linked via an amine, is the azetidin-1-ium-3-yl group, and it is a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge, which recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand; azetidine is a four-membered saturated nitrogen heterocycle, representing the smallest and most strained cationic ring in this series, this highly constrained small cationic side chain is utilized to explore the structure-activity relationship of small molecule kinase inhibitors, aiming to optimize selectivity through conformational rigidity and ring strain, however, the ring strain may also affect its chemical stability, and the unique structure of 7-(azetidin-1-ium-3-ylamino)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one makes it a valuable probe for studying the role of ultra-small cationic side chains in drug delivery and target binding.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)naphthalen-1-amine, also referred to as a compound possessing moderate binding affinity and confirmed to be an active inhibitor of Dicer cleavage, features the 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine core connected via a methylene bridge and a secondary amine to a naphthalen-1-yl group. Naphthalene is a bicyclic aromatic hydrocarbon that imparts significant lipophilicity, a common feature in many drugs. This design merges the lipophilic naphthalene ring with the basic pyrrolopyridine scaffold, aiming for favorable membrane permeability and bioavailability. Naphthylamine derivatives are often explored as ligands for G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs), particularly antagonists for adrenergic or serotonergic receptors. This compound is likely a candidate for cardiovascular or CNS disorders, such as a potential -blocker or serotonin transporter inhibitor.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-3, is an ultra-long bis-head molecule based on the bis-benzimidazole-DNA binder, characterized by the inclusion of four repeating polyamide units as a flexible spacer arm between the two active units, while 2H-3 itself incorporates three propylamine spacers between Ht-N3 modules to increase flexibility and the potential to span larger RNA repeat distances, its added length enhances its ability to engage multiple binding sites resulting in improved activity in cell-based assays and it shows slightly better selectivity than earlier analogs, functionally it can restore FXTAS-associated splicing defects in model systems more robustly than Ht-N3 likely due to a closer alignment with RNA repeat spacing, though its scaffold flexibility still prevents maximal preorganization restricting affinity compared with optimized derivatives like 2H-5; this compounds further increased spacer length is intended for the systematic study of the influence of linker length on the spatial adaptability and binding affinity of multivalent DNA binders, a longer spacer allows the two benzimidazole heads to bind to more distantly separated DNA sites or maintain flexible mobility in more complex biological environments, therefore it is a key molecule for structure-activity relationship studies, potentially applicable in long-range DNA cross-linking or multi-target anti-cancer drugs.

Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-8-methyl-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM36, is a non-planar coumarin derivative characterized by an additional methyl substituent at position 8 of the coumarin ring, possessing a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and an imidazopyridine-thiazole DE-fused system with a 1 charge, position 3 is substituted with the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group, and position 7 carries the chiral (3S) methylpiperazinium cation, which targets the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatic interactions as validated by FP and SAR analysis, acting as a selective RNA ligand, the methyl group at position 8 is likely to impose significant steric hindrance on the molecules binding to the target, thus modulating its activity and selectivity, it may also influence the electronic density and metabolic stability of the coumarin ring, and the chiral quaternary ammonium salt is crucial for improved water solubility and electrostatic binding, this type of structure is frequently designed to target specific protein kinases, such as FLT3 or BTK, for the treatment of cancer or autoimmune diseases.

Small_molecule_information: 6,7-dimethylquinoline is a nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure uses the quinoline core: a benzene ring fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, with methyl substituents at the 6- and 7-positions on the benzene portion. The molecular formula is C11H11N. The compound exhibits a planar conjugated system. In terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, antispasmodic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects.

RNA information: (CUG)₅₀ is a toxic CUG trinucleotide repeat RNA linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it adopts a hairpin structure with multiple 1×1 UU internal loops (its key RNA Motif) and has the sequence 5'-r(CUG)₅₀-3'.Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1, causing RNA mis-splicing; it’s used as a model to screen ligands (e.g., pentamidine, EBAB) via affinity capillary electrophoresis (ACE) for DM1 therapy .
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-acetamidophenyl)-2-[(4-chlorobenzoyl)amino]-1,3-thiazole-4-carboxamide, also referred to as entamidine (PTMD), is a complex thiazole derivative belonging to the amide class of compounds, in which the 1,3-thiazole-4-carboxamide core bears a (4-chlorobenzoyl)amino group at the 2-position and a 3-acetamidophenyl group attached to the nitrogen atom of the carboxamide at the 4-position, and while such a combination of a thiazole ring, chlorinated benzoyl moiety, and acetamidophenyl substituent suggests potential anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, or kinase-inhibition activities, this compound additionally exhibits bisamidinium-like polar interaction properties characteristic of entamidine, functioning as an antiprotozoal/antifungal agent that can reduce DM1 nuclear foci in HeLa cells but displays high toxicity, making it useful as a positive control in DM1 drug-screening assays.

RNA information: A designed Watson–Crick stem-loop RNA with a 7-bp stem and 4-nt loop (AGUG). It serves as a non-G4 control structure. Ligands 2a and 2b bind to it with lower affinity , confirming their specificity for G4 structures.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione, also referred to as a thiophene-fused analogue of 2a with similar side chains, is an organic compound featuring a complex polycyclic core of naphthoquinone and benzothiole, forming the naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione skeleton. This framework is a sulfur-containing heteroaromatic system with a highly conjugated electron structure and redox-active quinone carbonyl groups at positions 5 and 10. The substituents at positions 4 and 11 are two 2-aminoethylamino groups, which introduce primary amines capable of protonation, granting the molecule polycationic character and increasing its water solubility under physiological conditions. This specific structural combination, characterized by its planarity, extended conjugation, redox activity, and cationic side chains, makes it a molecule of interest in biomedicine, typically investigated as a potential DNA intercalator or topoisomerase inhibitor, suggesting potential cytotoxic or anticancer activities; it has moderate cellular uptake, stabilizes RG4s, and also suppresses translation and promotes apoptosis, though less potently than 2a.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-9-chloro-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 10, is a derivative of the complex chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, characterized by the 3-amino, 2-benzoyl, and 4-phenyl substituents. It features a chloro atom at position 9 (and overall contains a chlorine atom) and displayed significant binding affinity for CAG RNA, with effectiveness in gel shift and PCR stop assays indicating strong interaction with target RNA; the molecule is a highly rigid, planar polycyclic compound with multiple potential hydrogen bonding sites and stacking regions, and the chloro substitution serves to fine-tune the molecules physicochemical properties and steric hindrance. This fused polycyclic structure is an important template in anti-cancer drug design, with its pharmacological mechanism often hypothesized to involve DNA/RNA interference or inhibition of key enzymes regulating the cell cycle, and the chemical profile of this compound makes it a candidate for research as a cytotoxic agent or anti-proliferative drug.

RNA information: ATP-40-1 is an ATP-binding RNA aptamer with distinct structure-function traits. Structurally, it contains a conserved 11-nucleotide motif and forms a stable stem-loop secondary structure—both are critical for maintaining its active conformation. Functionally, it binds ATP specifically. It also binds AMP and adenosine but shows no affinity for GTP, UTP, CTP, or ITP. As a key model, it aids in elucidating RNA-ligand interaction mechanisms by linking structural conservation to specific, moderate-affinity binding.
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3R,4S,5S)-3,4-dihydroxy-5-purin-9-yloxolan-2-yl]dihydrogenphosphate, also referred to as Adenosine Monophosphate (AMP), with the chemical structure C10H13N5O7P, is a typical nucleotide molecule. The compound is composed of a purine nucleobase (specifically 1 molecule of adenine), a ribose (oxolan ring), and 1 molecule of phosphate group, linked via the characteristic N-glycosidic bond and phosphate ester bond (additionally connected by a phosphodiester bond), bearing high stereochemical similarity to natural nucleotides like Guanosine Monophosphate (GMP); compared with ATP, it lacks 2 phosphate groups and has no high-energy phosphate bonds, making it one of the basic units of RNA. Nucleotides are the fundamental building blocks of all living organisms, participating in essential life processes such as energy storage (ATP), nucleic acid synthesis (DNA/RNA), and cell signaling (cAMP/cGMP), and for this compound specifically, functionally it mainly serves as a precursor for nucleotide synthesis: it can be phosphorylated to form ADP (adenosine diphosphate), which is further converted into ATP, participating in the substrate cycle of cellular energy metabolism, meanwhile it is an important component of RNA, involved in the transmission and expression of genetic information, and like ATP and adenosine, it can effectively elute specifically bound RNAs from ATP-agarose columns, demonstrating that its adenosine moiety (the core structure shared with ATP) is key for RNA binding, while the number of phosphate groups has little impact on this binding process; thus, if this compound is the natural nucleotide itself, it performs its endogenous physiological functions within the cell; if it is a synthetic analog, it would be designed as an inhibitor or substrate for purine metabolism enzymes or reverse transcriptase, primarily used for antiviral or anti-cancer therapy by being incorporated into nucleic acid chains to cause chain termination.

RNA information: HIV-1 FS RNA is a highly conserved RNA stem-loop element that drives programmed −1 ribosomal frameshifting at the gag-pol junction. By transiently pausing translating ribosomes on the slippery sequence UUUUUUA, it enforces a precise ~20:1 Gag:Gag-Pol protein ratio that is essential for proper virion assembly and replication. Small-molecule ligands such as DB213/RG501 bind in the major groove of the stem-loop, thermally stabilize the RNA and increase frameshifting efficiency; this shift in the Gag/Gag-Pol ratio blocks particle formation, making the frameshift site an attractive, druggable RNA target for novel anti-HIV therapeutics.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as DB213 (1,4-bis-[N-(3-N,N-dimethylpropyl)amidino]benzene tetrahydrochloride), this compound is a benzodicarboximidamide derivative. The combination of the amidine structure and polyamine side chains often endows compounds with the ability to interact with DNA or RNA, and this compound is specifically an RNA-binding small molecule designed to target the HIV-1 frameshift site (FS) RNA stem-loop, which mimics the electrostatic and groove-binding properties of natural ligands but achieves enhanced stability and specificity through its bisamidinium-benzene core flanked by dimethylaminopropyl arms; in vitro, it binds in the major groove of the FS RNA across multiple base pairs, forms hydrogen bonds with phosphate oxygens, and elevates the RNA melting temperature, thereby increasing programmed 1 ribosomal frameshifting efficiency, a shift that disrupts the precise Gag:Gag-Pol ratio required for virion assembly and potently inhibits HIV-1 replication in both acute and chronic cell-culture models while exhibiting a favorable therapeutic index, and such compounds are frequently used in medicinal chemistry as potential antiparasitics, antifungals, or anti-tumor agents, and may also be explored for their inhibitory activity against serine proteases due to their amidine structure.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 5-carbamimidamido-2-[[3-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-2-[[2-[(5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]acetyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]pentanoicacid is a complex peptide-dye conjugate. The molecule combines a peptide chain featuring arginine (guanidine group) and histidine (imidazole group) with a 5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium unit, a positively charged, strongly fluorescent tetracyclic aromatic system known to be an effective DNA intercalating dye. The guanidine moiety (carbamimidamido) of the arginine residue is strongly basic and positively charged at physiological pH, which synergizes with the quinolinium charge to enhance electrostatic interaction with nucleic acids. This molecule is designed as a fluorescent peptide probe, primarily aimed at high-affinity binding and fluorescent signaling of DNA. It is used in reality for detecting specific nucleic acid sequences or as a potential anti-tumor DNA damaging agent.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide is a complex, linear peptidic analog. Its structure features two (2S)-2-(N-methyl-(2S,4R)-4-hydroxy-2-(N-methyl-N-phenylalaninyl-3-aminopropanamide)pyrrolidine-1-carbonyl)butanoyl units linked by a 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole ring, with both termini acylated by a 2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl group. Compared to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, a 4-hydroxy group (4R configuration) is introduced on the proline residue, and the terminal amino group of the phenylalanine is replaced by 3-aminopropylamine. The molecule contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds. This compound adopts a non-planar, flexible conformation. The 4-hydroxy group enhances hydrophilicity and hydrogen bonding capability, and the quinoline core allows for aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interactions. In terms of biological function, peptides and their analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands, enzyme inhibitors, or signaling molecules, and quinoline compounds are broadly known for diverse activities including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, spasmolytic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects.

RNA information: Prokaryotic 16S rRNA has 5', central, and 3' domains; its helix 34  is critical for spectinomycin binding. It assembles into the 30S ribosomal subunit, facilitates mRNA-tRNA interaction in translation, and is a major antibiotic target. Mutations cause bacterial resistance to spectinomycin, affecting antibiotic efficacy against pathogens.
Small_molecule_information: (1R,3S,5R,8R,10R,11S,12S,13R,14S)-8,12,14-trihydroxy-5-methyl-11,13-bis(methylamino)-2,4,9-trioxatricyclo[8.4.0.03,8]tetradecan-7-one, also referred to as Spectinomycin, mainly acts on Gram-negative bacteria like Neisseria gonorrhoeae, clinically treats gonorrhea, and bacterial resistance to it occurs via 16S rRNA mutations (e.g., G1064/C1193) which weakens its therapeutic efficacy; this compound has an extremely complex polycyclic structure with multiple hydroxyl, methylamino, and ether functional groups, its structure strongly suggests it is a natural product or a semi-synthetic analog, often associated with the macrolide antibiotic or related complex polyketide families, and given its complexity and functional groups, this compound is expected to possess potent antibacterial activity, likely by inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis or cell wall formation, positioning it as a potential anti-infective drug.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: Disodium;[(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(2,6-dioxo-3H-purin-9-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl phosphate, also referred to as Xanthosine monophosphate (XMP, or xanthylic acid) and Guanosine Monophosphate Disodium (GMP Disodium), is a non-azaaromatic purine nucleotide with the formula C10H12N5Na2O8P that exists as a disodium salt, structured with a xanthine base, a ribose sugar (the oxolan ring), and a single phosphate group linked to the riboses 5-carbon, which is composed of the guanine base (2,6-dioxo-3H-purin) linked at N9 to a D-ribose sugar that is phosphorylated at the 5 position, it lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch), and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, with its core role being as a critical intermediate in purine nucleotide biosynthesis, acting as the precursor for both guanosine monophosphate (GMP) and adenosine monophosphate (AMP) to support nucleic acid synthesis and cellular metabolism, and GMP is also a precursor for Ribonucleic Acid (RNA) and Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA), and participates in energy storage and signal transduction (e.g., in the G-protein coupled receptor system) as Guanosine Triphosphate (GTP) within the cell, in real-world application, GMP Disodium is primarily used as a food additive, specifically as a flavor enhancer, which is often combined with Inosine Monophosphate (IMP) (known as IG) to provide and intensify the savory or umami taste in foods, widely used in soups, instant noodle seasonings, snacks, and processed meats.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as a key hit compound featuring a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core with a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and a 2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)ethylamine side chain at the 11-position, contains the 2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethylamine side chain, which combines a short ethylamine spacer with the relatively lipophilic pyrrolidine five-membered ring, and the lipophilicity of the pyrrolidine ring enhances cell membrane permeability; this compound is a nucleic acid binding agent that demonstrated excellent binding affinity and selectivity for the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex and potent, broad-spectrum anti-proliferative activity, could effectively repress NRAS translation by stabilizing the RNA G4 and interfering with DHX36 helicase binding thus leading to G0/G1 cell cycle arrest, and has potential in anticancer, antifungal, and antiprotozoal drug research for studying the impact of short-chain, lipophilic substituents on biological activity.

Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-3-[1-[(3S)-4-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-4-oxobutyl]triazol-4-yl]-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 5, is a complex, linear peptidic analog and a triazole analog with an N-methylation pattern distinct from compound 4, it has binding capacity that is affected by the presence of tRNA, strongly reduced infectivity of pseudotyped HIV and inhibited replication of HIVIIIB, notably, it was essentially nontoxic at high concentrations, making it the safest compound in the series, and its favorable combination of moderate binding, strong antiviral activity, and low toxicity identifies it as a promising lead for therapeutic development; its structure is similar to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[1-[(3S)-4-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-4-oxobutyl]triazol-4-yl]-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, featuring two units containing (2S)-phenylalaninyl-3-aminopropylamine and (2S)-proline fragments linked by a 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole ring, with both termini acylated by a 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group, the connectivity of the two units to the triazole ring is reversed compared to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[1-[(3S)-4-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-4-oxobutyl]triazol-4-yl]-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, but the substitution pattern of the two units themselves (one containing methylcarbamoyl, the other containing carbamoyl) is the same, it incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline aromatic heterocycle and contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds, this compound adopts a non-planar, flexible conformation, the benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the 3-aminopropylamine group enhances hydrophilicity and cationic properties, in terms of biological function, peptides and their analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands, enzyme inhibitors, or signaling molecules, and benzo[g]quinoline compounds typically possess diverse biological activities, with this compound specifically exhibiting potent anti-HIV activity and low toxicity.

Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions. Its dicationic structure includes the core N-methylation and the 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium group at position 7, along with a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino substituted chain at position 11. This structure specifically incorporates two morpholine groups into the molecule. The compound has practical potential in bio-probes and drug delivery vehicles. As a therapeutic agent, its cationic and polycyclic properties allow it to act as a DNA damaging agent or a chemotherapy sensitizer, enhancing the efficacy of existing chemotherapeutic drugs by binding to tumor cell DNA or inhibiting key proteins related to DNA metabolism.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 24-methyl-5,7,18,20-tetraoxa-24-azoniahexacyclo[11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21]tetracosa-1(24),2,4(8),9,11,13,15,17(21),22-nonaene, also referred to as Sanguinarine, is a highly complex, polycyclic cationic compound, which can be chemically identified as the N-methyl derivative of a macrocyclic tetraoxaza-heteroatom system, and its molecular structure is dominated by its extensive ring fusion as indicated by its IUPAC name, and Sanguinarine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant structural changes in the RNA, Sanguinarine is also a benzophenanthridine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and pro-apoptotic effects, it targets multiple cellular pathways, including NF-B, and is studied for anticancer applications despite potential toxicity concerns, the compounds formula, C21H16NO4, reveals a structure containing 21 carbon atoms, 4 oxygen atoms, and a single nitrogen atom that carries a permanent positive charge (azonia) due to methylation, the core hexacyclic structure suggests a rigid, cage-like framework featuring two major 12-membered rings bridged by various fused smaller rings (implied by the [11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21] nomenclature), leading to a highly planar and stiff molecular geometry, the presence of four oxygen atoms and nine double bonds (nonaene) indicates extensive unsaturation, and the presence of oxygen atoms at positions 5, 7, 18, and 20 suggests they are likely incorporated into ether or furan-like structures, strongly suggesting this molecule is an analog of macrocyclic dyes or natural products with rigid, fluorescent properties, potentially related to porphyrins or cryptands in shape, although Sanguinarine has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, it fails to activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, possibly due to its toxicity to cells, nevertheless, Sanguinarine, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch, due to its fixed positive charge and large, planar aromatic system, this compound is highly likely to function as an intercalating agent for nucleic acids, making it a potential DNA/RNA binding fluorescent probe for biological imaging, or a promising scaffold for anti-cancer or antimicrobial drug development, where its rigidity could confer high target specificity.

Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of a 3-styryl substituted benzo[furo[3,2-b]quinoline with a 2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain, is the most complex quaternary ammonium salt in this series, featuring the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core and an N-methylpiperazine side chain, it binds to the NRQ, exhibits potent anti-proliferative activity particularly in Huh7 cells, was one of the compounds identified in the lower left quadrant of the binding vs. activity plot though it had poor selectivity, and is a highly optimized candidate for anticancer research, aiming for maximum DNA-intercalating potency and favorable pharmaceutical properties.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

RNA information: The 161 yjdF RNA from B. subtilis, part of the azaaromatic riboswitch class, has a four-stem (P1-P4) structure with conserved regions. It binds diverse azaaromatics  via a single site, triggering similar structural changes. Ligand binding activates gene expression by unblocking the ribosome-binding site, aiding potential detox. It doesn’t bind phenolic acids/bile salts well and differs from FMN riboswitches in ligand specificity.
Small_molecule_information: acridine-3,6-diamine, also referred to as Proflavine (commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine), is commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine, a representative acridine dye, structured as a tricyclic acridine core substituted by amino groups at positions 3 and 6, molecular formula C13H11N3, and Proflavine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, the acridine scaffold is characterized by extreme planarity and conjugation, in terms of real-world activity, acridine compounds are indispensable tools and drugs in biology and medicine, its most famous application is as a fluorescent stain for nucleic acids; it intercalates into the DNA and RNA double helix, emitting different colors of fluorescence depending on the nucleic acid type and aggregation state, widely used for cell cycle analysis and nucleic acid quantification, as a pharmaceutical agent, acridine dyes, due to their potent DNA intercalation and disruption effects, are developed as broad-spectrum disinfectants/antiseptics (e.g., acriflavine) and cytotoxic anticancer drugs, Proflavine can induce significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that Proflavine is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA. RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions. Its dicationic structure includes the core N-methylation and the 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium group at position 7, along with a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino substituted chain at position 11. This structure specifically incorporates two morpholine groups into the molecule. The compound has practical potential in bio-probes and drug delivery vehicles. As a therapeutic agent, its cationic and polycyclic properties allow it to act as a DNA damaging agent or a chemotherapy sensitizer, enhancing the efficacy of existing chemotherapeutic drugs by binding to tumor cell DNA or inhibiting key proteins related to DNA metabolism.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 7-methylquinoline is a nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure uses the quinoline core, with a methyl substituent at the 7-position on the benzene portion. The molecular formula is C10H9N. The compound exhibits a planar conjugated system. In terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, antispasmodic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]pentanedioic acidThis is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline-peptide conjugate similar to 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]butanedioic acid, maintaining the potentially cytotoxic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. The peptide chain consists of Arginine and Glutamic acid. As an acidic amino acid, Glutamic acid enhances hydrophilicity. The molecule is likely designed as a cell-penetrating or tumor-targeted agent. The Glutamic acid residue might be leveraged for transport via amino acid transporters or cleaved by tumor-specific enzymes to release the active drug, indicating potential applications in targeted cancer therapy.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]ethyl]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-2NPr, is a polymeric peptidomimetic based on the benzimidazole-DNA binding unit, linked to an ethylenediamine backbone, which contains two repeating D-alanine-amide units, while 2H-2NPr itself incorporates two N-propyl tertiary amides; in this series of compounds, this variant has the shortest repeating peptide segment, and this specific design is intended to systematically investigate the effect of the shortest linker arm on the activity of the bis-benzimidazole molecule, a shorter linker arm brings the two benzimidazole active units into closer proximity, potentially making it more suitable for tightly spaced DNA binding sites or encouraging an intramolecular folded conformation, though 2H-2NPr exhibited weaker potency than 1NPr, was not reported to perform well in cell-based splicing correction, and its stability was not highlighted as superior, with results indicating that this particular linker length is less favorable for aligning recognition units, and the compound mainly helped define the lower end of potency within the NPr subclass and was not advanced as a promising lead; this molecule exhibits high-affinity DNA binding, and its potential application lies in the discovery of antitumor or antimicrobial drugs with specific binding distance requirements.

RNA information: r(CUG)₄ is a short ribonucleic acid fragment consisting of four consecutive CUG repeat units. Structurally, it tends to form a more stable hairpin secondary structure than r(CUG)₂, with multiple characteristic U·U mismatches in the stem region (the number of mismatch sites increases with the number of repeat units). In some cases, its conformation can be further stabilized by GC base pairs at both ends (similar to the GC clamp design of (CUG)₁₂ mentioned in the document), making it a key short-chain model for simulating the structural characteristics of long-chain CUG repeat sequences. Functionally, it does not possess the toxicity of long expanded r(CUG)ⁿ (n≥50, pathogenic type) — which forms nuclear foci and sequesters MBNL1 (Muscleblind-like family proteins) — but it can specifically bind to MBNL1 (with lower affinity than long pathogenic sequences). Therefore, it is mainly used as an in vitro research tool to analyze the binding mode between MBNL1 and CUG repeat sequences, verify the inhibitory effect of small-molecule compounds on their interaction, and provide structural and functional references that are closer to the characteristics of long-chain sequences for exploring the pathogenic mechanism of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and the preliminary screening of related therapeutic drugs.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-(cyclohexylmethoxy)phenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5d (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a cyclohexylmethoxy group, the compound was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings and characterized by methods like NMR and SPR, the cyclohexyl group is a saturated alicyclic ring that can significantly increase the molecules lipophilicity but removes the possibility of - stacking, as a tetracationic polyamine, this increase in lipophilicity may enhance its interaction with lipid-rich environments (such as the interior of cell membranes), functionally, Compound 5d binds to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB with low binding affinity and has corresponding binding specificity relative to control hairpins GGh and TARh, shows higher IC in inhibiting the Bh-Rev interaction and no antiviral activity in cell assays, while such derivatives may be explored for targeting membrane-associated proteins or interfering with lipid metabolism pathways, for instance, as novel antiviral or antifungal agents.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol;sulfuric acid, also referred to as Paromomycin sulphate, is an aminoglycoside antibiotic existing as a sulfate salt, and Paromomycin sulphate is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, causing misreading of mRNA and inhibiting protein synthesis and is used to treat parasitic and bacterial infections, its core structure, (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, is similar to (2R,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(1R,2R,3S,4R,6S)-4,6-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,5R)-4-[(2R,3R,4R,5S,6S)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-4,5-dihydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, consisting of multiple aminosugar units linked via glycosidic bonds, featuring numerous amino and hydroxyl groups, the presence of multiple amino groups makes it strongly polycationic, which is the structural basis for its biological activity, allowing it to specifically bind to the bacterial ribosome and disrupt protein synthesis, its formulation as a sulfate salt is typical for this class of drugs, enhancing its water solubility and stability for clinical applications such as injection or solution preparation, this molecule is highly likely to be a clinically relevant antibiotic, such as a derivative of streptomycin, kanamycin, or tobramycin, employed as a broad-spectrum bactericidal agent primarily against aerobic Gram-negative infections, with caution required due to the risk of ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[(E)-2-chloro-1,2-diphenylethenyl]phenoxy]-N,N-diethylethanamine, also referred to as clomiphene, is a classic example of a triphenylethylene derivative, defined by its core alkene scaffold with an (E)-configuration and a terminal diethylaminoethoxy side chain, this structural class is renowned for containing well-known Non-Steroidal Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulators (SERMs), such as tamoxifen and clomiphene, these compounds are widely used clinically for treating hormone-dependent breast cancer and infertility, and clomiphene was originally developed to treat female infertility by inducing ovulation as it functions by binding to estrogen receptors and modulating their activity, the E-geometry is crucial for determining the molecules stereoselective activity at the estrogen receptor, influencing whether it acts as an antagonist or a partial agonist, besides its role in modulating estrogen signaling, this compound has also been repurposed as an inhibitor of HIV-1 replication, in the context of HIV-1, it interferes with viral gene expression by targeting the Rev protein-RNA interaction, a critical step for viral replication, it binds to the Rev response element (RRE) in viral RNA, thereby disrupting the formation of the Rev-RRE complex and inhibiting the nuclear export of unspliced or partially spliced viral transcripts, this mechanism is distinct from that of traditional antiretroviral drugs, offering a novel therapeutic approach, and in cellular assays, it demonstrates potent anti-HIV-1 activity and exhibits a selective index indicating a favorable therapeutic window, these findings suggest that this compound or its analogs may represent promising candidates for the development of new antiretroviral therapies targeting HIV-1, therefore, 2-[4-[(E)-2-chloro-1,2-diphenylethenyl]phenoxy]-N,N-diethylethanamine is highly likely to be explored for its potential to modulate estrogen signaling in the treatment of estrogen-dependent diseases and for its anti-HIV-1 therapeutic potential.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)-3-(trifluoromethoxy)aniline, also referred to as Compound 4g, is a dihydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine derivative (Compound 4g is additionally categorized as a 2-aminomethylene-dihydropyrrolo[3,2]-pyridine derivative) synthesized via ruthenium-catalyzed [222] cycloaddition, amine substitution, and Boc deprotection, where the aniline is substituted with a trifluoromethoxy (OCF3) group at the meta position; the trifluoromethoxy group is a more polar and strongly electron-withdrawing substituent compared to trifluoromethyl, and this structural change is designed to optimize the molecules pharmacokinetic properties, such as improving water solubility and altering metabolic pathways, while the dihydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine core suggests potential binding to nervous system receptors, and the compound (Compound 4g) binds pre-miR-21 with moderate affinity and shows selectivity over duplex DNA/tRNA, though it lacks Dicer cleavage inhibition activity, likely due to its O-containing aniline substituent failing to form critical H-bonds or block pre-miR-21s G28-A29 cleavage site, limiting its ability to suppress oncogenic miR-21 biogenesis, and this compound is investigated as a potential drug acting on neural or signaling pathways, offering potential advantages in absorption and distribution.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₂ RNA is a repetitive hairpin-forming sequence that adopts a stable secondary structure characterized by regularly spaced U–U mismatches within a C–G base-paired stem. These noncanonical interactions create specific binding pockets that sequester the splicing regulator MBNL1, thereby disrupting its normal function and driving disease phenotypes such as mis-splicing in myotonic dystrophy type 1. The structured repeats also provide discrete molecular recognition elements that can be selectively targeted by small molecules, enabling competitive displacement of MBNL1 and partial restoration of RNA processing functions.
Small_molecule_information: 1,8-diamino-3,6-dipyrrolidin-1-yl-2,7-naphthyridine-4-carbonitrile, also referred to as Compound 1 (2) (N-(3-(1H-imidazol-1-yl)propyl)-2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)quinolin-4-amine), is a compound featuring a highly substituted 2,7-naphthyridine scaffold with multiple amino and pyrrolidine substituents, and a cyano group at position 4, with the formula C19H23N7, and Compound 1 (2), identified via Inforna, selectively binds miR-544 precursors Dicer site and r(CUG)exp U loops, naphthyridine cores are often investigated as fluorescent dyes and DNA binders, the high polarity and multiple hydrogen bond sites suggest good water solubility and potential biological activity, it is likely designed as a DNA minor groove binder, aiming to interfere with replication and transcription processes through non-covalent binding, thus having prospects in antimicrobial and anticancer research, it inhibits miR-544 biogenesis, derepresses mTOR, disrupts ATMmTOR axis (induces TNBC apoptosis to a notable extent, with significant efficacy vs antagomirs), displaces MBNL1, improves DM1 splicing/translation, and disperses foci, a single i.p. injection of a low dose abolishes MDA-MB-231 tumor growth (low toxicity), acting as an RNA-directed probe for TNBC and DM1. Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-phenylmethoxyphenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5a (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) p-terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a benzyloxy group, and Compound 5a is synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions and was characterized by methods such as Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR); the benzyloxy group is an aromatic side chain that increases lipophilicity, and Compound 5a functionally can bind to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, has limited selectivity for Bh over DNA, exhibits a high half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC) in inhibiting the interaction between Bh and Rev, and shows no antiviral activity in cellular assays; as a tetracationic polyamine, its primary mode of action remains strong interaction with negatively charged biomolecules (nucleic acids, membranes), and in practical applications, this benzyloxy-substituted polyamine is often used in Structure-Activity Relationship (SAR) studies to evaluate the effect of simple aromatic lipophilic side chains on the polyamine cores cytotoxicity, transfection efficiency, and selectivity, to find the optimal drug design.

Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-chloro-2-(4-fluorobenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 13, is a complex polycyclic derivative based on the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, substituted with 3-amino, 4-phenyl, and a 2-(4-fluorobenzoyl) group, while featuring a chloro atom at position 9 and both chlorine and fluorine atoms overall; the chloro atom is an electron-withdrawing group, introducing smaller steric hindrance at position 9 compared to the bromo analogue, and this sophisticated planar polycyclic structure, combined with multiple electron-withdrawing groups (fluoro, chloro, carbonyl), results in high conjugation and rigidity, additionally exhibiting strong RNA-binding affinity and being useful in evaluating the synergistic effects of multiple halogens; in medicinal chemistry, this large molecular scaffold is frequently designed as a Topoisomerase inhibitor, and by disrupting Topoisomerase function, the compound can interfere with DNA replication and transcription, thus exerting cytotoxicity, consequently, its activity is mainly focused on the development of anti-tumor drugs.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. Small_molecule_information: (2Z)-3-methoxy-2-[(5-methyl-4-pentyl-1H-pyrrol-2-yl)methylidene]-4-(1H-pyrrol-2-yl)pyrrole, also referred to as prodigiosin (the namesake member of the prodiginine family), is a relatively simple tripyrrole compound that shares the common tripyrrolic pharmacophore responsible for RNA binding; its core structure consists of three pyrrole rings connected by a conjugated methylidene double bond, forming a planar, conjugated system with extensive -electron delocalization, the cis (Z) configuration of the linkage ensures a specific geometry conducive to planar stacking, molecules with such multi-aromatic and highly conjugated features often exhibit fluorescent properties and tend to act as nucleic acid dyes or DNA binders, this compound likely interacts with DNA via intercalation or minor groove binding while also being able to bind pre-miR-21 with notable thermal stabilization and strong affinity, thereby interfering with cellular genetic material function as well as inhibiting miRNA processing (a new complementary mechanism of action for the compound), the methoxy and pentyl substituents modulate the molecules polarity, lipophilicity, and metabolic stability, and its potential activity includes applications as an antimicrobial or anti-cancer agent through DNA interference, in addition to its documented mechanisms of inducing DNA damage via copper-mediated cleavage, intracellular acidification and displaying potent anti-proliferative activity.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (4S,4aS,5aS,6S,12aR)-4-(dimethylamino)-1,6,10,11,12a-pentahydroxy-6-methyl-3,12-dioxo-4,4a,5,5a-tetrahydrotetracene-2-carboxamide, also referred to as nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, is the core structure or a derivative of a tetracycline antibiotic, which is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that binds to the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis by interacting with bacterial rRNA but not directly with mRNA or other RNA types; tetracycline has a rigid hydrophobic skeleton with four rings connected together, the side chains of C-10 phenol dione and C-1 to C-3 triketone formamide provide negative charge and hydrogen bond donors, this planar aromatic region can be stacked at the single-stranded/double-stranded junction of RNA, meanwhile, magnesium ions mediate the electrostatic bridging between its dimethylamino group and the phosphate skeleton, inducing conformational distortion at the active site and demonstrating a non-competitive inhibitory function for Class I intron splicing; tetracyclines are clinically important broad-spectrum antibiotics used in reality to treat various bacterial infections like pneumonia, acne, and Lyme disease, by inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.
