RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-4, is an ultra-long chain bis-head benzimidazole derivative, featuring six repeating polyamide units as the flexible spacer arm connecting the two DNA binding units, while 2H-4 itself features four propylamine spacers linking the Ht-N3 modules to extend the intermodule distance further; this compound (2H-4) shows modest selectivity in relevant assays, is particularly effective against r(CUG) repeats which highlights the role of spacing in motif specificity, is less potent than the best analogs when tested against r(CGG)exp but still surpasses Ht-N3 in both activity and splicing rescue assays, and its structural flexibility may confer cross-reactivity between different repeat RNAs though at the expense of maximum affinity and selectivity, and the ultra-long length of the overall molecule suggests it is designed to span significant biological distances, in the context of DNA binding this length may allow for the simultaneous binding of two distantly separated minor groove sites on DNA or function as a molecular tether to cross-link or bring together specific DNA regions, this ultra-long flexible linkage may also facilitate the molecule in bypassing steric hindrance or targeting oligomeric proteins in complex cellular environments, thus, it is a tool for studying long-range molecular interactions, with potential applications as a highly efficient DNA cross-linker or gene regulator.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 1-methyl-4-(2-tricyclo[9.4.0.03,8]pentadeca-1(15),3,5,7,9,11,13-heptaenylidene)piperidine, also referred to as Cyproheptadine (4-(5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-ylidene)-1-methylpiperidine), is a first-generation antihistamine used clinically to relieve allergy symptoms and also to stimulate appetite due to its serotonin receptor antagonist properties; in HIV-1 studies, it acts as a selective RNA-binding small molecule that docks into the internal loop of the RRE subdomain IIB RNA, competes with the arginine-rich Rev3450 helix, blocks assembly of the RevRRE ribonucleoprotein export complex, disrupts nuclear export of unspliced/partially-spliced viral transcripts, and inhibits post-integration HIV-1 gene expression without measurable cytotoxicity, exemplifying a clinically available drug that can be repositioned as a RevRRE-targeting antiviral scaffold.

RNA information: The r(CCUG)₁₀ sequence models the pathogenic RNA associated with myotonic dystrophy type 2. Structurally, it contains four-nucleotide repeats that also produce hairpins with mismatches, although their geometry differs slightly from CUG repeats. These motifs create similar alternating stripes of electrostatic potential, making selectivity between CUG and CCUG challenging for both proteins and small molecules. Functionally, r(CCUG)₁₀ sequesters MBNL1 in a manner comparable to CUG repeats, contributing to splicing defects. Its inclusion in binding studies demonstrates the difficulty of achieving discrimination between closely related repeat RNAs and emphasizes the need for ligands that exploit subtle structural differences.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-2-(propylamino)propanoyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 7 (2012), is a symmetric oligo-peptide analogue featuring multiple stereocenters and an internal (E)-alkene double bond within its oct-4-enediamide core, and it is the E-isomer analog of compound 6 that lacks the benzo[g]quinoline group; this type of peptidic structure, incorporating non-natural amino acid fragments and a geometrically constrained core, is typically pursued as a candidate for proteinase inhibitor or a G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR) ligand, while the absence of the aromatic moiety in this compound (relative to related structures) leads to only modest binding and a complete loss of selectivity among different RNA sequences, and structurally its configuration affects backbone orientation but does not compensate for the missing heteroaromatic system, functionally it shows minimal or no activity in biological assays (reinforcing the observation that effective RNA recognition requires both the aromatic stacking element and appropriate stereochemistry) and serves primarily as a comparative tool in evaluating structurefunction dependencies, meanwhile the rigid E-configuration (trans) of the alkene is critical for locking the molecule into a specific conformation, which is paramount for achieving the necessary molecular recognition and high-affinity binding at the proteins active site, and due to its multiple basic amine groups, the compound is likely polycationic at physiological pH, suggesting potential use in areas like antiviral, anti-inflammatory, or immunomodulatory therapies.

The Gly-Gly sequence often serves as an enzyme cleavable linker, while lysine provides alkaline targeting. This structure is used in prodrug design, aiming for tumor-specific drug release by enzymatic hydrolysis in the tumor microenvironment to achieve targeted delivery and reduce systemic toxicity.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 3,6-bis[(E)-2-(1-methylpyrazin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-9H-carbazole diiodide, also referred to as BMVC/BMVC2/BMVC4 (a series of carbazole derivatives and well-known fluorescent probes), is a dicationic cyanine dye based on the highly conjugated carbazole core, substituted with two 1-methylpyrazin-1-ium (quaternary ammonium) groups; these compounds have high affinity for DNA/RNA G-quadruplex structures and served as representative G-quadruplex ligands in a related study, and despite binding strongly to the G-quadruplex form of r(G4C2)exp in vitro, they were poor inhibitors of RAN translation in cellular models, while the dicationic charge and large planar system of 3,6-bis[(E)-2-(1-methylpyrazin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-9H-carbazole diiodide make it a potent DNA intercalator, and these polycationic dyes are used in the development of anticancer and broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents, and as high-performance fluorescent dyes for nuclear staining and photodynamic therapy (PDT) research.

Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-16-[3-(3-methylimidazol-3-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a molecule with a dicationic character, consisting of the core cationic pentacyclic scaffold linked via a propoxy bridge to a 1-methylimidazolium ring. Imidazolium is a common quaternary ammonium structure. The presence of two positive charges significantly enhances the molecules polarity and lipophilicity/amphiphilic dual characteristics. This structural motif is often designed in medicinal chemistry as a cell membrane permeabilizer or nucleic acid transfection agent. In practical applications, dicationic structures are frequently explored as novel antifungal or antibacterial agents due to their strong surface activity and membrane-disrupting capabilities. The imidazolium unit may also confer potential as an ionic liquid or phase-transfer catalyst.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3-tricyclo[4.3.1.13,8]undecanyl)ethyl4-[(2,5-dihydroxyphenyl)methylamino]benzoate, also referred to as Compound H3 (a bis-benzimidazole analog of Hoechst 33258), is a large, highly lipophilic molecule with the molecular formula C27H33NO4, and Compound H3 is specifically designed to enhance RNA-binding affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic r(CUG)exp RNA associated with myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), retaining the core benzimidazole structure of Hoechst 33258 but incorporating structural modificationsnamely, an extended hydrophobic tail and an additional methoxy substituentthat improve its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat; in a TR-FRET displacement assay, H3 dissociates MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 with greater potency than Hoechst 33258 yet is less selective than H1, binding with comparable affinity to RNAs containing 5CUG/3GCC or 5CGG/3GGC motifs and to the human A-site, and in DM1 cell models, H3 partially shifts a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal, though cytotoxicity becomes evident at higher doses, exemplifying how computational shape similarity can evolve a DNA-binding dye into a low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics, while the main compound contains a large, rigid tricyclo[4.3.1.13,8]undecanyl skeleton, analogous to adamantane, which is an effective structural motif for increasing molecular hydrophobicity, filling hydrophobic pockets in enzymes or receptors, and resisting metabolic degradation, also incorporates a 2,5-dihydroxyphenyl (hydroquinone or catechol derivative) moiety, a classic structure known for its antioxidant and free-radical scavenging properties, and also structurally related to dopaminergic neurotransmitters, with its functional units connected by an ester bond and a secondary amine, and based on its structural features, this compound likely exhibits neuroprotective activity through its antioxidant properties or by acting as a ligand for Central Nervous System (CNS) receptors, potentially affecting dopamine or glutamate signaling.

RNA information: The structure of MAPT pre-mRNA contains a hairpin structure located at the junction of exon 10 and intron, which is crucial for splicing regulation. Under normal circumstances, the hairpin structure is stable and helps maintain the correct splicing ratio of exon 10. However, certain mutations (such as C+14U) can disrupt the stability of hairpins, leading to excessive inclusion of exon 10 and subsequently the production of 4R-tau protein, which is associated with the neurodegenerative disease FTDP-17. The article points out that small molecule compounds can promote the excretion of exon 10 by binding to and stabilizing the mutated hairpin structure, thereby restoring the normal proportion of tau protein and having potential therapeutic effects.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[(6-amino-1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)amino]-N-[4-[(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)amino]phenyl]benzamide, also referred to as the compound that can bind to DDPAC MAPT RNA, is a complex asymmetric dicationic molecule linking a quinolinium and a pyridinium quaternary salt via an anilino-benzamide bridge; this compound has weak affinity for DDPAC MAPT RNA and only shows a slight reduction in luciferase expression at relatively high concentrations, indicating it has a certain ability to promote the exclusion of exon 10 but with much lower activity than Compound 1 and 2 and relatively weak function, while due to its dicationic nature and multiple planar aromatic rings, it is extensively studied as a DNA-binding dye or fluorescent probe, functionally, these types of molecules are known to effectively bind into the minor groove of DNA in the cell nucleus, making them useful for fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry to label genetic material, and its strong nucleic acid affinity also suggests potential antiproliferative activity in anticancer drug research.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6: This is a six-repeat control RNA with perfect Watson-Crick base pairing. It is used to evaluate the specificity of compound binding and shows minimal structural perturbation upon ligand interaction, confirming that small molecules do not significantly bind to regular duplex RNA.; The (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6 sequence serves as the extended control duplex. Its repetitive yet perfectly paired structure lacks the distinctive features of disease-repeat RNA, allowing researchers to conclusively prove that compound efficacy is due to specific recognition of the pathological mismatch motif and not general RNA binding.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 3, shares the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core with 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one but lacks the bromine atom, and Compound 3 emerged as a lead compound with high affinity and selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, its large aromatic and amino-substituted structure suggests high lipophilicity and the potential to interact strongly with biological targets, it is likely investigated as an antiproliferative agent targeting intracellular enzymes or signaling pathways, Compound 3 significantly reduced polyQ aggregation in cellular models, showed low cytotoxicity, and was cell-permeable, localizing primarily in the cytoplasm. RNA

RNA information: RNA3 is a key model RNA that incorporates both the Drosha site (1x1 UU loop, target of 1) and the adjacent site (1x1 GG loop, target of 2). It binds the dimeric compound 3 with high avidity (Kd = 85 nM), demonstrating simultaneous engagement of both modules.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2,6-dichloro-7H-purine, also referred to as 2,6-Dichloropurine, is a simple aromatic heterocycle based on the purine scaffold with a bicyclic heterocyclic core (fused pyrimidine and imidazole rings), substituted with chlorine atoms at positions 2 and 6, with the formula C5H2Cl2N4, and it is a non-planar purine derivative that lacks planar azaaromatic features; purine is a fundamental component of nucleic acids, and this compound is a key intermediate not only in nucleic acid synthesis but also in pharmaceutical synthesis, particularly for antiviral and some anticancer drugs, though it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis and is not involved in azaaromatic sensing, its biological function is centered on its ability to act as a purine antimetabolite, interfering with the biosynthesis of endogenous purines or signal transduction pathways in the cell, thereby exhibiting potential antitumor or immunosuppressive activity.

Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)quinolin-3-amine, also referred to as a high-affinity binder and potent inhibitor of Dicer processing featuring an amine substitution crucial for its activity, links the 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl moiety via a secondary amine to a quinolin-3-yl group, and, with quinoline as a fundamental aromatic heterocycle that underlies classic antimalarial, antibacterial, and anticancer agents, the combination of the quinoline ring with the basic pyrrolopyridine scaffold is designed to provide broad-spectrum antimicrobial or antiproliferative properties, while the rigid planar quinoline structure enables effective stacking and hydrogen bonding with nucleic acids or protein binding pockets, making this molecule a promising anticancer or anti-infective lead compound.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(1-methylpiperidin-1-ium-1-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is another dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions. It possesses a 1-methylpiperidin-1-ium quaternary ammonium side chain and a 2-piperidin-1-ylethyl substituted chain. The presence of two piperidine rings enhances its lipophilicity and membrane permeability. In the pharmaceutical field, this class of dicationic polycyclic compounds is often utilized as live-cell nuclear stains or DNA electrophoresis visualization reagents due to their fluorescent properties and cell permeability. As a potential drug, its potent antiproliferative activity makes it a focus in oncology research, especially as a precursor for chemotherapeutic agents targeting nucleic acids.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-4H-thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazine, also referred to as SMSM6, is a coumarin-derived small molecule characterized by the 4H-thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazine core, a fused tricyclic system containing sulfur and nitrogen atoms, linked to an 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridine ring and a 4-methylpiperazine group, which functions as a selective RNA binder targeting single G bulges with high selectivity over other bulge types and GA-rich loops, and its rod-like, planar structure and positive charge facilitate minor groove binding, supported by computational and experimental data, positioning it as a key compound for studying RNA-ligand interactions, while thiazine derivatives have a long history in medicinal chemistry, with many anti-inflammatory, anti-allergic, and diuretic drugs containing this structure, the molecule has an 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl group at C2 and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl group at C6, importantly, the piperazine ring at C6 is not quaternized, making the compound a neutral or weak base at physiological pH, this lack of a fixed positive charge results in higher lipophilicity, which facilitates membrane penetration and intracellular action, therefore, 2-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-4H-thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazine is likely designed to target intracellular enzymes or GPCRs, such as a potential PDE inhibitor or histamine receptor antagonist.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-(diethylamino)ethyl]-2-[[11-[2-(diethylamino)ethylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]acetamide iodide is a benzofuroquinoline quaternary ammonium salt derivative. Its structure features the benzofuroquinoline scaffold, with the 5-position nitrogen quaternized, and both the 11- and 7-positions attached to diethylaminoethyl-containing side chains (molecular formula C30H41N4O2I). This compound possesses a planar conjugated system, a positive charge, and multiple amine groups, indicating potential nucleic acid binding activity as an anti-cancer agent.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-2-methoxypropan-1-one, also referred to as Compound 11 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative with modifications on the piperazines secondary amine where R1-methoxyethyl and synthesized via one-step coupling), is similar to 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-2-methylpropan-1-one, the core activity of this compound is predicted to be 1-adrenergic receptor antagonism, and it binds the influenza A RNA promoter similarly to compound 1 (causing U27 imino proton line broadening) yet exhibits no significant anti-influenza activity in cell-based assays; its side chain, 2-methoxypropanoyl, introduces a methoxy group to increase local polarity, which can affect the molecules aqueous solubility and biological membrane permeability, and in practice, this polarity adjustment is used to optimize the compounds pharmacokinetic (PK) profile and metabolic pathways, potentially resulting in a different half-life or excretion pattern for treating specific patient populations with hypertension or BPH.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(4-chlorophenyl)-5-fluoro-7-(4-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as C26, is a cationic heterocyclic molecule based on the coumarin mother nucleus, which is a non-planar coumarin analog possessing a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and an imidazopyridine-thiazole DE-fused core with a 1 charge; it features the fluorescent coumarin scaffold, a permanently cationic 4-methylpiperazin-4-ium group at C7, a fluorine atom at C5, and a para-chlorophenyl group at C3, and can engage the single-G bulge of RNA1 via minor-groove H-bonding, -stacking and electrostatics (confirmed by fluorescence-polarization assays and SAR comparison), serving as a selective RNA ligand, while coumarin derivatives are widely researched as fluorescent probes, anticoagulants, and potential anti-cancer drugs, and given its cationic nature and fluorescence, it is studied as a fluorescent dye targeting organelles like mitochondria or as a potential inhibitor of kinases or proteases relevant to cancer and inflammation.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: acridine-3,6-diamine, also referred to as Proflavine (commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine), is commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine, a representative acridine dye, structured as a tricyclic acridine core substituted by amino groups at positions 3 and 6, molecular formula C13H11N3, and Proflavine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, the acridine scaffold is characterized by extreme planarity and conjugation, in terms of real-world activity, acridine compounds are indispensable tools and drugs in biology and medicine, its most famous application is as a fluorescent stain for nucleic acids; it intercalates into the DNA and RNA double helix, emitting different colors of fluorescence depending on the nucleic acid type and aggregation state, widely used for cell cycle analysis and nucleic acid quantification, as a pharmaceutical agent, acridine dyes, due to their potent DNA intercalation and disruption effects, are developed as broad-spectrum disinfectants/antiseptics (e.g., acriflavine) and cytotoxic anticancer drugs, Proflavine can induce significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that Proflavine is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA. RNA

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol, also referred to as Amodiaquine Di HCl, is a quinoline derivative structured around a quinoline core, with a chloro substitution at the 7-position and an amino link at the 4-position to a benzene ring containing a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group (molecular formula C20H22ClN3O), and it is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory effects that shares mechanisms with chloroquine but has higher toxicity risks, limiting its use despite efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains; this compound possesses a planar conjugated system and multiple functional groups (chloro, amine, phenol, quinoline), functionally, the quinoline core is famous as the scaffold for well-known antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine), and derivatives are commonly investigated for broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antimalarial, and anti-cancer activities, the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups suggests potential for metal complexation or binding to enzyme active sites.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₀ sequence represents a pathogenic-length model for expanded CUG repeats and folds into a stable hairpin with regularly spaced U–U mismatches. Structurally, it provides multiple equivalent binding sites, enabling one ligand to associate with each trinucleotide repeat and producing cooperative binding. This repeat length captures essential features of toxic RNA found in myotonic dystrophy, including the ability to sequester MBNL1 and form nuclear foci. Functionally, r(CUG)₁₀ is the most relevant in vitro construct for evaluating selective small-molecule recognition, since its length allows the manifestation of cooperative interactions that mimic disease-relevant RNA–protein complexes.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 42012, is the cis (Z) isomer of (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, a benzo[g]quinoline-containing Z-isomer designed through olefin metathesis, with the double bond in the core oct-4-enediamide scaffold having the cis configuration, and its geometry places the heteroaromatic moiety in an orientation that promotes favorable stacking interactions with RNA bases, compared to earlier disulfide-linked scaffolds, the olefin bioisostere of this compound improves chemical stability and compatibility for cellular studies, structurally, it exemplifies the importance of precise spatial placement of aromatic and peptide elements to achieve cooperative binding across repeat sequences, functionally, it enters cells efficiently and demonstrates biological activity by alleviating RNA-mediated toxicity in model systems, making it a strong lead structure for RNA-targeted small-molecule drug design, this conformational difference introduces a fixed bend in the molecules three-dimensional structure, in complex peptidomimetics, such cis-trans isomerization is often the decisive factor for biological activity, as it affects the precise spatial matching with the target binding pocket, the compound maintains the poly-cationic and highly aromatic features of its counterpart, its potential applications are similar to those of peptidomimetics, but the activity and selectivity profile of (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide will be significantly altered due to the cis conformation, making it a critical tool for studying the impact of molecular conformation on biological activity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR(WT) RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[5-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]phenyl]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole, also referred to as DB60 (furimidazoline, 2,5-bis-[4-(2-imidazolin-2-yl)phenyl]furan), is a symmetric or near-symmetric structure centered around a biphenyl-linked furan ring, with a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) group attached to each end. The entire molecule forms a conjugated system consisting of two imidazolinium groups, two phenyl groups, and a furan ring, where the imidazolinium groups are connected to the 2- and 5-positions of the furan ring via the phenyl linkers. Imidazolinium groups are typically weakly basic and can bind to biomacromolecules like DNA or RNA, and such polyaromatic heterocyclic systems often possess fluorescent properties. This compound was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii activity but has been repurposed as a selective HIV-1 TAR RNA antagonist, binding the pyrimidine bulge of the TAR hairpin, displacing the Tat protein, blocking Tat-mediated trans-activation, stabilizing TAR structure, and suppressing HIV-1 LTR-driven gene expression without cytotoxicity, illustrating a clear RNA-versus-DNA selectivity switch compared with amidine congeners, and identifying it as a lead structure for developing small-molecule inhibitors targeting the TARTat axis of HIV-1 replication.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-[(4-chlorophenyl)methyl]-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 11, is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with a 4-chlorobenzyl side chain modified from initial active analogs, which features the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, with the carboxamide at the 2-position linked to a 4-chlorobenzyl group; this 4-chlorobenzyl group is a common lipophilic substituent used to improve cell permeability and metabolic stability, and this N-benzyl substituted amide structure is frequently observed in kinase inhibitors, consequently, this molecule is a strong candidate for a kinase inhibitor intended for anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory research, and it also binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key elements for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs with moderate affinity while lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity, and its structure represents a typical lead compound in medicinal chemistry used to probe the activity of the thienopyridine scaffold against specific drug targets.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(7-methoxyimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl)-7-piperazin-1-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C34, is a hybrid molecule incorporating the coumarin, imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine, and piperazine scaffolds, which is a coumarin derivative featuring a 7-fluoroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridine ring (DE) and a piperazine group (A), with a key structural feature being a methoxy substituent at the 4 position on its E ring that enhances the molecules planarity and stabilizes a second protonation site on the imidazole ring via resonance; this planar, diprotonated structure is optimal for minor groove binding, endowing the molecule with an exceptionally high binding affinity and strong selectivity for single G-bulge RNA over flexible GA-rich sequences, making it one of the most potent compounds in the series, while coumarin structures are known for anticoagulant and antitumor activities, and the combination of its three scaffolds suggests potential utility as a protein kinase inhibitor or a fluorescent probe in oncology or cardiovascular research.

RNA information: MALAT1 is a well-studied long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) known to form a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure consisting of three G-tetrads. It functions as a key regulatory molecule in processes such as alternative splicing modulation. The rG4 structure within MALAT1 has been confirmed in vitro through techniques including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays and fluorescence-based methods using probes like NMM and ThT. Studies show that in potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, MALAT1's rG4 forms efficiently, causing significant reverse transcriptase stalling (~60%). Notably, ligands such as PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, inducing and stabilizing MALAT1's rG4 structure even under unfavorable lithium (Li⁺) conditions, highlighting its dynamic structural plasticity and potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)naphthalen-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PhenDC3, is a large, structurally symmetrical bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative) with the molecular formula C37H37N5O5, centered on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core; this core is linked via two amide bonds to two identical naphthyl units, which are functionalized with aminoethoxy chains, and its structure further enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations). The molecule features multiple aromatic rings (pyridine and naphthyl) providing a hydrophobic, planar surface that allows it to bind rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, and four primary amine groups (-O-CH2CH2NH2) contributing to hydrophilicity and multiple positive charges; this combination of hydrophobicity and multiple positive charges is characteristic of amphiphilic molecules, often designed as DNA minor groove binders or transmembrane transport vectors, and it can interact with DNA bases via - stacking interactions while electrostatically attracting the negatively charged phosphate backbone. Functionally, it acts as a molecular chaperone, inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation, aiding G4 biology studies, and consequently, this compound has potential as an anti-tumor or antiviral drug by interfering with the replication or transcription of nucleic acids.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: (7S,9S)-7-[(2R,4S,5R,6S)-4-amino-5-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-6,9,11-trihydroxy-9-(2-hydroxyacetyl)-4-methoxy-8,10-dihydro-7H-tetracene-5,12-dione, also referred to as Epirubicin, is the chemical name for, or a very close analogue of, the anthracycline antibiotic Epirubicin, which is an anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent that intercalates into DNA, inhibiting topoisomerase II and causing DNA strand breaks; Epirubicin is a widely used chemotherapy drug in clinical practice for treating various cancers such as breast cancer and ovarian cancer, and its mechanism involves DNA intercalation and Topoisomerase II inhibition.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(1H-indol-3-yl)ethanamine, also referred to as Tryptamine, is a planar azaaromatic biogenic amine consisting of an indole ring (a fused benzene and pyrrole ring) covalently linked to an ethylamine side chain (-CH-CH-NH), and it is a fundamental biological molecule that is the decarboxylated product of the amino acid Tryptophan; it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and it is a neurotransmitter and neuromodulator found in the central nervous system and the gastrointestinal tract, with its core role being as a biological precursor: it is the building block for neurotransmitters (e.g., serotonin) and alkaloids (e.g., melatonin), also acts as a signaling molecule in plants and microorganisms, and serves as the biochemical precursor for many more complex psychoactive and regulatory molecules, including serotonin, melatonin, and psilocybin; in the real world, Tryptamine and its derivatives are key focuses of neuroscience research due to their influence on mood, sleep, and cognitive function, forming the structural basis for numerous psychedelic and psychoactive drugs (e.g., DMT).
