Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-3H-pteridin-4-one, also referred to as Pterine, Pterin or Isoxanthopterin, is a planar azaaromatic heterocyclic compound consisting of a fused pyrimidine and pyrazine ring system with two nitrogen atoms in each ring and a basic heterocyclic structure, it is the core structural unit and core scaffold of folates, pterins and related molecules like folic acid (for one-carbon metabolism) and the essential biological cofactor Tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis while its core role is as a building block that forms the basis of biologically active molecules related to pigmentation and enzyme function in organisms, in addition, BH4 is a crucial cofactor for aromatic amino acid hydroxylases (like phenylalanine hydroxylase) and nitric oxide synthases, consequently, the compound is primarily studied in the context of enzyme cofactor replacements or modifiers, potentially for the treatment of metabolic diseases associated with BH4 deficiency, such as phenylketonuria.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as the saturated analog of DAPI (whose full name is 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide), is a compound featuring a dihydroindole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, with the formula C16H16N4, while DAPI is a fluorescent stain binding AT-rich DNA regions, widely used in microscopy for nuclear labeling and is cell-impermeant unless membranes are compromised, making it useful for viability assays and chromosome visualization; this compound is the saturated analog of 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, the saturation of the indole ring removes aromaticity and significantly increases the flexibility and changes the 3D shape of the molecule, this structural change dramatically influences its binding mode and affinity for the DNA minor groove, and this compound is investigated to explore the impact of molecular rigidity/flexibility on the activity of DNA-binding drugs, showing potential for anticancer and anti-infective activity.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 6 which is a triazole analog with altered N-methylation, is a complex, linear peptidic analog that has a relatively strong affinity for HIV-1 FSS RNA with reduced selectivity under tRNA competition, alters the Gag/Gag-Pol ratio, suppresses pseudotyped HIV infectivity to show strong antiviral activity, and displays a certain level of cytotoxicity that makes it more toxic than compound 5 despite their chemical similarity, highlighting how subtle structural changes in the triazole scaffold can shift the balance between potency and safety; its structure features two units containing (2S)-phenylalaninyl-3-aminopropylamine and (2S)-proline fragments, linked by a 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole ring, with both termini acylated by a 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group, and similar to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide and N-[(2S)-1-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, this compound incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline moiety, and the two repeating units are slightly different: one unit has a methylcarbamoyl substitution on the phenylalanine, while the other has a carbamoyl substitution, the molecule contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds, adopts a non-planar, flexible conformation, the benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the 3-aminopropylamine group enhances hydrophilicity and cationic properties, in terms of biological function, peptides and their analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands, enzyme inhibitors, or signaling molecules, and benzo[g]quinoline compounds typically possess diverse biological activities.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic compound based on the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium core with diiodide ions. Its dicationic structure results from the core N-methylation and the 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium quaternary ammonium group at position 7. The morpholine group, a common medicinal scaffold, influences the molecules hydrophilicity and targeting. This compound is researched for antiviral and anticancer applications. Its polycyclic planar structure and cationic charge allow it to interact with viral nucleic acids or host cell DNA, thereby interfering with viral replication or cellular proliferation.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(1-methylpiperidin-1-ium-1-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: SA1 RNA is an FMN riboswitch located upstream of the rib operon in methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Its specific sequence folds into a highly conserved aptamer structure, which can specifically bind to flavin mononucleotide (FMN). When the concentration of FMN increases, SA1 undergoes a conformational transition and forms a terminator hairpin, causing RNA polymerase to terminate transcription prematurely. This thereby shuts down the expression of downstream genes responsible for de novo riboflavin synthesis. The synthetic compound ribocil-C and the natural product roseoflavin competitively occupy the same binding site, mimic the role of FMN, induce conformational changes in SA1, and further block endogenous riboflavin synthesis in bacteria, exerting a bacteriostatic effect.
Small_molecule_information: [5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), is a derivative of riboflavin generated through phosphorylation, which is a coenzyme produced by the phosphorylation of riboflavin; its isoalloxazine ring has a planar aromatic structure, while the phosphate group is flexibly linked to the ring system, and its isoalloxazine tricyclic and ribitol-phosphate skeleton together form a reversible electron-transferring redox center; as the natural ligand of bacterial riboswitches, FMN inserts into the aptamer pocket and induces conformational changes in RNA, causing the expression platform to form a terminator/sequestering loop, and it can also induce RNA structure folding into terminator clips or blocking ribosome binding sites by binding to the SA1 riboswitch upstream of riboflavin synthesis genes and the SA2 riboswitch upstream of transport genes, using its own concentration changes as a signal, which consequently shuts down the transcription and translation of the downstream riboflavin synthesis gene ribB as well as the de novo synthesis and uptake pathways of riboflavin, thereby achieving negative feedback regulation of riboflavin biosynthesis; because it is crucial for bacterial survival and does not exist in mammals, FMN and its riboswitch have become targets for antibiotic design, which can be competitionally occupied by structural analogues such as rosettin and ribocil-C, blocking the supply of riboflavin and forcing bacteria to starve to death.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(7-aza-2-azoniaspiro[3.5]nonan-7-yl)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM19, is a non-planar coumarin derivative where the substituent at position 7 is the 7-aza-2-azoniaspiro[3.5]nonan-7-yl group, bearing a 1 charge, with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system; this is a double-nitrogen spirocyclic structure, created by the fusion of a four-membered ring and a six-membered ring (like piperidine) sharing one common atom, the quaternary ammonium positive charge is located on the six-membered ring, enhancing water solubility and electrostatic affinity to negatively charged targets, and this spirocyclic structure confers high rigidity and a specific three-dimensional conformation, crucial for achieving greater geometric selectivity in target binding, which targets the single-G bulge of RNA via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatic interactions, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, offering moderate selectivity for bulged-G RNA, and the combination of the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group and the coumarin core suggests potential as a highly selective ligand for targeted receptors or kinases, possibly targeting receptors associated with neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimers or Parkinsons.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-6-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-2-hydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-7-ol, also referred to as adenosine 3,5-cyclic monophosphate (cAMP), is a complex adenine nucleoside analog that is a non-azaaromatic cyclic nucleotide with an adenosine core (adenine ribose) and a single phosphate group forming a 3,5 cyclic bond, which lacks planar azaaromatic features and binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; its core structure is adenine (6-aminopurin-9-yl) linked to a highly modified and complex sugar ring, which features a fused cyclic phosphate structure (furo[3,2d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin), with the molecular formula C10H12N5O7P and multiple chiral centers, and in terms of real-world activity, this compound, due to its cyclic nucleotide structural feature, is considered a synthetic second messenger analog, mimicking molecules like cAMP or cGMP, may be designed as an enzyme substrate or inhibitor to disrupt intracellular signaling pathways, and given the importance of nucleoside analogs in antiviral and anticancer drugs, this molecule may exhibit potential in regulating cell proliferation or immune responses by competing with ATP or acting as a non-hydrolyzable cAMP analog, affecting key metabolic enzymes like adenylate cyclases and phosphodiesterases, while its core role is also acting as a second messenger in cellular signaling, regulating processes like metabolism and gene expression, unrelated to azaaromatic sensing.

RNA information: TCT8-4 has two conserved regions (5'-AUACCA-3' and 5'-CCUUGG(C/A)AG-3') . It binds theophylline  in a Mg-dependent way, showing 10,900-fold higher affinity for theophylline than caffeine. It aids in diagnosing diseases like asthma/bronchitis, where theophylline is used as a bronchodilator, by distinguishing theophylline from similar compounds.
Small_molecule_information: 3,7-dihydropurine-2,6-dione, also referred to as Xanthine (systematically 9H-xanthine or 2,6-dioxopurine), is a naturally occurring purine base and oxopurine bearing carbonyls at C-2 and C-6 and a proton on N-9, with the formula C5H4N4O2. Its structure is a purine ring with keto groups at positions 2 and 6, and it exists in tautomeric equilibrium with its 7H form as well as tautomeric forms with NH bonds at positions 3 and 7. Xanthine is a crucial intermediate in the purine catabolism pathway, functions as an intermediate in purine catabolism, is a Saccharomyces cerevisiae metabolite, being the product of guanine and adenine breakdown, and is subsequently oxidized by xanthine oxidase to uric acid, making it a precursor for uric acid generation in the human body. Biologically, Xanthine itself is generally inactive, but it is the parent scaffold for many important alkaloids such as caffeine, theophylline, and theobromine, which are its methylated derivatives; notably, because it lacks the N-3 methyl and the N-7 hydrogen bond donor present in theophylline, xanthine binds the TCT8-4 RNA only weakly, illustrating how the RNAs hydrophobic pocket and hydrogen-bond network have been evolved to discriminate sub-angstrom differences within the xanthine scaffold. In clinical medicine, drugs like Allopurinol are used to treat gout and hyperuricemia by inhibiting xanthine oxidase, thereby reducing the conversion of xanthine to uric acid.

RNA information: GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-2 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-3 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-4 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 6b, is another highly complex and large tetracationic polyamine, containing four primary amine groups and a phenol functional group, which was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions, binds to HIV-1 RRE Bh with high affinity and can induce loop conformational changes, potently inhibits the relevant protein-RNA interaction and HIV-1 replication, and blocks Rev-mediated RNA transport; the presence of the phenol confers potential for antioxidant or kinase inhibitor activity, and unlike simple linear polyamines, this complex rigid scaffold gives it a unique spatial conformation, while in practical applications, polyamines with such complex, highly charged, large structures may be explored for highly specific nucleic acid binding or as multivalent ligands for binding specific cell surface receptors or metal ions (chelating agents).

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 5b (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a structurally simpler tetracationic polyamine (compared to 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]phenol and 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]-2-phenylmethoxyphenyl]ethanamine), containing four primary amine groups and a phenol, and is synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings, characterized by NMR, SPR, and ITC, its rigid scaffold features only methoxy substituents, resulting in less steric hindrance, functionally, due to its high symmetry and lower steric hindrance, it may possess a higher nucleic acid binding affinity or more effective gene transfection ability than more complex derivatives, while it also binds to HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIBs hairpin RNA (Bh) with high-affinity binding detected by ITC, induces chemical shift changes in Bhs loop, has certain specificity ratios for related nucleic acid structures, shows limited DNA selectivity, has high IC in inhibiting Bh-Rev interaction, and no antiviral activity in cell assays, in practical applications, such compounds are often used as probes to study the biological effects of polyamine analogs on cell proliferation and apoptosis, as polyamine metabolism in cellulo is closely linked to cancer cell growth.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-2-amino-N-[12,17-bis[[(2S)-2-amino-5-(diaminomethylideneamino)pentanoyl]amino]-4-pentacyclo[6.6.6.0{2,7}.0{9,14}.0{15,20}]icosa-2(7),3,5,9(14),10,12,15(20),16,18-nonaenyl]-5-(diaminomethylideneamino)pentanamide possesses a highly complex cage-like structure and multiple arginine-derived amidine groups. The pentacyclo[6.6.6.0{2,7}.0{9,14}.0{15,20}]icosane scaffold is often used to construct multivalent ligands. The molecule is a polyamidinated peptide/cage molecule conjugate, likely designed as a high-affinity nucleic acid or protein binding agent, for applications such as gene transfection or as a potent broad-spectrum antiviral/antibacterial agent. The complex structure suggests specialized use in nanomedicine or biomolecular interaction studies.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Nitidine, is another methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid, similar to 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium but with different methoxy substitution (2,3-positions), and as a benzophenanthridine alkaloid it also possesses antitumor and antimalarial activity, can inhibit topoisomerases and telomerase to disrupt DNA replication, and its fluorescent properties aid in cellular imaging studies; due to its planar, rigid, and positively charged structural features, its primary activity in practice is as a DNA intercalator and cytotoxic agent, exhibiting strong anti-tumor properties, minor changes in the substitution pattern (from 1,2- to 2,3-dimethoxy) are critical in drug design to modulate the molecules charge distribution and steric bulk, which can alter its interaction strength and selectivity for specific DNA sequences or Topoisomerase isozymes, and this makes it valuable in the structure-activity relationship studies of novel anticancer agents.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 9-methoxy-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 4, is a pyridocarbazole derivative structurally related to the powerful natural antitumor agent Ellipticine; it is an optimized, selective lead that potently binds the GG internal loop, selectively inhibits RAN translation without affecting canonical translation, prevents hnRNP H sequestration, and visualizes RNA foci in cells (establishing the hairpin as the therapeutically relevant target), while its large planar aromatic system facilitates DNA intercalation and topoisomerase inhibition, a characteristic that makes it a highly promising candidate in the development of new generation chemotherapeutic agents for various solid tumors.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(6-methoxyimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl)-7-piperazin-1-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C34b, is a yellow coumarin-imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine hybrid containing a piperazine group, which features a coumarin core (BC ring), a 6-methoxyimidazo[1,2-a]pyridinyl DE ring, and a piperazinyl A ring; coumarin derivatives are researched for their anticoagulant and antitumor properties, and this molecule can selectively bind RNA single G bulges (e.g., RNA1) with high affinity, with its methoxy group stabilizing protonation to enhance ionic interactions with RNA while its planar BC-DE rings facilitating minor groove binding via -stacking and hydrogen bonding, and its structure also suggests potential application as a kinase inhibitor or a biological fluorescent tracer due to its extended conjugation and basicity.

RNA information: NRQ mutant is a G-base mutant of NRQ that prevents the formation of the G4 structure.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of 4b-2, is the quaternary ammonium salt based on the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. It is designed as a potent cytotoxic agent, likely acting as a DNA intercalator, and is investigated for its structure-activity relationship in antitumor drug development; this derivative is one of the strongest binders in its series for the NRQ, exhibits strong anti-proliferative activity in A375, yet has poor selectivity due to its binding to mutant and hairpin oligomers.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₆ oligonucleotide is a key in vitro model used to study the pathogenic CUG repeat expansion. Its repetitive sequence forms a stable stem-loop structure characterized by canonical GC base pairs and non-canonical, dynamic UU mismatches. This unique structural motif is the primary binding site for both the Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein and the designed small molecule ligands. The RNA was synthetically labeled with a TAMRA fluorophore at its 5' end for binding assays. In these assays, ligand binding to the UU mismatches induces a structural change that quenches the TAMRA fluorescence, allowing the direct measurement of binding affinity (K_D), thus establishing r(CUG)₆ as a critical tool for quantifying ligand-RNA interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]ethylamino]ethylamino]ethylamino]ethyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 10, is an acridine-triazine dimer with acridine substituted at position 4, and the linker is a longer polyethylamino chain containing four ethylamino units (an oligoamino linker), molecular formula C60H67N21O2, and this potent dimeric ligand demonstrated strong binding and inhibition with a notable Tm, a much lower KD than the monomer, and a significant bivalent effect. In terms of real-world activity, its core function is as an ultra-potent DNA intercalator/cross-linker and topoisomerase inhibitor. Compared to 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, the increase in linker chain length means the molecule carries more positive charges, which generally further enhances its non-specific electrostatic affinity for DNA and may alter the span across the DNA double helix, affecting DNA cross-linking efficiency and topoisomerase toxicity. The synthesis and study of such compounds aim to systematically explore the influence of linker structure on the activity and pharmacokinetics of multi-target drugs, to develop more effective anticancer therapies.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.
Small_molecule_information: tetrakis(4-methylbenzenesulfonic acid);5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)-21,23-dihydroporphyrin, also referred to as TMPyP4, is an organic salt containing a porphyrin macrocycle and toluenesulfonate counterions, which is a cationic porphyrin and a widely used pan-G-quadruplex binder that bound the r(G4C2) quadruplex with high affinity among the tested ligands and whose inability to inhibit RAN translation was particularly significant, proving that even high affinity for the quadruplex does not translate to functional activity against the pathogenic hairpin; its structure is based on the porphyrin parent skeleton: four pyrrole rings linked by methine bridges form a conjugated macrocycle, where the four meso-positions of the macrocycle are substituted with 1-methylpyridinium-4-yl groups, and in terms of biological function, porphyrin compounds, due to their unique structure and photosensitivity, are often used in photodynamic therapy, bioimaging probes, and as DNA-binding agents (e.g., TMPyP).

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)isoquinolin-4-amine, also referred to as a potent binder and confirmed active inhibitor of Dicer-mediated cleavage with favorable amine substitution, links the 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine core at the 6-position, via a methylene bridge and a secondary amine, to an isoquinolin-4-yl group. Isoquinoline is a fused aromatic heterocycle and a significant pharmacophore found in various medicinal agents. Isoquinoline derivatives are widely utilized in cardiovascular drugs, anticancer agents, and anti-inflammatory medicines. The structure combines two important nitrogen heterocycles through a flexible amine linkage, suggesting a potential for multi-target activity, possibly as a tyrosine kinase inhibitor or a GPCR ligand, making this compound a promising candidate for research in cardiovascular diseases or oncology.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[6-[2-(3-aminophenyl)-3H-benzimidazol-5-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]aniline, also referred to as Compound H1, is a complex bis-benzimidazole compound (a bis-benzimidazole analog derived from Hoechst 33258) with a structure composed of two benzimidazole units and two meta-aminophenyl units linked covalently, molecular formula C26H21N5; the molecule exhibits a polyheterocyclic structure that is highly conjugated and notably planar, featuring multiple amino and imidazole nitrogen atoms capable of extensive hydrogen bonding, and it retains the core DNA/RNA-binding benzimidazole scaffold of Hoechst 33258, which was rationally designed to target the expanded CUG repeat RNA (r(CUG)exp) that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and optimized through chemical-similarity screening to gain higher affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic 5CUG/3GUC RNA motif; in terms of real-world activity, this bis-benzimidazole structure is highly analogous to the structural characteristics of known DNA minor groove binders (e.g., Hoechst dyes), it is designed for high-affinity, specific binding to the DNA minor groove particularly in AT-rich regions, and it also has the ability to disrupt the toxic r(CUG)expMBNL1 complex in vitro, correct MBNL1-dependent splicing defects in DM1 patient-derived cells, disperse nuclear foci without global off-target splicing effects, restore a significant portion of the aberrant splicing of Clcn1 and Serca1 transcripts in the HSALR DM1 mouse model with good tolerance, minimal cytotoxicity and no overt microbiota disturbance, thus establishing itself as a lead small-molecule RNA binder that selectively rescues DM1 molecular phenotypes with potential for further development toward DM1 therapeutics, while it is primarily explored as a lead candidate for novel antitumor or antiviral drugs, acting by interfering with DNA replication, repair, and transcription, and also holds potential as a nuclear DNA stain in biological imaging.

RNA information: r(AAUU): This RNA serves as a model hairpin construct designed with a specific AU-rich sequence in its stem region, featuring a contiguous 5'-AAUU/3'-UUAA stretch. Its primary function is to act as a tool for probing the binding specificity and affinity of small molecules towards defined AU base pair orientations. The RNA was used in fluorescence-based binding assays, where it helped establish that certain benzamidine-based compounds, like molecule 2, display a significant binding preference for AU base pairs over GC-rich counterparts. Its thermodynamic stability is lower than that of GC-rich hairpins, which is a characteristic feature influencing its interaction with small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(5-phenylfuran-2-yl)benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 1, is a compound containing a mono-imidamide group and a furan-phenyl structure, with the formula C17H14N2O; it binds to both AU and GC base pairs with high affinity but lacks selectivity, also recognizing RNA internal loops in disease repeats, making it non-ideal for specific targeting. As a mono-cationic imidamide derivative, its biological activity is distinct from diamidines; it likely lacks the potent DNA minor groove binding of its dicationic counterparts. Such compounds are often researched as enzyme inhibitors. In real-world applications, mono-imidamidines are frequently developed as serine protease inhibitors (such as thrombin or trypsin) for potential use in anticoagulant or anti-inflammatory therapy.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-cyclopropyl-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 10, is an amide compound containing thienopyridine and cyclopropyl structures, modified from initial active analogs; its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a cyclopropylcarboxamide group at C2; in terms of biological function, Thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamides are a new class of antiplasmodial agents (anti-malarial) with strong antiparasitic activity, and this compound binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, exhibiting moderate affinity and lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 1,4-dihydroxy-5,8-bis[2-(2-hydroxyethylamino)ethylamino]anthracene-9,10-dione, also referred to as Mitoxantrone, is a synthetic anthraquinone derivative with the formula C22H28N4O6, originally developed as a DNA-reactive antineoplastic and immunosuppressive agent, its core structure is the anthraquinone (anthracene-9,10-dione) backbone, substituted with hydroxyl groups at positions 1 and 4, and two hydroxyethylamino-ethylamino side chains at positions 5 and 8, it is a cytotoxic agent that primarily acts by intercalating into double-stranded DNA (inserting between the base pairs of the DNA double helix) and forming stable cross-links with topoisomerase II, thereby blocking DNA unwinding and RNA synthesis, disrupting DNA replication and repair, leading to cancer cell death, this planar anthraquinone core also confers non-selective affinity for structured RNA motifs including the HIV-1 Rev-response-element (RRE) subdomain IIB, in HIV-1 cellular assays it inhibits viral replication by acting primarily on pre-integration steps (integration/uncoating), while at higher near-cytotoxic doses it additionally suppresses LTR-driven transcription and NF-B activity most likely through generalized nucleic-acid intercalation, in real-world clinical use, Mitoxantrone is mainly employed in the treatment of various malignancies including Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), non-Hodgkins lymphoma, and prostate cancer, it is also used to treat progressive multiple sclerosis, though its use is carefully monitored due to the potential for cardiotoxicity, and the compound exemplifies an anthraquinone scaffold that can be repurposed or optimized to yield dual-action antiviral leads targeting both viral DNA integration and RNA-regulatory elements.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(1-methylpiperidin-1-ium-1-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is another dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions. It possesses a 1-methylpiperidin-1-ium quaternary ammonium side chain and a 2-piperidin-1-ylethyl substituted chain. The presence of two piperidine rings enhances its lipophilicity and membrane permeability. In the pharmaceutical field, this class of dicationic polycyclic compounds is often utilized as live-cell nuclear stains or DNA electrophoresis visualization reagents due to their fluorescent properties and cell permeability. As a potential drug, its potent antiproliferative activity makes it a focus in oncology research, especially as a precursor for chemotherapeutic agents targeting nucleic acids.
